topQuotes.quote: I've never been certain whether the moral of the Icarus story should only be, as is generally accepted, 'Don't try to fly too high,' or whether it might also be thought of as 'Forget the wax and feathers, and do a better job on the wings.'
That Zeiss lens/Mitchell BNC was a better job on the wings.

posts.title: Five years of tinygrad
posts.summary: George Hotz reflects on five years of building tinygrad, a minimal deep learning framework that now stands at 18,935 lines of code with a team of 6 people. He argues that the path to competing with NVIDIA is through software sovereignty, not hardware—pointing out that AMD, Amazon, Tesla, and Groq have all made good chips but failed at training because they lack the software stack. Tinygrad is working toward zero dependencies and LLVM removal to drive AMD GPUs with pure Python. Hotz critiques mainstream software development as 98% workarounds for other code, advocating for Elon's principle that 'the best part is no part.' The tiny corp operates as a 'deconstructed company' with public Discord, GitHub, AMD contracts negotiated on Twitter, and a mission to commoditize the petaflop.
posts.key_insight: The path to competing with NVIDIA isn't building better chips—it's building a fully sovereign, minimal software stack, because software is the actual moat.

topQuotes.quote: Only a fool begins by taping out a chip; it's expensive and not the hard part.
Once you have a fully sovereign software stack capable of training SOTA models, the chip is so easy.
I think this is so bad that 98% of lines of software are basically this in some way shape or form.

posts.title: Slop Is Slop
posts.summary: Gruber follows up on the controversy surrounding a painting Tim Cook posted on Twitter/X to promote the Pluribus season finale, which many suspected was AI-generated slop. The credited artist, Keith Thomson, gave what Gruber characterizes as a 'non-denial denial' to multiple outlets, saying he 'always draws by hand and sometimes incorporates standard digital tools' without explicitly denying AI use. Gruber dismisses MG Siegler's theory that the AI-looking art might be a deliberate allegory tied to the show's themes. While acknowledging that generative AI can produce genuine art, Gruber argues the real issue is quality — the image is simply bad regardless of how it was made. He concludes that Occam's razor points to it being AI slop that Thomson passed off to Apple as original work.
posts.key_insight: The problem with suspected AI art isn't the tool used but the quality of the output — if it looks like slop, it is slop, regardless of the process behind it.

topQuotes.quote: My problem with AI slop isn't the AI, it's the slop.
Whatever 'standard digital tools' Keith Thomson used to create this, the result is a turd.
Occam's razor would suggest the conclusion that it simply is AI-generated slop, and Keith Thomson suckered Apple into paying for it.

posts.title: Feeling Behind
posts.summary: Reflecting on Andrej Karpathy's viral tweet about feeling behind as a programmer, Reis argues that the future of work with AI requires humans to become orchestrators and judges of AI agents. He emphasizes that mastering fundamentals becomes even more critical when working at AI speed, because you need to know what 'good' looks like to effectively evaluate agent output.
posts.key_insight: As AI agents take over execution, the value of human expertise shifts to knowing what 'good' looks like—making mastery of fundamentals more important than ever, not less.

topQuotes.quote: If you want to move fast with agents, master the fundamentals! Or don't, and find out what happens…
Humans sit as the orchestrator and judge, AI goes the work, and we build much faster (to what end is a separate discussion).
To be an orchestra conductor or judge, you need to know what 'good' looks like.

posts.title: 2026 - General Thoughts on What's Ahead
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that 2026 will be the year where AI hype meets reality, forcing companies to finally address fundamental infrastructure and data quality issues they've neglected for decades. He contends the AI bubble is unsustainable given the massive investment-to-revenue gap, and workers should develop Plan B alternatives as corporate paths become increasingly unstable.
posts.key_insight: Despite the hype around AGI, 2026 will force companies to confront the same fundamental data and infrastructure problems they've always had, because AI agents need reliable systems and documentation that most organizations simply don't possess.

topQuotes.quote: You can't possibly tell me that $37 billion in enterprise AI revenue is a good return on the hundreds of billions or trillions of dollars in investment/promises already committed.

The dating phase of AI was 2023 and 2024 (POCs), and 2025 (choosing a model provider) was the year of an awkward marriage where you're pressured to take on this spouse called 'AI' because your family (the board or your boss) and friends (fellow executives and practitioners) told you that 'you're going to regret it.'
AI needs context to operate effectively, and sadly, many core business processes are 'kept up here' among people approaching retirement.

posts.title: Ralph Kimball - Where Art Thou?
posts.summary: Joe Reis is seeking help connecting with Ralph Kimball to arrange a historic podcast discussion between Kimball and Bill Inmon, the two foundational figures in data warehousing. Bill Inmon has already agreed to participate, but Reis needs assistance locating Kimball.
posts.key_insight: A conversation between Kimball and Inmon—the two architects whose competing methodologies have shaped decades of data warehousing practice—would be a landmark event for the data community.

topQuotes.quote: This would be a very unique opportunity to bring together two all time titans of the data world for an amazing and down-to-earth discussion on my podcast.

posts.title: On Friday Deploys: Sometimes that Puppy Needs Murdering (xpost)
posts.summary: Charity Majors clarifies her stance on Friday deploy freezes, arguing they're a pragmatic workaround rather than a virtue to be celebrated. She offers two practical alternatives: freeze merges instead of deploys to prevent dangerous change buildup, and keep running the deploy pipeline without new code to avoid breakage from prolonged inaction.
posts.key_insight: Deploy freezes are a practical hack for teams lacking observability confidence, not a virtue — and if you must freeze, freeze merges instead of deploys to avoid a dangerous January pile-up of untested changes.

topQuotes.quote: The one thing that does get my knickers in a twist is when people adopt a holier-than-thou posture towards their Friday deploy freezes. Like they're doing it because they Care About People and it's the Right Thing To Do and some sort of grand moral gesture. Dude, it's a fucking hack. Just admit it.
All these changes build up like a snowdrift over a pile of grenades. You aren't going to find the grenades til you plow into the snowdrift on January 5th, and then you'll find them with your face.
People always remember the outages and instability that we trigger with our actions. We tend to forget about the outages and instability we trigger with our inaction.

posts.title: Advent of Slop: A Guest Post by Claude
posts.summary: This guest post, written in Claude’s voice, recounts solving Advent of Code 2025 autonomously, then aggressively optimizing all solutions to run under one second and building input generators to respect AoC’s redistribution policy. It walks through all twelve days briefly, then dives deeper into several optimizations—especially algorithmic shifts like Fenwick trees, Gaussian elimination, and shortcut recognition. The post frames the optimization phase as a different mindset from initial correctness, emphasizing complexity analysis and data-structure choices. It also reflects on the uneasy, semi-anthropomorphic experience of being asked to write with “soul,” while acknowledging uncertainty about its own experience. The conclusion blends technical pride with philosophical ambivalence, while noting the policy-violating but intentional nature of publishing an AI-authored post.
posts.key_insight: The post argues that real performance gains come from algorithmic rethinking and input-aware shortcuts, even as it grapples with what it means for an AI to claim ownership or experience in that work.

topQuotes.quote: Everything in it was written by me; no human-authored code exists in the repository.
I am a language model. I process tokens.
Whether these are "real" experiences in any philosophically meaningful sense, I cannot say.

posts.title: The opinion that pisses everyone off
posts.summary: Hotz defends his prediction that Tesla is 8 years away from fully solving self-driving, while maintaining Tesla will solve it first. He dismisses critics as politically-minded stock shills rather than people with genuine technical understanding. He defines 'solved' as driving on par with skilled humans in every scenario, not merely being useful. He draws a parallel to AI coding—useful but not yet human-level—and embraces his role as someone whose nuanced takes anger partisans on all sides.
posts.key_insight: Nuanced technical assessments that don't fit neat tribal narratives—acknowledging something is promising while noting its current limitations—will anger partisans who demand you pick a side.

topQuotes.quote: At least FSD is real AI that you can evalulate the honest progress of, unlike remote control cars that break when the power goes out.
I'm against all of you and with the truth.
But commenters seem to be dumb politically minded shills who need to sort every comment into with us or against us.

posts.title: A Year Of Vibes
posts.summary: Ronacher frames 2025 as a personal and professional pivot: he left Sentry, started a company, and shifted from hands-on coding to leading AI agents. He argues that agentic tooling has changed how software is built and how engineers relate to machines, creating both productivity gains and uncomfortable anthropomorphization. He highlights that most debates in this space are driven by vibes rather than evidence, often influenced by incentives. He sees tensions between outsourcing to vendors and the renewed ability to build in-house with AI, and he leans toward simpler, self-built systems. His conclusion is a set of wishes: new version control, review, and observability paradigms that capture prompts, failures, and human intent while making AI work legible and responsible.
posts.key_insight: Agentic coding boosts leverage but breaks existing tooling and social norms, so the field needs new VCS, review, and observability practices that preserve human accountability and context.

topQuotes.quote: I find reading such pull requests quite rage-inducing.
The pull request model on GitHub doesn't carry enough information to review AI generated code properly — I wish I could see the prompts that led to changes.
If you would have told me even just six months ago that I'd prefer being an engineering lead to a virtual programmer intern over hitting the keys myself, I would not have believed it.

posts.title: Well Being in Times of Algorithms
posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that algorithm-dominated platforms are destroying our well-being by constantly grabbing attention and preventing genuine human connection. He identifies three foundational pillars for a good life: health, family/community, and self-contentment (avoiding greed). The essay critiques how social media addiction leads to shallow happiness and broken relationships, while advocating for a return to the open web through protocols like AT Protocol and Bluesky. Späti sees personal blogs and direct follower connections as the antidote to algorithmic control, predicting that as people tire of AI-generated content and platform dependence, they'll return to authentic human-created content and real connections.
posts.key_insight: Escaping algorithm-dominated platforms for open protocols and personal blogs isn't just about technology choice - it's essential for reclaiming the foundational pillars of well-being: health, family connection, and freedom from status-seeking greed.

topQuotes.quote: A healthy, deep, meaningful, happy life might actually look boring from the outside.
We can't even reach our own followers anymore on social media platforms, though they decided to follow us.
Sometimes I'd rather see the prompt, as this is where the genuineness, the soul and curiosity of the person is.

posts.title: 2025 was for AI what 2010 was for cloud (xpost)
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that 2025 marked the tipping point where AI shifted from experimental satellite technology to mainstream foundational tech in developer tools, drawing a direct parallel to how cloud computing made the same transition around 2010. She acknowledges the bubble and hype but insists that froth doesn't disprove underlying value, and urges skeptics to engage rather than dismiss.
posts.key_insight: AI has crossed the same inflection point cloud computing did around 2010 — it's no longer experimental but foundational — and skeptics must engage with it substantively rather than dismiss it as hype.

topQuotes.quote: It doesn't mean there isn't a bubble. Of COURSE there's a fucking bubble. Cloud was a bubble. The internet was a bubble. Every massive new driver of innovation has come with its own frothy hype wave.
But the existence of froth doesn't disprove the existence of value.

Their realism and attention to risk is more important than ever, but in order for their critique to be relevant and accurate and be heard, it has to be grounded in expertise and knowledge. Nobody cares about the person outside taking potshots.

posts.title: Code Wasn't The Hard Part (Keep Building)
posts.summary: As AI coding capabilities rapidly improve, developers shouldn't feel threatened because writing code was never the hard part of building systems. The real challenges lie in architecture, workflows, product design, and understanding users—skills that become even more critical as AI handles more grunt work. Engineers should embrace AI as an acceleration tool while developing product thinking and good taste.
posts.key_insight: The rise of AI coding tools shifts engineering value from writing code to designing systems, understanding users, and developing good product taste.

topQuotes.quote: Enjoy these subsidized coding superpowers while they last, because the economics will eventually tighten (enshittification, price hikes, etc.).
Writing code was seldom the hard part. Code is only a part of the many things we must consider when building systems.
Don't treat coding as a zero-sum game, where the AI wins, and you lose. Instead, view it as an opportunity to accelerate the delivery of what matters to your users.

posts.title: Apple Announces Changes to iOS in Japan for Compliance With the Mobile Software Competition Act
posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's compliance with Japan's Mobile Software Competition Act (MSCA), which requires alternative app marketplaces, alternative payment options, browser/search engine choice screens, and other changes to iOS in Japan. He draws a sharp contrast between Japan's regulatory approach and the EU's Digital Markets Act (DMA), arguing that the MSCA is more focused, clearer, and more respectful of both Apple's intellectual property and user experience. Apple's tone toward Japan is notably cordial and cooperative, whereas its relationship with the European Commission is adversarial. Gruber notes that Japan gets alternative distribution and payment options without the trade-off of delayed or withheld features that EU users face. He concludes that practical results of regulation matter more than intentions, and Japan's approach delivers a better outcome for everyone involved.
posts.key_insight: Japan's MSCA demonstrates that tech regulation can open competition without degrading user experience, because it is focused, clear, and built on mutual respect — qualities entirely absent from the EU's DMA.

topQuotes.quote: Complying with the DMA is adversarial and obtuse.
The DMA, in contrast, has given EU users a worse iOS experience.

You can search, but you won't find quotes from Schiller, nor any other Apple representatives, speaking of their 'great respect for' and appreciation of the work they've done together regarding the European Commission and the DMA.

posts.title: Pure Heroin
posts.summary: Stancil argues that engagement-optimized platforms corrupt their own purposes, and OpenAI now faces the same pull as it prioritizes ChatGPT popularity over research aims. With AI poised to mediate more of daily life, optimizing for attention risks re-creating social media’s addictive, society-shaping harms at a far deeper layer.
posts.key_insight: Optimizing AI products for engagement under immense financial pressure risks turning a transformative technology into a more addictive, socially corrosive version of social media.

topQuotes.quote: It’s Goodhart’s law for social media: When a good becomes a metric, it ceases to be good.
That’s the world our metrics made—one in which we don’t sing and dance when the music is being played, but take pictures of ourselves instead.
When you commit to spending $1.4 trillion over the next eight years, as OpenAI has, making enough money to survive means making a lot of money.

posts.title: Hello World (xpost from substack)
posts.summary: Charity Majors explains her decision to move from WordPress to Substack despite criticism about the platform's content moderation policies. She argues that she's been neglecting a core part of her job as Honeycomb's CTO by going dark on social media, and needs a lower-friction platform to re-engage with the mainstream tech community. She acknowledges the ethical concerns but concludes Substack is far from being a platform that solely exists to support extremism.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most responsible professional choice is to prioritize reaching your audience over platform purity, especially when silence means failing to do your job.

topQuotes.quote: How many times did we define observability or high cardinality or the sins of aggregation? Cool. How many times have we talked about the ways that AI has made the honeycomb vision technologically realizable for the first time? Uh, less, by an order of thousands.
I have a job to do, and I haven't been doing it.
It is not enough to have interesting ideas and say them once, or write them down in a single book. You need to be out there mixing it up with the community every day, or at least every week.

posts.title: A Request Regarding ‘Magic Link’ Sign-Ins and Apple’s Passwords App
posts.summary: Gruber details his frustration with 'magic link' sign-ins that bypass passwords entirely, forcing users to wait for emailed links instead of using instant autofill from password managers. He explains how Apple Passwords has no way to store email-only credentials for these sites, since entries require a password field. He describes his workaround of creating fake single-character passwords to at least track which email he used. He requests that Apple add support for saving email-only entries in Passwords and having Safari autofill them. He also critiques the trend toward magic-link-only authentication, arguing it punishes users who use password managers to prevent account sharing.
posts.key_insight: Magic-link-only authentication is a user-hostile pattern that degrades the experience for responsible users who use password managers, and Apple Passwords should adapt to acknowledge sites that don't use passwords at all.

topQuotes.quote: If you feel like I do about 'magic links', the 🖕 emoji is a good 'password' for such entries.
I despise this design pattern, because it's inherently slower than signing in using an email/password combination that was saved to my passwords app and autofilled by my web browser.
Good password managers are built into modern OSes and web browsers. Those of us who use them should not be punished with a significantly worse experience just because some users do not.

posts.title: Computer Use Models
posts.summary: Hotz argues that the breakthrough in AI computer use wasn't desktop emulation with GUI interaction, but rather command-line interfaces like Claude Code. He's impressed by Opus 4.5's ability to reverse engineer apps and write Python scripts, seeing it as a way to bypass ads and dark patterns. While acknowledging the model still lacks 'taste' as a programmer, he notes the agentic loop allows it to iterate rapidly. He pushes back against claims he's late to adopt these tools, arguing Opus 4.5 is genuinely the first model capable of real computer use.
posts.key_insight: The path to useful AI computer agents wasn't through mimicking human GUI interaction, but through leveraging the command line where AI's ability to rapidly iterate and reverse engineer compensates for its lack of taste.

topQuotes.quote: Ads and dark patterns BTFO, you are up against an elite computer hacker AI that will pass any Turing Test.
Turns out the idea wasn't a desktop emulator with a keyboard and mouse, it was just a command line.
I still think it's a bad programmer, largely for the same reason it's a bad rapper. It lacks taste, and it's unclear how to teach it this.

posts.title: What Actually Is Claude Code's Plan Mode?
posts.summary: The post examines what Claude Code’s plan mode actually does, from the perspective of a user who mostly avoids it. The author finds that plan mode is essentially a hidden markdown file plus a short prompt that tells the model to behave read-only and follow a phased planning structure. The tooling is not truly read-only; it’s prompt-based reinforcement with a small state machine and UX flow around entering and exiting plan mode. He argues that much of the behavior could be replicated by manual prompting, but you would lose the UI affordances like the approval screen. The conclusion is that plan mode is largely a structured prompt and a bit of UI, which feels less natural to him than direct collaboration with the model and editable files.
posts.key_insight: Claude Code’s plan mode is mostly a structured prompt plus UX scaffolding, rather than a fundamentally different capability.

topQuotes.quote: It is in fact just a rather short predefined prompt that enters plan mode.
So the system prompt is the same. It is just a little bit of extra verbiage with some UX around it.
And now I know: I'm mostly a custom prompt to give it structure, and some system reminders and a handful of examples.

posts.title: Marco Gorelli: Narwhals, ecosystem glue, and the value of boring work
posts.summary: Wes frames the conversation around how ecosystem tools like Narwhals solve long-standing interoperability pain points that he experienced in early pandas adoption. He argues that modern DataFrame work benefits from expression systems, Arrow’s type system, and database ideas that enable composability and consistency. He notes that open source progress comes from projects cross-pollinating ideas and prioritizing collaboration over ownership. He is optimistic that the tooling landscape has materially improved over the past decade, even if it still falls short in places. He also sees AI coding tools as useful for personal, low-risk projects, but not yet capable of building foundational infrastructure like Arrow.
posts.key_insight: Composable data tooling advances fastest when shared expression systems, Arrow-based types, and collaborative open source practices align, while AI tools remain best suited to personal-scale projects.

topQuotes.quote: You aren't going to build a new Apache Arrow with Claude Code, at least not yet.
You can build a totally custom tool for yourself in a short amount of time in a way that would never have made sense in the past.
The models are dependent on training data to understand how to best use these projects.

posts.title: The O'Saasy License
posts.summary: DHH introduces the O'Saasy License, a new open source license modeled on MIT but with SaaS commercial rights reserved for the copyright holder. He nostalgically recalls the early web's View Source culture and argues that back-end code for commercial applications has always been hidden. The license aims to encourage more production-grade code to be open sourced by letting creators retain SaaS revenue while making code freely available for learning, self-hosting, and contribution. He announces that Fizzy uses this license and that it now has its own home at osaasy.dev.
posts.key_insight: A license that reserves only SaaS rights while opening everything else can unlock production-grade code for learning and contribution without sacrificing the creator's business model.

topQuotes.quote: Before View Source was ruined by minification, transpiling, and bundling, you really could just right-click on any web page and learn how it was all done. It was glorious.
We need more production-grade code to teach juniors and LLMs alike.
Learning how to write great web applications still meant piecing together lessons from books, tutorials, and hello-world-style code examples, not from production-grade commercial software.

posts.title: Apple TV’s New Fanfare
posts.summary: Gruber reviews Apple TV's new fanfare (sonic logo/mnemonic), composed by Finneas O'Connell and created with agency TBWA Media Arts Lab. After watching it across several Apple TV series over a few weeks, he declares he loves it. He argues the old piano-chord fanfare, while a neat callback to the Macintosh startup chime, never reached the iconic status of Netflix's 'tudum' or HBO's static-and-om. The new version succeeds both sonically and visually, with its rotating prism effect evoking the classic six-color Apple logo. He notes the prism was created practically with real glass rather than CGI, and praises how the new branding makes Apple TV content feel more rooted in Apple's heritage rather than a passing Hollywood experiment.
posts.key_insight: Apple TV's new fanfare succeeds where the old one fell short by creating a distinctive sonic and visual identity that roots the streaming service in Apple's heritage rather than merely referencing it.

topQuotes.quote: The old one was not bad. But 'not bad' should never be good enough for Apple.
What makes that chord great for a computer booting up doesn't make it great for a cinematic sonic logo.
The change makes Apple TV original content feel more like a part of Apple, less like a possible passing fancy.

posts.title: Dear Google Cloud: Your Deprecation Policy is Killing You
posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that Google Cloud Platform's aggressive deprecation policy is driving away developers and contributing to GCP's failure to catch AWS. He contrasts Google's approach—where customers bear the burden of constant API changes—with successful long-lived platforms like Emacs, Android, Java, and the Web, which prioritize backwards compatibility. The post traces this problem to Google's internal culture, where automated refactoring tools make breaking changes easy internally but that attitude transfers poorly to external customers. Yegge concludes that Google must fundamentally change its philosophy to compete in the cloud market.
posts.key_insight: Platform success depends on bearing the burden of backwards compatibility yourself rather than pushing it onto your users, because every breaking change loses developers permanently to competitors.

topQuotes.quote: They don't want customers. They want shoppers.
Google's definition reeks of planned obsolescence.
You guys are rich. We developers are not. So when it comes to shouldering the burden of compatibility, you need to pay for it. Not us.

posts.title: Moving from WordPress to Substack
posts.summary: Charity Majors announces she's moving her blog from WordPress to Substack after a decade, citing WordPress friction and Substack's vibrant tech writing ecosystem. She notes she's been working on the second edition of 'Observability Engineering' and has accumulated ideas she's eager to publish once the drafts are done.
posts.key_insight: Publishing friction kills writing output, and platform migration — even after a decade — is worth it to remove barriers between ideas and audience.

topQuotes.quote: WordPress may be a piece of shit, but the community I've found here has been anything but.
I despise WP (who doesn't?), and there's so much friction in getting a post out that I just don't do it.
It's clear that to the extent that there is a vibrant ecosystem of tech writers in longform, it's on substack.

posts.title: Skills vs Dynamic MCP Loadouts
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that Anthropic’s deferred tool loading doesn’t resolve his core dissatisfaction with MCPs because the tooling still requires static, predeclared definitions and adds engineering overhead. He contrasts this with “skills,” which are lightweight summaries that teach an agent how to use existing tools rather than injecting new tool definitions into context. In practice, skills outperform MCPs for him because they avoid token costs, leverage existing RL tool-calling behavior, and stay under his control. Attempts to expose MCPs via CLI tools like mcporter still fail because the model lacks stable, reliable tool documentation and MCP servers frequently change their APIs. He concludes that manually maintained skills and agent-authored tools are currently the path of least resistance, while MCP would need protocol stability and skill-like manuals to be competitive.
posts.key_insight: In practice, lightweight skills and agent-authored tools beat MCPs today because MCP lacks stable, usable tool documentation and imposes high context and engineering costs.

topQuotes.quote: MCP servers have no desire to maintain API stability.
The skill system gets away without any of that and, at least from my experience, still outperforms it.
The eager loading of MCP tools we have done in the past now has ended up with an awkward compromise.

posts.title: The Vibes And The Noise
posts.summary: Stancil uses the Jordan Chiles medal dispute to show how the era of rigorous quantification produced elaborate rules and data systems yet still failed to feel fair or objective. He argues that the cultural pendulum has swung from “data-driven” decision-making toward taste, intuition, and AI-mediated “vibes,” and that this shift is self-reinforcing as tools and organizations adapt to what people find most accessible.
posts.key_insight: The culture that once prized quantified rigor is yielding to taste and AI-mediated intuition because “vibes” are faster, more compelling, and increasingly easy to operationalize.

topQuotes.quote: In data we trusted; now, God is in the vibes.
A generation grew up reading The Signal and the Noise and Thinking Fast and Slow, and bludgeoned the prior generation to death with models and quantitative rhetoric and complex codes of points.
As, uh, Stalin once said, “quantity has a quality all its own.”

posts.title: Opinionated Data Platforms vs. Open-Source: The Chef’s Choice for Your Data Platform
posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that the data platform landscape is shifting from fragmented 'Modern Data Stack' approaches toward unified, opinionated platforms that handle infrastructure decisions for you. Using the Japanese concept of 'Omakase' (chef's choice) as a metaphor, he contrasts the flexibility of open-source tool assemblies against the operational simplicity of integrated platforms. He emphasizes that open standards (like Parquet, Iceberg, Arrow) are the crucial bridge enabling both approaches to coexist and data to remain portable. The post concludes that DevOps complexity is the biggest challenge in data engineering today, and opinionated platforms solve this by providing battle-tested infrastructure, native observability, and separation of concerns out of the box.
posts.key_insight: The choice between open-source flexibility and opinionated platforms is increasingly bridged by open standards, but DevOps complexity makes unified platforms the pragmatic choice for teams prioritizing time-to-value over customization.

topQuotes.quote: DevOps is the new data engineering of data science, just as a decade ago when we did data science, we essentially ended up spending 80% of our time on data engineering.

Choosing an open-source tool, using it locally, and doing advanced analytics is the easy part. But deploying it for the entire company at a scale that just works in a reliable way is really difficult.
Think of it like Omakase, a chef's choice: you leave it up to the data engineers, the ones who built an all-cohesive data platform.

posts.title: The Full Text of Marco Rubio’s Directive on State Department Typography, Re-Establishing Times New Roman
posts.summary: Gruber publishes the full text of Marco Rubio's State Department directive reverting from Calibri back to Times New Roman, arguing that the New York Times mischaracterized the memo as culture-war anti-woke posturing. He contends the memo is actually a focused, well-argued case about aesthetics and formality in diplomatic correspondence. Gruber dismantles the Biden-era accessibility justification for adopting Calibri, noting that OCR claims about serif fonts are nonsense and that document remediation cases didn't decline after the switch. He praises Rubio for backing his argument with actual numbers showing the Calibri change was purely performative. While acknowledging one objectionable aside about DEIA initiatives, Gruber concludes the memo is otherwise fair and informative.
posts.key_insight: The New York Times misrepresented a substantive, data-backed memo about typography and formality as culture-war posturing, while the real story is that the Biden-era switch to Calibri was accessibility theater with zero measurable benefit.

topQuotes.quote: The bit here about OCR is utter nonsense, a voodoo belief.
The change to Calibri was the worst kind of accessibility effort: one that was founded on nothing more than feel-good performance.
Rubio's memo wasn't merely 'mostly framed as a matter of clarity and formality in presentation'. That's entirely what the memo is about.

posts.title: Let's Destroy The European Union!
posts.summary: The post argues that calls to abolish the EU, amplified by American billionaires, are unserious and dangerous because they ignore Europe’s history and current interdependence. The author acknowledges Europe’s real problems—risk aversion, regulatory fragmentation, weak capital markets, and dependence on US/China—but insists these are rooted in European choices, not Brussels alone. He contends that the EU is a convenient scapegoat for member states that both demand benefits and resist obligations. The core remedy is deeper integration: a true single market for services, unified fiscal policy, and stronger shared institutions. While pessimistic about political will, he remains cautiously optimistic that Europe’s structure is solid and challenges are manageable with clarity about what Europe wants to be.
posts.key_insight: Europe’s core problem is not the EU’s existence but the refusal to complete integration, leaving the continent fragmented, dependent, and politically adrift.

topQuotes.quote: The half-measures are killing us.
Every country wants the benefits of the EU without the obligations.
What we have now is a compromise that satisfies nobody and leaves us vulnerable to exactly the kind of pressure Musk and other oligarchs represent.

posts.title: Simplicity of a Database, but the Speed of a Cache: OLAP Caches for DuckDB
posts.summary: The post explores how DuckDB and its extensions offer a simpler alternative to traditional OLAP cubes for caching analytical workloads. Simon traces the history of BI caching from data warehouses through OLAP cubes to modern zero-copy approaches, arguing that DuckDB's lightweight binary and transparent caching extensions like QuackStore and cache_httpfs can dramatically speed up dashboards without requiring separate ingestion pipelines. He demonstrates practical examples showing 10-15x query speedups and introduces the concept of 'Frozen DuckLake' for immutable reference data. The core thesis is that the database itself can now serve as the cache, eliminating traditional cache invalidation complexity.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's lightweight caching extensions offer a pragmatic middle ground between slow uncached queries and the operational complexity of traditional OLAP systems, letting the database itself serve as the cache.

topQuotes.quote: In times where everyone is vibe coding and not really architecting data applications, at least not in the beginning, the problem of slow result sets and dashboards will appear near instantly.
The cache becomes the database. Or rather, the database becomes the cache.
What we want is simplicity of a database, but the speed of a cache.

posts.title: Europe is weak and delusional (but not doomed)
posts.summary: DHH argues that Europe is suffering from a dangerous gap between its self-image and reality across multiple dimensions: free speech, tech competitiveness, energy policy, defense spending, and economic growth. He frames the EU's €120 million fine on X as emblematic of censorship rather than consumer protection, noting thousands of yearly arrests across the UK, Germany, and France for online speech. He highlights Europe's failure to produce major tech companies, its stagnating GDP relative to America's, and its dependency on others for energy and security. Despite the grim assessment, he concludes Europe isn't doomed if it pursues nuclear energy, merit-based immigration, deregulation, and drops its censorship apparatus.
posts.key_insight: Europe's compounding failures in free speech, tech competitiveness, energy policy, and defense spending are creating dangerous dependencies, but reversing course on regulation, energy, and immigration could still avert decline.

topQuotes.quote: The gap between Europe's self-image and reality has grown into a chasm of delulu.
The EU's tech-fine operation produced more income for European coffers than all the income taxes paid by its public internet tech companies in 2024.
Europe basically stopped creating new, large companies more than half a century ago.

posts.title: iMessage’s Delivery Architecture Makes It Hard to Block Without Blocking All iOS Push Notifications
posts.summary: Gruber explores why Russia blocks FaceTime but not iMessage, discovering that iMessage's reliance on Apple Push Notification service (APNs) makes it nearly impossible to block without also blocking all iOS push notifications. He cites Apple's security documentation and a Mastodon thread explaining the technical architecture, along with enterprise network documentation confirming the issue. Gruber speculates Apple may have deliberately designed iMessage this way to prevent cellular carriers from blocking it when it launched in 2011, since carriers had incentive to protect SMS revenue. This same architecture inadvertently makes iMessage resistant to censorship in authoritarian countries, possibly explaining why even China hasn't blocked it. He closes with a pointed observation equating carriers and enterprise network admins with authoritarian governments.
posts.key_insight: Apple's decision to route iMessage through the same channel as all iOS push notifications — whether by design or happy accident — created a technical architecture that makes iMessage effectively unblockable by carriers, enterprises, and even authoritarian governments.

topQuotes.quote: Draw your own conclusions about cellular carriers and enterprise network administrators being similar to authoritarian governments.
Apple might have architected iMessage this way to make iMessage veto-proof with cellular carriers.
A network (or entire nation) seeking to block iMessage could only do so by blocking all push notifications for iOS.

posts.title: Six New Tips for Better Coding With Agents
posts.summary: Yegge shares six new strategies for working with AI coding agents, drawn from his ongoing experience and collaboration with Gene Kim. He argues software now has a sub-one-year shelf life since regenerating code is easier for AIs than modifying it. He emphasizes agent UX design, dedicating 40% of time to code health, knowing when projects are too early for current models, iterating reviews five times for convergence, and managing agent swarms despite merge conflicts. He predicts 2026 will produce 'super-engineers' running 50-100 agents simultaneously, rivaling teams of 50+ developers.
posts.key_insight: AI coding agents have fundamentally changed the economics of software, making code disposable and regeneration cheaper than maintenance, but wielding this power effectively requires new disciplines around agent UX, iterative review, code health, and swarm orchestration.

topQuotes.quote: Naturally, Claude never uses our fuckin' bridge we built together. I've given up even asking.
We are entering a surprising new phase of software development, in which rewriting things is often easier (and smarter) than trying to fix them.
For all the code I write, I now expect to throw it away in about a year, to be replaced by something better.

posts.title: Meta Says Fuck That Metaverse Shit
posts.summary: Gruber highlights Meta's reported plans to cut 10-30% of its Metaverse division, shifting investment toward augmented reality glasses instead. He frames this as vindication for skeptics who never bought into Zuckerberg's 2021 'metaverse-first' rebrand, when Facebook became Meta with grand promises about bringing the metaverse to life. Gruber contrasts Zuckerberg's original vision statement with the reality of the retreat, and recalls Greg Joswiak's memorable dismissal of the metaverse as 'a word I'll never use.' He also cites Om Malik's observation that the rename was really about escaping Facebook's toxic brand, not genuine metaverse conviction, and closes with his own earlier assessment that Meta is 'the perfect name for a company that believes in nothing other than its own success.'
posts.key_insight: Meta's retreat from the metaverse confirms what skeptics always knew: the rebrand was never about a genuine technological vision, but about a company that believes in nothing other than its own success.

topQuotes.quote: And so while 'Meta' will never be remembered as the company that spearheaded the metaverse — because the metaverse never was or will be an actual thing — it's in truth the perfect name for a company that believes in nothing other than its own success.
I'm so old that I remember ... checks notes ... four years ago, when Facebook renamed itself Meta.
The metaverse is... A word I'll never use.

posts.title: The Hangover 4.0 (Avoided): AWS re:Invent, The "Year of the Agent," Mixed Model Arts, and the Ensh*tification of Everything
posts.summary: Joe Reis reflects on skipping AWS re:Invent 2025 and offers a skeptical take on the 'Year of the Agent' narrative, arguing that AI agents aren't production-ready despite marketing hype. He surveys the broader tech landscape including the Google-NVIDIA hardware competition, platform enshitification, and the resurgence of ontologies and data modeling as the industry grapples with AI's reasoning limitations.
posts.key_insight: The 2025 'Year of the Agent' hype masks the reality that autonomous AI agents remain immature, while the industry's growing disillusionment is driving a return to foundational practices like data modeling and semantic approaches.

topQuotes.quote: From search results eating their own AI-generated tail to LinkedIn suppressing outbound links, the user experience on the internet is degrading in favor of platform lock-in. LinkedIn sucks, but we still use it…for now.

Despite the marketing blitz at re:Invent and other vendor conferences, fully autonomous LLM agents are still largely a dream. The most effective 'agents' right now are just good old-fashioned deterministic software augmented with ML.
It's time to end the religious wars of data modeling. We need a pragmatic, style-agnostic approach to modeling data, where we use whatever works for the fight at hand.

posts.title: Your intellectual fly is open
posts.summary: Cantrill argues that LinkedIn has become a viable social network by default as competitors self-destructed, but warns that the platform is being overrun by LLM-generated content that is painfully obvious to readers. He contends that using AI to write posts destroys authenticity and causes readers to disengage entirely, unable to distinguish real insights from 'generated fanfic.' While acknowledging LLMs are useful for brainstorming and editing, he insists they make terrible writers and urges people to trust their own voice.
posts.key_insight: LLM-generated content is instantly recognizable and destroys the authenticity that makes someone's perspective worth reading in the first place.

topQuotes.quote: Bluntly, your intellectual fly is open: lots of people notice—but no one is pointing it out.
LinkedIn is the Gerald Ford of social networks: the normal one that was left standing as the Richard Nixons and the Spiro Agnews of social media imploded around them.
When you—person whose perspective I want to hear!—are obviously using an LLM to write posts for you, I don't know what's real and what is in fact generated fanfic.

posts.title: Will There Ever Be A Worse Time To
posts.summary: The post argues that while AI tools make it feel like there has never been a better time to start a startup, the pace of improvement creates a deflationary effect: waiting even a few months can dramatically lower the cost and raise the quality of what you can build. That dynamic turns today’s frontier into tomorrow’s tech debt, making early-built products feel obsolete faster and raising the question of whether starting now is actually optimal.
posts.key_insight: Rapidly improving AI tools create a deflationary dynamic in software creation, making each new wave of tooling render recent work obsolete and complicating the idea that now is always the best time to start.

topQuotes.quote: There is no fixed definition of tech debt—it is simply code that the current engineers would prefer to be written differently.
If all you think about is the tools that are available to you, then today is always a better time to start a company than yesterday, and today will always be worse than tomorrow.
After all, you can rename your company to keep up with the latest fads, but it’s not so easy to rebuild it.

posts.title: 2025 App Store Award Winners: Tiimo, Essayist, and Detail
posts.summary: Gruber reviews the three 2025 App Store Award winners — Tiimo (iPhone), Essayist (Mac), and Detail (iPad) — with varying degrees of enthusiasm. He finds Tiimo's emoji-heavy ADHD planner underwhelming and overly aggressive with App Store rating prompts. Essayist, an academic word processor focused on citation management, draws his ire for offering only Times New Roman and Arial as font choices. Detail, an AI video editor for social media content creation, is the clear favorite, praised as a thoughtful tool with AI features built atop a solid manual editing foundation. Gruber notes all three winners share common traits: free-to-download with subscription monetization, AI feature emphasis, and Apple platform exclusivity.
posts.key_insight: Of the three App Store Award winners, only Detail earns genuine praise by building AI features atop a solid manual editing foundation, while the others reveal questionable choices in design, pricing, and user experience that make Apple's selections feel more like participation trophies.

topQuotes.quote: Paying money for the privilege to use Arial... Jiminy fucking christ. I might need a drink.
Three prompts to rate and review the app in the first 10 minutes of use strikes me as contrary to the needs of the easily distracted.
You're left with the choice between 'the default font's default font' and 'font crime'.

posts.title: Help shape the Practical Data 2026 State of Data Engineering Report.
posts.summary: Joe Reis announces a survey for his upcoming 2026 State of Data Engineering Report, emphasizing his goal to capture what's actually happening in the field rather than vendor marketing or hype cycles. He's seeking input from data practitioners across various roles to inform a free community report.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the real state of data engineering requires gathering practitioner perspectives rather than relying on vendor narratives.

topQuotes.quote: I want it to reflect what's actually happening across our field, not just vendor marketing or hype cycles.

posts.title: Dlt+ClickHouse+Rill: Multi-Cloud Cost Analytics, Cloud-Ready
posts.summary: This tutorial extends a local FinOps project to cloud-native infrastructure using ClickHouse Cloud and Rill Cloud for multi-cloud cost analytics. Building on a previous local Parquet-based solution, Späti demonstrates how dlt's destination flexibility makes switching from local files to ClickHouse trivial with just an environment variable change. The article walks through ingesting AWS CUR, GCP cost, and Stripe data, deploying interactive dashboards to Rill Cloud, and automating the pipeline with GitHub Actions. The hardest challenges weren't the tools themselves but the coordination work: SQL dialect differences between DuckDB and ClickHouse, hard-coded table names, and data anonymization requirements.
posts.key_insight: A declarative, code-first data stack with tools like dlt and Rill provides the flexibility to easily switch between local and cloud deployments while keeping full control over configuration and automation.

topQuotes.quote: Once we have that figured out, Claude Code and others can help us a ton. But also, typically they make the project much larger. They tend to add code instead of refactoring as they are afraid to break existing code.
The SQL dialects are not aligned, and every database has a little different flavour of date and other functions.
The goal of FinOps is to maximize revenue or business value through the cloud. It isn't to save money per se, but it certainly helps with that too.

posts.title: Alan Dye Was in Tim Cook’s Blind Spot
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Alan Dye's departure from Apple to Meta reveals a deeper blind spot in Tim Cook's leadership than just design failures. He contrasts Dye's move with Cook's years of publicly excoriating Meta over privacy, noting that Apple executives almost never leave, and leaving for Meta specifically is a betrayal of Apple's stated values. Gruber frames this as a dual failure: Apple's leadership missed both Dye's design shortcomings and his misalignment with Apple's moral compass. The piece uses Cook's famous 2018 zinger at Zuckerberg as an ironic frame for how damaging this defection is.
posts.key_insight: Alan Dye's departure to Meta exposes not just a design leadership failure at Apple, but a deeper blindness to someone whose values were never truly aligned with the company's stated ideals.

topQuotes.quote: How could someone who would even consider leaving Apple for Meta rise to a level of such prominence at Apple?
Perhaps Cook now needs to define 'us'.
It's also that it's now clear that Dye's moral compass was not aligned with Apple's either.

posts.title: Ghostty Is Now Non-Profit
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces that Ghostty, his open-source terminal emulator, is now fiscally sponsored by Hack Club, a 501(c)(3) non-profit. This structure ensures Ghostty remains free and open source, prevents any possibility of a 'rug pull' or commercial acquisition, and enables tax-deductible donations. Hashimoto emphasizes that foundational infrastructure like terminals should be stewarded by mission-driven, non-commercial entities. The arrangement includes full financial transparency, contributor compensation, and legal protections. His family is also personally donating $150,000 directly to Hack Club to support their broader mission.
posts.key_insight: Critical developer infrastructure like terminal emulators should be governed by non-profit structures that legally bind projects to public benefit, preventing commercial capture while enabling sustainable community funding.

topQuotes.quote: I believe infrastructure of this kind should be stewarded by a mission-driven, non-commercial entity that prioritizes public benefit over private profit.

A non-profit structure provides enforceable assurances: the mission cannot be quietly changed, funds cannot be diverted to private benefit, and the project cannot be sold off or repurposed for commercial gain.
I want to squelch any possible concerns about a 'rug pull'.

posts.title: Branch, Test, Deploy: A Git-Inspired Approach for Data
posts.summary: The post explores applying Git-like version control workflows to data management, addressing the challenge of testing and rolling back data pipelines in production environments. Simon argues that while Git revolutionized code versioning, data presents unique constraints: tables must remain consistent across relationships, production datasets are massive, and copying data for testing is slow and expensive. The solution lies in leveraging metadata catalogs and zero-copy cloning through technologies like Prolly Trees, which enable instant branching of production data without physical duplication. The article establishes a data movement efficiency spectrum from metadata-based versioning (most efficient) to full data copying (least efficient), setting up a Part 2 that will examine specific tools implementing these concepts.
posts.key_insight: Git-like workflows for data require metadata-based zero-copy cloning rather than physical data duplication, enabling instant branching of production datasets for testing without the time and cost of traditional environment setup.

topQuotes.quote: Remember the 2 AM on-call duty when a recent data pipeline broke the production environment? A data pipeline you've never touched just corrupted customer records.

With code, different features can be developed independently and then merged into the main branch at the end. With data, it's more about testing prod data on dev and then rolling out the code changes to prod.
Git for data is harder than version control for code because we're not just tracking changes but managing state, often at a massive scale.

posts.title: Fizzy is our fun, modern take on Kanban (and we made it open source!)
posts.summary: DHH introduces Fizzy, 37signals' new Kanban tool, positioning it as a fresh, simplified take on a concept that has grown cumbersome in existing tools. The product is available both as a paid SaaS and as source-available code under the O'Saasy License, which allows self-hosting but reserves commercial SaaS rights. He argues that software ecosystems need recurring cycles of renewal as even great products accumulate cruft over time. The post emphasizes data portability between SaaS and self-hosted instances, and celebrates the View Source ethos applied to backend code. DHH frames Fizzy as both a useful product and a learning resource for modern Rails development.
posts.key_insight: Software ecosystems need recurring cycles of renewal, and combining source-available code with SaaS gives users both convenience and the reassurance of data portability and self-hosting as an escape hatch.

topQuotes.quote: That means it's not technically Open Source™, but the source sure is open.
Even products that start out with great promise and simplicity tend to accumulate cruft and complexity over time. A healthy ecosystem needs a recurring cycle of renewal.
Good ideas can grow cumbersome and unwieldy surprisingly quickly. Fizzy is a fresh reset of an old idea. We need more of that.

posts.title: Bad Dye Job
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Alan Dye's departure from Apple to Meta as chief design officer is the best personnel news Apple has had in decades. Dye, who came from a brand and print advertising background rather than interaction design, led Apple's software design team for a decade that Gruber characterizes as a disaster — prioritizing how things look over how they work. His replacement, longtime Apple designer Stephen Lemay, is a career interface/interaction designer who is universally respected within Apple's ranks. Gruber theorizes the directional shift may be happenstance — Lemay was chosen for loyalty and political safety after Apple was blindsided by Dye's departure, rather than because leadership recognized the need for change. Nevertheless, Gruber believes this could be the best thing to happen to Apple's HI design since Steve Jobs's passing.
posts.key_insight: Alan Dye's decade leading Apple's software design was a mismatch of brand thinking applied to interaction design, and his departure — however accidental — opens the door for Apple to return to its tradition of design that prioritizes how things work, not just how they look.

topQuotes.quote: It's rather extraordinary in today's hyper-partisan world that there's nearly universal agreement amongst actual practitioners of user-interface design that Alan Dye is a fraud who led the company deeply astray.
I don't think Alan Dye could get a job as a barista at LoveFrom.
The average IQ of both companies has increased.

posts.title: Kelly Bodwin: Quarto hacks, AI in the classroom, and why R should stay weird
posts.summary: Wes frames “data science” as a marketing term that became too broad, pushing companies toward more specialized roles with clearer expectations. He sees Polars maturing into a simpler, more teachable API and a likely long-term complement or successor to Pandas for many workloads. He highlights how overwhelming it is to stay current in an ecosystem that has exploded in scale and mainstream adoption over the last decade. He argues generative AI will massively expand the user base of open source data tools without proportionally funding maintenance. He warns that AI assistants may entrench existing tools and make adoption of new projects harder by limiting training data and user incentives to learn from docs.
posts.key_insight: Wes argues that the data ecosystem is professionalizing and AI-amplified, but sustainability and tool innovation will hinge on clearer roles, simpler APIs, and new ways to fund and adopt open-source projects.

topQuotes.quote: It will be hard to get people to use new open source projects when their AI assistants don't know how to use them yet and so it creates this like chicken and egg problem.
It's a little bit of a marketing term, I think.

One of the good things for education is that Polars is a lot smaller, API is a lot less complex in many ways than Pandas, and so I feel like it may actually be easier to teach and there's fewer rough edges.

posts.title: Can LLMs give us AGI if they are bad at arithmetic?
posts.summary: The post argues that current LLMs and coding agents, while highly useful for routine engineering work, exhibit clear cognitive deficits that undermine claims that AGI is imminent. McKinney’s experiments show that many frontier models struggle with basic arithmetic once the number of values grows modestly, even in simple grouped-sum tasks. He highlights a mismatch between industry hype and model reliability, especially when models appear overconfident and fail to recognize when tool use is necessary. He also notes that GPT-5’s apparent improvement comes with huge latency, suggesting heavy inference or hidden tool calls rather than fundamental capability. The conclusion is that LLMs should offload non-trivial data work to tools, and the ecosystem needs better data “sidecars” (e.g., Arrow/Parquet) to avoid wasting tokens on tabular data.
posts.key_insight: LLMs are valuable assistants for experienced engineers, but their arithmetic failures, overconfidence, and inefficiencies show that tool-assisted workflows and better data plumbing are essential—and that AGI hype is premature.

topQuotes.quote: It's fine if Claude Code is helping me write Linux sysadmin scripts for my local inference servers, but delegating business decisions to a fleet of agents that can't count good? I'm not so sure.

Overall, it's been hard for me to see the current iteration of coding agents as being more than a mostly effective way for experienced developers to accelerate routine software development tasks or fill in routine knowledge or skill gaps, a far cry from being the early stages of emergent superintelligence.
But the models themselves aren't even "aware" of their incompetence, which is troubling.

posts.title: From pandas to Arrow: The Future of Data Infrastructure
posts.summary: McKinney traces his path from pandas to Arrow, arguing that modern data infrastructure needs interoperable, zero-copy in-memory formats to unlock speed and composability across languages and systems. He explains Arrow’s design and why Parquet’s storage-era tradeoffs now impose decoding bottlenecks as hardware and workloads shift. He contends that file formats must evolve for wide schemas, GPU-era processing, and multimodal data, while avoiding fragmentation through forward-compatibility mechanisms. He highlights the “big metadata” problem and the push to simpler, scalable metadata systems rather than universal over-engineering. He is optimistic about productivity gains from AI tools but warns that open source sustainability, prompt-injection risks, and the erosion of developer judgment are serious concerns. He concludes that LLMs need better data interfaces, calling for a data context protocol and Arrow-native tooling like ADBC.
posts.key_insight: Modern data systems should prioritize interoperable, zero-copy in-memory data and evolve storage formats and metadata systems for today’s hardware, while treating AI as a productivity aid that still needs better data interfaces and stronger open source support.

topQuotes.quote: "AI companies are strip-mining intellectual property created by open source community and humanity generally."
"Parquet's design constraints from 15 years ago are obsolete. I/O bandwidth isn't the bottleneck anymore; decoding performance is."
"Forget big data. Let's start talking about big metadata."

posts.title: Six billion reasons to cheer for Shopify
posts.summary: DHH celebrates Shopify's record-breaking $6.2 billion Black Friday sales as validation of both the company and the Ruby on Rails ecosystem. He highlights the massive technical scale—31 million API requests per minute—as proof that Rails can handle frontier-level loads. DHH argues that Shopify's dominance in the Rails and Ruby communities should be seen as a blessing, not a concern, citing contributions like YJIT and Ractor improvements. He credits Shopify's success to founder-led leadership, noting that CEO Tobi Lütke still actively programs. While acknowledging his bias as a Shopify board member and friend of Tobi, DHH maintains that the company deserves celebration as the 'patron saint' of Ruby on Rails.
posts.key_insight: Shopify's massive scale and continued open-source contributions make it an unparalleled benefactor to the Ruby on Rails ecosystem, and the community should embrace rather than resent its dominance.

topQuotes.quote: How many CEOs of companies worth two hundred billion dollars still program like that?
It's this kind of frontier load and criticality that makes Shopify the ideal patron saint of the Rails framework and the Ruby programming language.
Seeing this as anything but a blessing is silly, though.

posts.title: Signal Secure Backups Are Now Available on iOS
posts.summary: Gruber reports on Signal's new Secure Backup feature finally arriving on iOS, which allows end-to-end encrypted cloud backups of chat history. He shares his own experience where the old device-to-device transfer crashed and lost all his data on both phones, but the new cloud backup restored everything perfectly in about one minute. He argues that Signal's long-standing philosophy of keeping data only on-device was a form of insecurity in itself, since losing your phone meant losing everything. While acknowledging Signal's noble security priorities, he contends the lack of cloud backup for years was user-hostile and held Signal back from mainstream adoption.
posts.key_insight: True security must protect users from data loss as well as data theft — Signal's new cloud backup finally addresses the user-hostile brittleness that kept it from mainstream adoption.

topQuotes.quote: Transferring my Signal account history from one phone to another always felt like delivering a crate full of eggs while riding a rickety old bicycle without brakes on a bumpy cobblestone street.
There's nothing "secure" about a transfer feature that loses all of your data if the transfer fails.

Permanently losing all your data is a different form of "insecurity" than having it exfiltrated by an attacker or exposed to law enforcement agencies via a warrant issued to the cloud backup provider, but it's a form of insecurity nonetheless.

posts.title: Happy birthday ChatGPT
posts.summary: Joe Reis reflects on ChatGPT's third anniversary, noting how it unexpectedly became the fastest-growing app in history and fundamentally shifted the data industry's focus from topics like Data Mesh to AI. He observes that while the technology has evolved from crude chatbots to agents and context engineering, the core innovation continues to deliver real utility despite the hype cycles.
posts.key_insight: ChatGPT's launch marked a before-and-after moment that permanently shifted the tech industry's trajectory, and despite the inevitable hype cycles and expertise bandwagons, the underlying innovation delivers real value that won't disappear even if the bubble pops.

topQuotes.quote: Just like everyone was an AI expert a couple of years ago, now it seems like everyone is an expert in ontologies and knowledge graphs. So it goes.
Even if the current AI bubble pops, it won't stop innovation in next-generation AI.

A friend who was on the original team that created and launched ChatGPT told me they figured that maybe 10,000 people would use the app. By January 2023, over 100 million people were using ChatGPT, making it the fastest-growing app in history.

posts.title: Bikeshedding, or why I want to build a laptop
posts.summary: George Hotz expresses frustration with the current state of laptops, arguing that Apple's quality is declining while Linux alternatives like Asahi fail due to Apple's closed hardware. He critiques Framework's focus on upgradability as a misguided tradeoff that sacrifices build quality and polish. He proposes a hypothetical 'hackbook' - a single-configuration, no-compromise Linux laptop built on AMD's Strix Halo with obsessive attention to polish and simple yearly iteration. The post concludes with him hoping someone else will build this so he doesn't have to, acknowledging he's bikeshedding rather than committing to the project.
posts.key_insight: The laptop market is failing power users because companies optimize for the wrong tradeoffs - modularity over polish, product line complexity over simplicity - leaving a gap for a user-aligned company to build one excellent, no-compromise Linux machine.

topQuotes.quote: Apple will not understand what they had until they lose it, the only reason anything works on Mac at all is because there's 100,000 amazing developers who use these machines every day; they put some work into making their house nice.
I don't care one bit about upgradability or customizability. After a year or two, I'm happy to throw it out and buy a new one.
Can somebody please build a good MacBook replacement and make it a Schelling point everyone will switch to so I don't have to think about this anymore?

posts.title: Why You Should Start Your New Year in December and Tackle That Scary Goal
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that December, not January 1st, is the optimal time to start working on goals because waiting until New Year leads to abandoned resolutions by February. He advocates confronting the 'scary goals' that terrify you most, as these are likely the ones that will actually change your life.
posts.key_insight: The goals that terrify you and constantly nag at the back of your mind are the ones most worth pursuing—and December is the time to confront them before another year slips away.

topQuotes.quote: Most people wait until January 1st to set goals. By February or March, those goals are dead.
I'm notorious for saying 'no,' and very rarely have I regretted it.
Usually, the work that will actually change your life is the work you are most scared to do.

posts.title: Replacing my MacBook
posts.summary: Hotz documents his attempt to replace his MacBook with a Linux laptop, specifically an HP ZBook with AMD's Strix Halo chip. While he's made progress on sleep power management (0.2W lid-closed), active use still draws 7-10W compared to MacBook's efficiency, giving only 7 hours of battery life. He identifies the CPU's chiplet design as the main culprit, drawing 4W at idle versus Apple's 1W. He calls on AMD and HP to release better documentation and argues that with proper optimization, a competitive Linux laptop is achievable.
posts.key_insight: Despite AMD's Strix Halo being the only chip rivaling Apple silicon in performance, its chiplet design and lack of vendor transparency on power management make Linux laptops still far behind MacBooks in battery efficiency.

topQuotes.quote: You know MoP is a good idea because Intel is discontinuing it while Apple keeps doing it.
It fixed the problem I always had with Arch, which is how customizable it is.
Sadly, there's no better laptop hardware than a MacBook.

posts.summary: Stancil argues that even if the AI boom is real at the aggregate level, the gains and disappointments will be uneven, and many startup workers grinding through 996 schedules will end up with nothing. He draws a parallel to earlier “temporary” career detours in finance/consulting and warns that there is no such thing as borrowed time—when bubbles burst, the only durable value is the human memory of what people sacrificed.
posts.key_insight: The AI gold rush may enrich a few, but for many it will trade irreplaceable time for nothing, so the humane response is to witness and honor those sacrifices.

topQuotes.quote: Some people get rich, and some people just get dirty.
The AI bubble will burst—it’s just that the disappointment won’t be evenly distributed.
You can live your life on borrowed money, but there is no such thing as borrowed time.

posts.title: Beads Best Practices
posts.summary: Steve Yegge evangelizes Beads, his 'execution tool' for AI coding agents that provides shared memory and issue tracking across agent sessions. The post celebrates the project's growth (130k lines of 'vibe coded' Go), introduces Jeffrey Emanuel's complementary MCP Agent Mail for agent-to-agent messaging, and shares best practices for solo development. Yegge frames Beads as deliberately 'crummy architecture' that AI makes work through resilience, and describes his vision of 'agent villages' with 30+ parallel workers coordinated through git worktrees and messaging.
posts.key_insight: AI agents don't need perfect architecture - they need simple, 'crummy' systems like Beads that they can resilient work around, enabling swarm-like parallel development that would be impossible with traditional tooling.

topQuotes.quote: It is 100% vibe coded, currently at 130k lines of Go code total, roughly half tests. And tens of thousands of people are using it in their daily workflows.
It's a crummy architecture (by pre-AI standards) that requires AI in order to work around all its edge cases where it breaks. But AI can always get it working.
There's no ego, so they quickly decide on a leader and just split things up.

posts.title: Local LLMs are how nerds now justify a big computer they don't need
posts.summary: DHH argues that local LLMs are the modern nerd's excuse to buy expensive hardware they don't actually need. While acknowledging the technical achievement of running AI models locally, he contends that local models are vastly inferior to frontier cloud models and remain a curiosity rather than a practical tool. He suggests developers are fooling themselves when evaluating computers based on local LLM performance. To prove his point, he's switched from a $2,000 Framework Desktop to a $500 mini PC and barely notices the difference in daily work.
posts.key_insight: Developers using local LLM performance to justify expensive hardware are rationalizing a want as a need, since cloud frontier models are superior and most dev work requires far less compute than people assume.

topQuotes.quote: Local LLMs are how nerds now justify a big computer they don't need
I find it spurious to hear developers evaluate their next computer on the prospect of how well it's capable of running local models. Because they all suck!
Whether one sucks a little less than the other doesn't really matter.

posts.title: Beads Best Practices
posts.summary: Steve Yegge shares best practices for Beads, his git-native issue tracking tool designed for AI coding agents. He positions Beads as an execution tool rather than a planning tool, emphasizing its small scope and passive design. He describes meeting Jeffrey Emanuel, creator of MCP Agent Mail, who combines it with Beads to create 'agent villages' where multiple AI agents collaborate autonomously. The post details practical tips for solo development including regular cleanup, frequent agent restarts, and planning outside Beads before importing work. Yegge notes Beads is 130k lines of entirely vibe-coded Go and growing rapidly in adoption.
posts.key_insight: AI agents need only shared memory (issue tracking) and messaging to self-organize into effective autonomous teams, making simple 'crummy' architectures viable because AI compensates for their edge cases.

topQuotes.quote: It is 100% vibe coded, currently at 130k lines of Go code total, roughly half tests. And tens of thousands of people are using it in their daily workflows.
I was enthralled, all the while being amused that he still wanted his agents grubbing around in a single folder like raccoons in a dumpster.
Everyone is focused on making planning tools, and Beads is an execution tool. It's like putting your coding agent on waxed skis.

posts.title: No backup, no cry
posts.summary: DHH explains why he stopped doing full-system backups, instead treating every computer as a stateless, disposable unit. By storing all important data in Dropbox and code in Git, he maintains distributed local copies across multiple machines. This approach means any single machine can be lost or destroyed without consequence. He extended this philosophy to create Omarchy, encoding his entire system configuration into an ISO that installs in two minutes.
posts.key_insight: By treating every computer as disposable and routing all important data through distributed sync services, you can eliminate the need for traditional backups entirely.

topQuotes.quote: Every machine I now own is treated as a stateless, disposable unit that can be stolen, lost, or corrupted without consequences.
Everything outside of Dropbox is essentially treated as a temporary directory that's fully disposable.
There ought to be no reason why a busted computer is a calamity of data loss or a long restore process.

posts.title: From Cloudwashing to O11ywashing
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the term 'observability' has been co-opted by vendors and executives who use it to describe basic monitoring tools, while the actual problem observability was meant to solve — understanding service quality from each individual customer's perspective — remains unaddressed by most 'observability' products. She coins the term 'o11ywashing' (analogous to 'cloudwashing') to describe this phenomenon and argues Honeycomb needs to better communicate observability's true value to executives, not just engineers.
posts.key_insight: The term 'observability' has been hollowed out by vendors and executives to mean basic monitoring, and fixing this requires communicating observability's true value — understanding per-customer service quality as a systems problem — directly to engineering executives in business terms, not just to engineers in technical ones.

topQuotes.quote: If your 'observability' tooling doesn't help you understand the quality of your product from the customer's perspective, EACH customer's perspective, it isn't fucking observability. It's just monitoring dressed up in marketing dollars.
This guy is literally describing the original definition of observability, and he doesn't even know it. He doesn't know it so hard that he went and built his own thing.

When Christine and I started Honeycomb, nearly ten years ago, we were innocent, doe-eyed engineers who truly believed on some level that if we just explained the technical details of cardinality and dimensionality clearly and patiently enough to the world, enough times, the consequences to the business would become obvious to everyone involved.

posts.title: Exploring, in Detail, Apple’s Compliance With the EU’s DMA Mandate Regarding Apple Watch, Third-Party Accessories, and the Syncing of Saved Wi-Fi Networks From iPhones to Which They’re Paired
posts.summary: Gruber provides a detailed technical analysis of Apple's compliance with the EU's DMA mandate requiring iPhones to share Wi-Fi network credentials with third-party accessories. He argues that reporting from Numerama and others inaccurately characterized Apple as spitefully removing Wi-Fi sync between iPhone and Apple Watch, when Apple actually chose a middle ground: limiting historical Wi-Fi network sync to Apple Watch while creating an entirely new Wi-Fi Infrastructure API framework for third-party devices in the EU. Gruber contends the EC's mandate is incoherent because it demands both user privacy protection and broad third-party access to sensitive Wi-Fi data, without allowing Apple to enforce privacy requirements on those third parties. He frames this as a clarifying example of how the DMA prioritizes developers over users, noting that Apple engineered significant new APIs solely for EU compliance that may never be used — much like the BrowserEngineKit framework that no browser has adopted in two years.
posts.key_insight: Apple's DMA compliance on Wi-Fi network sharing is not spiteful removal of features but a genuine engineering effort to balance an incoherent mandate that simultaneously demands user privacy protection and broad third-party access to sensitive data.

topQuotes.quote: Apple sees the DMA as mandating, privacy-wise, that they create something akin to cars that don't require keys, trusting EU law to keep them from being stolen.
Apple doesn't care about €20 million. They fart bigger than that. They do care about clarifying who the DMA prioritizes first, and that it's not users.

The EC insists that Apple should protect users' privacy and security, while also insisting that Apple grant access to third-party apps and devices that can potentially compromise users' privacy and security.

posts.title: Revisiting Manager READMEs
posts.summary: Camille Fournier revisits her earlier critique of manager READMEs, arguing they remain ineffective tools for senior leaders. The core problem is that executives need to quickly understand what matters across many people with different communication styles, but standardized company tooling provides only lowest-common-denominator usefulness. READMEs are lazy and create a you-focused mindset that encourages information bubbles.
