When powerful voices in tech normalize the idea that some people don't belong based on how they look or where they're from, they're building the ideological infrastructure for the enforcement we're now seeing.
The lines on the map are only as real as we allow them to be.

posts.title: Got opinions on observability? I could use your help (once more, with feeling)
posts.summary: Charity Majors asks her audience for help answering specific questions about observability tooling for the second edition of her book, covering migrations, cost management, observability teams, and build-vs-buy decisions. She frames this as a follow-up to an earlier call for advice on software buying, which she argues is one of the highest-leverage activities a senior engineer can perform.
posts.key_insight: Vendor engineering — the art of evaluating, buying, and managing software tools — is an underrated, high-leverage skill that lets staff+ engineers develop executive-level strategic thinking while remaining individual contributors.

topQuotes.quote: I've come to believe that vendor engineering is one of the subtlest and most powerful practical applications of deep subject matter expertise, and some of the highest leverage work an engineer can do.
How often do you get to make decisions that leverage the labor of hundreds or thousands of engineers per year, for fractions of pennies on the dollar?
It is one of the only ways a staff+ engineer can acquire and develop executive-level communication, strategy, and execution skills while remaining an individual contributor.

posts.title: Vibing a Non-Trivial Ghostty Feature
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto details how he shipped a non-trivial Ghostty feature—unobtrusive macOS update notifications—using AI-assisted coding across 16 sessions costing $15.98. He emphasizes that effective AI use requires domain expertise, careful planning, and treating AI as an assistant rather than replacement. The post walks through his complete process: prototyping UI, hitting walls where AI failed, extensive cleanup sessions, backend development, and simulation testing. He stresses that he always reviews and often rewrites AI-generated code, and that the real value isn't just speed but the ability to make progress while doing other things.
posts.key_insight: Effective AI-assisted coding requires domain expertise to guide the AI, knowing when to take back control, and never shipping code you don't fully understand.

topQuotes.quote: AI is no longer the solution; it is a liability.
If the agent figures it out and I don't understand it, I back it out. I'm not shipping code I don't understand.
I very often use AI for inspiration. I will very often prompt an agent, throw away everything it did, and redo it myself.

posts.title: Scared Money Don't Make Money
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that AI adoption is now mandatory for career survival, dividing people into 'Builders' who experiment with AI and 'Holdouts' who resist it. He claims his AI-native workflow compresses weeks of work into days and warns that those who remain spectators will be 'crushed beyond recognition' by the coming AI wave.
posts.key_insight: AI adoption is no longer optional—those who refuse to integrate AI into their workflows risk becoming professionally obsolete as the technology creates an insurmountable productivity gap between adopters and holdouts.

topQuotes.quote: I'm betting on the builders. Everyone else is too late and f'd.
Scared money don't make money. The biggest tsunami you've ever imagined is approaching your business, your careers, and you personally.
If you remain a spectator, the wave won't slow down for you. You're going to get crushed beyond recognition.

posts.title: Notes on switching to Helix from vim
posts.summary: Julia Evans shares her experience switching from Vim to the Helix text editor after 20 years of Vim usage. The primary motivation was getting language server support without extensive configuration. She finds Helix's built-in LSP support, search with full context, and inline keybinding help significantly better than her Vim setup. While she notes several annoyances including poor Markdown list handling and occasional crashes, the switch was easier than expected. Her Helix configuration is remarkably minimal compared to her hundreds-of-lines Neovim config.
posts.key_insight: The best tool configuration is the one you don't have to maintain — built-in sensible defaults can outweigh decades of customized muscle memory.

topQuotes.quote: After using Vim/Neovim for 20 years, I've tried both "build my own custom configuration from scratch" and "use someone else's pre-buld configuration system" and even though I love Vim I was excited about having things just work without having to work on my configuration at all.
I appreciate that my configuration is really simple, compared to my neovim configuration which is hundreds of lines. It's mostly just 4 keyboard shortcuts.
The first time I tried Helix I tried to force it to use keybindings that were more similar to Vim and that did not work for me. Just learning the "Helix way" was a lot easier.

posts.title: Julia Silge Part 1: Positron, pineapple pizza, and the art of iteration
posts.summary: Wes frames Positron as a long-needed bridge between terminal-first Python workflows and the interactive exploration he relied on through Jupyter. He argues that jumping between notebooks and code editors creates friction that an integrated, data-centric IDE can reduce. He highlights Positron’s data explorer as a concrete improvement for inspecting frames and files during iterative analysis. He also notes that VS Code’s open, MIT-licensed substrate enables experimentation in IDE design, but creates a gravity well where extensions become the default distribution path for tools. Overall, his conclusion is that Positron’s synthesis of exploration and development workflows makes data analysis more fluent and productive.
posts.key_insight: Positron’s integrated IDE experience resolves long-standing friction between notebook exploration and full development workflows, while leveraging and being shaped by the VS Code ecosystem.

topQuotes.quote: But it’s been amazing how much Python has become just total AI land.
I’ve run into a lot of the limitations and the rough edges of jumping back and forth between an actual development environment and a Jupyter notebook.
Now the table stakes is that people see something new and they’re like, can I get that in a VS Code extension or as an open VSX extension?

posts.title: Give me AI slop over human sludge any day
posts.summary: DHH argues that the hand-wringing over AI-generated content ('slop') ignores the fact that humans have been producing equally terrible content ('sludge') for years. He points to SEO-driven content mills, clickbait, and conversion-optimized garbage as proof that human-made content was already degraded. He contends that letting AI handle this soul-crushing work is actually more humane than forcing people to produce it. The post concludes with a fatalistic observation that most people won't opt out of consuming low-quality content regardless of whether humans or machines produce it.
posts.key_insight: AI slop is a more humane replacement for the soul-crushing content drudgery humans were already doing, and the people complaining about it were never going to stop consuming garbage content anyway.

topQuotes.quote: Let the little robots barf up tokens to unlock the next basis point of incremental conversion. Better them than us, I say.
So stop whining about the AI slop. You're already steeped in human sludge.
Turning human potential, creativity, and ingenuity into content sludge is a process no more dignified than turning pink slime into chicken nuggets.

posts.title: We have always been at war with Eastasia
posts.summary: Hotz argues that technology has been quietly captured by power structures in ways most people don't even remember or recognize. Drawing on the dystopian novel 'Manna,' he poses the fundamental question of whether machines will serve humanity or enslave it. He contends that centralized technology inevitably leads to a zero-sum power struggle that no one truly wins—even the final 'winner' ends up with worthless dominance. The post is a direct plea to technologists to stop building centralized systems and instead focus on decentralized alternatives before it's too late.
posts.key_insight: Centralized technology is a trap that leads to universal enslavement, and technologists must personally reckon with their role in building it before the 20-year countdown runs out.

topQuotes.quote: If technology is centralized, you will be a slave.
If one person ends up with all the money, the money is worthless.
Your vacations aren't worth it. Your house isn't worth it. Eating fancy food isn't worth it.

posts.title: A parade in Oakland
posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill reflects on the Oakland Ballers' 2025 Pioneer League championship victory, weaving together his family's history as Oakland A's fans, the trauma of the A's relocation by owner John Fisher, and the redemptive emergence of this independent league team. The essay explores how sports teams become vessels for family memories across generations, and how the scrappy Ballers—staffed largely by local NorCal players—provided Oakland with an unexpected path to healing after losing their MLB franchise.
posts.key_insight: Sports teams matter not for the entertainment they provide but for the generations of family memories they hold—and while a franchise can be stolen, a community's love of the game cannot be extinguished.

topQuotes.quote: To rip a team out of a city, then, is to sour some of our dearest memories, ones that cannot be recreated; it is to come into a home and desecrate the family photo album.
For all of them, wherever their careers may take them, they will forever remember that electric Sunday night in West Oakland, when they proved what is possible when we refuse to give up on ourselves.
The beauty of baseball is the narrative beyond the diamond.

posts.title: Fivetran + dbt Rumors, Modern Data Stack Consolidation, and More
posts.summary: Joe Reis analyzes the rumored Fivetran-dbt merger as symptomatic of broader Modern Data Stack consolidation, arguing that decade-old 'modern' tools now face existential threats from AI-native startups. He predicts a mass extinction event in data tooling and BI as vendors either evolve to become AI-native or get replaced by leaner offerings.
posts.key_insight: The Modern Data Stack era is ending as AI-native tools threaten to make current data vendors obsolete, forcing rapid consolidation or extinction.

topQuotes.quote: My hunch is we'll see a mass extinction event in the data tooling and BI space, where vendors either evolve to become AI-native or are replaced by far leaner offerings.
With LLMs writing more and more code, integrating, and generating insights, the big question is how many of these vendors are truly necessary over the next few years?
Lots of money has been plowed into AI, especially infrastructure, and companies need to maintain growth at all costs. If that means layoffs, so be it.

posts.title: Pay yourself first
posts.summary: DHH argues that knowledge workers must prioritize personally meaningful work over the endless stream of emails, meetings, and updates that could consume their entire workweek. He shares his own practice of frequently skipping check-ins to dive into programming, experimenting, and researching. He frames this as a virtuous cycle: by solving your own problems and chasing your own curiosity, you develop competency that earns you more privilege to do even more meaningful work. The post rejects any guilt about this privilege and urges readers to carve out time for their own intellectual priorities.
posts.key_insight: Prioritizing personally meaningful work over reactive busywork isn't selfish — it's the engine that builds competency and earns greater autonomy.

topQuotes.quote: In another age, I might have been tempted to apologize for such privilege, but screw that. Privilege is wonderful.
To stay sane and sharp, you must pay yourself first by doing the work that actually means something to you.
There'll always be more emails in need of reply, more meetings to attend, and more updates to read. A person can fill the entire workweek with these tasks over and over again.

posts.title: Zig Builds Are Getting Faster
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto shares real-world build time improvements after upgrading Ghostty to Zig 0.15.1, demonstrating significant speedups across all build scenarios. The build script compilation dropped from 7 seconds to under 2 seconds, while full uncached builds improved from 41 to 32 seconds. Most impressively, libghostty-vt incremental builds now complete in under a second using Zig's self-hosted backend. These improvements come despite Ghostty still relying on LLVM for most builds and incremental compilation not yet being functional, suggesting even greater gains are coming.
posts.key_insight: Zig 0.15.1 delivers substantial build time improvements that are already transforming daily development workflows, even before the most impactful optimizations like incremental compilation are fully realized.

topQuotes.quote: Andrew Kelley famously (or infamously, depending on your views) said 'the compiler is too damn slow, that's why we have bugs.'
Previously, I might tab out to read an email between builds or tests, but now its so fast I can stay in flow in my terminal.
These improvements are real, and they're here now. And I suspect in the next couple years, the results posted today will look downright slow.

posts.title: Something In The Orange
posts.summary: Using the interstate as a metaphor, Stancil argues that startup life feels like an endless sprint whose intensity and meaning are hard to convey from the outside. He reflects on dbt Labs’ rumored sale and the changing tech narrative, suggesting that even a “successful” exit can feel hollow when the culture’s scoreboard only values acceleration. Ultimately, he reframes the journey as something defined less by valuation and more by human ties and the desire for a quieter home.
posts.key_insight: The startup journey’s real meaning is felt by those inside it, yet the industry’s fixation on speed and scale can make even great outcomes feel empty compared to the human relationships and the pull of home.

topQuotes.quote: Now, the modern data stack is a punchline.
We don’t value people by where they are. We value them in the same way we value our companies—by how fast they’re rising.
If you live for long enough, nobody is left to give your eulogy.

posts.title: Untitled
posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that Android's notoriously painful development experience has spawned a proliferation of cross-platform frameworks (React Native, Flutter, Xamarin) that threaten Google's control over the mobile ecosystem. He contends that Google is doubling down on the wrong strategy—making native Android development incrementally better with Kotlin and complex Gradle builds—while developers flee to faster, cross-platform alternatives. Beyond the dev stack, Android faces threats from competing app stores (Amazon) and in-app platforms (Facebook, WeChat) that bypass Google's ad channel entirely. Yegge concludes that smart companies should hedge their bets with cross-platform frameworks, and that mobile developers are currently the hottest commodity in tech.
posts.key_insight: Google's defensive strategy of improving native Android development misses the point: the market is moving toward cross-platform frameworks, and whoever wins that ecosystem war could effectively steal Android's developer community from Google.

topQuotes.quote: Google responded by saying, 'Oh yeah? Well you can't compete with us, because we're going to compete with us!' and they launched Flutter.
The only reason Google created Android was because it's an ad channel.
It's like everyone who's ever tried to do Android programming gave up and declared: 'This is so bad that I will do my own startup to make it better.'

posts.title: Complying With ‘Demand’ From Trump Administration, Apple Removes ICEBlock From App Store
posts.summary: Gruber reports that Apple removed the ICEBlock app from the App Store after Attorney General Pam Bondi 'demanded' its removal, despite the app being entirely legal. ICEBlock allowed users to anonymously report ICE agents' locations, and Gruber argues its removal is protected speech under the First Amendment. He compares this unfavorably to Apple's 2019 removal of HKmap.live in Hong Kong, noting that ICEBlock is worse because there's no web fallback and Apple could have fought this in U.S. courts but chose not to. Gruber acknowledges the 'pick your battles' reality while expressing deep concern about the authoritarian implications and what future demands Apple might face regarding user data.
posts.key_insight: Apple's capitulation to an extralegal government demand to remove a legal, privacy-protecting app reveals both the fragility of digital civil liberties when they depend on a single corporate gatekeeper and the accelerating authoritarian trajectory of the Trump administration.

topQuotes.quote: I believe there are many lines Apple would not cross, even if it means taking on the ire of Trump administration lickspittles, if not the barely literate wrath of the mad king himself on his sad little blog.

It took them months to issue this demand because they're so goddamn stupid and incompetent. We should be thankful for that. In a competent regime attempting an authoritarian takeover of a liberal democracy, it would have been taken down in days, not months.

No one, not even Bondi, is claiming any aspect of ICEBlock is illegal. Thus it's not merely inappropriate but outrageous — and yet another among dozens of other causes for alarm regarding Trump 2.0's decidedly authoritarian turn — for the DOJ to 'demand' that Apple do anything about it.

posts.summary: Ronacher argues that “90% AI-written code” is already his lived reality on a serious Go infrastructure service, not a distant prediction. He emphasizes that this only works because he enforces strong architectural standards and treats every AI-generated line as his responsibility. The post details how he structures projects (raw SQL, OpenAPI-first, PR-sized chunks) and how he pairs tools like Claude and Codex for complementary strengths. He warns that agents are error-prone, create inappropriate abstractions, and can hide failures unless tightly guided. His conclusion is that AI accelerates engineering and unlocks better trade-offs, but it never replaces the need for strong engineering judgment and accountability.
posts.key_insight: AI can now generate the bulk of production code, but only engineers with strong judgment and ownership can keep those systems reliable.

topQuotes.quote: I cannot stress enough how bad the code from these agents can be if you're not careful.
It is easy to create systems that appear to behave correctly but have unclear runtime behavior when relying on agents.
The fact that I no longer have to write SQL because the AI does it for me is a game changer.

posts.title: We Were Hired To Do The Grunt Work
posts.summary: Stancil argues that most tech work is unglamorous grunt work and that the belief we’re just one reorg or tool upgrade away from “strategic” work is mostly a comforting illusion. He suggests the mundane tasks are often the real value, and AI is likely to shift the tedium rather than eliminate it, creating new layers of coordination and review.
posts.key_insight: The promise that better tools or AI will free knowledge workers for “strategic” work is overstated; in practice, technology tends to rearrange and multiply the tedium rather than remove it.

topQuotes.quote: The rapture never comes.
There are only so much strategy to discuss, and so many banner projects to be done, and most of what most companies need is tedium and organizational caulk.
No matter how many things technology makes convenient, the supply of inconvenient things seems to remain constant.

posts.title: Michael Chow: From psychology and Python to constrained creativity
posts.summary: Wes frames open source tool building as a response to the pain of working with data, arguing that firsthand scientific and analytical experience is essential for designing good ergonomics. He highlights a shift toward a richer, more modular ecosystem where specialized libraries can own concerns like table styling, avoiding tightly coupled releases inside core projects like pandas. He traces how experiments with lazy expression APIs across projects like Ibis and Siuba influenced today’s DataFrame tooling, including Polars and compatibility layers. He sees AI as a watershed comparable to the advent of the internet and expects LLM discoverability to strongly shape adoption of new open source projects. He also stresses that enterprise constraints and governance needs create a persistent demand for tooling that bridges open source innovation and organizational realities.
posts.key_insight: Wes argues that durable data tools come from hard-earned workflow pain and are now shaped by a modular open source ecosystem and an AI-driven shift in how users discover and adopt software.

topQuotes.quote: There's going to be a growing segment of users that if you're building a new open source project, people aren't going to use your open source project until they can use it via vibe coding essentially, or until it gets indexed by the LLMs.
It feels like as big of a change as going from no internet to internet in many ways.
And I think that all of this work definitely, directly or indirectly influenced the design of Polars and Polars' expression system, which I think works really well and people seem to be happy with.

posts.title: Is Design Dead?
posts.summary: Fowler examines whether Extreme Programming's rejection of up-front design means design is dead, concluding it is not—but its nature has changed. He contrasts planned design (detailed blueprints before coding) with evolutionary design (design emerging during implementation), arguing that XP's enabling practices—testing, continuous integration, and refactoring—flatten the software change curve enough to make evolutionary design viable. The key insight is that XP doesn't eliminate design but shifts the balance: instead of heavy up-front architecture, teams do just enough initial design while continuously refactoring toward simplicity. Success requires technical leadership focused on coaching rather than dictating, and a 'will to design' that ensures code quality remains high throughout development.
posts.key_insight: XP doesn't kill design—it transforms it from an up-front planning exercise into a continuous discipline of simplification, requiring even greater skill in refactoring, pattern recognition, and knowing when to defer decisions.

topQuotes.quote: In XP 'clever code' is a term of abuse.
If he doesn't trust his programmers why does he hire them?
It's easier to refactor over-design than it is to refactor no design.

posts.title: We've all had enough of this nonsense
posts.summary: DHH describes the latest failed attempt by Ruby community critics to 'cancel' him from Rails, contrasting it with a successful effort in 2022 when Ruby Central uninvited him from RailsConf. He argues that this time the campaign backfired spectacularly, with thousands of people publicly rallying to his defense on X. He frames this as the collapse of 'preference falsification,' where a silent majority finally feels empowered to push back against what he characterizes as a small group of aggrieved activists. He concludes with a dismissive call to ignore the critics and keep building.
posts.key_insight: DHH declares victory in the culture war within the Ruby community, arguing that the collapse of preference falsification has revealed his critics to be a tiny, powerless minority.

topQuotes.quote: Now RailsConf is dead, Rails World is thriving, and the cancellation nonsense is over.
I guess I would be sad too if I had named my group after THE GOOD GUYS and then it turned out that everyone thought I was THE BAD GUYS.
Every few years, the same sad contingent of Ruby malcontents tries to cancel me from Rails.

posts.title: Apple on the Digital Markets Act
posts.summary: Gruber highlights Apple's newsroom piece detailing how the Digital Markets Act is causing EU users to miss out on features like Live Translation with AirPods and iPhone Mirroring. He argues Apple structured the piece effectively by leading with the consumer harm. He then amplifies Apple's argument that the DMA is failing its stated goals of promoting competition and choice, instead reducing differentiation and creating unfair conditions. His central rhetorical move is a thought experiment: if Apple switched EU iPhones to Android, all DMA conflicts would vanish — but consumer choice would be destroyed, not enhanced.
posts.key_insight: The DMA's interoperability mandates paradoxically punish the existence of a competing platform, and the logical endpoint of full compliance — switching to Android — would eliminate consumer choice rather than expand it.

topQuotes.quote: If Apple were to just switch the iPhone's OS from iOS to Android, these DMA conflicts would all go away.
The DMA is supposedly intended to increase 'competition', which in turn should increase consumer choice. But the easiest way for Apple to comply with the DMA would be to switch EU iPhones to Android.
How in the world would that increase competition? iOS's unique and exclusive features — which, yes, in many cases, are exclusive to the Apple device ecosystem — are competition.

posts.title: A Very Early History of Algebraic Data Types
posts.summary: This post traces the origins of algebraic data types (AlgDTs) from John McCarthy's 1961 paper through their development in both imperative and functional programming traditions. McCarthy first defined the concepts of Cartesian products and direct unions (sum types) along with their algebraic properties like distributivity and associativity. The ideas influenced Tony Hoare and Rod Burstall, who developed them independently in different directions. While sum types disappeared from imperative languages due to Pascal and C's influence, they flourished in functional programming through ML, HOPE, and Miranda, eventually gaining features like tags, exhaustiveness checking, and pattern matching that define modern AlgDTs.
posts.key_insight: The algebraic properties that give algebraic data types their name—associativity, distributivity, cardinality relationships—were all present in McCarthy's foundational 1961 paper, decades before the terminology was standardized.

topQuotes.quote: It doesn't matter if you're the first to have an idea if nobody else hears you have it.
Pascal did not have sum types because Wirth thought they were less flexible than untagged unions.
Burstall is incredibly frustrating to research.

posts.title: Pydata Amsterdam Keynote
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces the evolution of data architecture from the 1985 three-tier model through cloud data warehouses to a proposed inverted architecture where compute happens on client devices. He argues that the traditional top-down pyramid—where a central data warehouse holds all power—has barely changed despite moving to the cloud, and that modern single-node query engines like DuckDB combined with lakehouse formats can flip this model. He demonstrates DuckDB processing 6 billion rows on a laptop with only 2GB of memory, and shows examples of running analytics on 13-year-old laptops, phones, satellites, and cars. The core proposal is that clients should be empowered with local compute while sharing data through commodity storage and open metadata formats.
posts.key_insight: The data architecture should be flipped upside down: instead of powerful central servers serving dumb clients, empowered client devices with local query engines should do the compute while sharing data through commodity storage and open lakehouse formats.

topQuotes.quote: These things are criminally incompetent at transferring data. And it might actually be strategic incompetence. Because if you're keeping the data hostage, then you can keep charging them for your superyacht purposes.
A halfway competent, single-threaded implementation can beat most distributed systems.
What you can do on a single computer is insane. You would not believe it if you wouldn't see it.

posts.title: Calling someone a "nazi" is a permission slip for violence
posts.summary: DHH argues that labeling political opponents as 'nazis' is an escalation tactic by what he calls the 'woke' movement, intended to justify violence against those who resist their orthodoxies. He frames this as a desperate response to declining institutional power, citing the dismantling of DEI programs, the shift in social media dynamics, and the 2024 political realignment. He contends that accusations of racism and misogyny no longer carry the social consequences they once did, so critics have escalated to the most extreme label available. DHH urges readers not to be intimidated, arguing that the movement behind these tactics is in decline.
posts.key_insight: DHH frames the escalation to 'nazi' accusations as a sign of weakness from a declining political movement that has lost its institutional leverage and now resorts to implicit threats of violence.

topQuotes.quote: Calling someone a "nazi" is a permission slip for violence.
The wheels have been falling off their political project for years now. You can and should say "no" when they come with the "nazi" nonsense too.
If you can't intimidate people into silence and compliance with the woke orthodoxies by threatening their job or their social circle, you might be able to threaten them with actual violence or worse.

posts.title: Cloudflare bankrolls fascists
posts.summary: DeVault argues that Cloudflare's sponsorship of Ladybird and Omarchy is troubling because both projects are led by people he identifies as fascists. He presents evidence that DHH (Omarchy) promotes racist, anti-feminist views and signals alliance with Elon Musk's politics, while Andreas Kling (Ladybird) has endorsed white replacement theory, mourned Charlie Kirk, and implemented policies hostile to inclusive language. DeVault questions why Cloudflare would direct approximately $100,000 to each of these projects, especially Omarchy, which is merely a preconfigured Arch Linux distribution. He frames this in the broader context of rising fascism in US politics and its presence within the FOSS community.
posts.key_insight: Corporate sponsorship of FOSS projects led by people with fascist politics normalizes and bankrolls fascism within the tech community, and the community should not be afraid to name it.

topQuotes.quote: If it walks like a fascist and quacks like a fascist… then why is Cloudflare giving them hundreds of thousands of dollars?
A multi-millionaire who can fund his 'Arch Linux for Obnoxious Reactionary Assholes' project his own damn self.
People should be much more afraid of being called out as fascist than they are afraid of calling someone a fascist.

posts.title: Libghostty Is Coming
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces libghostty, an embeddable library for terminal emulation that he first envisioned over two years ago. The first component, libghostty-vt, will be a zero-dependency library for parsing terminal sequences and maintaining terminal state, extracted from Ghostty's battle-tested core. The post argues that terminal emulation is a deceptively complex problem that hundreds of applications implement poorly with ad-hoc solutions. By providing a correct, fast, and portable C API, libghostty aims to eliminate this duplicated effort across the industry. The Zig API is available now for testing, with a C API coming soon and a stable release targeted within six months.
posts.key_insight: By extracting Ghostty's battle-tested terminal emulation into a zero-dependency embeddable library, libghostty can have far greater industry impact than the standalone application by eliminating redundant, buggy implementations across hundreds of products.

topQuotes.quote: Beyond correctness, implementing any form of terminal emulation is a waste of time for most developers.
Terminal emulation is not the core business of JetBrains, Visual Studio Code, GitHub, Vercel, Render, etc.
Terminal emulation is a classic problem that appears simple on the surface but is riddled with unexpected complexities and edge cases.

posts.title: The great falls of Boeing, Intel, and Apple
posts.summary: DHH argues that Boeing, Intel, and Apple all followed the same pattern of decline: installing CEOs without engineering or product backgrounds, then watching the company hollow out over roughly a decade. Boeing's troubles accelerated after 2005 with cost-cutting and outsourcing that led to the 737 MAX tragedies. Intel similarly installed a non-engineer CEO in 2005 and now teeters on the edge. Apple under Tim Cook initially appeared immune thanks to Jobs's pipeline, but the ten-year curse has arrived with failed bets on self-driving cars, embarrassing AI efforts, and the Vision Pro flop. DHH concludes that great companies need hands-on leaders who live and breathe their products, not bean counters who delegate product vision down the org chart.
posts.key_insight: When companies replace visionary engineer-CEOs with operations-focused leaders, they may coast on past momentum for a decade, but the inevitable cultural hollowing will eventually destroy what made them great.

topQuotes.quote: The profits still gush from glories past, and the tollbooth operation on the App Store, but the soul has left the machine.
It takes ten years for the culture of a great company to fall apart once the CEO seat is given to someone without an engineering or product background.
Legendary American companies that all got lost when a bean counter, marketing man, or logistics hand took over.

posts.title: How I'm Seeing AI Reshaping Work
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that AI is quietly revolutionizing how non-technical people work, citing examples of everyday people creating useful tools with ChatGPT. He emphasizes that AI is currently artificially cheap due to subsidies and venture capital, making now the optimal time to experiment and rework workflows before economics normalize.
posts.key_insight: The window to experiment with artificially cheap AI tools is open now, and the real competitive threat isn't AI itself but colleagues who embrace it while others hesitate.

topQuotes.quote: You won't be replaced by AI. That's the wrong fear. You'll be replaced by someone who learned to skate when you were still arguing about whether the ice is real.
AI reluctance feels very similar to the late '90s, when people thought texting was pointless or cell phones were a fad.
A lot of AI right now is artificially cheap. Subsidies, venture capital, or aggressive pricing strategies are offsetting the cost of running these systems.

posts.title: Is The Innovators Dilemma Outdated
posts.summary: Stancil argues that the classic innovator’s dilemma is breaking down because AI labs can ship generic models that accidentally cover niche products, blurring the line between platform and product. He extends this to cultural and corporate narratives: what once felt fundamental (launch events, polished messaging, quarterly reporting) can lose relevance as memes, AI, and narrative control reshape attention and disclosure. The result is a world where incumbents aren’t just slow to chase small markets; they may unintentionally subsume them while controlling the story around the data.
posts.key_insight: As AI models grow more general, they collapse the distinction between platform and product, letting incumbents accidentally subsume niches while reshaping how culture and corporate narratives are controlled.

topQuotes.quote: Apple’s playbook is stale; its vocabulary is stale; its entire economic model of demand generation is stale.
Everything becomes BI, but BI becomes the corporate spokesperson.
Now, when AI labs update their models or chat apps, products accidentally fall out.

posts.title: Are you an experienced software buyer? I could use some help.
posts.summary: Charity Majors announces she's been working on the second edition of 'Observability Engineering' and is adding a new section on 'Observability Governance.' She's asking for help from experienced software buyers because she lacks personal experience purchasing enterprise software at scale.
posts.key_insight: The observability field has matured so rapidly that even its leading practitioners need to crowdsource expertise on the governance, purchasing, and organizational challenges that now define how companies adopt observability at scale.

topQuotes.quote: I've never really bought software. Not like this. I've never even worked at a truly large, software-buying enterprise tech company.
Do you know, the phrase 'observability engineering teams' doesn't even occur in the first edition of the book? Try and search — it can't be found!
These days, observability engineering teams are everywhere.

posts.title: Personal Note
posts.summary: Gruber writes a brief personal note to readers explaining Daring Fireball's unusual week-long silence. He acknowledges the timing is particularly notable given it's new iPhone launch week. Without disclosing specifics, he assures readers he's dealing with a personal situation but is OK and recovering. He expresses eagerness to return to writing and promises iPhone reviews soon, closing with gratitude for readers' patience.
posts.key_insight: A rare moment of personal vulnerability from Gruber that inadvertently highlights both the fragility and authenticity of a one-person publishing operation.

topQuotes.quote: I mean, jiminy, it's new iPhones week.
I have been dealing with — and working through — a very personal situation for the past week. It's OK. I'm going to be OK.
Given the one-man nature of this site, that has meant that nothing has been published.

posts.title: Systems Software in the Large
posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on the unique challenges of developing 'systems software in the large' - complex foundational software that combines the difficulty of low-level systems programming with the organizational challenges of building big software across many people over extended periods. He uses Oxide's software update system as a case study, describing it as a 'wicked problem' that requires updating a distributed system while maintaining operability during the transition, all while supporting air-gapped deployments without the crutch of manual operator intervention. The post celebrates Dave Pacheco's OxCon talk on leading this two-year project, praising both the technical achievement and the hard-won organizational wisdom shared.
posts.key_insight: Building large-scale systems software compounds the already brutal challenges of low-level programming with organizational complexity, requiring not just technical excellence but hard-won wisdom about focus, scope, and team leadership.

topQuotes.quote: We cannot rely on the public cloud's hidden crutch of operators and runbooks. It is a problem so wicked, you can practically hear it cackle.
Should you be the kind of sicko inexplicably drawn to exactly the kind of nasty problems that Dave describes, consider joining us.
A system that is merely functional can be deceptively distant from the robustness required of foundational software.

posts.title: Get that job at Grab
posts.summary: Steve Yegge argues that software engineers should actively seek new jobs because global demand has outstripped supply, leading to unprecedented compensation packages and promotion opportunities. He revisits his famous Google interview advice, emphasizing that the fundamentals of computer science remain relevant after a decade. The post introduces a shocking discovery from recent interviews: multiple candidates from major tech companies couldn't perform basic arithmetic with large numbers like millions and megabytes. Yegge advocates for breadth-first interviewing to find knowledge gaps, while allowing one 'mulligan' for non-critical weaknesses. He concludes that knowing how to count—understanding orders of magnitude and computing units—should be non-negotiable for programmers.
posts.key_insight: The fundamentals of computer science—including the embarrassingly basic skill of understanding orders of magnitude—remain essential interview criteria, yet even engineers from top companies are failing these basics.

topQuotes.quote: If I ask you what you had for lunch, and you tell me that you're a little nervous but you're pretty sure you ate a hundred million sandwiches, then I think we probably have different definitions of 'nervous'.
They have run out of engineers in India. How the hell do you run out of engineers in India?!
I sort of feel like making a mistake that's off by eight orders of magnitude is hard to attribute entirely to nerves.

posts.title: Jeff Bezos, Jack Ma, and the Quest to Kill eBay
posts.summary: This post recounts Amazon's failed attempt to compete with eBay through their auction platform, symbolized by the forgotten mascot 'Mr. Tooth.' Yegge sets up a comparison between how Jeff Bezos and Jack Ma approached the challenge of breaking into eBay's locked market, suggesting Bezos got it 'spectacularly wrong' while Ma got it right. The post appears to be the beginning of a longer piece exploring the lessons from these contrasting approaches to market competition.
posts.key_insight: Even the most successful tech leaders have spectacular failures, and sometimes those failures fade from memory not because they were hidden, but because they were simply forgettable.

topQuotes.quote: Mr. Tooth wasn't purged from the internet through some vengeful move by Amazon to hide all traces of their embarrassment. No, the simple truth is that Mr. Tooth was so ineffectual that nobody ever bothered to remember him.
This is the story of the first time Jeff Bezos got something spectacularly wrong.
I wouldn't treat this post as a historical record so much as a way to get some life lessons from guys like Jack and Jeff—without spending as much tuition money as they did.

posts.title: Exploring Datasets in Positron
posts.summary: Wes McKinney presents a deep dive into Positron’s Data Explorer, motivated by his long-standing interest in visual data exploration and frustrations with console- and notebook-centric workflows. He argues that data viewers should reduce the friction of switching between coding and inspection while remaining responsive even for massive datasets. The core design emphasizes high information density to leverage human cognition, real-time updates, and consistent behavior across Python, R, and file-based workflows. Achieving this required a custom virtual data grid and extensive performance and memory optimizations. He concludes by positioning the tool as a community-driven effort, inviting feedback and outlining upcoming capabilities such as pinning, filtering, and broader backend integration.
posts.key_insight: A data explorer should be a fast, high-density, live-updating visual interface that keeps developers in flow and scales to modern datasets.

topQuotes.quote: We think that human cognition is underrated and we want to help augment your ability to see issues with your data.
We ultimately came to the conclusion that we needed to build a custom virtual data grid so that we could achieve a level of snappiness even with the most unwieldy datasets.
We wanted it to enable ephemeral exploration without disrupting your coding flow.

posts.title: Why I Don't Research; and Write from Experience
posts.summary: Simon Späti explains why he deliberately avoids traditional research when writing, preferring to draw entirely from personal experience and accumulated knowledge. He views his ongoing note-taking in his 'Second Brain' as a form of passive research that connects ideas over time. With two decades of field experience, he argues that things don't change as drastically as media suggests. His goal as a writer is not to recall information but to spark thinking and inspire readers through authentic, experience-based perspectives.
posts.key_insight: The most authentic and inspiring technical writing comes from lived experience rather than desk research, because a writer's goal should be to spark new thinking, not to relay information.

topQuotes.quote: The fun of writing is to express myself and explain something in my head. Not researching something and losing focus.
Things do not drastically change in an instant, even if the media and social media make us believe so.
I see my goal as a writer not to recall info, but to make you think, spark new ideas, inspire at best.

posts.title: A better future for JavaScript that won't happen
posts.summary: In response to a massive npm supply-chain attack, DeVault outlines a comprehensive vision for how the JavaScript ecosystem could fix its broken dependency management — then concludes none of it will happen. He argues that micro-dependencies, lack of a standard library, and npm's fundamentally flawed design enable these attacks. He points to Linux distributions as proven models for secure software distribution. The post is structured as an extended hypothetical of what responsible action would look like, only to deliver a bitter punchline: the industry will settle for symbolic gestures and learn nothing.
posts.key_insight: The JavaScript ecosystem knows exactly how to fix its supply-chain security crisis — Linux distributions solved it decades ago — but institutional inertia and misaligned incentives guarantee that nothing meaningful will change.

topQuotes.quote: No one will learn their lesson. This has been happening for decades and no one has learned anything from it yet.

Expect symbolic gestures – mandatory 2FA will be rolled out in more places, certainly, and the big players will write off meager donations in the name of "OSS security and resilience" in their marketing budgets.
This is the defining hubris of this generation of software development.

posts.title: Simplifying Data Lake Management with DuckLake
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt discuss DuckLake, their reimagination of lakehouse formats that replaces file-based metadata (JSON, Avro) with a SQL relational database for all metadata management while keeping data files on object stores. The project emerged from implementing Iceberg and Delta support in DuckDB and realizing the catalog database could handle everything more simply. DuckLake offers unique features like data inlining for small writes, file-level encryption with keys stored in the catalog, and cheap snapshots. The format scales from a single local DuckDB file to thousands of compute nodes, and can serve as a backend for the Iceberg REST catalog. They position it as DuckDB's multiplayer mode, fundamentally flipping the traditional architecture to make client-side compute the primary player.
posts.key_insight: By replacing file-based metadata with a SQL database, DuckLake dramatically simplifies the lakehouse stack while enabling features like instant small writes, cheap snapshots, and file-level encryption that are difficult or impossible with existing formats.

topQuotes.quote: It's not like you need 15 Docker containers, you need DuckDB and that's enough to basically run a local instance.

What surprised me the most was that it wasn't that hard. Like when I first got acquainted with Lakehouse formats, I thought this was very complex and I think it is. But then when we got started on Duck Lake, at every step of the way, it actually just kind of worked.
There's still such a huge gap between what is kind of possible and what is the daily reality of the data practitioners. It's almost like half of the population has to drive a car from the 1920s.

posts.title: As I remember London
posts.summary: DHH reflects on how London has changed since his youth, arguing that mass immigration has transformed the city beyond recognition, with native Brits dropping from over 60% to about a third of the population. He expresses sympathy for Tommy Robinson's march and the broader British frustration over demographic change and erosion of free speech, citing police arrests for online speech and the Pakistani grooming gang scandals. He draws parallels to Denmark, where the Social Democrat prime minister admitted that assumptions about immigrant integration were wrong. He concludes with encouragement that Britain can course-correct, pointing to Denmark's political evolution as a model.
posts.key_insight: The demographic transformation of London and the British state's suppression of dissent represent a failure of policy that mainstream politics must confront honestly, as Denmark's Social Democrats eventually did.

topQuotes.quote: British police are now making 30 arrests a day for wrongthink, wrongspeech, and other online transgressions against 'the regime narrative', as the BBC would have reported, if this were a statistic from a foreign nation.

There's absolutely nothing racist or xenophobic in saying that Denmark is primarily a country for the Danes, Britain primarily a united kingdom for the Brits, and Japan primarily a set of islands for the Japanese.
Unwilling to just let their society wither away while their bobbies chase bad tweets instead of the rampant street thefts or those barbaric rape gangs.

posts.title: What's a Foreigner?
posts.summary: The post argues that “foreigner” has no universal definition because legal categories and social perceptions diverge widely across countries. Using Austria and the EU, it shows how integration and free movement shift who is seen as foreign, often toward a practical “non‑EU” meaning. The U.S. debate, by contrast, centers on citizenship and allegiance, which shapes its policy fights. The author contends that legal immigration is misunderstood, being both more permissive and more restrictive than commonly believed, and that simplistic solutions like mass removal are impractical and unethical. The conclusion is that current immigration categories are misaligned with reality and will be increasingly strained by economic, demographic, and geopolitical forces.
posts.key_insight: Definitions of “foreigner” are contingent and increasingly incompatible with real-world mobility pressures, making today’s immigration frameworks both politically fraught and structurally inadequate.

topQuotes.quote: Most citizens of a country themselves would not qualify for skilled immigration visas.
On the one hand, what's called "illegal" is often entirely lawful.
Legal immigration itself is widely misunderstood.

posts.title: Apple has no one left who can say no
posts.summary: DHH argues that Apple has lost its ability to maintain product quality because there's no one left in leadership willing to say no to subpar releases. He uses CarPlay Ultra's buggy, laggy launch as the primary example, alongside Apple Intelligence's underwhelming gimmicks, pulled advertisements, and the persistent iPhone alarm bug. He attributes the decline to the absence of founder-led leadership obsessed with quality, contrasting Tim Cook's logistics-oriented management with Steve Jobs' product perfectionism. His conclusion is blunt: Cook has been a 'bozo on product' and needs to go.
posts.key_insight: Apple's quality decline isn't a talent problem but a leadership problem — without someone at the top who feels personal pain over shipping broken products, no organization can maintain its standards.

topQuotes.quote: Tim Cook was a masterful logistics hand to Jobs, but he's been a bozo on product, quality, and care. He's gotta go.
Apple no longer has anyone left who can say no.

I guarantee you there are programmers and designers inside Apple who know CarPlay Ultra wasn't ready to ship, but were overruled by managers who felt they needed to stick to their contractual obligations, quality be damned.

posts.title: Get Out of Technology
posts.summary: Hotz delivers a scathing attack on people who entered the technology industry purely for money and status rather than genuine passion for building things. He accuses these 'entryists' of corrupting tech culture and calls for radical action: making everything free to drive them out. The post argues that the profit motive has attracted the wrong people to technology. His proposed solution is to eliminate the financial incentive entirely, leaving only those who truly care about the work.
posts.key_insight: The only way to purify technology culture is to eliminate profit entirely, because mercenaries only leave when there's nothing left to take.

topQuotes.quote: You are an entryist piece of shit. And it's time for you to leave.
Give it all away to everyone for free. Then you'll have no reason to be here.
You heard there was money in tech. You heard there was status in tech. You showed up.

posts.title: The Weekend Windup #2
posts.summary: This is a casual weekend newsletter where Joe Reis shares personal updates on using AI to automate tasks, announces upcoming community events in Atlanta, Phoenix, and London, and curates interesting reads from the week. The post also provides an update on his upcoming data modeling book, targeting late January 2026 publication.
posts.key_insight: AI-powered task orchestration across multiple applications is becoming practical for personal productivity, though reliability issues remain a work in progress.

topQuotes.quote: I love clanker art, especially when it creates three hands
In general, the ability to chain multiple apps together and have AI orchestrate tasks and workflows is pretty damn cool.
It's not always perfect (the AI's time out a lot and don't finish tasks), but I expect the models and capabilities will improve a ton.

posts.title: AI Coding
posts.summary: Hotz argues that AI coding assistants are fundamentally just compilers that take English as input rather than a traditional programming language. He contends that English is actually a poor choice for specifying programs due to its imprecision, non-determinism, and non-local effects. The perceived value of AI coding tools is really an indictment of how bad existing programming languages, compilers, and libraries are. He cites a study showing AI makes developers feel 20% more productive while actually making them 19% slower, and criticizes the billions wasted on 'vibe coding' companies just as billions were wasted on flawed self-driving approaches.
posts.key_insight: AI coding tools are just compilers with English as the input language, and their perceived value says more about how bad our existing programming tools are than how good AI is.

topQuotes.quote: Is your way of thinking so fucking broken that you can't believe anyone cares more about the actual truth than make believe dollars?
Most people do not care to find the truth, they care about what pumps their bags.
That anyone uses LLMs to code is a testament to just how bad tooling and languages are.

posts.title: It Is The Internet
posts.summary: Stancil argues that our political extremity and violence are less about individual moral decline and more about the internet’s machinery amplifying our worst instincts. People are as mediocre as ever, but the systems around us now reward outrage, tribalism, and escalation, making polarization feel inevitable.
posts.key_insight: The internet’s incentive structure, not a sudden change in human nature, is the main driver of today’s polarized, violent political climate.

topQuotes.quote: We are all as mediocre as ever.
We can drive ourselves mad by living in the machine—posting to it, fighting on it, and, eventually, murdering for it.
Nobody is in charge of the thermostat anymore.

posts.title: Givers and Takers
posts.summary: Reis contrasts 'givers' who build trust by contributing freely to communities with 'takers' who constantly pitch and monetize every interaction. He argues that giving away most of your work for free and building relationships over time is paradoxically the fastest path to success, while aggressive cold-pitching is a losing strategy in a noisy market.
posts.key_insight: Building trust through generous contribution to your community creates more opportunities than aggressive self-promotion ever will.

topQuotes.quote: It feels weird having to remind grown adults about simple things like courtesy, but here we are.
If I tried to sell you something every five minutes, you'd tune out. And honestly, I'd feel gross about myself too.
Ironically, the long game is sometimes the shortest path to success.

posts.title: Joe Reis
posts.summary: Joe Reis hosts a spontaneous live AMA session where he fields audience questions about the current and future state of data and AI. The conversation ranges from his philosophical views on industry dynamics (referencing his 'Givers and Takers' article) to practical advice on organizing data teams and real-world AI use cases.
posts.key_insight: Reis offers unfiltered, wide-ranging perspectives on how data teams should be organized and where AI is actually proving useful in practice.

topQuotes.quote: The conversation covers a wide range of topics, from the philosophy behind my recent 'Givers and Takers' article to the practicalities of organizing data teams and the practical use cases for AI I'm seeing.

posts.title: This MCP Server Could Have Been a JSON File
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that the Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an unnecessary new standard when existing solutions like OpenAPI, gRPC, and CLIs already serve the same purpose of teaching LLMs to interact with software systems. He finds MCP's categorization of resources, prompts, and tools arbitrary and confusing, noting that the same flight search example appears in their documentation as both a tool and a resource. While acknowledging arguments about context window limitations and poor documentation, he's skeptical these justify a new protocol, especially as context windows expand rapidly. He concludes that developers should focus on building better tools using existing standards rather than adopting MCP.
posts.key_insight: MCP is a solution in search of a problem—existing standards like OpenAPI and CLIs already enable LLMs to interact with software systems, making a new protocol unnecessary overhead.

topQuotes.quote: The argument seems to be that the same people that wrote the bad OpenAPI specs are going to write good MCP specs. I don't buy it.
ChatGPT is actually better at CLIs than most humans—just watch Codex fly through sed, awk, and grep commands.
Viewed from this lens, MCP seems like a temporary kludge.

posts.title: Words are not violence
posts.summary: DHH reflects on thirty years of internet debate and argues that words must never be equated with violence. He recounts how the 2021 37signals controversy exposed him to real threats and mob behavior, which changed him but also prompted self-reflection about his own rhetorical sharpness. He condemns the trend of labeling ideological opponents as nazis or fascists to justify violent rhetoric, citing the morbid celebrations following Charlie Kirk's death as a sickening example. He concludes by urging people to keep showing up for earnest debate rather than retreating from public discourse.
posts.key_insight: Equating speech with violence erodes the foundation of civilized debate, and the proper response to intimidation is not silence but a renewed commitment to showing up for honest argument.

topQuotes.quote: What I cannot come to terms with, though, is the modern equation of words with violence.

There are plenty of factions here who've taken to justify their violent fantasies by referring to their ideological opponents as 'nazis', 'fascists', or 'racists'. And then follow that up with a call to 'punch a nazi' or worse.
The growing sense of permission that if the disagreement runs deep enough, then violence is a justified answer to settle it.

posts.title: DuckLake: Reinventing Data Lake Management
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt introduce DuckLake, a reimagined lakehouse format that replaces file-based metadata (JSON, Avro) with a SQL relational database for all metadata management while keeping data files on object stores. The project emerged from critically examining the Iceberg and Delta tech stacks while implementing support in DuckDB, realizing the catalog database could handle everything. DuckLake dramatically reduces complexity, cutting query round trips from ~7 to 2, and enables features like data inlining (small writes go directly to the metadata store), per-file encryption with a single boolean flag, and cheap snapshots. The format scales from local single-file setups to thousands of distributed compute nodes, and can serve as a backend for Iceberg REST catalogs.
posts.key_insight: By replacing file-based lakehouse metadata with a standard SQL database, DuckLake eliminates enormous complexity while preserving all the benefits of open table formats, enabling deployment scenarios from a single laptop to thousands of distributed nodes.

topQuotes.quote: There's still such a huge gap between what is kind of possible and what is the daily reality of the data practitioners. Sometimes we see what people are doing and you just want to cry a little bit.
It's almost like there's half of the population has to drive a car from the 1920s. That would not be considered great. Let's maybe get them a 1980s car.

I think what surprised me the most was that it wasn't that hard. When I first got acquainted with Lakehouse formats, I thought this was very complex. But then when we got started on Duck Lake, at every step of the way, we didn't really face any big hurdles.

posts.title: chapter five: sleuthing
posts.summary: This chapter follows the protagonist investigating their mother's disappearance while uncovering family secrets. After a conversation with Dave about their fathers both working at the mysterious 'Operant' company, the narrator searches through their mother's dresser drawers. They discover a document showing their mother receives $10,000 monthly from something called the Triangle Trust. When they can't reach their mother and try to track her car through the Mercedes app, they find the account has been changed to an unknown email address, 'skinner666@gmail.com', suggesting something sinister is happening.
posts.key_insight: The mundane act of searching through a parent's belongings reveals how little we know about the people closest to us and the secrets that structure our lives.

topQuotes.quote: I'd always just assumed my Dad was like, a Wall Street guy. Boring. Get money, fuck bitches.
It was always strange to me that that generation would just drop by. Like she didn't text her first?
I know that the people on there are out of the normal distribution of people, but those podcasts are one of my only exposures to the outside world.

posts.title: chapter seven: nudge
posts.summary: This speculative fiction piece follows Detective James Reese investigating Operant, a tech company he suspects of using subliminal environmental manipulation to control behavior. The investigation centers on Tom Park, a researcher who died falling from Operant's roof under suspicious circumstances—his phone records show he was home, but security footage shows a silhouette on the roof. Reese discovers the company uses precisely timed environmental cues (lighting, vibrations, HVAC sounds, elevator movements) to nudge people's decisions without their awareness. A leaked issue tracker reveals engineers debating whether their 'prediction' system is actually causing the outcomes it claims to predict. The detective concludes that the real sophistication isn't the technology itself, but the careful bookkeeping that makes everything look normal and leaves nothing illegal in the logs.
posts.key_insight: The most dangerous form of control isn't force or even persuasion—it's precise timing that makes manipulation feel like free will, hidden behind systems designed to leave no evidence.

topQuotes.quote: That's the thing about timing: when it works, it feels like you were going to do it anyway.
You can make a person look like a prediction if you take away all the moments where they would have surprised you.
The real trick isn't the tile or the lights. It's the bookkeeping. You arrange your systems so that there's nothing to subpoena.

posts.title: chapter six: dashcam
posts.summary: In this chapter of a serialized fiction story, the narrator discovers their mother is missing and enlists their tech-savvy friend Dave to help investigate. They find that someone from a VPN in Atlanta has been changing passwords on family accounts. Using a commercial dashcam data marketplace, they track the mother's car movements through Manhattan and Brooklyn. Despite finding a reassuring note from the mother claiming she went to the Hamptons, the narrator remains suspicious about the circumstances and the mysterious 'skinner666' who accessed her accounts.
posts.key_insight: In a world of ubiquitous surveillance, the infrastructure exists to track anyone, but it's commodified, fragmented, and ultimately still leaves gaps that enable both privacy and crime.

topQuotes.quote: One government mooching pig was the same as the next, and none of them were on his side.
Ending crime was always more a question of will than a question of ability.
Don't let the being high all the time fool you, he was the smartest guy in our friend group.

posts.title: chapter four: bagel
posts.summary: This is a fictional narrative chapter following John and his friends Brian and Dave at a bagel shop the morning after a night of smoking weed. The story reveals backstory about Dave's brother's suicide and its devastating effect on his family, while John realizes that cops who visited him were actually looking for his father, not him. The chapter establishes the dynamics between three high school friends - Brian the crass but loyal one, Dave the traumatized stoner, and John the narrator with an absent, possibly troubled father. The tone blends mundane teenage life with darker undercurrents of family tragedy and mystery.
posts.key_insight: This fictional chapter explores how teenage friendships form around shared brokenness - absent fathers, family tragedy, and coping mechanisms - while hinting at a larger mystery connecting the characters' families.

topQuotes.quote: He called himself a gentleman, but not in the creepy Elliot Rodger way, or in anything resembling the real meaning of the word.
At least my dad isn't some kind of Kaczynski freak.
His mom's credit card and we Venmo him. That's the type of guy he was.

posts.title: chapter three: father
posts.summary: This fictional narrative follows Jonathan, a brilliant quantitative trader at Operant Capital, who has been leading the RPS (rock-paper-scissors) project—an attempt to predict human choices before they're made. The research builds on neuroscience findings that brain scans can detect decisions before conscious awareness. After years of incremental progress reaching 54% first-round accuracy, Jonathan returns from a weekend away to discover his team has achieved something far more disturbing: perfect prediction of his entire sequence of moves, including when he would leave the room, all computed before he played a single game.
posts.key_insight: If prediction becomes perfect, the distinction between foreknowledge and determinism collapses—free will becomes an illusion we haven't yet instrumented away.

topQuotes.quote: Technical analysis is complete bullshit. This is a simple betting game played against other agents.
The market moves based on the thoughts and feelings of billions of humans, and they were all trying to do this same prediction thing.
If it knows all the predictions are going to be correct, why does it need any feedback?

posts.title: chapter two: party
posts.summary: This is a fictional narrative chapter following a protagonist who finds a detective's business card at his door and spirals into paranoia about why Nassau County police would visit his Brooklyn home. Despite not having committed any crime, he obsesses over police interrogation tactics and surveillance possibilities. He eventually calms himself, attributing the visit to his father's business dealings, and goes to a friend's basement party where they smoke weed and watch videos. The chapter explores the psychological weight of unexpected police contact and the comfort found in mundane social rituals.
posts.key_insight: The absence of guilt doesn't protect you from the psychological weight of institutional power - uncertainty about why you're being watched can be more destabilizing than knowing you've done something wrong.

topQuotes.quote: I had watched enough true crime to know that you should never talk to the police. And I wasn't arrogant enough to believe that I was different.
If I did commit a crime this would all be a lot easier, I'd know why they were here and what they wanted and could plan my next move accordingly.
Another system carefully crafted based on years and years of 'user feedback' designed to manipulate you into doing what it wants.

posts.title: The Weekend Windup #1
posts.summary: Joe Reis introduces a new weekend newsletter format called 'The Weekend Windup' designed to share curated reading recommendations and upcoming events. He acknowledges that weekend newsletters are challenging given limited reader attention, so he's keeping the format brief with short rants and curated links.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry is rushing ahead with AI implementations faster than the technology warrants, and we need more practical, grounded perspectives on its current capabilities.

topQuotes.quote: This week is frankly about how people are f*cking AI up. I say that in this specific order because we're getting ahead of our skis with where AI is right now.
Weekend newsletters are hard. You have things to do, and your attention is very limited. I want to respect that.
That said, I love AI, and want to support my friends pushing the field forward.

posts.title: chapter one: john
posts.summary: This is the opening chapter of a dystopian fiction piece set in a near-future where young people have become passive, asexual consumers glued to their phones. The protagonist John, a 19-year-old, reflects on his absent father, his aimless generation, and the sanitized internet before being interrupted by police detectives leaving a cryptic message. The story establishes a world where algorithms have neutered human desire and curiosity, redirecting people away from anything not 'advertiser friendly.' John's father abandoned Wall Street for an off-grid cabin filled with mysterious lab equipment, hinting at larger forces at play.
posts.key_insight: When algorithms become sophisticated enough to seamlessly redirect rather than overtly censor, they don't just control information—they extinguish desire itself.

topQuotes.quote: I tried asking ChatGPT about hookers and it told me to call them sex workers. And this was kind of triggering. Who the fuck does this machine think it is?
It wasn't actually censored, it would just redirect you elsewhere. And if you didn't pay careful attention, you wouldn't even notice it happening.
Once the algorithms got good enough and it was technically easy to censor, there was nothing holding them back.

posts.title: Engineering excellence starts on edge
posts.summary: DHH argues that the best engineering teams run their dependencies on edge (unreleased versions) in production, actively contributing to the frameworks they use rather than passively consuming them. He points to Rails 8.1 as proof, where Shopify, GitHub, and 37signals ran pre-release code in production for nearly a year before the beta announcement. He contends this practice transforms teams from open source consumers into real-time co-creators, producing a step function improvement in engineering competence. The post is a call to action for more companies to adopt this frontier approach.
posts.key_insight: Engineering excellence comes not from safely consuming stable releases but from running on edge, finding bugs yourself, and becoming a co-creator of the open source tools you depend on.

topQuotes.quote: Do you think bugs either just magically appear or disappear? No, they're put there by programmers and removed by the very same.
The best engineering teams take control of their tools.
If you want bug-free frameworks and libraries, you have to work for it, but if you do, the reward for your responsibility is increased engineering excellence.

posts.title: Thrice charmed at Rails World
posts.summary: DHH recaps the third Rails World conference in Amsterdam, celebrating its growing success with tickets selling out in under two minutes and expanded capacity. He reflects on the thriving Rails ecosystem, noting an influx of new programmers and renewed excitement around the framework. He credits multiple factors including his Lex Fridman appearance, Shopify's success, and the Rails Foundation's execution, but ultimately attributes the momentum to Rails simply being good. He announces next year's conference will move to Austin with dramatically expanded capacity, and expresses deep personal gratitude for finding his life's work early in his career.
posts.key_insight: Rails is experiencing a genuine renaissance driven by new developer adoption, institutional maturity through the Rails Foundation, and the framework's enduring quality after 22 years.

topQuotes.quote: There are many auxiliary reasons why we're riding a wave at the moment, but key to it all is also that Ruby on Rails is simply really, really good!
It actually took guts to sign on back then. I approached quite a few companies at that time who could see the value, but couldn't find the courage to support our work.
Not everyone is so lucky as I've been to find their life's work early in their career, and see it continue to blossom over the decades. I'm eternally grateful that I have.

posts.title: Stuff Costs Money
posts.summary: Stancil argues that software businesses inevitably need to charge for products, and the open‑source “build in public, then monetize” path predictably leads to backlash even though the outcome is obvious. He uses the Fivetran–Tobiko deal and OpenAI’s Statsig acquisition to show how monetization and data access incentives shape behavior, especially in AI and data tooling. The pattern repeats in data infra: niche open‑source tools drift toward BI and LLM‑adjacent products because that’s where the money is.
posts.key_insight: The incentives in software and data businesses make “open, community-first” narratives converge on monetization, and in AI the race for data turns acquisitions and telemetry into competitive weapons.

topQuotes.quote: If you build an open source data orchestration tool in 2025, not only will you eventually want to charge money for it; you will also realize that your tool understands business context, that business context is what makes LLMs better at answering questions, and that people seem to pay a lot of money for charts and chatbots.
Nobody is upset at Costco for charging people to shop there because they give away free samples!
Open core is a business model for the monetization of commercially produced open-source software; of course it will eventually cost money!

posts.title: The Pedantic Layer
posts.summary: The data industry wastes enormous energy debating pedantic definitions (like what counts as 'unstructured data') while practitioners who ignore these debates are quietly building valuable systems. This 'Pedantic Layer' creates unnecessary gatekeeping and slows progress, especially as AI is collapsing traditional data categories anyway.
posts.key_insight: Stop arguing about data definitions and start measuring success by the value you extract from data, because the future is collapsing all these categories anyway.

topQuotes.quote: The data industry often feels like a retirement home, where residents keep arguing about the same old things, year after year.
So next time you see someone arguing on LinkedIn about what unstructured data is, remember that while they're debating, someone else is shipping.
This obsession forms what I call the Pedantic Layer, the fog of war, where we endlessly quibble over minute variations of wording and meaning, in ways that float above the actual work of using data.

posts.title: Data Modeling Guide for Real-Time Analytics with ClickHouse
posts.summary: This comprehensive guide explains how to model data effectively in ClickHouse for real-time analytics, moving from theory to a practical implementation using NOAA weather data. The author argues that ClickHouse can serve as both storage and transformation engine, eliminating traditional ETL bottlenecks. Key strategies covered include denormalization, materialized views, partitioning, and pre-aggregation using MergeTree engines. The post demonstrates a complete data pipeline from S3 ingestion through ClickHouse transformation to Rill visualization, all configured in a single YAML file. The fundamental principle is minimizing joins at query time while choosing where in the pipeline to handle complexity based on performance and freshness requirements.
posts.key_insight: ClickHouse can serve as both your transformation engine and storage layer, handling complex aggregations at ingestion time rather than requiring traditional ETL pipelines, while its modeling strategies let you explicitly choose where in the pipeline to handle complexity based on your specific performance and freshness requirements.

topQuotes.quote: The moment the data is loaded, it is outdated.
ClickHouse doesn't only serve as storage but can handle much of the transformation and aggregation work directly, eliminating traditional ETL bottlenecks.
Data sketches use clever algorithms to deliver 99%+ accuracy for 1% of the cost and storage.

posts.summary: Ronacher pushes back against the glamorization of 996/007 work schedules, even while affirming his own love of intense work. He argues that meaning and output matter more than raw hours, and that burnout is not a badge of honor. The post draws a sharp line between a founder’s choice to work long hours and the ethics of demanding the same from employees. He frames sustainable success as a marathon, not a sprint, and says 12-hour days six days a week are incompatible with a full life. The conclusion is a clear call to resist normalization of 996 culture.
posts.key_insight: Intensity is valuable, but institutionalizing 996 is unethical and unsustainable because output and a full life matter more than hours logged.

topQuotes.quote: When you devote 72 hours a week to someone else's startup, you need to really think about that arrangement a few times.
Burning out on twelve-hour days, six days a week, has no prize at the end.
It's unsustainable, it shouldn't be the standard and it sure as hell should not be seen as a positive sign of a company.

posts.title: The impending crypto market crash
posts.summary: O'Neil draws a parallel between the 2008 financial crisis and the current cryptocurrency bubble, arguing that both involve government protection of markets for political reasons. She recounts her early skepticism of Bitcoin evangelists at Occupy Wall Street who couldn't explain how crypto would democratize banking.
