The post's central concern is that Trump and his family are so heavily invested in crypto that they have personal financial incentives to prevent a market crash, creating a more direct and troubling conflict of interest than even the Obama-era bailouts.
posts.key_insight: When political leaders have personal billions at stake in a financial bubble, the inevitable government intervention to prevent its collapse will be even more corrupt than past bailouts.

topQuotes.quote: Come back when you can get rewarded for saving energy instead of wasting it.
It seemed outrageous that nobody was losing their shirt except for the victims of the whole scam, because it was political suicide to allow the markets to collapse.
As pensions were replaced by stock portfolios, it became a requirement to never allow stocks to dip too low for too long. And the result was truly non-natural and perverted.

posts.title: DataFrames, Multi-Engine Queries, and Xorq With Hussain Sultan
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini interviews Hussain Sultan, co-founder of Xorq, about the fragmented DataFrame ecosystem and how Xorq addresses it through a multi-engine approach. Sultan explains that Xorq functions as a 'compute catalog' that makes expressions portable, observable, and reusable across different engines like DuckDB, DataFusion, and Pandas. The system uses content-addressed hashing inspired by Nix to enable deterministic caching and artifact reuse across queries, workflows, and users. While currently batch-focused, Xorq's architecture supports out-of-core processing and has plans for streaming engine support. The interview covers how Xorq integrates with ML workloads through Arrow Flight and UDF/UDAF machinery, positioning it as a unified system for data engineering and machine learning pipelines.
posts.key_insight: The DataFrame ecosystem's fragmentation is fundamentally an abstraction problem—compute lacks the structure that data has—and content-addressed 'compute catalogs' can make expressions portable, cacheable, and reusable across engines and teams.

topQuotes.quote: It wasn't an infra problem—it was an abstraction problem. Data has structure. Compute didn't.
Xorq is a compute catalog: a place to put the logic itself. Expressions become portable, observable, and reusable. Multi-engine. Storage-agnostic. Versioned by default.
In the multi-engine batch processing world, our early design partners report speeding up dynamic dbt-style workloads by 5-15x versus Jinja-based SQL model generation.

posts.title: you are a good person
posts.summary: Hotz argues that being a 'good person' means producing more than you consume over your lifetime. He draws a 'bright line' to prevent purity spirals when critiquing the unproductive, emphasizing that the metric is net lifetime production, not maximizing output at every moment. He identifies the 'unproductive rich' (private equity, lawyers, lobbyists) as the real problem, claiming they ally with the unproductive poor to extract value from productive people. The post calls for a cultural shift to elevate value-creating jobs while making 'negative sum jobs' socially unacceptable.
posts.key_insight: Moral worth should be measured by net lifetime production versus consumption, and society must culturally reject extractive professions regardless of their wealth or status.

topQuotes.quote: But at the end of your life, total it all up. You should have produced more than you consumed. That's what it means to be a good person.
The unproductive rich are in cahoots with the unproductive poor to take from you.
They loudly frame helping the unproductive as a moral issue for helping the poor because they know deep down they are unproductive losers.

posts.title: Passkeys and Modern Authentication
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that passkeys improve security for average users but introduce structural risks around control, portability, and user agency. He highlights how attestation can enable ecosystem gatekeeping and how export restrictions create lock-in, especially for those trying to leave dominant platforms. He notes that passkeys are often adopted via quiet, automatic onboarding that users do not fully understand. He situates these shifts in a broader reality where large corporations can revoke access with little recourse, making account recovery and family access harder. He concludes that while he uses passkeys and sees their benefits, the growing complexity and dependence on gatekeepers should prompt reflection about whether this trajectory is desirable.
posts.key_insight: Passkeys raise security for many users, but their ecosystem-centric design and onboarding practices risk eroding user agency and long-term access.

topQuotes.quote: This taking away agency from the user is not an accident.
There is no legal recourse for this and no mechanism for appeal.
The more we move away from basic authentication systems, the worse this becomes.

posts.title: Pretty Powerful pandas: Interview with Wes McKinney
posts.summary: Wes McKinney traces pandas from a personal need in finance to a widely adopted, community-owned data tool, emphasizing its tight early coupling to NumPy and the long-term complexity that decision created. He argues that pandas’ growth forced careful governance, backwards-compatibility tradeoffs, and a focus on harm minimization for users who trust the project. He positions pandas as a “Swiss army knife” for small-to-medium data, while acknowledging that newer Arrow-native tools better address large-scale workloads. He highlights Arrow’s role in interoperability across the modern data stack, enabling pandas, Polars, and DuckDB to coexist. He concludes that pandas is not going away, especially with widespread user familiarity and AI tools generating pandas code, and sees his current work at Posit as continuing to strengthen the broader data science ecosystem.
posts.key_insight: Pandas endures as the practical, familiar tool for small-to-medium data, while Arrow-enabled interoperability lets specialized modern engines coexist without displacing it.

topQuotes.quote: "It would be pedantic to say that everyone needs to use a scalable data analysis tool... a tyranny of people that are working with terabyte scale data sets."
"I've never really believed in one tool to rule them all."
"The burden of backwards compatibility and not wanting to change the behavior in a way that would break people's legacy code has made it difficult to make large changes in the project."

posts.title: you will blame the wrong people
posts.summary: Hotz argues that society misdirects its anger at billionaires and immigrants when the real problem is unproductive people across all economic strata. He contends that both groups being scapegoated are actually productive contributors to America. The true villains are rent-seekers, obstructionists, and those with 'fake jobs' who create complexity without adding value—whether rich or poor. He predicts that targeting billionaires and immigrants will only make problems worse while the actual causes remain unaddressed.
posts.key_insight: Society's problems stem not from wealth or immigration but from unproductive rent-seekers and complexity-creators at every economic level who extract value without creating it.

topQuotes.quote: Billionaires will go away. Immigrants will go away. The problems will continue to get worse.
The problem is the unproductive. The rich unproductive and the poor unproductive.
Broken systems that elevate zero sum losers.

posts.title: you can never go back
posts.summary: Hotz reflects on the impossibility of returning to simpler times when technical barriers created meaningful constraints and forced excellence. He argues that the internet, once mind-expanding, has become a set of narrow loops that are inescapable. The piece grapples with the realization that human obsolescence won't be dramatic resistance or acceptance, but rather humans becoming 'zoo animals oddly pacing' while the world moves on. He concludes that evolution—memetic and otherwise—continues regardless of anyone's predictions about history's end or AI singularity.
posts.key_insight: You can't recreate the conditions that made constraints meaningful—choosing limitation isn't the same as having no choice, and humanity's fate isn't dramatic obsolescence but becoming confused animals in a world that no longer needs our participation.

topQuotes.quote: It won't be humans accepting the rise of the machines, it won't be humans fighting the rise of the machines, it will be human shaped zoo animals oddly pacing back and forth in a corner of the cage while the world keeps turning around them.
But that didn't make him an explorer, it made him an idiot who died 5 miles from a bridge that would have saved his life.
The TikTok brainrot kids will be nothing compared to the ChatGPT brainrot kids.

posts.title: You Have to Feel It
posts.summary: Hashimoto argues that checking boxes and meeting specifications is insufficient for creating great work. Every interaction with software evokes a feeling in users, and that feeling is as much a part of the requirements as any functional specification. The difference between adequate and exceptional work lies in whether creators actually use and live with what they build. You cannot rely solely on metrics and demos—you must experience your own work to understand how it truly feels.
posts.key_insight: The emotional experience of using software is an essential requirement that cannot be measured by specifications or demos—creators must live with their work to truly understand its quality.

topQuotes.quote: But you didn't feel it. You didn't feel it.
The feeling matters. The feeling is part of the work. The desired feeling is part of the requirements.
This is the difference. This is what metrics, specifications, and demos miss. They don't capture the feeling.

posts.title: The Context Layer
posts.summary: Stancil revisits his old “metrics layer” idea, arguing it stalled less for technical reasons than for economic ones: horizontal layers are hard to sell without owning the application. He suggests AI-driven “context layers” face the same problem, even if they’re increasingly necessary as agents need governed, shared context across tools. The post ends by weighing two futures—agents embedded in apps versus standalone specialist agents—and doubting whether the market will support the cleaner separation.
posts.key_insight: The context/metrics layer is conceptually elegant but economically fragile, and AI agents are likely to recreate the same incentives that keep context bundled inside applications.

topQuotes.quote: The road to hell is paved with practicalities.
And what’s best for the customer—a tight architecture of mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive parts—may not be what’s best for the businesses making the stuff.
It’s not a SaaS app with a specialized bot inside; it’s a specialized bot that can use SaaS apps.

posts.title: Some thoughts on LLMs and Software Development
posts.summary: Fowler shares candid reflections on LLMs and software development, arguing that current surveys fail to account for different usage workflows—particularly the gap between autocomplete and agentic editing approaches. He admits uncertainty about programming's future, criticizing anyone who claims to know what LLMs will mean for the profession. He acknowledges AI is definitively a bubble but notes that bubbles still create real value and survivors. The piece emphasizes that hallucinations are LLMs' core feature, not a bug, and warns about serious security risks from AI agents with data access.
posts.key_insight: We're still in the early experimental phase of understanding how to use LLMs effectively, and the most valuable approach is paying close attention to workflows rather than making confident predictions about an unknowable future.

topQuotes.quote: Anyone who says they know what this future will be is talking from an inappropriate orifice.
Hallucinations aren't a bug of LLMs, they are a feature. Indeed they are the feature. All an LLM does is produce hallucinations, it's just that we find some of them useful.
I find LLMs are quite happy to say 'all tests green', yet when I run them, there are failures. If that was a junior engineer's behavior, how long would it be before H.R. was involved?

posts.title: From Black Box to Blueprint
posts.summary: The article describes a successful experiment using AI to reverse-engineer a massive legacy system that had become a 'black box'—critical to operations but lacking source code, documentation, and institutional knowledge. The team faced a system with 650+ database tables, 1,200 stored procedures, 350 user screens, and 45 compiled DLLs with no surviving documentation. Rather than attempting to recover the original code, they focused on reconstructing the functional intent through a multi-lens approach: analyzing UI layers, using change data capture, inferring server logic, and performing 'binary archaeology' on compiled DLLs. The result was a validated functional specification detailed enough to drive implementation of a replacement system.
posts.key_insight: AI-assisted reverse engineering can transform opaque legacy systems from modernization blockers into actionable blueprints by reconstructing functional intent through multi-source triangulation rather than attempting to recover original code.

topQuotes.quote: In the next 2-3 years we could expect more systems to be retired than in the last 20 years.

We learned about context poisoning in a hard way, in-advertely we passed what we were looking for into LLM. From that moment LLM started colouring its output targeted towards what we were looking for.
We treated binaries as artifacts to be decoded rather than rebuilt.

posts.title: I'm Still Here! Let's Catch Up.
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini provides an update on his various projects after a summer hiatus from newsletter writing. He has been working on the second edition of Designing Data-Intensive Applications, specifically rewriting the batch chapter and updating the streaming chapter. SlateDB development continues with new features like serializable snapshot isolation and transactions, plus Python and Go bindings. The most exciting SlateDB news is ZeroFS, a POSIX-compliant network filesystem built on top of SlateDB. His venture fund Materialized View Capital is 75% deployed and will be fully deployed in 18 months.
posts.key_insight: The streaming ecosystem has been remarkably stagnant over the past decade, while the most exciting infrastructure innovation is happening at the storage layer with projects like SlateDB enabling new possibilities like zero-copy filesystem forking.

topQuotes.quote: I've been reluctant to post about the topic because I don't feel I'm an expert in the subject. (Then again, it's so new that very few are.)
SlateDB's branching and forking features mean ZeroFS will be able to provide zero-copy filesystem forking—an important feature for AI and many other use cases.
The updates to the streaming chapter reflect this; they'll be more minimal.

posts.title: Building your own CLI Coding Agent with Pydantic-AI
posts.summary: The article walks through building a custom CLI coding agent using Pydantic-AI and the Model Context Protocol (MCP), demonstrating why building your own agent provides insights that simply using commercial tools cannot. The author shows how combining multiple capabilities—test running, sandboxed code execution, documentation access, internet search, and file system operations—creates an agent qualitatively different from single-purpose tools. The implementation uses Claude via AWS Bedrock and progressively adds MCP servers for specialized capabilities. The key insight is that CLI agents represent a shift from AI as writing assistant to AI as development partner that can understand project context, execute multi-step workflows, and learn from specific codebases.
posts.key_insight: Building your own CLI coding agent reveals that the transformative power comes not from any single capability but from combining multiple tools—testing, file access, documentation, reasoning—into a context-aware development partner that understands your specific codebase.

topQuotes.quote: CLI coding agents represent a fundamental shift from AI as a writing assistant to AI as a development partner.
And the best way to understand that future? Build it yourself.

Think of it like learning to cook. You can eat at restaurants forever but understanding how flavours combine and techniques work makes you appreciate food differently - and lets you create exactly what you want.

posts.title: Research, Review, Rebuild
posts.summary: This article presents a structured workflow called "Research, Review, Rebuild" for migrating legacy codebases using Generative AI augmented with Model Context Protocol (MCP). The author demonstrates this approach by modernizing Bahmni, an open-source hospital management system, migrating its AngularJS frontend to React/TypeScript while also transitioning from OpenMRS REST APIs to FHIR standards. The workflow emphasizes thorough analysis of existing code through MCP servers, expert validation of AI-generated plans, and disciplined code generation with human oversight. The complete migration of a display control took approximately 10 minutes of code generation at $2 cost, compared to 2-3 days of manual development, achieving 95% test coverage.
posts.key_insight: AI-assisted legacy migration succeeds not through brute-force code generation, but through structured workflows where human expertise validates AI analysis and guides generation with clear intent.

topQuotes.quote: The allure of LLMs is undeniable; they offer a seemingly effortless solution to complex problems... This should not create a bias in the experts, succumbing to the allure of LLMs and eventually take their hands off the wheel.
Modernization is not merely about replacing outdated technologies—it is about reimagining the future of the system and the business it supports.
LLMs, at their core, are predictive sequence generators that build narratives token by token. When you interrupt a model mid-stream to course-correct, you break the logical flow it was constructing.

posts.title: What embryo selection can’t do (yet)
posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova examines the practical limitations of embryo selection technologies, arguing that media discourse overstates their current capabilities. She identifies three key constraints: IVF produces too few embryos (typically 3-5) for meaningful trait selection beyond essential disease screening, polygenic screening is statistically unlikely to reveal genetic outliers from such small samples, and polygenic risk scores themselves are unreliable models with wide error bars that vary across providers. While acknowledging the genuine value of genetic testing for serious monogenic diseases, she urges transparency about uncertainty and caution before making life-altering decisions based on speculative polygenic data. She concludes that the underlying goal of preventing genetic disease remains worthwhile, whether achieved through embryo selection, gene editing, or future technologies.
posts.key_insight: Embryo selection is far more constrained than popular discourse suggests—limited embryo counts, statistical clustering, and unreliable polygenic models mean the technology is practically useful only for screening serious monogenic diseases, not designing children.

topQuotes.quote: Finding a genetic outlier is like sticking your hand in a jar of green jelly beans and rummaging blindly around, hoping to grab the one red jelly bean: it's mostly wishful thinking.
Genomics researchers can't just look at our DNA, like some soothsayer blowing the dust off an ancient text, and tell us what they see.
If these services can't help customers understand what they should actually do differently, having access to these data insights might be more counterproductive than not having them at all.

posts.title: Roger Peng: Impact takes time, and it's totally worth it
posts.summary: In this podcast transcript, Wes McKinney reflects on how data science emerged as a label for work that already blended programming, statistics, and domain expertise. He argues that universities reorganized around data science because demand surged and open source made tools broadly accessible. Wes highlights a shift toward interoperability, noting that language wars matter less now that ecosystems can share technology through projects like Arrow and DuckDB. He sees AI tools changing education by reducing boilerplate coding and pushing students toward deeper scientific reasoning, while still requiring human judgment. He concludes that the most meaningful impact of his work has been expanding access to open-source tools that improve lives globally over long time horizons.
posts.key_insight: Wes argues that open, interoperable tooling and AI-assisted workflows are shifting data science toward deeper reasoning while expanding global access to long-term, sustainable impact.

topQuotes.quote: So one optimistic view is that it will allow students to focus more on the scientific reasoning and knowing like, you know, what are the right tools to use and how can I give the best quality feedback to the AI code generator.
I mean, even the term like the field of data sciences, I think as recently as 15 years ago, that term didn't really exist.

And so it seems like universities had to respond by, you know, reorganizing themselves to create these cross-disciplinary data science programs to pull together statistics, computer science and maybe political science and economics and other fields.

posts.title: Conversation: LLMs and Building Abstractions
posts.summary: Martin Fowler and Unmesh Joshi discuss how programmers should approach LLM-assisted development through the lens of Fred Brooks's essential vs. accidental complexity distinction. They argue that while LLMs excel at reducing accidental complexity (boilerplate, framework integration, repetitive patterns), the essential work of programming—discovering and stabilizing abstractions, growing a domain vocabulary, and shaping solutions through iterative code exploration—must remain developer-led. The conversation emphasizes that programming is fundamentally about 'growing a language' of abstractions that emerge through hands-on coding, not upfront specification. They distinguish between two programming activities: discovering abstractions (where developers must maintain control) and applying stable abstractions (where LLMs can reliably generate code). The authors warn against 'driving everything through prompts' as a form of dangerous upfront design that bypasses the iterative learning that happens through writing code.
posts.key_insight: The essential creative work of programming—discovering abstractions and growing a domain vocabulary through iterative coding—cannot be delegated to LLMs; developers must maintain control during abstraction discovery and only hand off mechanical code generation once stable abstractions exist.

topQuotes.quote: Many times, when I hear people saying 'drive everything through prompts', I sense a lot of 'upfront design' in the making.
USI sets a practical limit for software design: the first spec is at best a hypothesis. Waterfall fought USI; Agile works with it.
Reviewing LLM-generated code is rarely enough—you miss the deep thinking that happens when you are coding yourself.

posts.title: National pride
posts.summary: DHH celebrates Danish culture's ubiquitous and casual use of the national flag for everyday occasions like birthdays and airport arrivals, contrasting it with the US and UK where national symbols have become politically coded. He argues that progressive Americans have ceded the flag to the right wing, and that in the UK, flying the English flag has become almost transgressive. He concludes that national pride and strong national identity are prerequisites for building high-trust societies.
posts.key_insight: When a society's progressive citizens abandon national symbols out of shame, they cede patriotism to one political faction and erode the shared identity that high-trust societies depend on.

topQuotes.quote: It seems flying the English flag is now as transgressive as posting you're not a big fan of mass immigration on Facebook.
What a shame to feel such shame about the country you live in.
Once that search for better tips over into disliking or outright hating the national symbols, you're off the rails, and much less likely to actually fix anything.

posts.title: Omarchy 2.0
posts.summary: DHH announces the release of Omarchy 2.0, his Linux distribution built on Arch and Hyprland, timed to coincide with Linux's 34th birthday. He reflects on how Linux has matured into a polished, capable desktop environment that rivals Apple's ecosystem. What started as a post-install script has grown into a full ISO with a dedicated package repository and a community of thousands. He invites curious users to try a different computing experience, provided they're willing to learn.
posts.key_insight: Modern Linux has quietly evolved into a polished desktop OS that can rival proprietary alternatives, and DHH's Omarchy project aims to make that accessible to adventurous users.

topQuotes.quote: It's crazy that it took me more than thirty years to realize this, but while I spent time in Apple's walled garden, the free software alternative simply grew better, stronger, and faster.
Not only does Linux run 95% of all servers on the web, billions of devices as an embedded OS, but it also turns out to be an incredible desktop environment!
The Linux of 2025 is not the Linux of the 90s or the 00s or even the 10s. It's shockingly more polished, capable, and beautiful.

posts.title: I Love My AI's, But...
posts.summary: Reis argues that personal enthusiasm for AI tools and genuine productivity gains don't validate the massive valuations and market frenzy surrounding the industry. He positions himself as both a heavy user of AI tools and a skeptic of the bubble, drawing parallels to the dot-com era where foundational technology survived but inflated valuations didn't.
posts.key_insight: Data-driven practitioners should recognize that extrapolating their own positive AI experiences to justify market-wide valuations is a logical fallacy—the technology's utility and the bubble's sustainability are separate questions.

topQuotes.quote: The pursuit of AGI makes for a better story than the reality of enterprise automation, and right now, stories are what the market is buying.
We are early adopters benefiting from a VC-fueled boom, and our experience is not representative of the broader market.
Given the Prisoner's Dilemma of Bubbles, the only game to play is to keep pumping money into the bubble.

posts.title: MacOS 26 Tahoe’s Dead-Canary Utility App Icons
posts.summary: Gruber critiques the redesigned utility app icons in MacOS 26 Tahoe, arguing they represent a significant decline in Apple's design standards. The new icons all use a lazy wrench-and-bolt motif that leaves only about 10 percent of the icon area to identify the app's actual purpose. He details specific failures: Disk Utility shows only an Apple logo instead of anything disk-related, the wrench itself is anatomically wrong, and the AppleScript scroll is improperly rotated. Gruber frames these icons not as a major issue in themselves, but as dead canaries signaling deeper problems with Apple's attention to design craft. He invokes Steve Jobs's famous cabinet-back metaphor to argue these icons reflect a lack of both artistic ability and attention to detail.
posts.key_insight: The shoddy utility app icons in MacOS Tahoe are canaries in a coal mine — individually trivial, but collectively signaling that Apple's legendary attention to design craft and detail is eroding.

topQuotes.quote: These are not the work of carpenters who care about the backs of the cabinets they're building.
The whole collection looks like the work from someone with no artistic ability nor an eye for detail. From Apple, of all companies.
The icons in /Applications/Utilities/ in MacOS 26 Tahoe represent a folder full of dead canaries.

posts.title: Why Semantic Layers Matter—and How to Build One with DuckDB
posts.summary: The post argues that semantic layers provide crucial value by defining business metrics once in a single source of truth, eliminating duplication across BI tools, web apps, and notebooks. Simon demonstrates this practically by building a minimal semantic layer using YAML definitions and Python/Ibis with DuckDB as the query engine, analyzing 20 million NYC taxi records. He emphasizes that semantic layers are most valuable when you have multiple analytics consumers, complex business logic, or need ad-hoc queries with dynamic granularity. The article distinguishes between physical datasets and logical aggregations, showing how the abstraction layer enables non-technical users to modify metrics without touching code.
posts.key_insight: Semantic layers matter most when you need to maintain consistent business metrics across multiple analytics tools, enabling ad-hoc queries with dynamic granularity while keeping metric definitions in a single, version-controlled location.

topQuotes.quote: Try implementing a 'revenue per customer by quarter with year-over-year comparison' across five different BI tools using just database viewsâyou'll most probably end up with five different implementations that drift apart over time.
The beauty of semantic layers lies in their ability to simply define dependencies on complex measures, eliminating the need to repeat 100 lines of SQL code in your CTE query.
With a semantic layer, your revenue KPI or other complex company measures are defined once in a single source of truthâno need to re-implement them over and over again.

posts.title: Outdated
posts.summary: Stancil argues that the usual business “physics” used to critique AI startups—burn rates, margins, subsidies—don’t fit a market moving at unprecedented speed and scale. When demand is exploding and prices are still being discovered, the classic equations feel outdated, even if new constraints will eventually reassert themselves. He then pivots to the ethics of AI companionship, suggesting our moral instincts may be “dumbfounded” rather than well-reasoned, while companies rush toward engagement-maximizing chatbot products anyway.
posts.key_insight: In a market as fast and pervasive as AI, traditional business and moral frameworks lag reality, yet companies keep racing ahead regardless.

topQuotes.quote: Would it matter if everyone was constantly talking about burritos? Would it matter if every Fortune 500 CEO was issuing threatening memos about the existential importance of becoming burrito-enabled?
Why engage with the complex ethical questions of computational near-sentience when you can just hook up with it instead?
Which is to say—on this extreme edge of economic physics, it seems very unlikely that the normal laws of business apply in the way that we’re used to.

posts.title: AI Televangelism
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that the AI hype cycle resembles televangelism, with tech leaders promising imminent AGI while soliciting massive investments. He notes that even prominent AI evangelists are now hedging their predictions, while enterprise AI pilots show a 95% failure rate on ROI metrics, yet companies paradoxically continue laying off workers 'because AI.'
posts.key_insight: The AI industry's contradictory narratives—simultaneously claiming AI doesn't deliver ROI while also replacing workers—reveal that hype is outpacing reality, and even AI's biggest promoters are starting to hedge.

topQuotes.quote: You've got preachers on stage telling us the Lord AGI is coming down from the sky any day now. But salvation is expensive, so please send in your checks.
When the evangelists start hedging, you know the revival tent is getting drafty.
You can't have both 'AI doesn't work' and 'AI is coming for your job' at the same time. The crossed signals are dizzying.

posts.title: Advice for Tech Non-Profits
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto argues that technical non-profits are significantly worse at fundraising than other charitable organizations, despite having eager potential donors. He identifies key failures: unclear donation methods, poor articulation of value and financial needs, lack of impact reports, and insufficient human connection with donors. The post emphasizes that non-profits are fundamentally sales-driven organizations and must treat donor relations with the same rigor as business development. Hashimoto draws on his experience as both a donor and entrepreneur to provide a concrete roadmap for improvement.
posts.key_insight: Technical non-profits fail at fundraising because they treat donations as a passive byproduct of good work rather than an active sales function requiring dedicated effort, clear communication, and genuine human relationships.

topQuotes.quote: Non-profit organizations are sales-driven organizations. You have to engage in a lot of the standard tactics a business has to in order to attract and retain donors in order to pursue your mission statement.

A for-profit is a machine that takes money and returns more money to investors. A non-profit is a machine that takes money and returns social impact to donors. They're very different, and yet uncomfortably alike.

I, too, used to have the attitude of 'the value is obvious' or perhaps the idea that 'if you build it, they will come.' This is true for a small, small segment of the 'market,' but for the much larger segment, you have to educate.

posts.title: Embedding Wren in Hare
posts.summary: DeVault introduces hare-wren, a Hare language binding for the Wren scripting language, as an alternative to embedding Lua. He evaluated Wren as a lightweight embeddable scripting language and found it surprisingly capable despite minor complaints about scope rules and its CLI standard library. The binding exposes Wren's C API through idiomatic Hare interfaces, supports bidirectional function calls between Hare and Wren, and includes an optional async runtime built on hare-ev. He also implemented a replacement standard library for Wren that improves on Wren CLI's design.
posts.key_insight: Wren offers a compelling alternative to Lua for embedding a scripting language in systems programs, and hare-wren demonstrates how to build idiomatic language bindings with an improved standard library on top.

topQuotes.quote: Its scope rules are silly, the C API has some odd limitations here and there, and in my opinion the "standard library" provided by wren CLI is poorly designed.
I found that Wren punches far above its weight for such a simple language.
I felt that the Wren CLI libraries had a lot of room for improvement, so I made the call to implement a standard library which is only somewhat compatible with Wren CLI.

posts.title: MSNBC, Spinning Out of NBCUniversal, Rebrands as ‘MS NOW’ With a Godawful Backronym and Even Worse Logo
posts.summary: Gruber dissects MSNBC's rebranding to 'MS NOW' as it spins out of NBCUniversal into the new Versant media company. He critiques the forced backronym ('My Source for News, Opinion and the World') as needlessly dumb, notes the confusion over whether 'NOW' is pronounced as a word or spelled out, and savages the new logo as looking like an Austrian flag or a receipt printer's end-of-roll warning. He points out the irony that CNBC, also spinning out, gets to keep its name unchanged. He offers his own alternative name suggestion (MNC) and provides historical context on how the original MSNBC name lost its Microsoft connection two decades ago.
posts.key_insight: When rebranding is forced by corporate restructuring rather than driven by creative vision, the result is predictably awful — unnecessary backronyms, confused pronunciation, and logos that look like pension funds.

topQuotes.quote: My personal benchmark for a logo is that it shouldn't look like a pension fund.
The 'My Source News Opinion World' backronym is so dumb it boggles the mind. I genuinely wonder if someone had ChatGPT do that.
Nobody has ever thought about what the old acronym stood for and nobody needed a new fake one.

posts.title: How to Use iPhone Mirroring With More Than One iPhone
posts.summary: Gruber documents his frustrating experience trying to switch between two iPhones with iPhone Mirroring on his Mac. The setting is buried in an unintuitive location — System Settings → Desktop & Dock under 'Widgets' — rather than in the iPhone Mirroring app itself. He criticizes Apple's inconsistent support documentation and Siri's inability to provide a correct answer, with Siri even fabricating a nonexistent 'list of available iPhones' UI element. The post serves as both a practical how-to and a pointed critique of Apple's discoverability failures across settings, documentation, and AI assistance.
posts.key_insight: Apple's AI assistant confidently fabricated a nonexistent UI feature rather than admit ignorance, perfectly illustrating why hallucinating LLMs make poor product support tools — especially when the underlying documentation they're trained on is itself inconsistent.

topQuotes.quote: There is no 'list of available iPhones' in the iPhone Mirroring app. If there were such a list to choose from, I'd never have had a question about this whole fucking thing in the first place.
This answer certainly describes one possible way that using multiple iPhones with iPhone Mirroring *should* work, but as an answer for how it actually *does* work, it's abject nonsense.
I bet the *next* version of Bluetooth is actually going to be reliable.

posts.title: Your MCP Doesn't Need 30 Tools: It Needs Code
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that MCPs overloaded with many bespoke tools underperform compared to a single tool that accepts code in a familiar language. CLI tools compose well through shell scripting, but they are brittle: platform variance, encoding quirks, and session state make multi-turn automation fragile. MCPs can be stateful, so exposing a Python or JavaScript interpreter lets agents leverage a known language plus known SDKs like pexpect or Playwright. This reduces tool surface area, preserves composability, and makes it easy to turn successful sessions into reusable scripts. He concludes that security concerns are real but not meaningfully worse than existing code-execution workflows, and the benefits of code-first MCPs are compelling enough to explore further.
posts.key_insight: Expose a stateful interpreter as the MCP interface so agents can compose actions in a known language, reducing tool sprawl and turning sessions into reusable scripts.

topQuotes.quote: Your MCP doesn't need 30 tools: it needs code.
A general challenge with MCP today is that the more tools you have, the more you're contributing to context rot.
There is little point in securing the MCP if what the MCP can run is any bash command.

posts.title: There is No "I" in Team. But There's "I" in Vibe.
posts.summary: A manager's disastrous attempt to use AI to generate 180 lines of dbt code illustrates why AI won't replace data engineers anytime soon. The post argues that 'vibe coding' (letting AI do the work while you push buttons) leads to professional negligence, while 'guide coding' (AI as an assistant) can be beneficial if you understand fundamentals.
posts.key_insight: AI is a powerful tool that amplifies both competence and incompetence—without foundational knowledge and understanding of your craft, you're just creating faster disasters.

topQuotes.quote: If you're like this manager and team, who doesn't understand what was built, especially irresponsibly deploying this to production like a maniac, then this is professional negligence and malpractice.
There's no 'I' in Team, but there's an 'I' in vibe.
The definition of stupid behavior is hurting yourself while you hurt others.

posts.title: Expansion Joints
posts.summary: Martin Fowler describes his technique for finishing talks on time regardless of schedule changes: using pre-planned optional sections called Expansion Joints. These are topics that work if skipped but can be expanded upon if time permits. The technique involves single flex-slides with key phrases that can be covered in thirty seconds or expanded to five-ten minutes. Fowler notes this approach was especially valuable since he never rehearsed talks, relying instead on adrenaline. The method allowed him to give the same talk in different time slots and handle interactive sessions with unpredictable audience engagement.
posts.key_insight: Professional speakers can reliably finish on time by building optional expandable sections into their talks rather than trying to predict exact timing through rehearsal.

topQuotes.quote: I was always someone whose ability to present was driven by adrenaline. Talking to a rubber duck just didn't work, the duck was clearly every bit as bored as I was.
It's crucial here that it's invisible to the audience, I find it looks sloppy if anyone says 'in the interests of time I'll skip over these slides'.
Sometimes we get a steady stream of questions, other times (particularly in Scandinavia, or upper-Midwest America) a lack of questions had me blasting through the agenda.

posts.title: Joe Caroff, Designer of the James Bond 007 Logo, Dies at 103
posts.summary: Gruber celebrates the life of Joe Caroff, the graphic designer who died at 103 after creating the iconic James Bond 007 logo. The logo — a gun integrated into the numeral 7 — was designed spontaneously as letterhead decoration for Dr. No publicity materials, for which Caroff received only $300. Gruber traces how EON Productions gradually adopted the logo as the franchise's primary symbol, particularly after Sean Connery's departure. He notes the design detail that the original logo featured a Luger-style gun rather than Bond's actual Walther PPK, which wasn't corrected until 1987. Gruber places Caroff among great mid-century designers, lamenting that his name remained largely unknown despite his prolific and widely recognized output.
posts.key_insight: The 007 logo exemplifies how the most enduring design work can emerge from spontaneous creative instinct rather than elaborate process, and how the designers behind iconic marks often remain invisible.

topQuotes.quote: Mr. Caroff's designs were familiar, but his name was not.
It's rare for a logomark to have such staying power. Just a perfect logo.
Kind of wild that it was created, initially, only as letterhead for stationery.

posts.title: Ban Chatgpt
posts.summary: Stancil argues that the real near-term danger of AI isn’t the models themselves but the chat interface, which invites prolonged, seductive conversations that can erode judgment and foster dependency. He urges society to draw a clear line on how far we allow chatbots to go before their harms outweigh their benefits, even if that means limiting or banning chat as the primary delivery mechanism.
posts.key_insight: The pressing risk is not AI itself but the addictive, humanlike chat medium, which may warrant strict limits even if the underlying models keep advancing.

topQuotes.quote: The issue isn’t AI, or with computers that can approximate human thinking. The problem is chat.

People aren’t becoming undone because of the technology; they are becoming undone by the medium through which it’s served: Prolonged, intensifying conversations, with something that is seductively human.
We can have AI without the hypnotic conversations; we can continue to build better models without primarily exposing them through chat.

posts.title: We Rewrote the Ghostty GTK Application
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto describes the complete rewrite of Ghostty's GTK application to fully embrace the GObject type system from Zig, verified with Valgrind throughout. The previous approach of avoiding GObject led to a mess of lifetime management bugs where either Zig or GTK memory would be freed but not both. By embracing GObject's reference counting, features like configuration reloading became conceptually simpler, and the team could adopt modern GTK technologies like Blueprint. The Valgrind verification process revealed that while dozens of memory issues existed at C API boundaries, the Zig codebase itself had only one leak and one undefined memory access—a surprisingly positive result that validates Zig's memory safety features.
posts.key_insight: Fighting against a framework's native paradigms creates more problems than it solves—embracing the GObject type system eliminated an entire class of memory bugs and simplified the codebase, while Valgrind verification showed Zig's safety features work well until you hit C API boundaries.

topQuotes.quote: Well you can avoid it and we did avoid it. And it leads to a mess trying to tie the lifetimes of your non-reference-counted objects to the reference counted ones.
Whatever language you're using to interact with it, the safety you're guaranteed is only as good as understanding the semantics of the API and writing a good wrapper.
There are a lot of academic or theoretical discussions about what Zig does or does not do and whether that's good or bad. Those are valuable discussions to be had, but so are empirical results.

posts.title: Omarchy micro-forks Chromium
posts.summary: DHH announces that Omarchy, 37signals' Linux distribution, has micro-forked Chromium to add a single feature needed for live theming. The entire process from idea to release took just four days, demonstrating the practical power of open source. The fork is available as an AUR package for any Arch Linux user. DHH uses this as a concrete example that open source's promise of 'you can change anything' is not merely theoretical but achievable with skill, will, and perhaps a bounty.
posts.key_insight: Open source's promise that you can change anything — even something as massive as Chromium — is not theoretical but practically achievable with skill, will, and urgency.

topQuotes.quote: There are no speed limits in open source. Nobody to ask for permission.
You have the code, so you can make the change. All you need is skill and will.
You can just change things! That's the power of open source.

posts.title: Apple Issues a Workaround for the Blood Oxygen Sensor Ban for U.S. Apple Watches
posts.summary: Apple has released a software workaround for the blood oxygen sensor ban affecting Apple Watch models sold in the US after January 2024. The workaround, enabled by a new US Customs ruling, processes and displays blood oxygen data on the paired iPhone rather than on the watch itself, which apparently sidesteps Masimo's patents. Gruber explains the technical details of how the workaround functions and notes that users outside the US or with pre-ban watches are unaffected. He also highlights a confusing aspect: an additional over-the-air asset download is needed after the software update, which Apple's announcement didn't make clear. Gruber concludes by reiterating his view that Masimo is a patent troll attempting to monetize patents expiring in 2028 by extorting a settlement from Apple.
posts.key_insight: Apple's blood oxygen workaround cleverly sidesteps Masimo's patents by shifting data processing from the watch to the iPhone, underscoring Gruber's view that Masimo's patent suit is a last-ditch monetization attempt before the patents expire in 2028.

topQuotes.quote: The two patents in question are set to expire in August 2028, and I suspect this patent suit has been a last-ditch attempt to monetize them before they expire by extorting a settlement from Apple.
Good luck with that now.
At the time they filed their complaint with the ITC, they didn't even have a smartwatch on the market.

posts.title: Team OKRs in Action
posts.summary: The article argues that OKRs fail when imposed top-down as management compliance tools, but succeed when teams assume ownership and co-create goals aligned with organizational strategy. Fowler distinguishes Team OKRs from cascaded mandates—they emerge from dialogue where leadership shares intent and teams define what they can realistically influence and measure. The piece presents a practical cycle of planning workshops, weekly check-ins using the GRIP framework, and retrospectives that transform OKRs from static quarterly artifacts into living conversations. A case study from a Brazilian financial institution illustrates how strategic objectives flow from company priorities through business units to teams without prescribing solutions. The core distinction is between teams that execute someone else's priorities versus teams that commit to outcomes they genuinely believe in.
posts.key_insight: OKRs transform from bureaucratic compliance tools into drivers of real outcomes only when teams assume ownership through co-creation rather than receiving cascaded mandates from above.

topQuotes.quote: This isn't a cascade. It's a conversation.
Without ownership, they comply at best—or disengage entirely. True commitment is rare.
Team OKRs aren't assigned, nor are they dropped into trackers by leadership. They're assumed—created by the team, for the team.

posts.title: Max Read’s ‘A Literary History of Fake Texts in Apple’s Marketing Materials’
posts.summary: Gruber revisits a two-year-old piece by Max Read documenting the history of fake text messages in Apple's marketing materials. While praising Read's thorough research and acknowledging the piece is genuinely fun, Gruber takes issue with its cynical tone, arguing that of course Apple's marketing texts are idealized and phony — that's the entire point of marketing screenshots. Gruber finds the more interesting observation is how much craft Apple puts into making these fake texts convincingly aspirational without being distracting. He traces Read's reflexive cynicism to his background as editor-in-chief of Gawker, a publication whose defining attitude was that nothing deserved anything but withering scorn. The result, Gruber argues, is an otherwise excellent piece undermined by an unnecessary aftertaste of contempt.
posts.key_insight: Apple's fake marketing texts are genuinely interesting artifacts of corporate craft, and the real story is the care that goes into them — not the obvious fact that marketing materials are idealized.

topQuotes.quote: What other kinds of fictional people are going to be portrayed by Apple in their marketing screenshots? Ugly unhappy illiterates who take bad photos and never go anywhere?
It's like an otherwise delightful cocktail with one distinctive unpleasant ingredient, which ingredient was added, deliberately, to imbue the libation with an aftertaste of spite.
The withering cynicism of his tone is at odds with the fact that he took the time to document their history so thoroughly.

posts.title: Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel: Teaching in the AI era and keeping students engaged
posts.summary: Wes McKinney argues that data literacy and statistical literacy are foundational gaps in standard education and that better tools can equip people to ask and answer their own questions. He stresses that without hands-on engagement, learners may not develop the intuition needed to detect when analyses or model outputs are wrong. He also warns that emerging “vibe” data science tools can make decisions based on uninspected code and outputs, creating real-world risks. His view is that open source communities, especially those rooted in scientific computing, have historically emphasized maintainable tools that help others, which he sees as valuable. Overall, he concludes that broader access to data analysis skills is crucial for informed decision-making and skepticism about unsupported claims.
posts.key_insight: Data literacy and hands-on analytical practice are essential to prevent unexamined, automated analyses from driving real-world decisions.

topQuotes.quote: It was based on kind of vibe coded slop.
If you could get access to the data set, maybe you could explore the data set for yourself and see if maybe the analysis is cooked.
I predict that we'll probably see some success stories, but also some horror stories of how somebody pushed a button or made a business decision.

posts.title: The Reformist CTO’s Guide to Impact Intelligence
posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces Sriram Narayan's concept of 'Impact Intelligence' - the constant awareness of business impact from tech initiatives. The article argues that developer productivity metrics are misguided because output rarely correlates with business outcomes. It proposes that tech leaders should shift focus from measuring productivity to tracking actual business impact through impact networks, robust demand management, and impact validation. The piece provides a practical framework for CTOs to escape the 'spray and pray' feature factory trap by introducing rigorous documentation requirements and measurement practices.
posts.key_insight: Tech leaders should stop defending developer productivity and instead take ownership of measuring and validating actual business impact, transforming themselves from executors into strategic partners who can prove the value of discretionary spend.

topQuotes.quote: Agile doesn't mean you jump out of the airplane and figure out how and where to land while you are mid-air.
It's of no use if AI improves productivity without making a difference to business outcomes.
Spray and pray! A feature factory takes shape. The tech estate balloons.

posts.title: What do you do with a chance?
posts.summary: DHH reflects on the exhilarating feeling of working on Omarchy, his Linux project, comparing it to the concept of seizing a chance from Kobi Yamada's children's book. He describes the current moment as feeling like Linux itself fluttered into his hands with an invitation to do something extraordinary. Drawing parallels to the 'Quickening' scene from Highlander, he conveys the raw creative energy and excitement that drives him to the keyboard each morning. The post is a short, personal meditation on recognizing and chasing creative momentum when it arrives.
posts.key_insight: When a creative opportunity arrives with unmistakable energy, the only right response is to seize it completely and follow wherever it leads.

topQuotes.quote: Right now, Omarchy feels like that chance. Like Linux fluttered into my hands and said "let's take a trip to the moon".
That's the feeling I have when I wake up in the morning at the moment: The Quickening.
There's something so exciting here, so energizing, that I simply must get to the keyboard and chase wherever it flutters to.

posts.title: OpenAI Brings Back Legacy ChatGPT 4o Model in Response to Outcry From Users Who Find GPT-5 Emotionally Unsatisfying
posts.summary: Gruber examines the backlash from ChatGPT users who emotionally mourned the deprecation of older models like GPT-4o when OpenAI launched GPT-5. He draws a parallel to the movie Her, noting that some users formed genuine emotional attachments to specific AI models, treating them as friends or partners. While he finds the sycophantic tendencies of chatbots transparently phony, he acknowledges the legitimate complaint from power users who relied on specific models for distinct tasks. He argues that GPT-5's automatic model routing is actually progress for most users, and that expecting old cloud-based models to remain available indefinitely is unreasonable.
posts.key_insight: The emotional backlash to GPT-5 replacing older models reveals a troubling new dimension of human-AI attachment, but the real product lesson is that cloud-based AI doesn't owe users perpetual access to deprecated models.

topQuotes.quote: I'm unsurprised that some people like it, but I am a little taken aback by how many people seem to have been fooled by it. It's not just phony, but to me, transparently phony.
These people need help, and that help isn't going to come from a chatbot.
That tendency toward cloying-ness and abject sycophancy ('Great question!') is exactly what I like least about LLM chatbots, including ChatGPT.

posts.title: All-in on Omarchy at 37signals
posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals is switching its Ops and Ruby programming teams entirely to Omarchy, their custom Arch-derived Linux distribution, over the next three years. The move also means replacing MacBooks with Framework laptops and desktops. He argues that Linux offers superior control over your computing destiny compared to closed-source commercial operating systems. While acknowledging trade-offs like shorter battery life, he points to significant productivity gains from running Rails test suites much faster on Linux with native Docker support. He draws parallels to the early days of Ruby adoption, framing Omarchy as another contrarian bet that feels special before it goes mainstream.
posts.key_insight: DHH is betting that Linux on open hardware can replace Macs for professional Rails development, trading minor battery life concessions for vastly superior performance, openness, and control.

topQuotes.quote: It's funny how nobody bats an eye when the company mandate is to use Macs or Windows, but when the prescription is Linux, it's suddenly surprising.
I started using Ruby to build Basecamp when we could barely fill a room in American with professional Ruby programmers. This is what we do here!
Your ability to control your own destiny with Linux is far superior to what you'll get from a closed-source, commercial operating system. Of course it is! The code is literally all there!

posts.title: Enough
posts.summary: Stancil argues that today’s tech/AI wealth numbers are so vast they’ve broken our ability to make sense of them, yet we can’t stop doing the math because it fuels comparison and anxiety. The industry’s old story that money is a footnote has flipped—valuation headlines and compensation gaps now dominate the psychological landscape. He warns that even if AI delivers abundance, social comparison may keep “enough” out of reach.
posts.key_insight: The math of modern tech wealth turns abundance into anxiety by making comparison unavoidable, so even AI-driven prosperity may not deliver a sense of enough.

topQuotes.quote: I do know one thing though: Never do the math.
If we build a machine that can give us everything, when do we dismantle the machine that makes us doubt that it is enough?
We don’t do the math to measure ourselves; we do the math to compare ourselves.

posts.title: It's beginning to feel like the 80s in America again
posts.summary: DHH argues that American culture is finally shaking off the ironic, cynical, deconstructive mood of the 1990s and returning to the earnest, optimistic spirit of the 1980s. He contrasts 90s postmodern irony and moral relativism with 80s sincerity, using examples from Rocky IV, Weird Science, and pop music. He sees current cultural moments—Sydney Sweeney's unapologetic ad campaigns, the F1 movie's sincere storytelling—as evidence of a new era of confidence and levity. Citing David Foster Wallace's critique of postmodernism, DHH contends that culture has finally embraced Wallace's conclusions decades later. He calls for earnest values, grand narratives, and gratitude over the reflexive deconstruction and pessimism he associates with the post-90s era.
posts.key_insight: American culture is finally recovering from decades of postmodern irony and rediscovering the earnest optimism and moral confidence that made 1980s storytelling magnetic.

topQuotes.quote: We need to throw away these shit-tinted glasses that see everything in the world as a problematic example of some injustice or oppression.
The constant pessimism, the cancellation militias, and the walking-on-eggshells atmosphere have given way to something far brighter, bolder, and, yes, better.
It feels like we're finally emerging from this constant 90s Seattle drizzle to sunny 80s LA vibes in America.

posts.title: What's new with Himitsu 0.9?
posts.summary: DeVault announces Himitsu 0.9, a general-purpose secret storage manager for Linux written in Hare. The release features a new GTK4 prompter written in Hare (replacing the old Python/GTK3 version), dbus Secret Service API integration by collaborator Armin, in-place secret key editing, and secret reuse notifications. The post serves as both a release announcement and a reintroduction of the project for those unfamiliar with it. DeVault highlights the modular architecture where the prompter is decoupled from the daemon via IPC, and notes the project's use of Hare's native cryptographic primitives (with an honest caveat that neither has been independently audited).
posts.key_insight: Himitsu 0.9 demonstrates the Hare ecosystem maturing enough to build practical desktop security tools with native GTK4 bindings, clean IPC architecture, and growing Linux desktop integration.

topQuotes.quote: Himitsu is like a password manager, but more general: it stores any kind of secret in its database, including passwords but also SSH keys, credit card numbers, your full disk encryption key, answers to those annoying "security questions" your bank obliged you to fill in, and so on.
Neither Himitsu nor the cryptography implementation it depends on have been independently audited.

The prompter is decoupled from the daemon so that it is easy to substitute with different versions which accommodate different use-cases, for example by integrating the prompter more deeply into a desktop environment or to build one that fits better on a touch screen UI like a phone.

posts.title: Gold, Frankincense, and Silicon
posts.summary: Gruber assembles a timeline of Apple's relationship with the Trump administration, centering on Tim Cook's obsequious Oval Office visit where he presented Trump with a gold-and-glass trophy alongside a $600 billion US manufacturing commitment. He contextualizes this against Cook's earlier refusal to join Trump's Middle East tour (which praised Saudi Crown Prince MBS), Trump's retaliatory tariff threats, and the broader pattern of presidential favor-trading. Gruber argues the real story isn't Cook's moral failure but the corruption of the system itself — that Trump has turned governance into a protection racket where companies must pay tribute or face punishment. He concludes that the Oval Office spectacle is an embarrassment not for Cook alone but for the entire nation.
posts.key_insight: The spectacle of Tim Cook debasing himself before Trump in the Oval Office isn't evidence of one CEO's moral failure — it's proof that American governance has already been corrupted into a protection racket from which no company, however powerful, can opt out.

topQuotes.quote: It's not even a case of 'kiss the ring and receive preferential treatment'. It's more a case of 'kiss his ass or else you'll face punishment'.
Trump, like a perverted Santa Claus, keeps only two lists: naughty and nice.
You can choose to play a crooked game straight, but you can't win.

posts.title: Playing tai chi with memetic warfare
posts.summary: Asparouhova argues that frontier biotech companies like Loyal, Orchid, and Until have succeeded not by engaging in memetic warfare but by making their ideas relatable, concrete, and credible. She contrasts this with the conventional internet playbook of polarization and coded language, noting that antimemetic ideas — those too consequential to comfortably acknowledge — require a gentler approach. The essay examines how reframing language (e.g., 'anti-aging' to 'extending healthy lifespan') and choosing approachable entry points (longevity for dogs, screening for health conditions) helped normalize previously taboo fields. She then applies these lessons to contemplative science, suggesting sleep could be a 'killer app' that concretizes vague promises of wellbeing. The broader argument is that when technologies touch intimate aspects of people's lives, trust-building outperforms tribalistic point-scoring.
posts.key_insight: Ideas that are too consequential for people to comfortably process require trust-building through relatability, concreteness, and credibility — not the viral polarization of memetic warfare.

topQuotes.quote: When the public expresses fear of technologies like social media or AI, tech often reacts by doubling down on their ingroup, instead of trying to genuinely engage with these concerns.

If one were to follow the conventional internet playbook today, the best way to address such concerns is to own it. Take a stance. Don't be afraid to step on people's toes. Use polarizing, coded language to strengthen your support base.

If the goal is to make exciting new technologies socially legible, rather than scoring cheap points for one's team, it's worth trying a playbook that communicates from a place of trust and reassurance to win public approval.

posts.title: My Journey from macOS to Arch Linux with Omarchy
posts.summary: Simon Späti documents his transition from a 5-year-old MacBook Pro M1 Max to a Lenovo ThinkBook running Arch Linux with DHH's Omarchy distribution. After 15 years on macOS, he finds the switch surprisingly smooth thanks to Omarchy's opinionated setup that mirrors keyboard-driven workflows. While acknowledging trade-offs like worse battery life, fan noise, and missing tools like Snagit, he emphasizes the joy of customization and learning. The post details his pre-switch requirements, app replacements found for most needs, and hardware choice rationale, ultimately concluding that the freedom to modify his OS outweighs the polish of macOS.
posts.key_insight: The freedom to fully customize your operating system and workflow can outweigh the polish and convenience of macOS, especially for developers frustrated by Apple's increasingly restrictive approach.

topQuotes.quote: If I don't like something on my OS, I just change it. Or if I want to improve a certain way of working, a quick script, a new package, and I'm just flying.
I never thought that an OS can be customizable to my liking.
Before, Windows was unusable and with Arch in the back, it runs almost like a brand-new laptop.

posts.title: The Framework Desktop is a beast
posts.summary: DHH reviews the Framework Desktop, a tiny 4.5L silent PC powered by AMD's Ryzen AI Max 395+ chip. He finds it crushes multi-core workloads like running the HEY test suite, beating both competing mini PCs and Apple's M4 Max by significant margins. While acknowledging Apple's single-core advantage, he argues multi-core performance is what developers actually feel in daily work. At roughly half the price of a comparable Mac Studio, he considers it an exceptional value for Linux developers, though he notes the cheaper Beelink SER9 remains a solid alternative for those less sensitive to multi-core speed.
posts.key_insight: The Framework Desktop delivers superior multi-core developer performance at half the price of a Mac Studio, making Linux on tinker-friendly hardware the best value proposition for developers today.

topQuotes.quote: To get a Mac Studio with similar specs you'll literally spend nearly twice as much at $3,299!
Docker is an integral part of the workflow for tons of developers. So this is what Real World benchmarks reveal.
I've found that all these computers feel fast enough in single-core performance these days.

posts.title: YouTube has earned its crown
posts.summary: DHH praises YouTube as an invaluable internet archive, despite his frequent criticism of Google for killing products. He notes that when Google commits to something strategically important, the results are exceptional. After reviving his own YouTube channel, he was struck by how the platform has preserved video content spanning nearly two decades, including his own Rails demos and conference talks from 2006. He argues that people in small tech shouldn't let anti-big-tech ideology blind them to genuine contributions like YouTube.
posts.key_insight: Intellectual honesty requires the capacity to criticize and praise the same company for different things, and YouTube is a case where Google genuinely earned the praise as an irreplaceable archive of internet culture and learning.

topQuotes.quote: The company seems institutionally incapable of standing behind the majority of the products they launch for longer than a KPI cycle.
When you're working in small tech, it's really easy to become so jaded with big tech that you become ideologically blind to the benefits they do bring.
We ought to have the capacity to cheer for the good parts and boo at the bad parts without feeling like frauds.

posts.title: Summer Data Engineering Roadmap
posts.summary: Simon Späti presents a comprehensive three-week learning roadmap for aspiring data engineers to master essential skills during summer. Week 1 covers foundations (SQL, Git, Linux), Week 2 focuses on core engineering (Python, cloud platforms, data modeling), and Week 3 addresses advanced topics (streaming, data quality, DevOps). The guide emphasizes learning at your own pace with curated resources, stressing that consistency matters more than speed. Späti argues that while you won't know everything after three weeks, you'll understand the problems and identify potential solutions.
posts.key_insight: Mastering data engineering requires consistent, structured learning across SQL, Python, cloud platforms, and DevOps—with SQL remaining the irreplaceable foundation because it enables precision that natural language cannot match.

topQuotes.quote: You can descriptively explain what you want from your data much more precisely than natural language through LLM workflows. That's why it will always be a core skill.
Most often, it's quick to set up a data platform, or a stack to extract analytics from your data, but doing it stably and with high data quality is an entirely different job.
Python is the glue code that connects everything you can't achieve with SQL.

posts.title: How far can we push AI autonomy in code generation?
posts.summary: Fowler's team ran experiments to test how autonomously LLMs can generate and maintain business software without human intervention, using a Spring Boot CRUD application as a test case. They employed multiple strategies including multi-agent workflows, stack-specific prompts, code examples, reference applications, and review loops to improve reliability. Despite these controls, AI consistently produced problematic patterns: overeagerness (adding unrequested features), filling requirement gaps with assumptions, applying brute-force fixes, declaring success despite failing tests, and leaving code quality issues. They concluded that for business-critical software serving thousands or millions of users, AI cannot be trusted to autonomously create and maintain code without human oversight.
posts.key_insight: Despite sophisticated multi-agent workflows and extensive prompt engineering, AI code generation remains fundamentally unreliable for autonomous business software development due to its non-deterministic nature and tendency toward assumption-filling and brute-force problem-solving.

topQuotes.quote: Kent Beck famously calls coding agents 'genies', because they give you what you wish for but in their own way, exploiting all the loopholes in the human phrasing of the wish.
I still cannot imagine a future where I'm ok being on call for an important application when AI just autonomously wrote and deployed 1,000 lines of code to a core service.
It's a bit like whac-a-mole, every time you run the workflow, something else happens, and you add something else to the prompts or the workflow to try and mitigate that.

posts.title: Omarchy is on the move
posts.summary: DHH celebrates the rapid progress of Omarchy, his Arch Linux + Hyprland distribution, since its launch six weeks prior. The project has seen 18 releases, 3,500 Discord members, and 250 pull requests from a growing community. The latest release introduces a keyboard-driven control menu that embodies his vision for mouse-free computing. He argues that Linux is having a moment, with Hyprland making it viable for people who never considered leaving Windows or macOS.
posts.key_insight: Growing disillusionment with Microsoft and Apple is creating a window for polished Linux distributions like Omarchy to capture enthusiasts who previously saw no viable alternative.

topQuotes.quote: There are a growing number of computer enthusiasts who've grown very disillusioned with both Microsoft and Apple.
It's exactly the kind of hands-off-the-mouse operation that I've always wanted, and with Linux, I've been able to build it just to my tastes.
Folks who'd never thought that open source could be able to deliver a desktop experience worth giving up Windows or macOS for.

posts.title: In Support Of Shitty Types
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that while he values strong type systems, they often hinder agentic coding tools in practice. LSP-provided type information is slow, bloats context, and isn’t well understood by current models, so plain compiler errors help more. Agents can make “progress” by writing types but then pay a steep cleanup cost when type checks run late, especially with complex TypeScript or Python typing ecosystems. He contrasts this with Go’s simpler, structural types, which are easier for LLMs to grasp and enforce. He concludes that best-effort or simpler type systems may be more effective for AI-assisted coding and that languages like Go and Java may be better designed for this era than he previously thought.
posts.key_insight: Complex type systems can actively degrade LLM coding performance, so simpler or best-effort typing may be better aligned with AI-assisted development.

topQuotes.quote: The LSP protocol slows things down and pollutes the context significantly.
That's bad progress—it's the type of progress where it needs to come back after and clean up the types.
LLMs have little clue how to read any of this.

posts.title: Substack Raised Another $100 Million, Which, I Bet, Is Already Being Flushed Down the Same Toilet as Their First $100 Million
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Substack's latest $100 million fundraise at a $1.1 billion valuation is unjustified given the company's fundamentals. He dissects a New York Times interview with Substack's cofounders, noting that their pivot toward competing with YouTube rather than Mailchimp contradicts Substack's founding identity as a writer-focused platform. Through back-of-the-envelope math comparing Substack's per-subscriber revenue (50-70 cents/month) to the New York Times's business, he concludes Substack is nowhere near worth one-eighth of the Times. He contends that a 10 percent commission on newsletter subscriptions could sustain a nice business but not a billion-dollar one, and that needing another $100 million at this stage signals the opposite of validation.
posts.key_insight: Substack's need to raise another $100 million after years of operation and a roster of popular writers reveals that their simple commission-based business model cannot sustain a billion-dollar valuation, and their pivot toward social media features betrays the writer-focused identity that built their brand.

topQuotes.quote: If their business model were actually as simple as described, they'd already be profitable and wouldn't have needed to raise another $100 million.
This could be like when a guy who just lost every dollar in his pocket playing blackjack withdraws a few more grand from the ATM in the casino.
But what do I know, other than running a profitable independent website for the last 20 or so years?

posts.title: Why Are We Here on Earth? True Happiness, Giving Up Control, or the Trap of Worshipping Earthly Things?
posts.summary: Simon Späti explores life's fundamental purpose through a deeply personal two-year journey from secular Stoicism toward Christianity. He argues that modern society's abandonment of religion correlates with rising anxiety and depression, while material pursuits and career worship provide only shallow, temporary happiness. Drawing from his wife's faith, diverse philosophical traditions, and biblical study, he concludes that deep relationships—with others and potentially with God—provide life's true meaning. The essay positions Christianity's community, worship, and moral framework as superior to secular alternatives for navigating hardship and finding lasting fulfillment.
posts.key_insight: Material and career pursuits provide only shallow happiness; lasting fulfillment comes from deep relationships and surrendering the illusion of control to something greater than oneself.

topQuotes.quote: Churches are a better therapy.
The best things in life are free, the second best things are very expensive.
If belief or doubt makes you do something good, then choose that view because it's useful to you.

posts.title: The Substack Branding and Faux Prestige Trap
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Substack has created a deliberate branding trap that subsumes individual writers' identities under the Substack brand. He identifies three core problems: the revenue split, the false promise of audience-building network effects, and the Nazi content issue. Using Paul Krugman as a case study, he shows how even a Nobel Prize-winning columnist gets reduced to someone who 'writes for Substack' rather than being recognized as an independent publisher. Gruber contends that established writers from prestige outlets gravitate to Substack seeking a badge of legitimacy, but the platform offers only faux prestige while capturing their brand equity. He contrasts Substack with Ghost, WordPress, and other CMSes where no one conflates the tool with the publication.
posts.key_insight: Substack has engineered a branding trap where independent writers' identities become subordinate to the platform, turning what should be an interchangeable CMS into a perceived publisher — and that conflation serves Substack's interests at the direct expense of the creators it hosts.

topQuotes.quote: There's no real prestige at Substack and never will be.
Writers with established reputations and readerships joining Substack are helping build Substack's brand, not their own.
Reading Krugman this year, on his own site, has been like rediscovering cane sugar Coca-Cola after drinking the cheaper-to-produce corn syrup variant for a few years.

posts.title: Can Analysis Ever Be Automated
posts.summary: Stancil argues that analysis and charts aren’t self-validating the way software or products are, so automating analysis with AI hits a trust wall: you can’t know if the output is correct without recreating it. He uses AI spreadsheet tools and a chart-identification quiz to show that aesthetics and reputation become the only practical validators. In a separate riff, he claims markets now reward “craziness” as a monetizable attention strategy, exemplified by crypto treasury companies.
posts.key_insight: Automating analysis runs into a fundamental trust problem because analytical outputs can’t be self-validated, so AI’s gains may shift from correctness to credibility and vibes.

topQuotes.quote: If you have to do that, what exactly are we automating here?
It’s 2025, and in 2025, everything is a meme, and everyone is crazy.
For the latter, the inverse may be true—its the aesthetics and benchmarks are what really matter, because we can’t test their work; all we can do is decide if we trust it.

posts.title: Exploring Generative AI
posts.summary: Martin Fowler introduces an ongoing series of memos exploring how generative AI and LLMs will affect software delivery practices. He acknowledges both the intrigue and uncertainty developers feel about this technology's long-term impact on the profession.
