The series documents learnings from Fowler and his Thoughtworks colleagues as they investigate AI coding assistants, autonomous agents, and related topics. The collection spans from foundational explorations in 2023 to more recent pieces on spec-driven development and vibe coding in 2025.
posts.key_insight: Rather than making definitive pronouncements about AI's role in software development, the most valuable approach is systematic, ongoing investigation documented as it unfolds.

topQuotes.quote: If you're wondering why we use a donkey in our series image, read why I made up a persona for an eager, yet unreliable, coding assistant.
Like many software developers I am intrigued by the possibilities, but unsure what exactly it will mean for our profession in the long run.
I'm posting various memos here to describe what my colleagues and I are learning and thinking.

posts.title: Executives should be the least busy people
posts.summary: DHH argues that executives with packed calendars have made a poor trade, sacrificing availability for the illusion of efficiency. He points to old-school executives who kept open schedules, ready to act when critical moments arose. Modern executives, he contends, are insecure about appearing busy and damage their businesses by grinding constantly. He uses the Mad Men character Bert Cooper and even Elon Musk as examples of executives who maintain availability despite their responsibilities. The core message is that open calendars signal intentional readiness, not laziness.
posts.key_insight: Executive value lies in availability for critical moments, not in performative busyness that crowds out the capacity to respond when it matters most.

topQuotes.quote: You've traded all your availability for efficiency. That's a bad deal.
But modern executives are so insecure about seeming busy 24/7 that they'll wreck their business trying to prove it.
Sacrificing themselves thin so they can run a squirrel-brain operation that's constantly chasing every nutty idea.

posts.title: Our $100M Series B
posts.summary: Oxide Computer Company has raised a $100M Series B led by USIT, more than doubling their total capital raised to date. The post recounts their journey from skeptical VCs in 2019 who doubted the market existed, through building an integrated hardware-software stack from first principles, to shipping their first system and achieving growing customer adoption. The funding will enable them to scale manufacturing, systems, and operations to meet increasing enterprise demand.
posts.key_insight: Radical transparency—through podcasts, public RFDs, documentation, and open source code—became Oxide's unexpected competitive advantage, dramatically shortening enterprise sales cycles by letting customers deeply evaluate the product before engaging.

topQuotes.quote: The on-premises market was sick of being told that they either don't exist or that they don't deserve modernity.

We felt that there was in fact substantial technical risk in the enormity of the task we put before ourselves—but we also knew that if we could build it (a huge if!) there was a huge market, desperate for cloud computing on-premises.
We're going to kick butt, have fun, not cheat (of course!), love our customers—and change computing forever.

posts.title: Agentic Coding Things That Didn't Work
posts.summary: Armin argues that most agentic-coding workflow automations he tried didn’t stick, not because the ideas are bad, but because they didn’t outperform simply talking to the model with rich context. He frames automation with strict rules: only automate frequent tasks and delete automations that don’t get used. Slash commands, hooks, print mode, and sub-agents all fell short due to friction, lack of structure, poor reliability, or context chaos. He finds that copy/paste, speech-to-text, and ad‑hoc guidance are more effective than rigid prompts. The post concludes that context management and reliability are still the bottlenecks, and over-automation risks mental disengagement and quality regressions.
posts.key_insight: Agentic workflows only help when they beat direct, high-context communication, and today the reliability and context-management gaps often make automation a net loss.

topQuotes.quote: Using an LLM for tasks that don't require inference is the wrong approach in my book.
There is a big hidden risk with automation through LLMs: it encourages mental disengagement.
There are real dragons there!

posts.title: Tea, the Women’s Dating Gossip App Riddled With Security Vulnerabilities, Remains #3 on the US iPhone App Store
posts.summary: Gruber examines the Tea dating gossip app, which remains #3 on the US iPhone App Store despite being riddled with serious security and privacy vulnerabilities. He questions whether Apple should remove or delist the app, noting the tension between App Store curation promises and user choice. The app's developers have barely acknowledged the breaches, burying their response on Instagram rather than their website or App Store listing. Gruber finds it fascinating that Tea is virtually nonexistent on Android's Play Store, not even cracking the top 200, and suspects Google has quietly delisted it from discovery while keeping it available. He argues this measured approach — delisting from popularity charts without full removal — is the right balance, and urges Apple to follow suit.
posts.key_insight: Apple's failure to act on a virally popular but security-compromised app undermines its core App Store safety promise, and Google's apparent quiet delisting offers a sensible middle-ground approach Apple should adopt.

topQuotes.quote: I can't recall anything like this before, where an app riddled with outrageous security/privacy vulnerabilities remains virally popular.
Here's Tea, sitting at #3, providing a service that many women want, and the entire thing is shockingly untrustworthy.
If this is what Google is doing, Apple should follow their lead.

posts.title: Funding the Future of Open Source
posts.summary: Wes argues that open source funding is harder than adoption because it runs into budgeting, accounting, and internal approval, requiring developers to make a business case and act as evangelists. He says projects like Apache Arrow needed risk-tolerant corporate backers and that the lone-developer myth ignores the real need for organizational resources. The Apache Software Foundation matters not just for licensing but for neutral, public governance that enables competitors to collaborate and build trust. He connects these governance lessons to his long-term focus on interoperability and composability across languages, including Feather and the Arrow ecosystem. At Posit, he applies those ideas to Positron’s data viewer, emphasizing fast, live, and scalable data exploration for humans. He concludes that emerging LLM workflows will require new ways to expose data efficiently beyond context windows.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable open source requires corporate-grade funding and transparent governance, and the same long-term interoperability mindset should now shape data tools for both humans and LLMs.

topQuotes.quote: People have this idea that a lot of open source projects succeed on single passionate developer working nights and weekends, squirreled away in the attic, but the reality is that it takes—you know, corporate resources—it takes a village essentially.
Because the person who wants to fund the project, they want to fund the project, but they have to convince the powers that be to allocate budget to do the work.
We're like, how can we build tools that could help maybe tear down the language walls and some of the language wars?

posts.title: The beauty of ideals
posts.summary: DHH argues that ideals exist to inspire humanity toward excellence and that the cultural movement to dismantle beauty and achievement standards is misguided. He traces a line from 90s grunge and loser culture through Naomi Wolf's critique of beauty standards to modern body-positive advertising campaigns like Calvin Klein's. Using a Copenhagen billboard featuring an obese model as his central example, he argues that 'representation' culture patronizingly validates mediocrity rather than inspiring improvement. He concludes that the human yearning for excellence is reasserting itself after decades of postmodern deconstruction.
posts.key_insight: Ideals exist to inspire people toward excellence, and cultural movements that replace aspiration with mere representation are patronizing and ultimately futile against humanity's intrinsic drive to admire and pursue the best.

topQuotes.quote: It proposes we're all better off if all we see is a mirror of ourselves, however obese, lazy, ignorant, or incompetent, because at least it won't be 'unrealistic'. Screw that.
It was a decade that made it cool to be a loser.
The last thing we need is a patronizing message that however little you try, you're perfect just the way you are.

posts.title: From Async/Await to Virtual Threads
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that while async/await in Python exposed more developers to concurrency, it also imposed complex machinery, colored functions, and leaky abstractions. With free-threading on the horizon, the neat guarantee of async suspension points erodes, leaving developers to juggle both async and thread complexity. He proposes re-centering concurrency on threads—specifically virtual threads—paired with structured concurrency so the runtime, not user code, carries most of the complexity. He sketches a “better API” that keeps blocking-style code while enabling structured parallelism, showing how this could avoid futures for most common cases. He concludes that the idea is a conversation starter, but the direction—virtual threads plus structured concurrency—could make async/await largely unnecessary for future code.
posts.key_insight: Virtual threads plus structured concurrency could let Python keep a simple blocking style while moving concurrency complexity into the runtime, making async/await largely unnecessary.

topQuotes.quote: Unfortunately, recent changes with free-threading make that guarantee rather pointless.
In many ways, what we really want is to go back to the drawing board and say, "What does a world look like that only ever would have used threads with a better API?"
The idea of no longer having to deal with colored functions really appeals to me and I hope we can explore it a bit.

posts.title: Everyone Is Crazy Now
posts.summary: Stancil argues that social media’s attention mechanics push people toward performative extremity, and that attention has shifted from a path to power into the power itself. As a result, the influencer logic now governs politics, business, and culture, creating a leadership class selected for virality rather than competence or decorum.
posts.key_insight: The attention economy has fused celebrity with authority, so the people who can go viral increasingly become the people who run everything.

topQuotes.quote: Elon Musk is not the richest man in the world despite acting manic; he is richest man in the world because of it.
Everyone is forged in the same fever, by the same game that rewards the same behavior: Be unhinged.
Attention is no longer a bridge to power; it is the power.

posts.title: Building competency is better than therapy
posts.summary: DHH argues that building competency through hobbies and educational pursuits is a more effective treatment for mild depression than talk therapy, especially for men. He cites studies showing exercise outperforms therapy and pharmaceuticals for garden-variety depression, then extends the argument to competency-building activities like learning Linux, history, or motorcycle repair. Drawing on Csikszentmihalyi's concept of flow, he contends that stretching your abilities through challenging pursuits produces genuine happiness. He emphasizes that these pursuits also build community through topic-based connections, and holds up open source as the ideal combination of mastery, community, and meaningful purpose.
posts.key_insight: Mild depression is better treated through building competency, finding community, and pursuing purpose than through talk therapy — and open source offers all three.

topQuotes.quote: Most people just don't need therapy, they need purpose, they need competence, they need exercise, and they need community.
At least for men who'd prefer to feel useful over being listened to, which, in my experience, is most of them.
Leave it available for the severe, pathological cases that exercise and the pursuit of competence can't cure.

posts.title: You expect principles but should wish for none
posts.summary: DHH argues that people shouldn't be disappointed when public companies shift their marketing and values with cultural trends, because that's exactly how capitalism is supposed to work. He contrasts the owner-operator model (where he can afford unpopular principles) with professional managers who answer to shareholders and must follow demand. Using Nike and American Eagle's dramatic pivot from early-2020s progressive messaging to 2025's traditional imagery, he illustrates how corporations are weathervanes, not moral actors. He concludes that the 'woke theocracy' temporarily captured culture and thus capitalism, but couldn't hold it, and commerce has now reverted to giving people what they actually want.
posts.key_insight: Corporate principle-lessness is actually capitalism working as intended — corporations follow demand, so changing the culture automatically changes commerce without requiring companies to have principles at all.

topQuotes.quote: Selling 2020 woke platitudes into a 2025 market is a clear way to lose your job as a professional manager.
Blond babies and boobs are back in business!
If you can capture culture, you can capture capitalism. But it's one thing to capture culture, it's quite another to hold it.

posts.title: How We Migrated the Parse API From Ruby to Golang (Resurrected)
posts.summary: Charity Majors resurrects a lost blog post from her Parse days documenting the two-year rewrite of Parse's API from Ruby on Rails to Go. The migration was driven by Rails' one-process-per-request model failing to scale during hockey-stick growth, and the team used production traffic shadowing to catch undocumented Rails behaviors. The experience of diffing individual API responses field-by-field during the migration directly inspired what would become Honeycomb's approach to observability.
posts.key_insight: The grueling process of migrating Parse's API from Ruby to Go — and the production traffic diffing tools required to do it safely — directly seeded the observability philosophy that became Honeycomb.

topQuotes.quote: // single geo query, don't do anything. stupid and does not make sense but ruby does it. Changing this will break a lot of client tests. just be nice and fix it here.
Once you've used a tool like that, you're hooked.. you can't possibly go back to metrics and aggregates. The rest, as they say, is history.

Rails HTTP processing is built on a philosophy of 'be liberal in what you accept'. So developers end up inadvertently sending API requests that are undocumented or even non-RFC compliant … but Rails middleware cleans them up and handles it fine.

posts.title: ICEBlock, an iOS Exclusive
posts.summary: Gruber highlights ICEBlock, a crowdsourced iOS app that lets users anonymously report ICE activity in their area, modeled after Waze. He notes the app is exclusively available on iOS because Apple's push notification system allows true anonymity without storing device IDs, unlike Android's requirements. Gruber draws a pointed parallel to Apple's 2019 removal of HKmap.live from the App Store under pressure from China, suggesting the Trump administration may similarly demand ICEBlock's removal. He argues that unlike HKmap.live, ICEBlock has no web fallback — it can only exist as a native iOS app — making Apple's decision about whether to defend it far more consequential.
posts.key_insight: ICEBlock's iOS exclusivity — driven by Apple's superior privacy architecture for push notifications — means that if the Trump administration pressures Apple to remove it, there is no fallback, making Apple's response a definitive test of whether it will defend privacy principles at home as readily as it capitulated abroad.

topQuotes.quote: What 'brown shirts' were to the Nazis, we should make 'red hats' to MAGA.
The app broke no Hong Kong laws, but scared the thin-skinned skittish lickspittles in the Chinese Communist Party.
ICEBlock is interesting in and of itself, as will be Apple's response if (when?) the Trump administration takes offense to the app's existence.

posts.title: Twitter, LinkedIn, and Failing in Public
posts.summary: Matt Rocklin contrasts Twitter's former culture of working in public with LinkedIn's polished, success-only content. He argues that Twitter's casual tone enabled vulnerability about work's messy nature, while LinkedIn feels emotionally hollow because it shows only final victories. Drawing on storytelling principles, he notes that we connect to people during their struggles, not just their successes. He advocates for more public sharing of failures and experiments as a way to rebuild community and transform scary work into play.
posts.key_insight: Authentic professional community depends on shared vulnerability about failures and struggles, not just polished announcements of success.

topQuotes.quote: LinkedIn is like watching only the last 20 minutes of the movie.
LinkedIn feels emotionally hollow because we share only the final act.
Fail heroically in public.

posts.title: Why AI Has Knowledge But Humans Have Creativity
posts.summary: The post argues that AI possesses accumulated knowledge rather than genuine creativity, and that the apparent scarcity of human creativity is caused by structural constraints like lack of time, autonomy, and economic security rather than innate limitations. Using examples from Adam Smith to Leonardo da Vinci, Khailo contends that AI makes everyone a 'Henry Ford' who can synthesize existing knowledge, but cannot replace the perspective-shifting creativity that comes from lived human experience.
posts.key_insight: AI democratizes knowledge synthesis the way the printing press democratized information, but genuine creativity — the ability to shift paradigms and generate new perspectives — remains uniquely human and is constrained not by talent but by socioeconomic structures that deny most people the time and autonomy to develop it.

topQuotes.quote: AI is to intelligence as airplanes are to flight. Airplanes fly faster than birds, but certainly can't land on a tree branch.
AI makes everyone Henry Ford, but we still need Leonardo da Vincis.
What most people lack isn't creative ability or intelligence. It's not even knowledge anymore. It's what they've always lacked: time, autonomy, and space to develop their unique perspective.

posts.title: It's a Beelink, baby
posts.summary: DHH enthusiastically recommends Beelink mini PCs as the ideal hardware for running Linux, particularly his Omarchy distribution. He profiles three models at $289, $499, and $929 price points, highlighting their AMD processors that rival or exceed Apple M-chip performance. He praises Beelink's build quality, silent operation, and Linux compatibility compared to competitors. The post notes that his advocacy on X has driven significant sales as programmers explore Linux on affordable hardware.
posts.key_insight: Beelink mini PCs offer Apple M-chip-rivaling performance at a fraction of the price, making them the ideal on-ramp for programmers exploring Linux.

topQuotes.quote: There are a ton of programmers who have become Linux curious, and Beelink offers no-brainer options to give that a try at a bargain.
Beelink stands out with their superior build quality, their class-leading cooling and silent operation, and their use of fully Linux-compatible components.
It uses the incredible 12-core AMD Zen5 HX370 that hits 2990/15611 in Geekbench, and runs our HEY test suite faster than any Apple M chip I've ever tested.

posts.title: Who is LLM?
posts.summary: Fowler explores different personas developers use to conceptualize LLMs, from Birgitta Böckeler's 'stubborn donkey' to Kent Beck's 'genie' and 'slot machine' metaphors. He introduces his own preferred persona: Uriah Heep from Dickens, the obsequious servant who manipulates those he claims to serve. The post argues that these metaphors help us understand LLMs' contradictory nature—helpful yet unreliable, powerful yet undermining. Fowler suggests that the slot machine comparison explains why LLMs seem easy to use (occasional jackpots) while actually requiring significant effort to wield effectively.
posts.key_insight: The personas we use to understand LLMs—donkey, genie, slot machine, Uriah Heep—reveal a technology that is simultaneously powerful and treacherous, helpful and manipulative, requiring far more effort to use well than its occasional successes suggest.

topQuotes.quote: The persona perfectly captures the mix of great power, eagerness to help, and an almost pathological ability to undermine whatever help it's supposedly trying to give you.
The genie seems to assume that its planetary-sized brain is capable of handling any amount of complexity, so it needn't ever reduce complexity. It's right until it isn't.
Very well-read, but inexperienced (for Dungeons and Dragons fans: high intelligence, low wisdom)

posts.title: Welcoming The Next Generation of Programmers
posts.summary: Ronacher reflects on his roots in the Python community and how AI-driven "vibe coding" is bringing a new wave of people into programming. He argues that if you build software—whether by hand or with an agent—you are a programmer, and the community must embrace that reality. While he has seen dismissive attitudes toward AI, he observes a shift toward acceptance and expects AI to increase, not reduce, the number of programmers. The real risk is not code quality but the loss of human mentorship and community connection when AI is the entry point. He concludes that Python should actively welcome, mentor, and integrate these newcomers so they grow beyond tool dependence.
posts.key_insight: AI-driven coding will grow the programmer population, so communities must actively welcome and mentor these newcomers to replace missing human connections and prevent tool lock-in.

topQuotes.quote: AI will bring more people into programming than anything else we have done in the last decade.
It would be a mistake to treat them as outcasts or “not real” programmers.
We need to turn a solitary interaction with an AI into a shared journey with a community.

posts.title: The World After Wireheading
posts.summary: Hotz argues that humans are fundamentally predictable systems, just with trillions of parameters instead of simple equations, and that this realization from studying machine learning reveals how vulnerable we are to manipulation. He warns that AI and targeted advertising are forms of 'wireheading' that threaten human free will and autonomy. The post criticizes Silicon Valley's lack of humanities education while wielding unprecedented power over human behavior. He concludes that current AI won't end humanity directly, but could trigger a collapse of machine civilization, leaving only the 'street people' and ushering in a new dark age.
posts.key_insight: Understanding machine learning reveals that human behavior is just as mechanistic and predictable as any algorithm, making us vulnerable to manipulation at scale - and the tech industry is exploiting this without understanding the civilizational consequences.

topQuotes.quote: The amount of power over nature that the Silicon Valley death cult is stumbling into is horrifying, and these high priests don't have a basic grasp of people.
Nukes don't end humanity. Current path AI doesn't end humanity. It just ends all the machines and hands the world over to the street people.
This will not stop until they take our free will from us.

posts.title: A Cold Play
posts.summary: Stancil argues that the dominant advantage in consumer AI isn’t marginally better models but the product wrapper and habits around them, with chatgpt.com as the default destination. He uses Google’s partial Windsurf acquisition to question whether Google bought the wrong half by prioritizing model talent over the product and UX expertise that actually drive usage.
posts.key_insight: In consumer AI, the UX wrapper and default habits matter more than small model gains, so buying talent without the product is likely the wrong bet.

topQuotes.quote: Who is actually more valuable to Google: Some AI engineers that can make Gemini a little bit better, or the people who can make a thin, addictive product that pushes massive amounts of traffic to Gemini?
OpenAI’s biggest edge today might be that when people think to use a chatbot, they think to go to chatgpt.com.
OpenAI is winning because they built the first good chatbot product and because it became the default.

posts.title: Curse Not the King
posts.summary: Gruber argues that CBS's cancellation of The Late Show with Stephen Colbert is not the financial decision the network claims, but rather a political gift to Trump designed to smooth the FCC approval of Skydance's acquisition of Paramount. He traces a pattern from CBS's settlement of Trump's frivolous 60 Minutes lawsuit to Colbert's cancellation, connecting it to the broader phenomenon of media oligarchs — Bezos at the Washington Post, Soon-Shiong at the LA Times, and now the Ellisons at Paramount — prioritizing their business interests with Trump over journalistic and creative independence. Gruber frames late-night political comedy as a vital cultural check on authoritarianism, likening its suppression to the censorship seen in Russia and China. He notes the cancellation defies TV logic since Colbert's show is the top-rated in its timeslot, and dismisses CBS's claim of $40 million in losses as cover. The piece warns that this represents a distinctly American form of media acquiescence — not state censorship, but oligarchic self-censorship driven by corruption and cronyism.
posts.key_insight: The cancellation of America's top-rated late-night show isn't a business decision but a symptom of oligarchic media capture, where billionaire owners sacrifice editorial independence to curry favor with an authoritarian-minded president.

topQuotes.quote: While our Constitution and cultural fabric protect our media from government interference, there's no such protection from ownership interference.
Cancelling this particular hit show isn't poison to the deal for Skydance to buy Paramount — it's a sweetener.

CBS declaring in its announcement that 'This is purely a financial decision against a challenging backdrop in late night' is obvious bullshit — and, in light of its timing, extra stinky bullshit at that.

posts.title: American hype
posts.summary: DHH argues that America's unique culture of hype is a genuine competitive advantage, contrasting it with Europe's instinctive skepticism toward commercial optimism. He acknowledges his complicated relationship with venture capital, having built his career proving bootstrapping works, but concedes that VC excess is integral to America's hype engine. Using the dot-com boom as precedent, he suggests that AI's current hype cycle, despite its absurdities, will likely produce transformative outcomes. The core argument is that collective optimism, even when irrational, creates the conditions for real progress.
posts.key_insight: America's culture of irrational optimism and venture capital excess, while producing spectacular failures, is the engine that turns hype into real transformation — and skeptical Europe pays the price for its prudence.

topQuotes.quote: Most Europeans are allergic to anything that even smells like a commercial promise of a better tomorrow.
There's no country on earth that does hype better than America.

Europeans are just as smart, just as inventive as their American brethren, but they don't do hype, so they're rarely the ones able to sell the sizzle that public opinion requires to shift its vision for tomorrow.

posts.title: Linux crosses magic market share threshold in US
posts.summary: DHH celebrates Linux reaching 5% desktop market share in the US, double what it was three years ago. He notes Windows still dominates at 63% with Apple at 26%, down from a 33% peak in 2023. He contrasts the US trend with Denmark, where Linux sits under 2% and Windows commands nearly 70%. DHH connects Denmark's slow adoption to the same cultural conservatism that drove him to move to the US in 2005 when Rails was taking off.
posts.key_insight: Linux's doubling to 5% US desktop market share signals meaningful momentum for open-source adoption, while culturally conservative markets like Denmark continue to lag behind.

topQuotes.quote: Danish companies were too conservative, too complacent, too married to Microsoft to really pay attention.
So while the Danes continue to fiddle, the Americans continue to push forward. Linux is on the up and up!
Linux has claimed 5% market share of desktop computing in the US. That's double of where it was just three years ago! Really impressive.

posts.title: What’s the right way to talk about AI?
posts.summary: O'Neil responds to a Matteo Wong article in The Atlantic arguing that both AI hype men and critics are missing the point. She acknowledges that dismissive critiques like calling AI 'a racist pile of linear algebra' may understate its real-world impact. The core argument is that debates about whether AI 'thinks' are beside the point when it's actually replacing workers. O'Neil advocates for taking AI seriously as a transformative technology while remaining clear-eyed that it primarily benefits those making billions from it.
posts.key_insight: The AI debate should move past abstract arguments about machine cognition and focus on the concrete ways the technology is reshaping work and society—primarily to the benefit of those profiting from it.

topQuotes.quote: Let's take it seriously, but not pretend it's the answer to anyone's dreams, except the people for whom it's making billions of dollars.
Who cares if AI 'thinks' like a person if it's better than you at your job?
AI not going away, it's real, it's replacing people at their job, and we have to grapple with it seriously.

posts.title: Building pandas, Arrow, and a Speedrunning Legacy
posts.summary: Wes McKinney traces his origin story in data tooling to early community leadership in the GoldenEye 007 speedrunning scene, where he learned governance and process-building. He describes creating pandas at AQR to solve personal productivity pain points in Python, inspired by the R ecosystem’s strengths and gaps at the time. As pandas grew, he shifted to open source full-time, wrote “Python for Data Analysis,” and eventually handed leadership to new core maintainers, emphasizing community scale and inclusivity as the driver of its success. He connects his speedrunning mindset to an obsession with efficiency, highlighting how performance improvements like faster CSV reads materially change workflows. He then situates Apache Arrow as a long-term foundational effort for interoperable data frames and file formats, and Ibis as a bridge from pandas-style expressions to SQL backends. The conclusion is that durable, composable data infrastructure emerges from sustained community building, performance focus, and long-term collaboration with trusted contributors.
posts.key_insight: Lasting data tools come from obsessing over workflow efficiency while building inclusive, collaborative communities that scale the work beyond any single creator.

topQuotes.quote: It frustrates me that doing this task related to data processing or related to data science is tedious or inefficient.
If you can take something that takes three seconds and take that down to 300 milliseconds, that's a big difference.

By really casting a wide net and making the tent bigger to invite as many people from all around the world to make contributions to the project, both big and small—because you do see some open source projects where the contributors maybe don't communicate so openly, or there's a small group of developers that are insular and they don't try to recruit and bring people into the project and be inclusive.

posts.title: I Walked Across Spain
posts.summary: Matt Rocklin reflects on his 500-mile walk across Spain along the Camino de Santiago, describing it as an overlapping physical, social, cultural, religious, humanist, and cloistered experience. The physical challenge involved accumulated stress and injury management over 27 days of continuous walking. Socially, he bonded quickly with diverse fellow pilgrims while ultimately recognizing that everyone walks their own path. The experience naturally detached him from digital compulsions and news cycles, leading to lasting changes in introspection, calm, and life satisfaction. He encourages others in their 30s-50s to consider taking this step outside their productive lives.
posts.key_insight: Stepping completely outside your life—not vacation, but genuine detachment—can quiet the internal voice of dissatisfaction and create lasting shifts toward introspection and calm.

topQuotes.quote: The novelty and buzz of the world felt boring and unwelcome. I didn't check my e-mail or the news for days at a time, not through intention and discipline, but through lack of interest.
There's a frequency and mania of staying on top of things that I don't think meaningfully helps; and does considerable harm.
Despite most pilgrims not being religious, the Camino's culture was one of the better expressions of some Christian values I've experienced.

posts.title: The 6 Hours of Lex
posts.summary: DHH recounts his six-hour marathon conversation on the Lex Fridman podcast, comparing the endurance required to his stints driving at the 24 Hours of Le Mans. He describes how the conversation flew by thanks to a flow state, covering topics from Ruby's beauty to AI, small teams, leaving the cloud, Elon Musk, fatherhood, and money. He praises Fridman's exceptional preparation and deep questioning style. The post serves as both a personal reflection on great conversation and a promotion of the episode.
posts.key_insight: A great conversation, like peak performance in any domain, induces a flow state that compresses hours into what feels like minutes.

topQuotes.quote: The magic of a good conversation, like the magic of driving at Le Mans, is that time flies by.
Those six hours felt more like sixty minutes. This is what flow does: it compresses the moment.
Talking with Lex Fridman in Austin on his podcast? Over six hours straight!

posts.title: Saas 20
posts.summary: Stancil argues that SaaS products like Salesforce are really “opinionated lists” that encode expert workflows, but those rules are blunt and generalized. He suggests a new model where LLM-powered specialists manage the lists directly—software as a service-as-specialist—making expertise explicit, flexible, and tailored. The ideal app becomes a trusted expert with a spreadsheet, not a UI full of nudges.
posts.key_insight: The next evolution of SaaS is not more software interfaces, but LLM-driven specialists who manage the underlying lists directly and embody the workflow expertise.

topQuotes.quote: The product is the prompt, and the prompt is the last sales guide you’ll ever need.
Salesforce helps people sell stuff by keeping lists, but it also helps people sell stuff by keeping opinionated lists.

It’s an inverted version of SaaS: Not software as a hosted service, nor a custom piece of software that I have to build, but a service—a matchmaker, an EA, a sales operations team—that is replicated by software, and software that acts like a specialist and a spreadsheet.

posts.title: The Data Engineering Toolkit: Infrastructure, DevOps, and Beyond
posts.summary: This second part of Simon Späti's data engineering toolkit series covers advanced infrastructure concerns: data processing tools, DevOps practices, and the expanding role of infrastructure as code. The post argues that modern data engineers must master not just SQL and Python, but also Kubernetes, Terraform, and GitOps practices to build production-grade platforms. Späti presents a comprehensive catalog of tools across databases, orchestration, BI, containerization, and observability, while noting that AI-enhanced workflows are reshaping pipeline development. The piece concludes that soft skills and business understanding are increasingly crucial as AI reduces pure coding demands.
posts.key_insight: Modern data engineering requires mastering a spectrum of DevOps abstraction levels and infrastructure tooling, but as AI handles more coding tasks, soft skills and business understanding become the true differentiators.

topQuotes.quote: One day, you're optimizing SQL queries, the next, you're debugging Kubernetes deployments, and by afternoon, you'll be explaining data quality metrics to stakeholders who just want to know why their dashboard is empty.
DevOps isn't binary; it's about selecting the appropriate level of control and abstraction for your specific needs.
Start with higher abstraction levels for faster delivery, then move toward more control only when specific customizations become necessary.

posts.title: Has Self-Serve BI Finally Arrived Thanks to AI?
posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that conversational BI powered by Model Context Protocol (MCP) finally delivers on the two-decade-old promise of self-serve business intelligence. Unlike traditional BI where self-serve meant filtering dashboards, MCP enables LLMs to autonomously query databases, verify facts, and generate BI artifacts through natural language prompts. The post identifies three distinct output types—numerical data retrieval, AI-generated text interpretations, and visual charts—that combine to create a more powerful workflow than either humans or AI alone. Späti emphasizes that narrowing the analytical context makes AI more deterministic and less prone to hallucination, while semantic layers provide the crucial business context LLMs need to understand domain-specific metrics.
posts.key_insight: True self-serve BI becomes possible when LLMs can autonomously query semantic layers through MCP, but effectiveness depends on narrowing analytical context and maintaining human oversight from domain experts.

topQuotes.quote: Conversational BI finally delivers on the promise of self-serve BI that the industry has pursued for two decades.
Many data warehouses or data lakes aren't fast enough to support conversation-speed interrogations.
By going narrower, like analyzing a spike in a dashboard, it's much less likely to hallucinate and makes its prediction more deterministic.

posts.title: Thoughts on Motivation and My 40-Year Career
posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on her unconventional path from a fundamentalist homeschooled upbringing in rural Idaho to becoming a tech company founder, arguing that work can be a source of liberation, meaning, and personal growth rather than mere exploitation. She makes the case that people with strong ethics should pursue business and institutional power rather than ceding it to those without principles, and that the second half of a career should be oriented around purpose and building things of enduring value.
posts.key_insight: People with strong ethics should actively pursue power and build institutions rather than ceding business and technology to those without principles, because creation and institutional stewardship — not critique — is what brings ideals to life.

topQuotes.quote: The most quietly radical, rebellious thing I can possibly do is to be an institutionalist, someone who builds instead of performatively tearing it all down.
If we prematurely abandon tech to its most exploitative elements, we guarantee its fate.
I worked a lot harder on the farm than I ever have in front of a keyboard, and got a hell of a lot less for my efforts.

posts.title: Save Yourself Some Dough on Apple Kit and Make Me Rich on Amazon Prime Day
posts.summary: Gruber promotes Amazon Prime Day deals through his affiliate links, highlighting significant discounts on Apple products including iPads, AirPods, and MacBooks. He frames it as a way for readers to support Daring Fireball at no extra cost to themselves. He shares personal purchase anecdotes and product recommendations, particularly endorsing AirPods 4 with ANC. The post also touches on his broader media philosophy — publishing freely without paywalls while finding alternative revenue streams like affiliate links, merch, and potentially future membership tiers.
posts.key_insight: Gruber uses Prime Day as both a genuine product recommendation post and an opportunity to articulate his publishing philosophy — free content supported by reader goodwill through affiliate purchases rather than paywalls.

topQuotes.quote: As the media industry moves more and more toward subscriptions and memberships — and, alas, paywalled content — I'm still freely publishing the entirety of my work other than Dithering.
Sometimes my procrastination pays off.
You should definitely get the ones with ANC, though — I never did write a proper review, but I tested a pair with ANC for months and they're quite amazing given their price and non-sealed ear fit.

posts.title: Jeff Williams, 62, Is Retiring as Apple’s COO
posts.summary: Jeff Williams, Apple's 62-year-old COO, is retiring, with longtime operations lieutenant Sabih Khan taking over the COO role. While the operations transition is characteristically smooth, the more intriguing story is what happens to Apple's design teams, which have reported to Williams since Jony Ive's 2019 departure. Gruber argues Williams oversaw but never truly led design, leaving no stylistic fingerprint on Apple's products over six years. With design temporarily reporting to Tim Cook (who is 64), Apple still has no clear answer for who will provide the taste and design vision the company needs going forward.
posts.key_insight: Apple's smooth COO succession masks a deeper unresolved problem: six years after Jony Ive's departure, the company still has no identifiable design leader whose taste shapes its products.

topQuotes.quote: Looking back at the last six years of Apple design — hardware, software, packaging, architecture — I detect not one fingerprint of style or taste that belongs to Jeff Williams.
It might have ultimately made Ive more difficult to replace than Steve Jobs.
Apple's design teams reporting to an operations executive makes no more sense than it would for Apple's operations teams to report to, say, Alan Dye.

posts.title: Full-Screen Ad for ‘F1 The Movie’ in Apple’s TV App Linked Directly to the Web, and Nothing Bad Seemed to Happen
posts.summary: Gruber examines Apple's full-screen ad for the F1 movie in the TV app, which linked users directly to the web to buy tickets without any warning interstitial or scare sheet. He highlights the hypocrisy: Apple requires third-party developers selling digital content to show scary warnings before linking out to the web, but exempts itself when promoting movie tickets. He explains the technical distinction between digital content and real-world purchases, but argues that this distinction is meaningless to actual users. The core argument is that Apple's position — that linking to the web is confusing for users buying subscriptions but not confusing for users buying movie tickets — is incoherent.
posts.key_insight: Apple's insistence that linking users to the web for purchases is dangerously confusing falls apart when Apple itself does exactly that — without warnings — to sell movie tickets.

topQuotes.quote: That's weird, Apple's new full screen F1 ad in the TV app links out to the browser to compete the transaction, but for some reason doesn't include any full screen interstitials warning of the big scary web, nor a confirmation dialog that it would open in the browser? Must be an oversight.

The hypocrisy isn't that Apple didn't show a full-screen scare sheet for this link-out to the web. It's that they require other developers, who are doing it to sell digital content, to show a scare sheet/confirmation.
Pay for a movie to watch on your TV at home? That's a digital purchase. Pay for a movie to watch in a theater? Not a digital purchase.

posts.title: Can tinygrad win?
posts.summary: George Hotz argues that tinygrad can win the ML framework competition, but acknowledges it's a much harder battle than comma.ai faced with self-driving cars. Unlike comma's competitors who were 'playing the wrong game,' tinygrad's rivals (PyTorch, JAX, MLIR) are competent and open source. His thesis is that the entire ML software stack is solving the same scheduling problem at different scales, but current frameworks use completely different codebases for each scale. If tinygrad can expose this underlying search problem in a simple, complete formulation and apply massive compute to optimize it, they can win with under 25k lines of code versus millions in competing frameworks.
posts.key_insight: The entire ML software stack is solving the same scheduling problem at different scales, and by exposing this unified abstraction in minimal code and applying massive compute to search, tinygrad can beat frameworks with 100x more lines.

topQuotes.quote: This is not going to be a cakewalk like self driving cars.
For tinygrad to win, it requires something much deeper to be wrong about software development in general.
tinygrad includes every single piece of code needed to drive an AMD RDNA3 GPU except for LLVM, and we plan to remove LLVM in a year or two as well.

posts.title: Are we the baddies?
posts.summary: Hotz rails against the pervasive use of AI and psychological manipulation by tech platforms to extract maximum value from users. He argues that apps like Hinge, Uber, and payment systems have become exploitative middlemen that use sophisticated algorithms to keep people 'perpetually on the edge.' The post contends that market forces won't fix this because companies preemptively optimize to prevent user exodus, and democracy can't help because the algorithms control too much. He sees no path to reform, only revolution—possibly requiring financial collapse or war to break the cycle of manipulation that he calls 'wireheading' and feeding customers to Moloch.
posts.key_insight: Modern tech capitalism has created an inescapable system of algorithmic manipulation that can only end through catastrophic collapse, not reform.

topQuotes.quote: Burn all the social capital, burn all your values, FEED IT ALL TO MOLOCH!
Quick Jeeves, tune the AI to make people feel less manipulated.
Your vote is as decoupled from action as the amount Uber pays the driver is decoupled from the fare that you pay.

posts.title: Ambition Fun And Not
posts.summary: Stancil argues that startups feel miserable not because the early freedom is fake, but because the moment they become real businesses, the operational grind and customer capture harden their ambitions. The Cluely story shows how bold visions get funneled into enterprise-friendly products, turning world-changing dreams into meeting productivity software.
posts.key_insight: Startups often trade their most ambitious visions for the practical demands of building a business, and the market ultimately corrals their bold ideas into familiar enterprise categories.

topQuotes.quote: The potential to end human thought, now trapped in a company that sells sales software.

Because the stylized definition of modern startup—a job to do whatever you want, with whoever you want, paid for by someone else, with $140 billion of upside and effectively no downside—sounds impossibly perfect.
Building websites with friends is a fairly common hobby; arbitrating disagreements about sales territories is not.

posts.title: Tools: Code Is All You Need
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that MCP’s current design is less effective than code generation because it relies too heavily on inference and consumes too much context. He says even domain-specific automation works better when LLMs generate scripts that can be reviewed, validated, and run repeatedly. His blog migration example shows an LLM-to-code-to-LLM pipeline that produced trustworthy results by focusing on verifiable transformations rather than direct inference. He concedes MCP can be useful in some UI-driving scenarios, but notes that scripts outperform tool calls when the domain is known. The conclusion is to favor code-first agentic workflows and only use inference as a judging layer after bulk execution.
posts.key_insight: LLMs are most powerful when they generate verifiable code and reserve inference for judging results, not driving every step.

topQuotes.quote: MCP suffers from two major flaws: it isn’t truly composable and it demands too much context.
In any case I can only encourage people to bypass MCP and to explore what else is possible.
Current MCP will always be harder to use than writing code, primarily due to the reliance on inference.

posts.title: Get in losers, we're moving to Linux!
posts.summary: DHH observes a notable vibe shift among developers toward Linux desktop adoption, driven by better hardware options like mini PCs, Apple's deteriorating developer relations, and cultural influencers like PewDiePie and ThePrimeagen embracing Arch Linux. He highlights Arch Linux and Hyprland as the surprising center of this movement, despite Arch being around since 2003. While acknowledging Linux remains a niche desktop OS by market share, he argues the mind share is shifting rapidly away from Apple and Microsoft. He positions his own Omarchy project as part of this wave and sees conditions ripe for a 'gradually, then suddenly' adoption shift.
posts.key_insight: A convergence of better hardware, Apple missteps, and cultural influencers has created the conditions for Linux desktop adoption to finally shift from perennial joke to genuine developer movement.

topQuotes.quote: Apple and Microsoft sit on the lion's share of the market share. But the mind share? They've been losing that fast.

People have been joking about 'The Year of Linux on the Desktop' since the late 90s. Just like self-driving cars were supposed to be a thing back in 2017. And now, in the year of our Lord 2025, it seems like we're getting both!
The perfect, free, and open alternative that was just sitting around waiting for developers to finally have had enough of the commercial offerings from Apple and Microsoft.

posts.title: Expert Generalists
posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that the software industry's obsession with narrow specialization is misguided. The most effective technologists are 'Expert Generalists'—people who combine deep fundamentals knowledge with curiosity, collaboration skills, and customer focus. These individuals can rapidly adapt to new technologies because they understand underlying patterns rather than just tools. Organizations benefit enormously from Expert Generalists who can work across specialty boundaries, unblock teams, and get things done, though teams still need some specialists for deep domain knowledge.
posts.key_insight: Expert Generalist should be recognized as a first-class skill that organizations deliberately identify, assess, and develop—because people who master fundamentals over tools and collaborate across boundaries are what make complex software actually ship.

topQuotes.quote: Once these words harden into career lanes, they solidify the very silos that the Agile and DevOps culture was meant to dismantle.
The characteristics that we've observed separating effective software developers from the chaff aren't things that depend on the specifics of tooling.
Being such a generalist is itself a sophisticated expertise.

posts.title: Just speak the truth
posts.summary: DeVault contrasts two approaches to dealing with reactionary bad-faith actors in FOSS communities. He critiques a detailed, measured technical response (Exhibit A) that inadvertently gives trolls ammunition for counter-arguments and concern trolling. He praises a concise, truth-speaking dismissal (Exhibit B) that denies reactionaries the engagement they seek. The post argues that FOSS community leaders should not waste time crafting long-winded technical justifications to exclude bad actors, but should instead speak plainly and ban them without ceremony. The underlying incident involves a reactionary fork of Xorg used as a vehicle for alt-right culture war politics against Linux distributions.
posts.key_insight: FOSS leaders should respond to bad-faith reactionary actors with blunt truth and swift bans, not detailed technical arguments that feed the trolls' attention-seeking behavior.

topQuotes.quote: If you are a community leader in FOSS, you are not obligated to waste your time coming up with a long-winded technical answer to keep nazis out of your community.
She probably understood at a glance that the community behind this "project" literally wants to lynch her.
Do not answer bad faith with good faith. This is a skill you need to learn in order to be an effective community leader.

posts.title: The end game for John Fisher
posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill analyzes the increasingly dire financial and operational situation facing John Fisher's Athletics franchise as it stumbles through a disastrous Sacramento residency while failing to secure funding for a Las Vegas stadium. The 'groundbreaking' ceremony revealed itself as a sham with rented construction equipment, exposing that Fisher lacks the approximately $1 billion needed to complete the project. Cantrill systematically dismantles Fisher's three potential funding options—debt, equity, and liquidating personal assets—concluding that none are viable enough to save the Vegas stadium plan. He predicts this will culminate in MLB finally forcing Fisher to sell the team after the 2028 opening day deadline becomes impossible to meet.
posts.key_insight: John Fisher's Athletics franchise is trapped in a financial death spiral where his refusal to sell control, combined with an alienated fanbase and ballooning stadium costs, makes all three funding paths—debt, equity, or personal liquidation—effectively impossible, inevitably leading to a forced sale.

topQuotes.quote: It would be gloriously fitting for John Fisher, whose two existential fears are clearly that he is an incapable dunce and that he will fumble his birthright, to manage to confirm both in a single, spectacular disaster.
It's hard to find the dumb money when you are, in fact, the dumb money.
In just a few months John Fisher has achieved in Sacramento what took him years in Oakland: fans that hate his guts.

posts.title: The parental dead end of consent morality
posts.summary: DHH argues that 'consent morality' — the idea that anything consenting adults choose is beyond criticism — has become a cultural dead end, particularly regarding parenthood. He contends that declining birth rates are partly caused by a culture that refuses to advocate for parenthood as an objective good, instead treating any lifestyle choice as equally valid. Drawing on Louise Perry's critique of the sexual revolution and Jordan Peterson's advocacy for embracing responsibility, he argues that modern malaise comes from carrying too little burden, not too much. He calls for culture to actively celebrate parenthood and family life rather than centering edge cases and pathologies, arguing this requires moving beyond consent morality to assert that some ways of living are genuinely better than others.
posts.key_insight: A culture that treats all lifestyle choices as equally valid cannot guide people toward the deeper meaning found in parenthood and responsibility, contributing to both demographic decline and widespread spiritual emptiness.

topQuotes.quote: You think you want the paradise of nothing ever being asked of you, but it turns out to be the hell of nobody ever needing you.
Giving up on the whole idea of objective higher values and virtues is a nihilistic and cowardly alternative.
Much of our modern malaise does not come from carrying too much, but from carrying too little.

posts.title: The End is Nigh (Again)
posts.summary: Joe Reis pushes back against the recurring narrative that SQL, data engineering, and other established technologies are dead in the AI era. He argues that while tools and job titles evolve, the fundamentals of data work persist because they solve real problems, and confusing 'what's hot' with 'what matters' is a classic mistake driven by hype cycles.
posts.key_insight: Technologies and roles that solve fundamental human problems persist through hype cycles because the underlying work doesn't disappear when job titles change or new tools emerge.

topQuotes.quote: Legacy is a condescending term for things that make money.
The end has been nigh for many decades. Yet somehow, we're still here.
SQL is Lindy. Data modeling is Lindy. Data engineering is Lindy. AI might reshape how we do those things, but it won't erase the need for doing them in the first place.

posts.title: Apple Announces Sweeping but Complicated Policy Changes for Apps in the EU, Attempting to Comply With the Latest Dictums Regarding the DMA
posts.summary: Gruber breaks down Apple's latest sweeping but convoluted policy changes for EU App Store compliance with the Digital Markets Act. The changes include a new tiered fee structure, replacement of the per-download Core Technology Fee with a percentage-based Core Technology Commission, and new freedoms for developers to link to external payment options. Gruber argues the complexity is a natural consequence of a byzantine law producing byzantine compliance. He notes the changes create winners and losers: small developers with free apps benefit from dropping the CTF, but mega-apps like Instagram that sell no digital goods will again pay nothing. He speculates that the new fee math might imply EU developers get a better 80/20 split, which would only make sense if Apple plans to reduce commissions worldwide.
posts.key_insight: The DMA's regulatory complexity has produced compliance rules so convoluted that neither developers nor commentators can clearly determine what anyone actually owes, which serves neither Apple's nor developers' interests.

topQuotes.quote: It's a natural consequence that an overly complicated law (the DMA) has resulted in an ever-more-complicated set of guidelines and policies (from Apple): byzantine compliance with a byzantine law.
Even if you understand it enough to just want to express anger at Apple for spiteful compliance and greed, it's hard to sum up why you're angry in a succinct headline or tweet.
Feel free to blame Apple as much as you want for spiteful compliance (especially when it comes to payments made on the web, from links in apps), but part of this is on the European Commission.

posts.title: Meme Company
posts.summary: Stancil argues that in the AI era, software is drifting from craft toward culture: marketing, mindshare, and founder “vibes” can outweigh product quality when underlying tech is commoditized. Using Cluely as a case study, he suggests startups are increasingly like memecoins—vehicles to monetize viral fame—because software creation costs are collapsing and attention is scarce.
posts.key_insight: As AI commoditizes software creation, startups can function more like meme vehicles where attention and brand eclipse product craft.

topQuotes.quote: Cluely isn’t a technology company; it’s a shock jock podcast that manufactures outrage.
Launching a new SaaS product isn’t that much harder than launching a memecoin.
In a world where foundation models improve 10x every 18 months, building infrastructure is like decorating a house you’re about to demolish.

posts.title: Why Databases are Worthy of Your Affection
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen argues that database abstractions—tables, SQL, and ACID—are fundamental civilizational technologies deserving of enthusiasm rather than dismissal. He traces tables back 5,000 years to Mesopotamian clay tablets, predating written text by a millennium. He contends that criticisms of databases conflate old implementations (like Oracle) with the underlying abstractions, which remain elegant and powerful. He uses MongoDB as a cautionary tale: by initially rejecting tables, SQL, and ACID, it forced developers to reimplement those concepts poorly in application code. He concludes with a technical demonstration of DuckDB's vectorized execution achieving near-theoretical hardware limits.
posts.key_insight: The enduring abstractions of tables, SQL, and ACID are fundamental civilizational technologies whose elegance should not be confused with the limitations of their oldest implementations.

topQuotes.quote: Tables, SQL and ACID do spark joy, and MongoDB does not.
There is a schema, it's just you don't know what it is.
The elegance of the abstractions isn't affected by the shittiness of the implementations.

posts.title: Self-driving is finally happening
posts.summary: DHH recounts his experience riding in a Tesla with Full Self-Driving (FSD) and declares that self-driving technology has finally arrived after years of broken promises. He recalls the hype of 2017 when the auto industry predicted imminent autonomy, followed by years of disappointment. After being skeptical even recently, a ride with Jason Fried convinced him that Tesla's FSD has made dramatic improvements in fluidity and reliability. He credits the achievement to AI advancement and Elon Musk's persistence, calling the transition from vaporware to credible reality remarkable.
posts.key_insight: Self-driving technology has crossed the threshold from overhyped vaporware to genuinely impressive reality, proving that delayed promises can still eventually deliver even after exhausting public trust.

topQuotes.quote: Elon, that son of a bitch, seems to have done it again! Proven everyone wrong. Proven me wrong.

If you promise them the moon in eight months, but don't end up delivering until eight years later, most will justifiably be skeptical that it's actually here — even in the face of gushing anecdotes and video evidence.
The self-driving dream has flipped from vaporware to credible near-term reality.

posts.title: More on Apple’s Trust-Eroding ‘F1 The Movie’ Wallet Ad
posts.summary: Gruber follows up on Apple's decision to send a push notification ad for F1 The Movie through the Wallet app, arguing it fundamentally undermines Apple's pitch for digital wallet trust. He uses a comedic Mastodon post as a springboard to make the serious point that Apple Wallet competes against physical wallets, which by nature cannot contain ads. Since Apple asks users to trust Wallet with finances, identity cards, and keys, any advertising in that space is uniquely destructive. He argues the ad damages not just actual privacy but the perception of privacy, and calls for whoever authorized it to be fired.
posts.key_insight: By sending an ad through Wallet, Apple violated the implicit contract that its most sensitive apps would remain ad-free, undermining the trust advantage that distinguishes it from other tech companies.

topQuotes.quote: I think whoever authorized this movie ad through Wallet push notifications ought to be canned.
They're competing against the privacy and intimacy of one of the most personal things people carry with them.
Apple Wallet ought to be sacrosanct — like the Passwords and Journal apps.

posts.title: Omarchy is out
posts.summary: DHH announces Omarchy, his opinionated setup of Arch Linux with the Hyprland tiling window manager, designed to provide a fully configured developer environment out of the box. He positions it as a sister project to Omakub (his Ubuntu setup), acknowledging that Arch and Hyprland have steeper learning curves. The project aims to solve Hyprland's bare-bones default experience by providing beautiful configurations and common tooling pre-installed. DHH argues that Linux desktop adoption is closer than ever, driven by growing dissatisfaction with Apple and Microsoft, Valve's Steam Deck running Arch, and major creators switching to Linux.
posts.key_insight: Linux is finally offering something genuinely unique rather than imitating commercial operating systems, and opinionated pre-configured setups like Omarchy can bridge the gap between Linux's infinite customizability and practical usability.

topQuotes.quote: I don't know when we'll literally get "The Year of Linux on the Desktop", but I've never been as convinced that its coming as I am now.
There's enough dissent in the water. Enough dissatisfaction with both Apple and Microsoft.
I do think that Hyprland deserves its reputation of being difficult! Not because the core tiling window manager is hard, but because it comes incredibly bare-boned in the box.

posts.title: LLMs bring new nature of abstraction
posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that LLMs represent a paradigm shift in programming comparable to the transition from assembler to high-level languages. While subsequent language improvements (from Fortran to Ruby) raised abstraction levels incrementally, they maintained the same fundamental nature of deterministic communication with machines. LLMs break this pattern by introducing non-determinism—prompts stored in version control won't guarantee identical behavior across executions. Fowler sees this as not merely moving up the abstraction ladder but moving sideways into uncharted territory, requiring programmers to develop entirely new mental models for working with these tools.
posts.key_insight: LLMs don't just raise programming's abstraction level—they fundamentally alter its nature by introducing non-determinism, forcing developers to abandon the assumption that the same code produces the same results.

topQuotes.quote: Talking to the machine in prompts is as different to Ruby as Fortran to assembler.
Ruby is a far more sophisticated language than Fortran, but it has the same ambiance, in a way that Fortran and PDP-11 machine instructions do not.
Large Language Models introduce a non-deterministic abstraction, so I can't just store my prompts in git and know that I'll get the same behavior each time.

posts.title: New zine: The Secret Rules of the Terminal
posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'The Secret Rules of the Terminal,' the product of months of deep-dive research into how terminals actually work. Despite using the terminal daily for 20 years, she discovered she had many misunderstandings about its inner workings. The zine explains how the four pieces of the terminal (shell, terminal emulator, programs, and TTY driver) fit together, along with core conventions and practical tips. She argues that while terminal internals are a mess of decades-old decisions, understanding select parts can meaningfully improve your daily experience. The project involved extensive collaboration with technical reviewers and was accompanied by numerous blog posts exploring specific terminal topics.
posts.key_insight: Even after 20 years of daily terminal use, the terminal's behavior remains poorly understood because it's not one system but many independent pieces of software with unwritten rules, and selectively learning its internals transforms vague intuition into genuine debugging ability.

topQuotes.quote: Terminal internals are a mess. A lot of it is just the way it is because someone made a decision in the 80s and now it's impossible to change.

The 'rules' for how the terminal works are extremely hard to fully understand, because 'the terminal' is actually made of many different pieces of software which are written by different people with different ideas about how things should work.
Even though I feel totally confident in the terminal and even though I've used it every day for 20 years, I had a lot of misunderstandings about how the terminal works.

posts.title: My First Open Source AI Generated Library
posts.summary: Ronacher recounts building a “sloppy” XML parser to evaluate LLM output quality without strict XML validity getting in the way. After failed attempts to coerce a model into fixing existing parsers, he had Claude generate a new library from scratch, then iterated with prompts and added tests, CI, packaging, and even branding. The result isn’t pristine and likely has bugs, but it was good enough to unblock his work with minimal manual effort. He stresses this is not an endorsement for serious open source use, but an experiment in what’s possible today. He closes by raising the unresolved question of licensing AI-generated code and its implications for open source.
posts.key_insight: AI can rapidly bootstrap usable tooling and infrastructure with minimal human effort, but its outputs are best treated as experimental—and they surface unresolved legal and quality questions.

topQuotes.quote: Is that even valid when there's barely a human in the loop?
Treat it as a curious side project which says more about what's possible today than what's necessarily advisable.
I asked Claude to `ultrathink` and write me a proper XML library from scratch.

posts.title: Small is The New Big
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that the era of measuring success by team size is ending, as companies aggressively cut workforces and embrace AI to do more with less. He predicts that bragging rights will shift from leading 800-person teams to leading 8-person teams that outperform their bloated counterparts, marking a fundamental inversion in how business success is measured.
posts.key_insight: Business success metrics are inverting from 'how many people do you manage' to 'how much can you accomplish with how few people,' fundamentally reshaping careers and organizational structures in the AI era.

topQuotes.quote: When teams are big, it's easy to blend into the woodwork and coast. Meanwhile, the leaders are busy doing 'leader stuff' - endless meetings, Machiavellian maneuvering, posting leadership tall tales on LinkedIn, angling for the next promotion or job to do it all over again.
The bragging rights will not be about an 800-person data team or an 8000-person data team, but about an 8-person data team that is producing more value per unit of time than an 8000-person data team.
By the time the big team comes to a consensus, the landscape has already shifted. In an age of exponential change, being slow and bulky is a death sentence.

posts.title: Fear And Self Loathing In Silicon
posts.summary: Stancil uses YC Demo Day as a lens to show a Silicon Valley that is richer, more confident, and more narrowly focused on profit than in prior eras. He argues that startups now feel unburdened by social context—hustling to build and automate everything—while the broader world is in crisis, leaving him unsure whether to feel excited or afraid about the scale and speed of what’s being built.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley’s new confidence and profit-first focus are accelerating sweeping AI-driven ambitions while leaving open—and increasingly urgent—the question of their social consequences.

topQuotes.quote: That freedom didn’t come from reform though, or as a reward for successfully “making the world a better place;” no, today’s Silicon Valley is free because it no longer cares.

The atmosphere—of Demo Day, and of San Francisco more widely—wasn’t the nerdy computer lab of the 2000s or the delinquent frat house of the early 2010s; it was the country club that the frat boys grow up into.
Startups are just businesses now. They aren’t the passion projects of technical enthusiasts who want to build something for the sake of creation and curiosity; they are an opportunistic hustle.

posts.title: DuckLake: SQL-Powered Data Catalog
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents DuckLake, a new lakehouse format that replaces Iceberg's complex file-based metadata architecture with a simple SQL database for catalog management. He traces the evolution from single-node databases through shared-nothing architectures to separated storage and compute, showing how Iceberg ironically reintroduced the database it tried to eliminate via its REST catalog. DuckLake acknowledges this reality by using a proper database for metadata alongside object storage for data files, eliminating layers of Avro and JSON metadata files. The result is dramatically simpler setup, fewer round trips, and 4x better performance than Iceberg in benchmarks.
posts.key_insight: The lakehouse world spent years trying to eliminate databases from metadata management only to add them back via catalogs, proving that a simple SQL database for metadata plus object storage for data was always the right architecture.

topQuotes.quote: Yesterday, somebody told me that databases are considered legacy. And I'm like, yeah, it's a 5,000 year cultural technology. It's not legacy.

You just told us you're doing all this stuff to not have a database. And now you're adding the database back, but you never go back to your original design decisions about not having a database and having all these files.
The biggest reaction we got was like, oh, it's so obvious. And we're like, yes, but you didn't do it.

posts.title: In Praise of “Normal” Engineers
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the tech industry's obsession with '10x engineers' is misguided because individual engineers don't own software—teams do. She makes the case that truly great engineering organizations are ones where normal, workaday engineers can consistently ship code and have business impact, rather than orgs that depend on a few brilliant individuals. The path to high performance is building better sociotechnical systems (fast deploys, observability, guard rails) that enable normal people to do great work.
posts.key_insight: The mark of a great engineering organization isn't hiring the most brilliant individuals, but building sociotechnical systems where normal engineers can consistently ship, learn, and move the business forward.

topQuotes.quote: Any asshole can build an org where the most experienced, brilliant engineers in the world can build product and make progress. That is not hard. And putting all the spotlight on individual ability has a way of letting your leaders off the hook for doing their jobs.
The smallest unit of software ownership and delivery is the engineering team.
When your systems are designed to be used by normal engineers, all that excess brilliance they have can get poured into the product itself, instead of wasting it on navigating the system itself.

posts.title: One Week Out, Some Brief Thoughts and Observations on WWDC 2025
posts.summary: Gruber reflects on WWDC 2025 one week later, noting Apple learned from its 2024 mistakes by only announcing features ready to ship this fall rather than making unfulfilled promises. He discusses the switch to year-based OS version numbers, praising the logic while noting it codifies Apple's annual release cadence as official policy. He celebrates iPadOS finally getting simple windowing after the overly complex Stage Manager detour. The bulk of the piece examines Liquid Glass, Apple's new design language, contrasting Alan Dye's 'soporific' introduction unfavorably with Steve Jobs's thrilling Aqua unveiling in 2000, while acknowledging the design itself is exciting if rough around the edges.
posts.key_insight: WWDC 2025 represents Apple returning to form — shipping what it promises, simplifying what it overcomplicated, and launching an ambitious new design language that, like Aqua before it, will need time to mature.

topQuotes.quote: Re-watching Jobs's introduction of Aqua for the umpteenth time, I still find it enthralling. I found Alan Dye's introduction of Liquid Glass to be soporific, if not downright horseshitty.
Apple, three years ago, jumped straight to a more complex, more fiddly option — Stage Manager — and only now has added the simpler, more obvious, not fiddly at all option (windowing).
It takes confidence to promise only what you know you can ship, and it takes execution to ship what you've promised.

posts.title: The DeepSeek Series: A Technical Overview
posts.summary: This technical overview synthesizes four DeepSeek papers that collectively demonstrate how architecture, algorithms, and hardware must be co-designed for efficient large-scale LLM training. The series progresses from foundational scaling law research through architectural innovations like Multi-Head Latent Attention and Mixture-of-Experts, to HPC-level optimizations enabling 671B parameter models. The culmination is DeepSeek-R1, which uses large-scale reinforcement learning to produce emergent chain-of-thought reasoning, with R1-Zero notably achieving this through pure RL without supervised fine-tuning. The key insight is that cost-effective frontier AI requires tight integration between model architecture and training infrastructure.
posts.key_insight: DeepSeek's breakthrough demonstrates that frontier AI capabilities can be achieved at dramatically lower cost through tight co-design of model architecture, training algorithms, and HPC infrastructure—not just through scaling compute.

topQuotes.quote: While 'pure RL' yields strong reasoning in verifiable tasks, the model's overall user-friendliness was lacking.
Higher-quality data can justify a bigger model for the same number of tokens.
R1-Zero learned complex chain-of-thought patterns from raw reward signals alone.

posts.title: We should not describe LRM’s as “thinking”
posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques an Apple paper that tests Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) against Large Language Models (LLMs) on problems of increasing complexity. While the paper shows both fail at complex tasks, O'Neil argues it misses a deeper point: the Tower of Hanoi puzzle isn't actually harder for more discs once you understand the recursive solution. The fact that LRMs fail at 10 discs when the solution is trivially iterative proves they never truly understood the concept. She calls for abandoning the word 'thinking' to describe these models, as they're merely predicting tokens, not achieving genuine mathematical comprehension.
posts.key_insight: LRMs' failure on Tower of Hanoi at 10 discs isn't about complexity scaling - it's proof they never understood the recursive solution at all, despite being trained on thousands of explanations of it.

topQuotes.quote: They are, as always, predicting the next word in someone's blog post who is writing about the Towers of Hanoi.

Even though it's a critique of the status quo thinking around LRMs and LLMs, still falls prey to the most embarrassing mistake, namely of assuming the pseudo-scientific marketing language of Silicon Valley, wherein the models are considered to be 'thinking'.
If AI cannot understand the simplest and most mathematical and sanitary issue such as the Tower of Hanoi for n discs, it definitely cannot help us out of an enormously messy human quagmire.

posts.title: We Can Just Measure Things
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that programming agents turn long-standing developer pain points into something measurable rather than anecdotal. He observes that teams routinely blame users for failures caused by inadequate tooling, documentation, and API design, but agents expose these weaknesses at scale. By running agents on real tasks, he finds objective differences in languages and ecosystems that go beyond training data, including tooling quality, stability, and usability. He proposes using agents as repeatable, low-emotion evaluators of developer experience, effectively proxying how humans feel when working with a codebase.
