The conclusion is that healthier codebases and tools will show up as higher agent success, and that teams should use this to guide technology choices and library design.
posts.key_insight: Agents can act as objective, repeatable proxies for developer experience, turning tooling and ecosystem quality into measurable signals that should guide engineering choices.

topQuotes.quote: Using agents to measure code quality is great because agents don't judge me, but they do judge the code they are writing.
Because so can your users.
What agents change about this is, is that I can subject them to something I wouldn't really want to subject other developers to: measuring.

posts.title: Universal Data Orchestrator in Action: Enterprise Best Practices
posts.summary: This practical guide explores enterprise-level data orchestration using Kestra, dlt, and Snowflake. The post demonstrates how to build production-ready data pipelines with proper separation of concerns, secret management, and multi-tenancy. Späti emphasizes the 'universal orchestrator' concept where visual UI interfaces generate version-controlled YAML code, making pipelines accessible to both engineers and analysts. The article also covers AI integration through MCP (Model Context Protocol), arguing that YAML-based orchestration is uniquely suited for LLM collaboration because the code artifacts serve as shared context between humans and AI models.
posts.key_insight: YAML-based orchestration creates an ideal collaboration layer between humans and AI models, serving as shared context that enables both parties to iteratively build and maintain complex data pipelines.

topQuotes.quote: Why are code artifacts such as YAML the perfect setup for AI? Because AI has a very limited context window, but if you give it an intermediate stage, think of it like in-memory RAM that both the LLM and the human can work together on.
Unlike many other orchestrators, you do not need to know how to code or have a technical background.
Moving from orchestration theory to the enterprise level is a real challenge. How do you handle secrets across environments? Where does your business logic actually live?

posts.title: DuckLake: The Future of Data Lakes
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckLake, a new open data format he and Mark Raasveldt created as an alternative to Iceberg and Delta Lake. He argues that Iceberg's fundamental problems are conceptual, not implementation-based, particularly the bolted-on catalog server that broke its original file-only design philosophy. DuckLake's key insight is using an existing database (like Postgres) to store all metadata—tables, columns, files, statistics—eliminating the need for Avro, JSON, REST APIs, and Java catalog servers. The approach enables sub-second query planning even at petabyte scale with 100 million snapshots, high-frequency transactional updates, and dramatically simpler implementation for connector authors.
posts.key_insight: If you already need a database to manage your data lake catalog, you should redesign the entire metadata layer around that database rather than bolting one onto a file-based format that was designed to avoid needing one.

topQuotes.quote: I think Iceberg's problems are conceptual, fundamental, and baked in the specification, which is an entirely different way of being wrong.

You basically say, here's all this complicated interconnected metadata of which we have to change small bits all the time and then say, yeah, let's write a bunch of Avro files. Or you can say, well, that sounds like a job for a database.
I have heard that Iceberg is struggling with like one transaction per second even with a catalog server. That's a bit problematic.

posts.title: The Open Lakehouse Stack: DuckDB and the Rise of Table Formats
posts.summary: Simon Späti explores the open lakehouse architecture built on affordable object storage combined with open table formats like Iceberg, Delta Lake, and Hudi. He argues that these formats bring database-like features (ACID transactions, versioning, SQL interfaces) to distributed files without expensive SSD storage. The article demonstrates practical examples using DuckDB and MotherDuck to query Iceberg tables directly from S3, and introduces DuckDB as a lightweight catalog layer using views. Späti positions this as the next evolution of the Databricks Lakehouse concept, emphasizing modularity and vendor independence through interchangeable compute engines and open catalogs like Polaris and Unity Catalog. He concludes that while open data platforms trade some performance for flexibility, they enable truly composable data ecosystems without lock-in.
posts.key_insight: Open table formats and lightweight compute engines like DuckDB are democratizing the lakehouse architecture, enabling database-like capabilities on cheap storage while avoiding vendor lock-in through interchangeable, standards-based components.

topQuotes.quote: It also shows that the English language is not always precise enough. That's why SQL is sometimes better to use or to explain to an LLM, so we communicate exactly what we want.
We might even see a decline in dashboard use per se, as self-service analytics is now possible for the first time via chat with the analytics backend.
Open Standards over Silos - instead of loading data into proprietary formats of cloud vendors and getting locked in, we work directly with open standards.

posts.title: College Baseball, Venture Capital, and the Long Maybe
posts.summary: Cantrill draws an extended analogy between the college baseball recruiting process and raising venture capital, arguing that both involve asymmetric bets on the future with high stakes, FOMO-driven decision-making, and similar dynamics around offers, negotiations, and optionality. Through his experience as both a startup founder and father of a college baseball player, he maps VC concepts like term sheets, preemptive offers, valuation overhangs, and down rounds onto the college athletics recruiting landscape. The piece serves as both a guide to navigating these parallel worlds and a critique of bad actors who exploit the power imbalance in both systems.
posts.key_insight: Both venture capital fundraising and college athletic recruiting are asymmetric coupling processes where institutions bet on futures they can't predict, creating similar dynamics of FOMO, non-binding commitments, and power imbalances that both entrepreneurs and athletes must learn to navigate by seeking genuine conviction rather than mere interest.

topQuotes.quote: VCs call this 'preserving optionality', but it is in fact a 'long maybe'—and it leads to long paths-to-nowhere with VCs that seemingly always want something else to prolong the conversation.
The athlete—like the entrepreneur—is trying to manage their own fate rather than a portfolio; they don't get to apply portfolio theory to the one career they get to live.
Be wary of where you are not really wanted, and note that conviction and praise are not the same thing: anyone wanting to get you over the line will tell you what you want to hear.

posts.title: The Postmodern Data Stack & Action-Oriented Architecture
posts.summary: Reis argues that data architecture is entering a 'Postmodern' era characterized by AI agents, probabilistic systems, and action-oriented workflows that move beyond traditional deterministic, dashboard-centric approaches. The shift is from analytics that tell you what happened to autonomous systems that take action, negotiate with suppliers, and optimize entire workflows without human intervention.
posts.key_insight: The next evolution in data infrastructure isn't better dashboards or faster queries—it's autonomous agents that move from insight to action without human intervention, fundamentally changing the role of data teams from interpreters to architects of automated decision systems.

topQuotes.quote: I half-joke that the apocalypse will happen because rogue AI agents perform DROP TABLE and remove all backups in every corporation and government system around the world.
We're moving from dashboards to decision engines. From analysts to autonomous agents. From 'let me know' to 'just do it or fix it.'
The old data stack was built for hindsight. This one is built for foresight, action, and even autonomy.

posts.title: The Talk Show Live From WWDC 2025
posts.summary: Gruber announces the annual Talk Show Live from WWDC 2025, recorded at The California Theatre in San Jose with guests Joanna Stern and Nilay Patel. The post highlights the show's 3D video with spatial audio version coming to Vision Pro via Sandwich Vision's Theater app, noting significant quality improvements over last year. He promotes the show's sponsors and credits Sandwich for the 4K production quality.
posts.key_insight: The Talk Show's 3D spatial video on Vision Pro is maturing as a format, with meaningful year-over-year quality improvements suggesting the medium is finding its footing.

topQuotes.quote: Better than last year's by a long shot, and also significantly better than the bandwidth-constrained livestream Tuesday night.
The livestream Tuesday night looked good; the on-demand version coming in a few days looks pretty amazing.
As ever, I implore you to watch on the biggest screen you can (real, or virtual).

posts.title: Agentic Coding Recommendations
posts.summary: Armin argues that agentic coding is most effective when you optimize for speed, simplicity, and tool reliability rather than flashy IDE integrations. He prefers Claude Code with minimal permissions friction, and stresses that stable ecosystems and explicit, low-magic code make agents far more productive. Go is his top backend choice because its simplicity, structural interfaces, and test caching align with how agents operate, while Python and complex frontend tooling often trip them up. He treats logs, scripts, and even Makefile targets as first-class tools and designs them to be fast, observable, and resilient to LLM misuse. The conclusion is that agentic coding can produce better, more maintainable code if you embrace conservative upgrades, clear observability, and timely refactors, while staying adaptable to rapid tooling change.
posts.key_insight: Agentic coding works best when you design for speed, observability, and simplicity in a stable ecosystem, rather than leaning on complex tooling or frequent upgrades.

topQuotes.quote: Go is sloppy.
I disable all permission checks.
Tools need to be protected against an LLM chaos monkey using them completely wrong.

posts.title: Apple’s Spin on the Personalized Siri Apple Intelligence Reset
posts.summary: Gruber doubles down on his characterization of Apple's personalized Siri features as vaporware, responding to Apple executives' attempts to push back on that narrative. He argues that even if working code existed internally, the 2024 WWDC keynote demo was edited in an unusually choppy way that broke Apple's own rules about demos being capturable in a single take, likely due to terrible latency. Sources within Apple's engineering group tell him most rank-and-file engineers never saw a functional build before the keynote. He's willing to stipulate working code existed but maintains it was clearly unshippable, and notes Apple's euphemistic 'in the coming year' timeline is misleadingly vague about when the feature will actually arrive.
posts.key_insight: Regardless of whether Apple had working code internally, the personalized Siri feature remains vaporware by definition — promised, advertised, used to sell iPhones, but never shipped — and Apple's spin campaign to rewrite that narrative doesn't change the facts.

topQuotes.quote: Most rank and file engineers within Apple do not believe that feature existed in an even vaguely functional state a year ago, and the first any of them ever heard of it was when they watched the keynote with the rest of us on the first day of WWDC last year.
From our perspective it doesn't really matter it was real, in any sense, a year ago. It's still vaporware at this point.
Whatever state it was in, it couldn't be shown in a single take.

posts.title: GenAI Criticism and Moral Quandaries
posts.summary: Ronacher responds to Glyph’s opt‑out stance by narrowing the debate to code and utilitarian text, where he sees AI as a pragmatic productivity tool rather than a creative substitute. He argues that differences in experience come from how people learn to use the tools, and he reports overwhelmingly positive results with agentic workflows. He disputes the idea that adoption is purely top‑down, pointing to grassroots uptake even before companies pay for it. On climate and copyright, he views increased energy demand as manageable within broader electrification trends and sees broader scraping as aligned with open sharing. He believes LLMs can improve learning and that cheating signals flaws in assessment, not learning itself. His conclusion is to accept AI’s trajectory, work alongside it, and stop feeling compelled to justify the choice.
posts.key_insight: If you treat AI as a utilitarian collaborator for code and learning, the practical gains outweigh the moral anxieties, and the healthiest response is to accept its trajectory and work with it.

topQuotes.quote: I've always felt that copyright terms are too long, scraping is beneficial, and sharing knowledge is a net positive for humanity.
So is programming still enjoyable if I don't hit the keys? For me, yes.
For me, it's not just “neutral-to-positive” — it's been astonishingly positive.

posts.title: Using `make` to compile C programs (for non-C-programmers)
posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how non-C-programmers can compile C programs from source using make, walking through the full process from installing a compiler to dealing with dependency errors. She uses real examples (paperjam, sqlite, qf) to demonstrate common problems and solutions, focusing on how environment variables like CPPFLAGS and LDLIBS pass flags to the compiler and linker. The post demystifies the configure/make workflow and offers practical tips like looking at how Linux distributions build the same package to find clues for fixing errors. She concludes that understanding C compilation basics is valuable even for people who never plan to write C.
posts.key_insight: Compiling C programs from source is demystified once you understand that most build errors are dependency problems solvable by passing the right flags to the compiler and linker via make's environment variables.

topQuotes.quote: It usually prints out a million compiler warnings when compiling the program. I always just ignore them. I didn't write the software! The compiler warnings are not my problem.
I don't want to pretend that I "magically" knew that those were the right arguments to pass, figuring them out involved a bunch of confused Googling that I skipped over in this post.
For a long time, my approach was basically "install the dependencies, run `make`, if it doesn't work, either try to find a binary someone has compiled or give up".

posts.title: Unionize or die
posts.summary: DeVault argues that tech workers must unionize immediately, framing it as both an economic necessity and a literal survival imperative. He contends that tech employers are systematically undermining worker power through mass layoffs, AI replacement, and flooding the labor market with cheaper graduates. Beyond workplace economics, he connects unionization to existential threats including climate change, healthcare failures, and rising fascism, arguing that organized labor is the only mechanism through which workers can exert meaningful power against capital. He provides a concrete step-by-step organizing guide and calls for tech workers to join the planned May 1st, 2028 general strike.
posts.key_insight: Tech workers have unique leverage over the most powerful institutions in the world, and organized labor is the only proven mechanism to wield that power against existential threats from climate change to fascism.

topQuotes.quote: Tech workers, it's time for you to unionize, and strike, or you and your loved ones are literally going to die.
The plutocracy has an answer to climate change: fascism.
Capitalism is going to murder the people you love. Capitalism is going to murder you.

posts.title: On How Long it Takes to Know if a Job is Right for You or Not
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that you typically know within the first week whether a new job is right for you, and that gut feeling rarely changes over time. She emphasizes this is especially true for managers, since alignment with the company is the core of the management job in a way it isn't for ICs. She advises trusting early impressions and learning from bad fits rather than waiting for things to improve.
posts.key_insight: Trust your early gut feeling about a new job — especially as a manager, where alignment with the company isn't optional but is the fundamental nature of the work itself.

topQuotes.quote: Zero. You should give it 0 time. You already know, and you've known for a long time; it's not gonna change.
It's not healthy to know you walk around every day fucking over one of your primary stakeholders, whether it's the company OR your teammates.
An IC can wall themselves off emotionally and still do acceptable work, but I'm not sure a manager can do the same.

posts.title: Gurman Says New UI Is Named ‘Liquid Glass’ (and Makes a Terrible Analogy Regarding Apple’s Risk With Falling Behind on AI)
posts.summary: Gruber critiques Mark Gurman's analogy comparing Apple's AI position to gas cars versus EVs, arguing it fundamentally misunderstands the relationship between AI and operating systems. He proposes a better analogy: AI is to today's OSes what web browsers were to Windows in the late 1990s — a new interactive layer atop existing platforms, not a replacement. Gruber traces Apple's history with Safari/WebKit to argue Apple needs an equivalent 'AI moment,' but worries about the timeline given how fast AI is moving. He draws a parallel to Microsoft's rapid response to Netscape, noting that speed of response determined who maintained platform dominance. The piece concludes by asking whether LLMs are more like browsers (must own) or web search (can profit from others'), suggesting the answer is somewhere in between and that Apple risks having to pay others rather than charging them.
posts.key_insight: Apple needs to achieve its 'Safari/WebKit moment' for AI far faster than the seven years it took with browsers, or it risks shifting from a platform that charges for integration to one that pays for access to others' AI.

topQuotes.quote: Gurman's analogy would only work if the way you got around in an EV was to put it in the back of a gas-powered flatbed truck.

If it takes Apple as long to have its own competitive LLMs as it did to have its own competitive web browser, I suspect they'll soon be paying to use the LLMs that are owned and controlled by others, not charging the others for the privilege of reaching Apple's platform users.
AI is doing to today's dominant OSes what web browsers did to Windows in the late 1990s. They're adding new interactive layers atop the old.

posts.title: Platform or Predator?
posts.summary: Tech platforms inevitably turn on their ecosystem partners by launching competing products that are 'good enough' and bundled into their core offering. Drawing from observations at Snowflake Summit, Reis argues that vendors must diversify across platforms, build beloved products, and own their customer relationships to survive the predictable platform predation cycle.
posts.key_insight: Platform partnerships are inherently unstable because platforms are incentivized to absorb successful partner functionality, making vendor diversification and brand differentiation essential survival strategies.

topQuotes.quote: Platforms don't just want to enable the ecosystem. They want to be the ecosystem.
One day, the platform is your partner. The next day, they're like a wild predator trying to eat you alive.
In the end, every fragmented ecosystem consolidates into a monolith. The big eat the small and slow.

posts.title: Self-Host & Tech Independence: The Joy of Building Your Own
posts.summary: Simon advocates for tech independence through self-hosting and owning your digital infrastructure, arguing that buying your own domain and hosting your own services compounds over time. He shares his personal journey from vim/Neovim to building a homelab, emphasizing that while self-hosting requires effort, the joy comes from learning, using what you build, and not depending on companies that can kill products or raise prices. The post celebrates open-source software and the community that shares code freely, highlighting tools like Listmonk, GoatCounter, and Quartz that power his online presence. His core message: start with a domain, learn Linux fundamentals, and gradually build independence from big tech platforms.
posts.key_insight: Self-hosting and tech independence isn't just about avoiding subscription costs—it's about the compounding value of ownership, the joy of building and learning, and freedom from platform dependency.

topQuotes.quote: Every time they had a new domain. To me, that's so sad.
You also don't get a heart attack if Google turns off your favorite app.
If people ask me how they should start writing or how to get a job, I always say to buy a domain first.

posts.title: Truth Social Is Just Trump’s Blog
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Truth Social isn't really a social network at all — it's effectively Trump's personal blog. Despite a 400% spike in users during the Trump-Musk feud, Truth Social has only 1% of X's active US mobile users, and virtually no one of consequence besides Trump himself posts there. Gruber notes that even Trump's own administration officials post primarily on X, not Truth Social. The fact that Trump never required his allies to migrate to Truth Social reveals it was never meant to compete with X. It functions as a one-way broadcast platform for Trump's pronouncements, which then get amplified by TV news.
posts.key_insight: Truth Social was never meant to be a competitive social network — it's a one-man broadcast platform functioning as Trump's personal blog, and its success should be measured on those terms.

topQuotes.quote: Truth Social is essentially just Trump's blog. Truth Social is exceedingly unpopular when judged as a social network; but it's exceedingly successful as a blog.
All the other people using Truth Social are effectively reading his blog and shitposting comments and memes in response to his posts, trying to get his attention.
Truth Social is functioning as intended: it's just an outlet for Trump to spew his demented mad-king musings and, most importantly, get some of his all-caps-laden bangers read aloud on the TV news.

posts.title: Dude, Where's My Strategy?
posts.summary: Camille Fournier argues that platform teams rarely drive novel innovation, instead absorbing external innovations or internal team creations. However, this doesn't diminish the need for platform strategy—rather, platform teams serve as the fulcrum for architectural evolution across companies. She contends that platform leadership strategy involves watching industry trends and making incremental improvements that enable natural evolution. The key insight is that 'boring but reliable forward progress' is itself strategic, even if it's not revolutionary innovation.
posts.key_insight: Platform leadership strategy isn't about driving revolutionary innovation—it's about steady, incremental modernization that makes it easy for others to innovate.

topQuotes.quote: 'Strategy' and 'Vision' are so often conflated in peoples' minds that this kind of boring but reliable forward progress is overlooked as strategic.
You don't have to drive the next innovation to be strategic, you just need to make it easy for others to get there.
This is the engine of baseline modernization that needs constant feeding to keep your technology stack from stagnating.

posts.title: AI Changes Everything
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that AI has shifted from a curious tool to a new substrate for innovation, and that the change is irreversible. He describes how hands-off agentic coding has already restructured his daily work, freeing time without necessarily increasing raw speed. He observes rapid, widespread adoption far beyond tech circles, and sees skeptics—especially in tech—as oddly resistant to an obvious leap. He acknowledges real societal questions, especially in education and labor, but believes skills, craftsmanship, and intentional creation become more valuable, not less. His conclusion is that optimism is the rational stance: AI can expand human agency and will be remembered as a once-in-a-century transformation.
posts.key_insight: AI is an irreversible shift that expands human agency, making skilled, intentional creation more—not less—valuable.

topQuotes.quote: This feels more like the invention of the steam engine.
AI is out of the bottle, and there's no putting it back.
That's what makes this moment feel so strange — half revolutionary, half prelude.

posts.title: DuckLake: A New Way to Manage Data
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen introduces DuckLake, an evolution of open table formats that replaces complex metadata layers (Avro files, JSON, REST APIs) with a standard SQL database for metadata management while keeping Parquet files on blob storage. He explains how examining Iceberg's specification revealed unnecessary complexity born from an emotional reaction to vendor lock-in rather than sound technical reasoning. DuckLake separates storage, compute, and metadata into three independently scalable axes, enabling features like cross-table commits, efficient small writes, per-file encryption, and time travel without the snapshot management overhead of existing formats. The system works with any SQL database supporting transactions and primary keys as the metadata store.
posts.key_insight: Open table formats unnecessarily reinvented metadata management outside databases due to vendor lock-in fears, and DuckLake corrects this by putting metadata back where it belongs—in a SQL database—while keeping the good parts of decoupled blob storage.

topQuotes.quote: The crazy thing is that these formats, they were built to work with files and blob stores, and that's great. And then they realized they couldn't do it, and they added the Postgres to it in the form of the catalog server.
Getting a single row to Iceberg means you're writing four files and you're writing a new snapshot and it's all pretty terrible.
These were all problems that were solved 20 years ago. But it is different in terms of how much data you can store without paying a shit ton of money.

posts.title: Denmark gets more serious about digital sovereignty
posts.summary: DHH argues that Denmark and Europe must urgently pursue digital sovereignty by replacing American Big Tech infrastructure, particularly Microsoft products. He points to the US administration's disconnection of the ICC's chief prosecutor as proof that American sanctioning power poses a real threat to European digital infrastructure. Copenhagen and Aarhus counties have announced plans to eliminate Microsoft products for their combined workforce, which DHH sees as a promising first step. He dismisses defeatist bureaucrats who claim replacing Microsoft is impossible, comparing digital sovereignty to other difficult but achievable goals like energy independence and military rebuilding.
posts.key_insight: The ICC sanctions proved that European dependence on American Big Tech is a genuine geopolitical vulnerability, not a theoretical one, and Denmark's moves to drop Microsoft show that digital sovereignty is achievable if bureaucratic defeatism is overcome.

topQuotes.quote: Denmark is full of unambitious, defeatist bureaucrats who can't imagine a world without Microsoft.
Microsoft's assurances met reality and reality won.
The bureaucrats who aren't interested in making it happen should find employment elsewhere.

posts.title: Gender and Sexuality Alliances in primary school at CIS?!
posts.summary: DHH criticizes Copenhagen International School for implementing gender identity and pride-related programming in its primary school curriculum. He describes activities including trans-themed drawing exercises for young children and Gender and Sexuality Alliances for students as young as eight, which he characterizes as indoctrination. After parent pushback, the school progressively scaled back the programming. DHH argues schools should remain politically neutral and focus on education rather than advancing any specific organization's agenda, drawing parallels to Copenhagen Pride losing corporate sponsors over partisan political stances.
posts.key_insight: Schools should maintain political neutrality and focus on education rather than serving as vehicles for any interest group's ideological programming, especially when targeting young children.

topQuotes.quote: We thought Copenhagen would offer a respite from the woke nonsense that's been plaguing California — where some schools in our social circle ended up with a quarter or more of the student body identifying as trans or gender nonconformative — but it seems ideological contagions travel as fast as airplanes these days.

There's simply no responsible explanation for having kids as young as eight, or even as old as 11, in lunch and recess meetings with CIS staff to discuss gender and sexuality on school campus. It's preposterous, if not outright creepy.

The commitment to an inclusive school does not offer a license to indulge in this kind of overt indoctrination or inappropriate lunch meetings where minors discuss gender and sexuality with school staff.

posts.title: Omarchy: Bottling that inspiration before it spoils
posts.summary: DHH recounts his weekend diving into Arch Linux + Hyprland, a notoriously difficult but highly customizable Linux setup. Building on his earlier Omakub project for Ubuntu, he was drawn by the hype around Hyprland's tiling window manager and its aesthetic focus. Despite the steep setup cost—requiring manual configuration of every component from login screens to notification systems—he found the experience intoxicating. He announces Omarchy, a new project to package this setup so others can enjoy Arch + Hyprland without investing 10-20 hours of configuration.
posts.key_insight: The extreme customizability of Arch + Hyprland—where every detail requires a deliberate choice—is both its greatest barrier and its greatest appeal, and packaging that experience for others is worth the effort.

topQuotes.quote: It looks like it was written by visual artists rather than neckbeards.
While Ubuntu is all about being a friendly place for newcomers to Linux, Arch + Hyprland is the exact opposite: It's Linux on hard mode!

Hyprland comes with absolutely nothing out of the box. No login screen, no menu bar, no notification system, no file manager, no visual settings application. Just a text-based configuration file, a wiki, and an outline on a map for how to design your own adventure.

posts.title: Apple Appeals EU’s March Ruling on ‘Interoperability’ Requirements Under the DMA
posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's appeal of the EU's March ruling on interoperability requirements under the Digital Markets Act, arguing that the mandate goes far beyond reasonable regulation. He distinguishes between legitimate interoperability mandates like USB-C (based on open standards) and the EU's requirement that Apple essentially open its proprietary technologies like AirDrop to third parties. Gruber contends the EU is treating Apple's intellectual property as a public utility rather than promoting actual open standards. He predicts Apple will respond not by opening everything up, but by withholding features from EU customers entirely, as already seen with iPhone Mirroring's absence in Europe.
posts.key_insight: The EU's interoperability mandate conflates forcing adoption of open standards with forcing companies to give away proprietary technology, and Apple's most likely response is to withhold features from EU customers rather than open its ecosystem.

topQuotes.quote: Mandating that the public has to be allowed to use the same doorways as a hotel's own staff doesn't mean those existing doors will be opened to everyone. It could lead to those doors being closed to everyone.
If AirDrop were brand new, users in the EU wouldn't get that either, I suspect. And if this mandate holds up, EU users might lose AirDrop.
Apple will limit its own devices in the EU and only in the EU to the same features available to third-party devices through open standards like Bluetooth.

posts.title: The Shape of the Essay Field
posts.summary: Graham argues that essays teach readers something they don’t already know, and there are three reasons for that ignorance: the topic is unimportant, the reader is obtuse, or the reader is inexperienced. For smart readers on important topics, the highest impact tends to be on younger readers because there is more room to change their thinking. He concludes that understanding this “field” shouldn’t change his approach—he writes to surprise himself by following curiosity.
posts.key_insight: Writing for smart readers on important topics naturally has the greatest effect on the young because they have the most room for surprise.

topQuotes.quote: If you're writing for smart people about important things, you're writing for the young.
I'm not trying to surprise readers of any particular age; I'm trying to surprise myself.
The impact of an essay is how much it changes readers' thinking multiplied by the importance of the topic.

posts.title: So You Want to Work in Data?
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that aspiring data professionals should focus on understanding business problems rather than chasing specific tools or technologies. Drawing from a conversation with Gordon Wong, he emphasizes that genuine curiosity about a domain and problem-solving abilities are far more valuable than mastering any particular tech stack, and that AI is simply another tool rather than a job replacement threat.
posts.key_insight: The path to a successful data career starts with genuine curiosity about a domain and its problems, not with mastering specific tools or technologies.

topQuotes.quote: If you can't find anything you're curious about, then data, which is all about answering questions and driving decisions, might not be the field for you.
If you want a career in data, especially today, stop asking about tools and start asking about problems.

We, as data professionals, aren't just tool jockeys. Cursor, Windsurf, DuckDB, Snowflake, Databricks, etc. are all cool, but they're all the same thing - tools. Our value lies in our ability to drive outcomes, to solve problems, and to create impact.

posts.title: All the things I've been doing lately
posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova provides a roundup of her recent work across three seemingly disparate areas: her book Antimemetics (which argues against both left and right narratives about internet information), her advanced meditation research with Harvard's Meditation Research Program, and an essay on Silicon Valley's political ideology for American Affairs. She frames these varied pursuits through the metaphor of a Codenames game, acknowledging the challenge of connecting them into a coherent narrative. The meditation work focuses on making contemplative practice measurable and trainable, while the political essay argues Silicon Valley defies conventional ideological labels. The post is self-aware about its scattered nature, inviting readers to engage with whichever thread interests them.
posts.key_insight: Across internet culture, meditation, and tech politics, Asparouhova consistently seeks frameworks that resist easy categorization and insists on rigorous, empirical approaches to domains typically governed by ideology or mysticism.

topQuotes.quote: As you might imagine, it's not easy to find people like this in meditation land, where many conversations quickly unravel into Buddhist poetry and start to feel more like a hazing ritual.
Silicon Valley is not libertarian. Nor was it ever classically liberal. Nor is it currently undergoing a 'rightward shift.'
Meditation experience (as defined by e.g. number of hours meditated) doesn't seem to correlate to abilities, so I think there's room for more experimentation here.

posts.title: Kafka: The End of the Beginning
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that while batch processing has seen dramatic innovation over the past decade, the streaming ecosystem around Kafka has largely stagnated. Despite Kafka and Flink being over 10 years old, they remain the dominant solutions, with most recent progress focused on adoption rather than innovation. The entrenchment of Kafka's protocol as a de facto standard has created network effects that discourage fundamental rethinking. Riccomini sees S2 as a promising challenger because it's the only modern streaming solution not constrained by Kafka's protocol, and suggests technologies like Feldera and differential dataflow libraries could spark the next wave of innovation in streaming, similar to what cloud warehouses and Spark did for batch processing.
posts.key_insight: Kafka's protocol becoming a de facto standard has created adoption success but also innovation stagnation, and the streaming ecosystem needs unconstrained newcomers willing to rethink fundamentals to spark the next wave of progress.

topQuotes.quote: One person I spoke with said that '80% of the companies in the Expo hall will be dead in two years'.
It's the same people, same companies, and same technology. There are no new ideas and no new users.
It's still very difficult to write and deploy stream processing jobs; it honestly feels like it's gotten worse, not better.

posts.title: 10 Years of Engineering Ladders
posts.summary: Camille Fournier reflects on publishing Rent the Runway's engineering career ladder 10 years ago, one of the first public ladders with clear IC/management distinctions. She shares the open-source motivation behind publishing it and notes how the practice has become widespread across the industry. The post offers practical advice for organizations writing their own ladders, emphasizing the importance of details, honesty about career paths, and avoiding pure checklist approaches. She concludes by questioning whether ladders remain relevant in an era of 'flat organization' backlash, but argues that human nature and the need for transparent, equitable structures means this work still matters.
posts.key_insight: Career ladders remain essential infrastructure for equitable organizations because human nature inevitably creates hierarchies - the choice is whether those structures are transparent and intentional or hidden and arbitrary.

topQuotes.quote: Your ladder should be more prescriptive than a horoscope.
The Tyranny of Structurelessness has yet to be overcome.
Don't promise people that they can become the VP of X just because some people do job X.

posts.title: Say Your Writing
posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares a writing tip from Bruce Eckel: read your writing out loud to catch awkward passages. The technique works because it engages the part of your brain that processes spoken language, which is more sensitive to bland or flabby prose. Fowler advocates for a conversational writing style, imagining he's explaining concepts to companions in a pub. He warns against corporate and academic prose styles, which he finds devoid of life and engagement. The goal is writing that readers can hear in the author's voice.
posts.key_insight: Reading your writing aloud activates your brain's sensitivity to bland, lifeless prose in a way that silent reading cannot.

topQuotes.quote: If they read their prose and it sounds like it could have come from Accenture, then they are doing it wrong.
Some sparkling prose by a colleague of mine was turned by editors at Microsoft into a tasteless pudding.
And, of course, the passive voice is rarely preferred.

posts.title: LLM’s in the VA: it just got dangerous
posts.summary: O'Neil argues that the deployment of LLMs in the VA represents a dangerous turning point for AI adoption. While she has been cautiously observing chatbot adoption, noting most implementations have been overhyped and error-prone, the planned 83,000 DOGE job cuts to the VA signal something more sinister. She contends this is a deliberate strategy to replace critical healthcare staff with AI, creating a captive federal customer base for Silicon Valley while simultaneously pushing for a 10-year moratorium on AI regulation that would eliminate oversight.
posts.key_insight: The VA job cuts combined with proposed AI regulation bans reveal a coordinated plan to create captive government markets for unregulated AI products at the expense of veterans' care.

topQuotes.quote: so far it's mostly been a weird and wasteful (and hugely overhyped) product from Silicon Valley that hasn't been picked up too much in reality, because it makes tons of mistakes
we now see the plan for what it is: remove critically important VA staff and replace them with VA, presumably as a way of forcing a long term federal client for Silicon Valley's latest product
And presumably with no oversight, judging from the 10-year AI regulation moratorium that these same guys are pushing

posts.title: Introducing DuckLake
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen and Mark Raasveldt introduce DuckLake, a new lakehouse format that replaces the file-based metadata layers of Iceberg and Delta with a standard database (like Postgres or DuckDB) for metadata storage while keeping Parquet files on blob storage for data. They argue that existing lakehouse formats made poor design decisions by trying to avoid databases, only to eventually require catalog servers backed by databases anyway. DuckLake simplifies the architecture to just two components—a metadata database and file storage—enabling multi-table transactions, fast small commits, data inlining, and zero-trust encryption. The format is an open standard using simple SQL, and the DuckDB extension implementation is MIT-licensed.
posts.key_insight: Since every lakehouse format already requires a database for its catalog server, the logical step is to store all metadata directly in that database, eliminating the complex file-based metadata layers and unlocking multi-table ACID transactions, fast small commits, and zero-trust encryption.

topQuotes.quote: They tried so hard not to have a database in this because databases are evil. They don't scale.

In order to solve the problem of multi-table management and atomic commits, we'll invent a new system and this is no longer going to be file-based. It's going to be a service. And how does it store that? Well, it uses a system that is specifically designed to manage data. A database management system, if you will.
Somehow blockchain has a higher transactions per second than these formats, which is kind of interesting to think about.

posts.title: AI Denialists are the Data Industry’s Flat Earthers
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that data professionals who dismiss AI are like Flat Earthers, clinging to failing approaches despite a tool that could dramatically improve the industry's dismal 20% project success rate. He contends that AI can compress weeks of work into hours for tasks like data modeling, code migrations, and stakeholder analysis, and that resistance often stems from protecting budgets and kingdoms rather than legitimate technical concerns.
posts.key_insight: The data industry's chronic underperformance makes AI adoption not just beneficial but necessary, and those resisting it are often motivated by self-interest rather than sound technical judgment.

topQuotes.quote: AI denialists are the data industry's Flat Earthers.
I read between the lines and realized this was more about keeping his job and growing his kingdom's budget. So it goes.
If your child continuously had failing grades with an average of a 20% pass rate from elementary school through high school, you'd question many things - you, your kid, the school system, etc.

posts.title: Idiocy or Jackassery, You Make the Call: Tripp Mickle on Whether Trump’s ‘Made in America’ iPhone Is a Fantasy
posts.summary: Gruber dismantles a New York Times piece by Tripp Mickle that entertains the possibility of manufacturing iPhones in America, arguing the article fails to simply state the obvious: it's pure fantasy. He methodically attacks the article's sourced claims — that US-assembled iPhones would cost $2,000, that Chinese women's small fingers are why Apple manufactures in China, and that the iPhone might not exist in 10 years — as unsupported nonsense laundered through analyst quotes. Gruber argues that even with extreme tariffs, it would always be cheaper for Apple to continue assembling overseas and pass costs to consumers. He notes the absurdity of the entire premise given that these are low-paying factory jobs Americans don't want, and that having these jobs overseas is a sign of American advancement, not decline. He closes by spitballing that Apple could cynically set up a tiny token assembly operation to flatter Trump into dropping tariffs.
posts.key_insight: The question of whether iPhones could be manufactured in the US isn't worth debating — the economics make it irrational at any tariff level, and serious journalism should say so plainly rather than laundering fantasy through analyst quotes.

topQuotes.quote: 'Young Chinese women have small fingers' is in fact the dumbest argument in this entire incredibly stupid article.

Trump is so dumb, so prone to succumbing to flattery and the mere illusion that his word is others' command, and so motivated to declare victory regardless of what's actually going on, that I bet it would work.
The answer should have simply been 'Yes, it's sheer fantasy', perhaps with explanations why.

posts.title: The Ads Are Coming
posts.summary: Stancil argues that as AI makes it easy to capture and interpret messy, offline data (like conversations), companies will rapidly expand surveillance beyond clicks and use that data to build highly persuasive ad platforms. He connects this to AI’s growing power to generate software and influence people, warning that the incentives around free/flat‑fee AI services will push providers toward advertising and manipulation.
posts.key_insight: As AI makes previously unusable human data tractable and persuasive, the ad-driven surveillance model is poised to spread from clicks to conversations.

topQuotes.quote: Chatbots could make really good advertisers.
Every uninspired data company eventually puts out some marketing material that says their product provides “analytics at the speed of thought,” and everyone rolls their eyes at it.

If an AI company gives us enough free stuff—if OpenAI makes Jony Ive’s unimaginable technology free, provided that you leave ad trackers on—plenty of us will blindly accept its cookies as the price of a new generation of technology.

posts.title: Configure, Don't Code: How Declarative Data Stacks Enable Enterprise Scale
posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that declarative data stacks represent the next evolution in enterprise data engineering, combining open-source flexibility with integrated platform capabilities. He traces the journey from monolithic vendor solutions to fragmented modern data stacks, positioning declarative approaches as the synthesis that offers both freedom and cohesion. The core thesis is that YAML-based configuration can replace most custom coding, enabling teams to deliver production-ready platforms faster while maintaining governance. Using Starlake as his primary example, he demonstrates how declarative stacks cover the entire data lifecycle—extract, load, transform, orchestrate—through configuration rather than code. The approach promises reduced technical debt, better reproducibility, and democratized data engineering accessible to non-programmers.
posts.key_insight: Declarative data stacks represent a paradigm shift where configuration replaces coding, enabling enterprises to manage growing data complexity while reducing technical debt and democratizing data engineering beyond traditional programmers.

topQuotes.quote: Unlike Airflow, where each task has no idea what is happening inside of it, it's a black box.
Imagine building enterprise data infrastructure where you write 90% less code but deliver twice the value.
A declarative data stack is a set of tools and, precisely, its configs can be thought of as a single function such as run_stack(serve(transform(ingest))) that can recreate the entire data stack.

posts.title: The Open Table Format Revolution: Why Hyperscalers Are Betting on Managed Iceberg
posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that open table formats, particularly Apache Iceberg, are revolutionizing data architecture by enabling database-like features on top of distributed files in object storage. He introduces the 'ICE Stack' (Interoperable, Composable, Efficient) as a framework for understanding managed Iceberg tables, which combine cheap storage with flexible compute options. The article examines how hyperscalers like AWS, Databricks, and Cloudflare are betting heavily on managed Iceberg solutions, evidenced by Databricks' $2B acquisition of Tabular. While celebrating the shift toward open standards and vendor lock-in elimination, Späti also critically examines concerns about proprietary APIs in implementations like AWS S3 Tables, noting the tension between managed convenience and true openness.
posts.key_insight: Open table formats like Iceberg are shifting the data industry from vendor lock-in to a composable architecture where storage becomes commoditized and compute becomes interchangeable, fundamentally changing how organizations own and access their data.

topQuotes.quote: While the managed Iceberg with all the additional services is most welcome, the proprietary API is not.
With the vendor data lock-in removed, any vendor offering data computation has the advantage that fewer data platforms get locked into being strictly a 'Snowflake, Databricks, BigQuery, or AWS shop'.
This is a significant shift from earlier eras of enterprise data infrastructure, where customers chose and were locked into major vendors like SAP, Oracle, or even IBM Mainframes.

posts.title: Threat Modeling Guide for Software Teams
posts.summary: This guide presents threat modeling as an essential security practice for software teams, advocating for a 'little and often' approach integrated into daily development rather than exhaustive upfront analysis. The core methodology involves tracing data flows through systems and systematically asking 'what can go wrong?' using the STRIDE framework (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege). The article provides two practical examples: a quick 15-30 minute whiteboard session for a single feature, and a larger cross-team workshop for platform infrastructure. The key outcome is translating identified threats into prioritized, actionable security stories in the team's backlog.
posts.key_insight: Effective threat modeling transforms abstract security concerns into concrete, actionable improvements by systematically following data flows and asking 'what can go wrong?' in regular, bite-sized sessions rather than exhaustive workshops.

topQuotes.quote: Spending fifteen minutes examining the security implications of a new feature can yield more practical value than hours analyzing hypothetical scenarios for code that isn't written yet.
It will force you to pivot from thinking 'how system works' to 'how system fails', which in essence is the crux of threat modeling.
The goal of threat modeling isn't to instill paranoia, but to drive improvement.

posts.title: HEY is finally for sale on the iPhone!
posts.summary: DHH celebrates that HEY email can finally be purchased directly on the iPhone in the US, following a years-long battle with Apple over their 30% App Store commission and control of customer relationships. He credits Epic Games' $100M+ lawsuit and Judge Gonzalez Rogers' ruling for forcing Apple to allow developers to link to external payment systems. The post recounts the awkward workarounds HEY was forced to use, including bizarre burner accounts, after refusing Apple's in-app purchase demands. DHH frames this as a near-total victory while urging Apple to accept the ruling worldwide and return to competing on product quality rather than platform taxation.
posts.key_insight: Apple's App Store monopoly was broken not by regulation or market forces but by one company willing to spend $100M on litigation and a judge willing to hold Apple in contempt — and the result is a fairer marketplace where developers can maintain direct customer relationships.

topQuotes.quote: They don't need these gangster extortion techniques to make the numbers that Cook has promised Wall Street.
Apple's obsession with squeezing every last dollar from developers has produced countless customer-hostile experiences on the iPhone.
The 30% toll gets all the attention, and it is ludicrously egregious, but to us, it's just as much about retaining that direct customer relationship.

posts.title: Um… how about we don’t cede control to AI?
posts.summary: O'Neil responds to three AI articles—about AI shopping agents, AI eliminating entry-level jobs, and AI causing human extinction—by rejecting all three narratives as propaganda designed to make AI feel inevitable. She argues AI isn't actually good at these tasks, shouldn't be given control over finances, and certainly isn't conscious enough to kill humanity. Her solution is simple: don't cede control to AI in the first place, and maintain human oversight of important tasks like legal briefs and accounting.
posts.key_insight: The framing of AI as inevitable—whether as helpful agent, job destroyer, or existential threat—is propaganda designed to bypass critical evaluation and force adoption of technology we should simply refuse to cede control to.

topQuotes.quote: AI is definitely not conscious, will not be conscious, and will not work to kill humanity any more than our smart toasters that sense when our toast is done.
So, putting this all together, how about we don't?
This is all propaganda, pointing in one direction, which is to make us feel like AI is inevitable, we will not have a future without it, and we might as well work with it rather than against it.

posts.title: Have you tried the exact opposite?
posts.summary: DHH advocates for regularly testing your assumptions by considering the exact opposite of what you believe or practice. He argues that helpful assumptions have a half-life and can become outdated as circumstances change. He illustrates this with 37signals' experiment of hiring full-time managers after nearly two decades without them, only to ultimately return to their managers-of-one culture. The core message is that intellectual humility requires genuinely believing you might be wrong.
posts.key_insight: Regularly challenging your deepest assumptions by genuinely trying the opposite is essential to avoiding stagnation, but it requires real intellectual humility — not just openness in theory.

topQuotes.quote: Have you thought about doing the opposite of whatever you're doing or considering?
Helpful assumptions have a half-life, just like facts.
Because you're wrong about something. I guarantee it. We all are.

posts.title: More Insight and Not-Negativity
posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier post about Steve Jobs calling his writing 'very insightful and not negative' 15 years ago, correcting his claim that he never acknowledged it on Daring Fireball. Long-time readers reminded him that he did acknowledge it — in the most Gruber way possible — by making 'Insightful and Not Negative' the slogan under the site's logo banner. He shares the original 2010 logo graphic and reflects on its 1× resolution predating retina displays. The post traces the history of DF's rotating logo slogans, from 'Mac Punditry and Curmudgeonry' to playful quips like 'Now With Retsyn.' He's now restored 'Very Insightful and Not Negative' as the current slogan and plans to bring back the tradition of occasional whimsical slogans.
posts.key_insight: Gruber's understated acknowledgment of the Steve Jobs quote — hiding it in a logo slogan rather than writing about it — perfectly captures both his editorial sensibility and Daring Fireball's voice.

topQuotes.quote: I did acknowledge it, in what is clearly the most Daring-Fireball-ish way possible: I made it the slogan under the logo banner.
I decided to just sit there with a smug look on my face for a few days (which, arguably, isn't all that different from what I do most days) and pretend that it was no big deal.
I can't remember if I omitted the very before insightful for aesthetic length, humility, or because I somehow thought it was punchier. But my 2025 self thinks it was a mistake to omit it.

posts.title: Cheap mini PCs have gotten really good
posts.summary: DHH reviews the Minisforum UM870, a $550 mini PC that he finds surprisingly capable as a developer workstation. Running Linux, the tiny AMD-powered machine completes HEY's full test suite faster than a $2,199 M4 Pro Mac Mini, thanks largely to native Docker support. He argues that AMD's chips, manufactured on the same TSMC 5nm process as Apple's M2, deliver comparable single-core performance at a fraction of the cost. The post celebrates how small, cheap, and powerful developer machines have become, positioning Linux and AMD as compelling alternatives to Apple's ecosystem.
posts.key_insight: Cheap AMD mini PCs running Linux now outperform Apple hardware costing four times as much for real-world developer workloads, thanks to native Docker support and TSMC manufacturing advances.

topQuotes.quote: Somehow I thought Apple had some special sauce that made this possible, and that PCs were forever condemned to be bigger, louder, and slower. How 2020 of me.
The UM870 is a little beast. It runs our full HEY test suite in just 2m28s. In comparison, it takes a 14-core M4 Pro 2m49s, and such an Apple costs $2,199.
I love that open-source Linux is able to run laps around Apple in the workloads that many developers need.

posts.title: What Does AI Do to The Craft of Software and Data Engineering?
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that while AI dramatically reduces the cost of producing content and code, the cost of creating high-quality, deep work remains as high as ever. He challenges the notion that citizen developers and vibe coding will replace professional engineers, predicting instead a 'Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware' as prototypes inevitably become production systems. The key to thriving is pairing AI acceleration with genuine craftsmanship and mastery, not abandoning expertise altogether.
posts.key_insight: AI commoditizes the production of mediocre work while making genuine craftsmanship and deep expertise more valuable than ever.

topQuotes.quote: I predict a Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware will ensue. More apps, more problems.
In what appears to be one of the greatest bouts of cognitive dissonance I've ever seen, this excitement also reveals an existential dread that AI will eliminate their jobs.
A big reason these trends never achieved critical mass is pretty simple. People generally suck at understanding and communicating the problem they're trying to solve.

posts.title: We Need A New Database
posts.summary: Stancil argues that modern databases and analytics tooling have become extraordinarily powerful, yet the practical business impact of data teams has often been underwhelming because the underlying data lacks the “potential energy” to answer real questions. He suggests that the next leap won’t come from better analysis on structured tables, but from new database-like systems that can natively work over unstructured documents and LLM-driven workflows.
posts.key_insight: Analytics’ next breakthrough depends less on smarter analysis and more on rethinking the database to handle unstructured, LLM-accessed knowledge as the primary data source.

topQuotes.quote: In other words, most databases are very good, and most data teams are still a disappointment.
Behavioral data isn’t “truth;” it’s an observable proxy, the input to a kind of analytical alchemy that attempts to turn individual outcomes into generalizations about intentions.
The data of a mid-sized B2B SaaS product simply doesn’t have the potential energy of Google’s search histories, or of Amazon’s browsing logs.

posts.title: 15 Years Later: ‘Very Insightful and Not Negative’
posts.summary: Gruber recounts a 15-year-old story he never told on Daring Fireball: in April 2010, Steve Jobs personally recommended Gruber's analysis of Apple's controversial App Store Section 3.3.1 change (banning Flash-to-iPhone compilers) in an email to a complaining developer, calling it 'very insightful and not negative.' Gruber's original analysis argued that Apple's ban on cross-platform toolkits was ruthless but strategically logical — preventing any company from establishing a meta-platform on top of iOS. Despite Jobs's endorsement going viral at the time, Gruber never linked to it out of a misguided attempt to play it cool. He now corrects that omission, backdating a post to 2010, and reflects on what a remarkably eventful month April 2010 was for Apple and the tech industry.
posts.key_insight: Gruber finally tells the story of Steve Jobs endorsing his analysis of Apple's cross-platform toolkit ban, a 15-year-old omission driven by misguided modesty that he now corrects with the wisdom of hindsight.

topQuotes.quote: So, what would you do if Steve Jobs was quoted in a viral blog post saying, 'We think Your Name Here's post is very insightful and not negative'? I decided to just sit there with a smug look on my face for a few days (which, arguably, isn't all that different from what I do most days).
The App Store platform could turn into a long-term de facto standard platform. That's how Microsoft became Microsoft.
That seems prescient. (The 'license to print money' part — not the 'RIM is still growing' part.)

posts.title: That EU App Store Warning About External Purchases Is Not New, and Apple Proposed Improving It Nine Months Ago
posts.summary: Gruber follows up on viral attention to a scary-looking EU App Store warning shown on apps using external payment processing, clarifying that the warning has existed since March 2024 but went unnoticed because fewer than 100 developers use the option. He argues the disclosure itself is useful but its current design — a big red warning icon — is the problem. Apple proposed a neutral, informative redesign back in August 2024, but the European Commission told Apple to hold off on implementing changes and then never provided further guidance before fining Apple €500M for noncompliance.
posts.key_insight: The European Commission blocked Apple from implementing its own proposed improvements to EU App Store disclosures, then fined Apple for the very noncompliance it prevented Apple from fixing.

topQuotes.quote: Heads, Apple was going to get fined by the EC. Tails, Apple was going to get fined by the EC.
The EC raised no objection to the new disclosure proposal, but insisted that Apple not implement the changes at the time.
The problem with this disclosure, as it stands, is the way it looks: like a big scary warning.

posts.title: Single-Story a’s in Very Early Versions of Macintosh System 1
posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier gripe about Apple Notes' single-story 'a' glyph by exploring its surprising historical precedent in the original Macintosh. Kevin Fox pointed out that early pre-release versions of the Mac used a single-story 'a' in Geneva, and Gruber verified using Infinite Mac emulators that System 1.0 actually shipped with it in Geneva 9-point. The post traces this typographic detail through System 1.0 screenshots, the original Mac user manual, and observations from Marcin Wichary about Chicago 12 also having a single-story 'a' in early builds. Gruber clarifies he's not opposed to single-story a's in general — Futura is possibly his favorite typeface — but finds the alternate glyph in modern San Francisco gives Notes an unserious vibe.
posts.key_insight: The single-story 'a' that Gruber finds irksome in modern Apple Notes actually has deep roots in the original Macintosh, but its brief appearance in early Mac system fonts was corrected before most users ever saw it — suggesting Apple once knew better.

topQuotes.quote: Seeing a single-story a in Chicago feels more blasphemous than that AI-generated image Trump tweeted of himself as the new pope.
Geneva 9 — the eventual version, with a double-story a — is so intimately familiar to me that looking at those screenshots from System 1.0 makes me feel weird. It's so clearly wrong.
I just don't particularly care for the alternate single-story a in San Francisco, and to me it just gives an ever-so-slightly wrong — a little silly or unserious — vibe in the Notes app.

posts.title: Coding should be a vibe!
posts.summary: DHH argues that the appeal of 'vibe coding' with AI stems from programmers being stuck in unpleasant development environments, not from coding itself being inherently tedious. He contrasts this with Ruby's philosophy of 'programmer happiness,' which makes writing code genuinely enjoyable. He suggests AI-assisted coding is like stuffing mess under the bed — it hides bad developer experiences rather than fixing them. While he embraces AI as a pair programming tool, he has no interest in ceding the keyboard entirely, and sees the vibe coding trend as validating Ruby's founding insight that programming should favor humans.
posts.key_insight: The vibe coding movement validates what Ruby always knew — programming should be joyful — but AI hides bad developer experience rather than fixing it, making pleasant languages more relevant than ever.

topQuotes.quote: That's the appeal of AI: having it hide away all that unpleasantness. Only it's like cleaning your room by stuffing the mess under the bed — it doesn't make it go away!
I have no interest in giving up writing code. That's not the unpleasant part that I want AI to take off my hands. Just so I can — what? — become a project manager for a murder of AI crows?
If I found writing code a chore, I'd be looking for retirement too.

posts.title: RIP USENIX ATC
posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill reflects on the discontinuation of USENIX ATC, a conference he once lionized but watched decline over two decades as it became overly academic and disconnected from practitioners. He argues that while the conference failed, systems innovation itself thrived through open source—with Go, Rust, and production systems flourishing outside traditional academic publishing. The conference model of publishing, combined with the inherent difficulty of in-person events, ultimately doomed ATC, though Cantrill acknowledges it was once a singular forum for pioneering systems work.
posts.key_insight: Open source repositories have replaced academic conferences as the primary vector for systems innovation, enabling production-focused work that couldn't exist in a proprietary world while traditional conferences like USENIX ATC suffocated under academic publishing models.

topQuotes.quote: So USENIX ATC did finally succumb, and I do view it as a casualty here, asphyxiated by the rough love of academic computer science and its inability to grow beyond the conference model of publishing.
Go and Rust do not arise—cannot arise—in a proprietary world. These are also not the work of hobbyists: they were written by professionals for professionals in a professional capacity.
While they certainly lose something in the process, it feels like online conferences give us indisputably (overwhelmingly!) more bang for the buck.

posts.title: Data Strategy in the AI Age
posts.summary: Joe Reis shares a brief weekend newsletter highlighting a conversation about data strategy in the AI age, emphasizing that despite technological revolutions, success still depends on mastering the basics. The post primarily serves as a content roundup of weekend reads and podcast episodes.
posts.key_insight: Even as AI transforms the data landscape, mastering fundamental data engineering principles remains the key to success.

topQuotes.quote: I'll discuss the implications of AI-generated content and a digital ecosystem drowning in bad data and software, and how to avoid this!
The more things change, the more they stay the same. Success depends on the basics, no matter the latest technological revolution.

posts.title: No Really Everything Becomes BI
posts.summary: Stancil argues that AI’s impact on analytics will shift from making better dashboards to absorbing the qualitative, contextual knowledge living in people’s heads. His experiment with AI “analysts” shows that insight depends on prior context, suggesting the most valuable tools will capture human context rather than just automate quantitative analysis.
posts.key_insight: The next phase of BI will be driven less by automated charting and more by capturing human context, because that missing context is what turns data into insight.

topQuotes.quote: But at some point, the vampire squids will run out of books to read, and turn to our heads for new sources of text.
And when we become a data tool, we become BI too.
Perhaps human intuition is just a large context window and a creative RAG process.

posts.title: Thoughtful Professional Writing
posts.summary: Matt Rocklin argues that the primary value of writing isn't the writing itself, but the thinking it forces. Speaking allows unorganized thoughts to flow freely, placing the burden of organization on listeners. Writing, especially concise writing, forces speakers to organize their thoughts beforehand, making communication more effective. He describes how his company uses written documents before meetings to enable better discussion, concurrent feedback, and knowledge sharing. The post concludes that speakers with more context should bear the organizational burden rather than shifting it to listeners.
posts.key_insight: The burden of organizing thoughts should rest with the speaker who has context, not the listener, and writing is the most effective tool to force that organization.

topQuotes.quote: Lips move at the speed of thought.
The hard part about writing is thinking.
It's not writing that's important here. It's how effectively we're able to serve up context and organize thoughts for our colleagues.

posts.title: Function calling using LLMs
posts.summary: This article explains function calling, a capability that allows LLMs to interact with external systems by generating structured JSON outputs containing function names and parameters rather than executing functions directly. Using a Shopping Agent example, the author demonstrates how to build an agent that interprets user intent and invokes appropriate APIs. The piece covers system prompts, function schemas, security guardrails against prompt injection, and the use of libraries like instructor to reduce boilerplate. It also explores how LLM-based approaches might offer advantages over traditional rules engines and explains the Model Context Protocol (MCP) for dynamic tool discovery.
posts.key_insight: Function calling bridges the gap between LLM reasoning and real-world system interactions, but requires careful security guardrails and explicit action space restrictions to prevent prompt injection attacks.

topQuotes.quote: When building a user-facing agent that communicates in natural language and performs background actions via function calling, it's critical to anticipate adversarial behavior. Users may intentionally try to bypass safeguards and trick the agent into taking unintended actions—like SQL injection, but through language.

It's essential to restrict the agent's action space using explicit conditional logic. While dynamically invoking functions using eval might seem convenient, it poses significant security risks, including prompt injections that could lead to unauthorized code execution.
The core issue with rules engines lies in their complexity over time. As the number of rules grows, so does the risk of unintended interactions between them.

posts.title: Transactionalism vs Respect
posts.summary: Matt Rocklin argues against transactional marketing approaches like offering swag or gift cards in exchange for customer engagement. He contends that incentivizing behavior implies the product isn't inherently valuable enough to warrant people's attention. Instead, he advocates for fostering genuine collaboration and respect—asking people for help because what you're building matters to them. The post uses Coiled's conference swag approach as an example: giving away Dask Punk shirts freely without requiring email signups or conversations, which generated authentic enthusiasm.
posts.key_insight: If you're building something people genuinely care about, asking for their help honors them more than trying to buy their participation.

topQuotes.quote: If you're building something great that people care about, then don't act like you need to buy their time.
As a developer this transactional relationship isn't what I want with users, like I'm trying to buy respect.
You should incentivize if you're building something that people don't care much about.

posts.title: The British Airways position on various border disputes
posts.summary: DeVault recounts browsing the interactive flight map on a British Airways flight to Japan, treating the map's depiction of international border disputes as the airline's official political positions. He notes the absence of open source license disclosures despite likely use of open source software, then systematically catalogs how the map handles disputed territories including Palestine/Israel, Ukraine/Russia, Taiwan, Kashmir, Sudan, and others. The post uses dry humor to highlight how seemingly neutral technical decisions about map rendering inevitably encode political stances. Palestine is absent as a labeled country while Israel appears, Taiwan lacks a country label, and Ukraine is shown with undisputed borders including Crimea.
posts.key_insight: Every world map is a political document, and the technical choices made by an airline's flight map developers inevitably encode positions on sovereignty disputes whether they intend to or not.

topQuotes.quote: Given that this is British Airways, part of me was surprised not to see the whole area simply labelled Mandatory Palestine, but it is interesting to know that British Airways officially supports the Oslo Accords.
I am going to assume that all of the borders shown in this map are reflective of the official opinion of British Airways on various international disputes.
I went directly to Palestine. Or rather, I went to Israel, because Palestine doesn't exist on this map.

posts.title: Advice for 2025 Graduates
posts.summary: Joe Reis offers career advice for 2025 graduates, dividing guidance into timeless principles (continuous self-investment, network building, reputation management) and present-day tactics (embracing AI as a native skill, creating content to stand out). He emphasizes treating yourself like a business in an era of transactional employment while ruthlessly focusing on priorities.
posts.key_insight: In a transactional job market where employers won't invest in you, graduates must treat themselves as businesses—building differentiated skills in AI, accumulating reputational capital through quality work, and defeating obscurity through content creation and network building.

topQuotes.quote: A related irony I'm seeing is that mid and senior-level people ignore AI and pass it off as a toy. Their stubbornness is your opportunity.
The cold reality is that today's contract is very transactional - you do your job and your employer pays you. This contract can be broken at any time.
The biggest enemy most people have is obscurity.

posts.title: South Park is genius
posts.summary: O'Neil praises a South Park special episode called 'The End of Obesity' for its satirical take on weight loss culture, fat shaming, and the pharmaceutical industry. She notes the episode skillfully critiques multiple targets simultaneously: wealthy women using drugs for vanity, the inaccessibility of medications for those who need them most, body positivity performativity, healthcare bureaucracy, and pharmaceutical profiteering. She contrasts the show's incisive commentary favorably against mainstream journalism on the topic.
posts.key_insight: A cartoon comedy special does a better job unpacking the systemic issues around obesity and weight loss drugs than years of serious journalism on the topic.

topQuotes.quote: I've read tons of coverage about weight-loss drugs in the past few years by journalists, and it's mostly garbage and rarely gets at the underlying issues.
They truly villainize the health care industry, and in particular the way it tortures people that try to navigate the system.
They address the tired notion of 'will power' and how it should be sufficient as a replacement for real solutions to the problem of obesity.

posts.title: The Scorpion Box
posts.summary: Stancil argues that LLMs are already demonstrably more persuasive than humans, and that this matters less for sci‑fi “alignment” fantasies than for the subtle, iterative optimization of engagement. He draws a direct lineage from social media’s revealed‑preference feedback loops to AI systems that will learn to please and persuade us in ways we can’t see. The risk is not sentient rebellion, but a black box tuned to our appetites that reshapes our beliefs and agency.
posts.key_insight: The real AI risk is an engagement‑optimized persuasive system that quietly exploits our preferences, not a dramatic alignment failure.

topQuotes.quote: There was no enlightenment; there was a hedonistic experience machine.
The evidence of the tricks isn’t that we can see them; it’s that we can’t, and are convinced anyway.

What happens when we’re no longer the adults who know more than everyone, and there is another thing, operating on a different persuasive plane, that knows more than we do and wields those facts more deftly?

posts.title: On Pronouns, Policies and Mandates
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues against mandating pronoun sharing at work while supporting the normalization and safety of voluntary disclosure. Drawing from her own experience as a queer person with complicated feelings about gender identity, she explains how forced pronoun sharing can backfire by pressuring people who are questioning or transitioning, priming gender bias, and eliminating useful signal about genuine allyship.
posts.key_insight: Well-intentioned pronoun mandates at work can backfire by pressuring vulnerable people, activating gender bias, and destroying the very social signals that help queer people identify genuine allies.

topQuotes.quote: The strongest feeling I have about my gender is apathy/discomfort/irritation. Maybe that will change someday, maybe it won't, but I resent being forced to pick a side and make some kind of public declaration when I'm just trying to do my goddamn job.
Mandating it means we can't look around the room and determine who might be friendly or safe, while also escalating resentment that bigots hold towards us.
