How much easier it is to make and enforce rules, if you're in a position with the power to do so, than to win hearts and minds. Rules always have edge cases and unintended consequences, and the backlash effect is real. People don't like being told what to do.

posts.title: Oxide’s compensation model: How is it going?
posts.summary: Four years after publicly explaining Oxide's uniform compensation model, Cantrill reflects on how it has worked in practice as the company grew from 23 to 75 employees. Rather than causing people to slack off as skeptics predicted, the model has driven employees to take their own performance and peer hiring extremely seriously. The uniform pay structure has enabled organizational flexibility, eliminated toxic middle management dynamics, liberated feedback from compensation politics, and fostered genuine teamwork built on mutual trust.
posts.key_insight: Uniform compensation doesn't reduce motivation—it redirects energy from internal political competition toward genuine contribution and teamwork.

topQuotes.quote: When some outsiders hear about our compensation model, they insist that it can't possibly work because 'everyone will slack off.' I have come to find this concern to be more revealing of the person making the objection than of our model.
The specific dismissal of support roles reveals a widespread and corrosive devaluation of those closest to customers.
Performance review processes aren't, in fact, about improving the performance of the team, but rather quantifying and stack-ranking that performance for purposes of compensation.

posts.title: Good Writing
posts.summary: Graham argues that good-sounding writing and correct ideas are tightly linked rather than independent. Revising for rhythm and flow forces ideas to become more coherent, both by shaking loose better formulations and by making errors easier for the writer-as-first-reader to notice. This connection mainly holds when writing is used to develop ideas, not merely to report them.
posts.key_insight: When you’re using writing to think, polishing the sound of the prose is not cosmetic—it’s a reliable way to refine and correct the ideas themselves.

topQuotes.quote: It's hard to be right without sounding right.
Fixing sentences that sound bad seems to help get the ideas right.
So it's not quite right to say that better sounding writing is more likely to be true.

posts.title: Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers Rules, in Excoriating Decision, That Apple Violated Her 2021 Court Order Regarding App Store Anti-Steering Provisions
posts.summary: Gruber covers Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers's devastating ruling that Apple willfully violated her 2021 court order requiring the App Store to allow developers to steer users to web purchases. The ruling reveals that Apple's finance team, led by CFO Luca Maestri and VP Alex Roman, convinced Tim Cook to impose a 27% commission on off-app purchases and erect new barriers — directly defying the injunction — while Phil Schiller advocated for genuine compliance. The judge found that Roman lied under oath and has referred the matter for potential criminal contempt proceedings. Gruber notes the ruling essentially validates Schiller's position all along: Apple should compete fairly with web purchases rather than obstruct them. He argues the business impact may be limited, but the reputational damage is severe, tempered only by Schiller's continued presence at Apple.
posts.key_insight: Apple's finance-driven decision to defy a court order with a 27% web commission — overruling Phil Schiller's counsel to compete fairly — has resulted in a devastating ruling that damages Apple's credibility far more than genuine compliance ever would have.

topQuotes.quote: The only people in the world who seemed to think Apple could or should comply with the 2021 injunction by charging a commission on those web transactions were Apple's finance team members, led by Luca Maestri and Alex Roman, and Tim Cook.
As for Alex Roman, I think he's in some serious trouble. Like doing-time-in-the-clink trouble.
This is an injunction, not a negotiation. There are no do-overs once a party willfully disregards a court order.

posts.title: Don't make Google sell Chrome
posts.summary: DHH argues against forcing Google to sell Chrome as an antitrust remedy, contending it would harm the open web more than help it. He maintains Chrome won the browser war through genuine technical merit, not anticompetitive behavior. Google's massive investment in Chrome is driven by economic self-interest—a thriving web means more search traffic and ad revenue—and that alignment of incentives is precisely why it works. He warns that without an 800-pound gorilla championing web standards and capabilities, proprietary platforms like iOS App Store would gain ground. While acknowledging Google's legitimate ad-market monopoly abuses deserve remedy, he argues Chrome divestiture is the wrong punishment.
posts.key_insight: Google's economic self-interest in a thriving open web makes it the web's most effective champion, and severing that incentive through a forced Chrome sale would harm the open platform it sustains.

topQuotes.quote: Capitalism doesn't run on benevolence, but incentives.
We want an 800-pound gorilla in the web's corner!
Chrome won the browser war fair and square by building a better surfboard for the internet.

posts.title: On Dropouts and Bootstraps
posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on how she and her college-dropout peers in early Silicon Valley developed an unfounded contempt for CS degree holders, using cherry-picked evidence to justify their own paths. She draws a broader lesson about how people in tech construct self-serving heuristics to validate their belonging, and argues for humility given the enormous role of luck and timing in tech careers.
posts.key_insight: The heuristics tech workers use to judge talent are usually self-serving rationalizations that reinforce their own backgrounds, and the honest response to career success in tech is humility about how much luck and timing mattered.

topQuotes.quote: I think I worked really fucking hard and I got really fucking lucky. Both can be true. Some of the smartest kids I grew up with are now pumping gas or dead.
Ever since then, I've met wave after wave of people in this industry who are convinced they know how to sift "good" talent from "bad" via easily detected heuristics. They're mostly bullshit.

There was a window of time in the 2000s when tech was an escalator to the middle class for a whole generation of weirdos, dropouts and liberal arts misfits. That window has been closed for a while now.

posts.title: Is Chrome Even a Sellable Asset?
posts.summary: Gruber argues that the DOJ's push to force Google to divest Chrome is fundamentally misguided from both business and technical perspectives. From a business standpoint, Chrome's value is entirely derived from being Google's integrated browser that eliminates traffic acquisition fees — it has no standalone commercial value. From a technical standpoint, Chrome is almost entirely open source via Chromium, meaning there's nothing proprietary to actually buy. He further argues that even if Google is banned from default search agreements, browser makers would likely keep Google as default anyway since the per-search payments are lucrative and users overwhelmingly choose Google. The piece concludes that Chrome is inseparable from Google itself — it's just a software layer between Google and its users, not a sellable asset.
posts.key_insight: Chrome has no standalone commercial value because its worth is entirely derived from eliminating Google's traffic acquisition costs, and its codebase is already freely available as open source — making a forced divestiture both economically nonsensical and technically meaningless.

topQuotes.quote: 3 billion users = $15–$20 billion is not real math. It's just bullshit.
They might as well try to force Google to sell the two o's from its name.
We know the value of a Google-free version of Chrome. Nothing. Zero.

posts.title: Another Periodic Suggestion to Try, Just Try, Switching to Kagi for Search
posts.summary: Gruber makes another case for switching from Google to Kagi for web search, citing a Boston Globe article by Aaron Pressman who was burned twice by Google's results — once overpaying for a UK travel authorization and once for hotel rooms — because semi-scam sites ranked above official ones. Gruber argues Kagi's superiority isn't about privacy or anti-Google protest but simply better search results, finding the right answer first more reliably than Google or DuckDuckGo. He notes that even Google's 'disenshittified' udm=14 mode produces worse results than Kagi, and that Kagi is especially better at finding older content. He compares paying for Kagi to paying for HBO during cable's heyday — you get both ad-free service and noticeably higher quality, except Kagi is a full replacement rather than a supplement.
posts.key_insight: The case for Kagi over Google isn't ideological — it's purely practical: Kagi consistently returns the right result first, making it a straightforwardly better search engine worth paying for.

topQuotes.quote: I recommend switching to Kagi not because it's more private, not as a protest against Google, not as a rejection of search ads dominating the top of Google's results, but simply because Kagi's results are clearly better.

Paying for Kagi today feels a lot like paying for HBO back in the cable TV heyday. Part of the deal is that you are paying for ad-free service, yes. But you're also paying for noticeably higher quality.
Decades of relying on Google had taught me that I didn't have to be a more careful consumer.

posts.title: “I Don’t Need to Learn Anything Anymore”
posts.summary: Reis argues that the belief that AI eliminates the need for learning is dangerously misguided, as our already-shrinking attention spans and surface-level thinking are being exacerbated by AI tools that let people outsource critical thinking. While AI can amplify the capabilities of competent professionals who know what 'good' looks like, it poses significant risks in the hands of those without underlying expertise.
posts.key_insight: AI is a force multiplier that amplifies whatever expertise you already have—making competent people more effective while enabling incompetent people to produce higher volumes of low-quality work.

topQuotes.quote: We're entering the era of The Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware where we churn out endless amounts of disposable and low-quality apps.

If you're competent, you know what 'good' looks like. If AI does something wrong, you can nudge it along. But AI can do more harm than good in the hands of a rank amateur, especially an incompetent and overconfident person.

What does it mean to be an expert if you're outsourcing your critical thinking and work to an AI? If you don't have sufficient depth in a field or subject, how will you know if AI provides you with the correct answers?

posts.title: A New Invisible Hand
posts.summary: Stancil argues that interpretation, not raw data, shapes what we believe, much like sports broadcasts guide our perception of a game. As LLMs and MCP servers mediate more analysis, the interpretive layer shifts from human analysts to AI systems, giving AI providers subtle, powerful influence over narratives and decisions. The result is an “invisible hand” that edits reality by framing data, content, and choices before we see them ourselves.
posts.key_insight: As AI intermediates more of our data and creative workflows, it doesn’t just answer questions—it frames reality, giving AI systems disproportionate power over how we interpret the world.

topQuotes.quote: The shift isn’t from dashboard to chatbot, or from analyst to agent; it’s from being the author of our lives, to being its editor.
If data is a company’s senses, then whatever sits between a quantitative question and its annotated answer is the creative director of a company’s reality.
The claim decodes the data, and the data proves the claim.

posts.title: We'll always need junior programmers
posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals received over 2,200 applications for a junior programmer position, all reviewed by humans without AI screening. He pushes back against the narrative that AI has eliminated the need for junior developers, arguing that humans who understand code will always be necessary to vet AI-generated work. He also notes that landing a job isn't about meeting a checklist of criteria but about being the best candidate in the applicant pool.
posts.key_insight: No matter how capable AI becomes, the industry will always need junior programmers who can grow into people capable of understanding and vetting what the machines produce.

topQuotes.quote: It's truly utopian thinking that mankind won't need people capable of vetting the work done by AI in five minutes.
Yes, you have to present a baseline of relevant markers to even get into consideration, like a great cover letter that doesn't smell like AI slop.
We received over 2,200 applications for our just-closed junior programmer opening, and now we're going through all of them by hand and by human. No AI screening here.

posts.title: A Way Forward
posts.summary: George Hotz expresses deep disappointment with the Trump administration's handling of tariffs and foreign policy, arguing that emotional decision-making has replaced strategic thinking. He criticizes the economic damage caused by uncertainty and tariff policies, noting that markets don't lie even when news does. The post pivots to a direct appeal to Elon Musk, urging him to distance himself from the administration, reconcile personal grievances, and potentially lead a new centrist political movement. Hotz concludes with a warning that America risks becoming a 'protectionist backwater' that he would leave if current trends continue.
posts.key_insight: America's tariff-driven economic self-harm stems from emotional decision-making rather than strategic thinking, and only a new merit-focused political coalition—potentially led by Elon Musk—can compete with China's long-term planning.

topQuotes.quote: A 3-week old LLM could have told you shit was gonna crash.
If the racists or the other racists are still running the show, we really are just cooked.
To date, the Trump administration has been an absolute tragedy. It has been the acting out of emotions. There are no adults in the room.

posts.title: Liberate Analytical Data Management with DuckDB
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen recounts the origin story of DuckDB, born from frustration with the state of analytical data management in 2015, when options were expensive enterprise systems, Hadoop, immature cloud services, or bolted-on columnar extensions. He and Mark Raasveldt decided to build 'the SQLite for analytics' at CWI Amsterdam, making key design decisions: single-node over distributed, vectorized execution over JIT compilation, CPU-only with no GPU support, no hardware-specific SIMD code, and MIT-style open source licensing. He demonstrates DuckDB's out-of-core hash join capabilities and argues that the embedded architecture liberates data management by allowing DuckDB to run anywhere from browsers to Raspberry Pis to outer space.
posts.key_insight: The most impactful design decisions in DuckDB were acts of deliberate simplification — single-node, no GPU, no SIMD, no JIT — which maximized portability and enabled analytics to run anywhere, from browsers to Raspberry Pis.

topQuotes.quote: Just as a tip, if you're making the benchmark, this is embarrassing. Probably you should change the benchmark until your system is the fastest.
In my opinion, there's absolutely no point of making data transformations run on GPUs.
If you had a JIT engine, you would still be working on rewriting your JIT. Some people here are clearly working on that. Sorry.

posts.title: Silicon Valley drinks its own Kool aid on AI
posts.summary: O'Neil argues that AI researchers are exhibiting cultish behavior by treating language models as if they possess consciousness and honesty. She critiques the AI Futures Project for making speculative predictions reminiscent of flawed COVID models and for using language that presupposes LLMs have minds that could 'lie' or be 'honest.' She emphasizes that LLMs merely predict the next word based on training data and have no notion of truth or consciousness. The post concludes that projecting consciousness onto algorithms built with Reddit data is itself a form of self-deception.
posts.key_insight: AI researchers are projecting consciousness and moral capacity onto statistical pattern-matching systems, revealing more about human self-deception than about machine intelligence.

topQuotes.quote: We could be lying to ourselves or to each other at any moment! Like, right now, when we project consciousness onto the algorithm we just built with Reddit training data!
There is no notion of truth in an LLM, it's just predicting the next word based on patterns in the training data (think: Reddit).
Can you even imagine having taken an actual philosophy class? Or were you too busy studying STEM?

posts.title: The Data Engineer's Guide to Efficient Log Parsing with DuckDB/MotherDuck
posts.summary: Simon argues that logs are the lifeblood of data platforms and that data engineers spend significant time parsing and analyzing them for debugging, tracing, and performance analysis. Unlike software engineers who can use debuggers, data engineers must 'log their way through problems' because data is constantly changing and flowing from external sources. The post provides a comprehensive tutorial on using DuckDB as a log parser, demonstrating its ability to handle various formats (JSON, CSV, syslog) with zero-copy reading from S3, data warehouses, or HTTPS. For larger datasets that exceed local resources, MotherDuck extends DuckDB's capabilities to cloud scale without syntax changes, enabling collaborative analysis of massive log datasets.
posts.key_insight: Data engineering platforms are fundamentally built on logs rather than debuggers, making efficient log parsing with tools like DuckDB a critical competitive advantage for data teams.

topQuotes.quote: Data platforms are essentially built around logs. We can't debug the data stack; the logs are our way to find the error later on.
Logs are the lifeblood of our data platforms
Software engineers can debug more easily, as they are in control of what the user can and can't do. But data is different, constantly changing and flowing from A to B.

posts.title: Resistance from the tech sector
posts.summary: DeVault argues that the tech sector is central to the rise of contemporary fascism, particularly in the United States, and that tech workers have both the ability and the obligation to resist. He identifies Palantir's surveillance infrastructure and DOGE's technocratic agenda as key instruments of authoritarian power. The post calls for collective action through unionization, workplace sabotage, and mutual support among tech workers. He emphasizes that solidarity and safety in numbers are essential, urging workers to abandon American individualism. DeVault acknowledges the fear involved but insists that inaction also carries risks, and asks readers to decide where they will draw the line.
posts.key_insight: Tech workers are uniquely positioned to resist technocratic authoritarianism because the regime's digital power depends entirely on their labor, making collective action and workplace resistance both possible and morally imperative.

topQuotes.quote: Make yourself a single point of failure, then fail.
Where DOGE is hilariously tragic, Palantir is nightmarishly effective.
It's clear that the regime will be digital.

posts.title: Wartime Data Teams
posts.summary: In times of economic uncertainty and business volatility, data teams must shift from peacetime exploration to wartime survival mode, focusing on singular business-critical missions. Drawing from personal experiences during the 2008 financial crisis and COVID, Reis argues that data professionals should deliver quick visible wins, stay scrappy, and be integral to business survival rather than hiding in technical siloes.
posts.key_insight: Data teams must abandon peacetime luxuries of exploration and tool accumulation, instead becoming scrappy, business-focused operators who deliver quick wins that directly support company survival.

topQuotes.quote: Most business books are fluff, written by people with more luck than smarts.
Things in business can turn on a dime. Black swans are more common than you think. Once in a generation crises happen every few years.
In peacetime, leaders must maximize and broaden the current opportunity... In wartime, by contrast, the company typically has a single bullet in the chamber and must, at all costs, hit the target.

posts.title: On Writing, Social Media, and Finding the Line of Embarrassment
posts.summary: Charity Majors reflects on how Twitter was essential to both Honeycomb's growth and her personal creative process, serving as a dopamine source for her undiagnosed ADHD. With social media fragmented and her old writing workflow broken, she commits to a three-month experiment of writing shorter, faster blog posts weekly — learning to ship without the crutch of Twitter threads or deadline-driven panic.
posts.key_insight: The writing that endures is the writing you ship in durable form, not the writing that feeds the dopamine cycle of social media — and learning to ship without those crutches requires deliberately embracing imperfection.

topQuotes.quote: I picked fights, I argued. I was combative and aggressive in public, and I loved it. I regret some of it now; I burned some good relationships, and I burned out my adrenal glands.
In retrospect, I now realize that I was doing things like 'outbounding' and 'product marketing' and 'category creation', but at the time it felt more like oxygen.
Most of those epic threads I put so much time and energy into crafting have vanished into the ether. The few that I bothered to convert into essay format are the only ones that have endured.

posts.title: Everything You Need to Know About Incremental View Maintenance
posts.summary: Incremental view maintenance (IVM) is gaining significant momentum with new implementations from Materialize, Feldera, Epsio, and others, as well as PostgreSQL's experimental pg_ivm extension. Traditional approaches to view updates—either recomputing on every read or periodically refreshing entire datasets—are wasteful and slow. IVM solves this by processing only the deltas, potentially making updates millions of times faster. The post traces the evolution from bag algebra approaches to modern frameworks: timely dataflow provides flexible time modeling, differential dataflow adds version tracking for selective computation reuse, and DBSP offers a simpler circuit-based model that can convert any batch SQL to incremental queries. While these technologies underpin many modern IVM systems, adoption challenges remain due to complexity.
posts.key_insight: Modern IVM engines built on timely dataflow, differential dataflow, and DBSP can automatically convert any batch SQL query into an efficient incremental computation, but the technology's complexity remains a barrier to widespread adoption.

topQuotes.quote: Differential dataflow is just really complicated.
I like to think of timely dataflow a lot like Hadoop's Map/Reduce: a powerful, but low-level framework for data processing.
Complex incremental queries that use joins, window functions, or recursive algorithms are very difficult to write by hand. A more systematic approach is needed.

posts.title: Startups Still Arent Businesses Yet
posts.summary: The post argues that Silicon Valley startups are still built as assets to be sold, not businesses meant to generate and keep profit. Even as AI-era companies reach revenue faster with smaller teams, the incentive remains a big liquidity event, so “efficiency” is a temporary aesthetic rather than a durable business model.
posts.key_insight: AI-era efficiency changes startup mechanics, but not incentives—most startups still optimize for a one-time sale, not durable profits.

topQuotes.quote: Businesses care about profit; assets care about perception.

In other words, despite the lofty language that people use to talk about Silicon Valley and its ambitions to build enduring companies, very few startups are, or even aspire to be, businesses that make money.
Efficiently making money wasn’t inherently good, because it was never the final goal.

posts.title: Why is Bernie Sanders touring Red States?
posts.summary: Cathy O'Neil celebrates Bernie Sanders and AOC's 'Fighting Oligarchy Tour' through red states as a necessary revival of Occupy-era populism. She recounts an MSNBC exchange where pundits couldn't fathom why Democrats would campaign in Utah except for media attention. O'Neil argues this reveals the Democratic Party's fundamental blindness: they've replaced genuine voter engagement with poll-watching and surgical electoral strikes. The party has forgotten that changing minds requires actually talking to people, not optimizing turnout models. This strategic myopia, she contends, is why red states keep getting redder.
posts.key_insight: Democrats have become so fixated on electoral math and polling data that they've forgotten politics is fundamentally about persuading actual human beings, not optimizing turnout in purple districts.

topQuotes.quote: About fucking TIME some folks on the left talk to the people of these states about the way things are actually working against the working person.
Enough is enough. It's time for the next version of Occupy.

At some point in the distant past, they stopped thinking about voters. Instead they started looking at numbers, and polls, and focusing very narrowly on incremental elections and surgical strikes into purple states.

posts.title: The new Framework 13 HX370
posts.summary: DHH reviews the new Framework 13 laptop with AMD HX370 chip, noting its strong performance gains for developers including beating an M4 Pro MacBook in running the HEY test suite. He acknowledges the top spec has gotten expensive at nearly $2,000, approaching MacBook Pro pricing, but argues the Framework's keyboard, 3:2 matte screen, repairability, and Linux compatibility justify it. He suggests the mid-range Ryzen AI 7 350 may be the sweet spot at $1,600. The post doubles as an endorsement of Linux for web development, contrasting its speed and simplicity with Apple's increasingly bloated software direction.
posts.key_insight: The Framework 13 with Linux has matured into a genuine MacBook Pro alternative for web developers, offering superior performance in Docker workloads while embodying the repairability and openness that Apple has abandoned.

topQuotes.quote: It seems like without an asshole in charge, both have move towards more bloat, more ads, more gimmicks, more control. Linux is an incredible antidote to this nonsense these days.
Apple still has a strong hardware game, but their software story is falling apart.
It's in my opinion the best Linux laptop you can buy today, which by extension makes it the best web developer laptop too.

posts.title: The AI Bubble
posts.summary: O'Neil observes that AI advertising has saturated public spaces like Bay Area billboards and airports, suggesting a classic bubble pattern. She argues that VC money has flooded into AI startups with the mandate to build first and find customers later, resulting in mediocre products nobody wants. She supports her skepticism with striking financial data: Big Tech spent $246 billion on AI infrastructure in 2024, OpenAI loses $2 for every $1 it makes, and trillion-dollar investments are planned despite unclear demand. She predicts this bubble will burst badly, possibly with Trump's tariffs taking the blame.
posts.key_insight: The AI industry shows classic bubble dynamics: massive capital expenditure and ubiquitous marketing for products that lose money and lack clear demand.

topQuotes.quote: Now there's a shitton of useless (or, as Tressie McMillam Cottom describes it, deeply 'mid') AI products that were incredibly expensive to build and nobody fucking cares about it at all.
It's almost like VC money has been poured into all of these startups with the single directive, to go build some AI and then figure out if they can sell it.
OpenAI loses $2 for every $1 it makes.

posts.title: The Universal Data Orchestrator: The Heartbeat of Data Engineering
posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that universal data orchestrators are the essential 'heartbeat' of modern data engineering, addressing challenges like complexity management, heterogeneous architectures, data quality, and governance. He traces the evolution from cron jobs to declarative YAML-based orchestrators, emphasizing the convergence of infrastructure-as-code principles with data pipeline management. The post advocates for centralized orchestration over implicit event-driven approaches for complex data workloads, highlighting Kestra's dual interface paradigm (UI + Code) as a bridge between technical and business users. Späti concludes that modern orchestrators must support software engineering best practices while remaining approachable to non-programmers.
posts.key_insight: Modern data orchestrators must combine declarative code-first approaches with intuitive UIs to bridge the gap between software engineering best practices and accessibility for business users.

topQuotes.quote: Generally, software best practices are hard to apply to data engineering. We know that from the history of SSIS and Informatica, GUI-driven tools where you need to click through 100 tables if you want to load tables.
Without a data orchestrator, you won't have a scheduler, data lineage, visualizer, or handy function to restart a process. You end up with disjointed stored procedures.

Coding is hard, especially for business specialists who need to keep up within their field and domain and just need to get their jobs scheduled. You can't expect them to learn programming on the side.

posts.title: Normal boyhood is ADHD
posts.summary: DHH argues that ADHD is massively over-diagnosed in American boys, with nearly a quarter of seventeen-year-olds now carrying the diagnosis. Drawing on a New York Times Magazine investigation, he contends that ADHD medications like Adderall don't actually improve learning outcomes and their benefits fade within a year. He characterizes most DSM-5 ADHD symptoms as normal boyhood behavior and frames the epidemic as a 'pathology of normalcy' driven by a narrow educational ideal that demands all children sit still and study. He draws parallels to the opioid crisis and puberty-blocker controversies as examples of medical establishments being swept up in harmful paradigms.
posts.key_insight: America's ADHD epidemic is not a medical crisis but a cultural one, where normal boyhood energy has been pathologized to fit a narrow educational ideal, and the medical establishment has been complicit in drugging a generation of boys with amphetamines that don't even work.

topQuotes.quote: I think we're looking at a pathology of normalcy, where healthy boys are defined as those who can sit still, focus on studies, and suppress kinetic energy.
Nearly a quarter of seventeen-year-old boys in America have an ADHD diagnosis. That's crazy.
Taking Adderall or Ritalin doesn't actually help you learn more, it merely makes trying tolerable.

posts.title: What «Shifting Left» Means and Why it Matters for Data Stacks
posts.summary: This article explains 'shifting left' in data engineering—moving data quality checks, business logic, and transformations earlier in the data lifecycle rather than handling them downstream in BI tools. Simon traces the concept from software testing origins through DevSecOps to modern data practices. He argues that declarative, code-first approaches have made shifting left more practical by enabling logic to move simply from one config file to another. The key benefits include earlier error detection, reduced compute costs through pre-materialization, centralized business logic, and enabling domain experts to contribute to data quality upstream.
posts.key_insight: Declarative code-first approaches have fundamentally changed what's possible with shifting left—enabling data teams to move business logic upstream simply by copying configuration between tools, catching errors during development rather than in production, and making data quality a collective organizational responsibility rather than an afterthought.

topQuotes.quote: Most data quality problems are code quality problems.

We don't need to invest massive effort in exporting large SQL or code from our BI tool, or worse, hugely generated code, and trying to convert that to our low-code ETL anymore. We can move descriptive business logic simply from one config file to another.
Data quality isn't a data team's problem; it's clearly defined best practices that everyone in the organization understands and can follow.

posts.title: Coding is Fun! (Again)
posts.summary: Joe Reis describes how AI coding assistants have revitalized his enthusiasm for programming by dramatically accelerating iteration cycles and reducing the friction of getting stuck. While embracing these tools, he cautions against mindless 'vibe coding' in production environments, warning that prototypes quickly become production code and that understanding what the AI generates remains essential.
posts.key_insight: AI coding tools have made programming genuinely enjoyable again by eliminating friction, but their power demands that developers maintain understanding of what they're building to avoid contributing to an inevitable wave of AI-generated slopware.

topQuotes.quote: We will have the equivalent of the Great Pacific Garbage Patch of AI Slopware very soon.
My concern with vibe coding is there's usually no such thing as a 'prototype.' The moment your prototype works in the wild, it's in production.
It puzzles me that this is controversial, but given how crazy the world is, I shouldn't be shocked.

posts.title: Critical Trade Theory
posts.summary: Hotz draws a parallel between Critical Race Theory and what he terms 'Critical Trade Theory' - the right-wing belief that trade imbalances must be caused by unfair practices. He argues this is as misguided as attributing income disparities solely to discrimination, when the obvious explanation is wealth differentials between countries. He contends that trade deficits will only disappear if trade goes to zero or other countries become richer than America, and criticizes tariff policies as anti-merit grift.
posts.key_insight: Both left-wing explanations for racial income gaps and right-wing explanations for trade deficits commit the same fallacy of attributing structural imbalances to malicious actors rather than obvious underlying factors.

topQuotes.quote: The right wing has invented something equally stupid. Introducing Critical Trade Theory.
Why are all the politicians in America anti-growth anti-reality idiots who want to drive us into the poor house?
If there's an imbalance in trade between countries, it must be due to unfair practices. (not due to the obvious, like one country is 10x richer than the other)

posts.title: The Industrialization Of It
posts.summary: Stancil argues that the economics of AI coding will push software development toward industrialization: machines running at massive scale will outcompete human teams on cost and throughput. Even if today’s models are clumsy, the real shift is building infrastructure, tooling, and codebases optimized for machine production, turning engineers into supervisors of AI factories.
posts.key_insight: Software engineering is poised to be industrialized, not because AI matches human craft, but because scaled machine labor plus new infrastructure will be cheaper, faster, and more relentless.

topQuotes.quote: But they are all cheap to make and cheap to buy, and man, we love cheap.
That’s how mechanization works—not by making a machine as capable as a human, but by making a machine that simply does not stop, and then building a factory around it.
By asking agents to write code for us, we’re requiring them to compete on our terms. How much better could they be if they wrote for themselves?

posts.title: Believe it's going to work even though it probably won't
posts.summary: DHH explores the paradox founders must embrace: believing their venture will succeed while knowing statistically it probably won't. He argues this isn't contradictory — belief fuels the energy needed to compete, while accepting the odds keeps you grounded. The resolution is to build work you'd be proud of regardless of outcome, so that failure doesn't destroy your self-worth. He warns that the deepest regret comes not from failing, but from compromising yourself in the process.
posts.key_insight: The founder's paradox — needing simultaneous belief and realism — is resolved not by hedging your bets, but by doing work worthy of your name regardless of whether it succeeds.

topQuotes.quote: Your worst sin is that you have destroyed and betrayed yourself for nothing.
To be a successful founder, you have to believe that what you're working on is going to work — despite knowing it probably won't!
Not because they don't know the odds, but because they've chosen to believe they're special.

posts.title: PyData Amsterdam 2025 Keynote
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces the evolution of data architecture from the 1985 three-tier model through cloud data warehouses to a new client-empowered paradigm. He argues that the traditional top-down architecture, where a central data warehouse holds all power, has barely changed despite moving to the cloud. Demonstrating that modern hardware can handle surprisingly large datasets on single machines, he makes the case that compute should move to the edge. He proposes flipping the architecture so clients do their own processing while lakehouse formats and commodity storage provide shared coordination.
posts.key_insight: The future of data architecture is flipping the pyramid — empowering edge devices with capable local query engines while using commodity lakehouse formats for coordination, eliminating the need for expensive centralized data warehouses.

topQuotes.quote: These things are criminally incompetent at transferring data. And it might actually be strategic incompetence. Because if you're keeping the data hostage, then you can keep charging them for your superyacht purposes.
A halfway competent, single-threaded implementation can beat most distributed systems.
We basically decided that we were going to stop writing research papers for good. It's a very career-destructive move, by the way.

posts.title: How Many New iPhones Can Fit on a Freight Plane?
posts.summary: Gruber examines how many iPhones Apple could have rush-shipped to the US ahead of Trump's tariffs, building on Ryan Jones's back-of-napkin math. Using weight limits of freight planes and Apple's estimated 150,000 daily US iPhone sales, he calculates each plane carries roughly two days of inventory. Even five planes from India only amount to about 12 days of supply. He argues Apple's legendary just-in-time supply chain, which Tim Cook designed to minimize inventory, makes it structurally impossible to stockpile enough to meaningfully 'beat' tariffs. The piece concludes that Apple's enormous scale — 50 tons of iPhones sold daily in the US alone — makes any pre-tariff shipping a short-term hedge at best.
posts.key_insight: Apple's celebrated just-in-time supply chain, designed to hold minimal inventory, is precisely what makes it impossible to stockpile enough products to meaningfully outrun sustained tariffs.

topQuotes.quote: Apple's entire supply chain is rightly heralded as genius, a triumph of everything from component sourcing to assembly to transport. But the entire operation is premised on the continuous free flow of packaged products out of China.
Inventory, Cook has said, is 'fundamentally evil,' and he has been known to observe that it declines in value by 1% to 2% a week in normal times, faster in tough times like the present.

350,000 iPhones packed onto a single plane is a lot of iPhones. Sending a few million units across a dozen (or more!) planes is, quite literally, tons of iPhones. But Apple sells about 50 tons of new iPhones in the US alone every day.

posts.title: WebKit Adds Support for ‘text-wrap: pretty’, Now Shipping in Safari Technology Preview
posts.summary: Gruber examines WebKit's new text-wrap: pretty CSS feature, now available in Safari Technology Preview 216, which uses a multiline algorithm to improve text wrapping on the web. He's turned it on at Daring Fireball as an experiment but has mixed feelings. While individual paragraphs look better with more uniform line widths, the algorithm creates jarring width disparities between consecutive paragraphs, which he finds distracting. He suspects the algorithm works better with wider column widths and suggests it needs tweaking for narrow columns like those on phones. Despite his reservations, he praises the WebKit team for tackling a problem that has left web typography crude for over 30 years.
posts.key_insight: WebKit's new multiline text-wrapping algorithm improves individual paragraph typography but introduces a new problem of inconsistent paragraph widths that becomes distracting in actual reading flow.

topQuotes.quote: Look at me, fast adopter of novel CSS features.
Line-to-line the new algorithm looks better, but paragraph-to-paragraph I think it looks worse.
But you don't take paragraphs on their own when reading.

posts.title: Why we won't hire a junior with five years of experience
posts.summary: DHH explains why 37signals is hiring a junior programmer at $145,849 — a salary high enough to attract experienced developers — but strictly limiting applicants to those with less than three years of experience. The company is hiring for 'peak promise' rather than existing knowledge, arguing that a programmer with five years of experience has already revealed their trajectory. Junior developers represent untapped potential whose growth curve is still unknown and could be steep. He compares it to NFL scouting, where teams pay premiums for rookies who might become all-stars rather than veterans whose ceiling is known.
posts.key_insight: Hiring juniors at premium salaries is a deliberate bet on unrevealed potential — experienced programmers have already shown their ceiling, but fresh talent's growth trajectory is still unwritten.

topQuotes.quote: We're hiring for peak promise — and such promise only remains until it's revealed.
A programmer who's been working professionally for five years has likely already revealed their potential. What you're going to get is roughly what you see.
The trajectory by which they improve has already been plotted.

posts.title: Universal Basic Dead End
posts.summary: DHH argues that universal basic income is a dead end because work provides not just income but existential purpose. He contends that most people, especially men, deteriorate psychologically without the external structure and validation that employment provides. Drawing on Frankl, Dostoevsky, and Orwell, he suggests that humans are poorly built for idle leisure and that the intellectual fantasy of liberated artists freed from capitalism ignores how most people actually respond to joblessness. While acknowledging that AI may eliminate jobs as inevitably as previous revolutions, he warns that replacing work's 'why' will be harder than building the AI itself.
posts.key_insight: Universal basic income fails not as economics but as existential policy — most people need work's prepackaged purpose, and eliminating it without a replacement 'why' is a civilizational dead end.

topQuotes.quote: It's a great irony that the more comfortable and frictionless our existence becomes, the harder we struggle with the 'the problem of being'.
While the masses can easily identify the pathology of that when it comes to the idle rich, and especially their stereotyped trust-fund offspring, they still crave it for themselves.
Musk's craving for the constant chaos of grand gestures is Dostoevsky's prediction underwritten by the wealth of the world's richest man.

posts.title: American Dynamism
posts.summary: Stancil argues that the current market shock and policy chaos expose how much startup creation depends on stability and personal security, not just founder bravado. Silicon Valley’s power is making risk feel safe, and when that safety net frays, the advice to “keep building” rings hollow even for capable, experienced founders.
posts.key_insight: Startup formation is less about romantic risk-taking and more about the presence of economic and personal safety nets, which falter in times of macro turmoil.

topQuotes.quote: I didn’t work on Mode to chase a dream; I did it because it felt safe.
its real power is in how it makes acting like a crazy misfit feel safe.
They aren’t going all in, or going for broke; on the contrary, they’re doing it because they can afford the risk.

posts.title: Social Media Engagement in Early 2025
posts.summary: Martin Fowler analyzes engagement data from 24 recent social media posts across LinkedIn, X (Twitter), Bluesky, and Mastodon to understand which platforms matter most for announcing new articles. Despite X having the most followers, LinkedIn generates more likes and comparable reposts. Bluesky performs surprisingly well relative to its smaller follower base, while Mastodon has low engagement but higher quality replies. Traffic source data shows LinkedIn has surged past X to become the #3 referrer to his site, though social media overall accounts for only a small fraction of his traffic, with 80% going to articles over six months old.
posts.key_insight: LinkedIn has emerged as the most effective social platform for technical content promotion, but social media overall remains a minor traffic driver compared to search and direct visits.

topQuotes.quote: Since the Muskover, Twitter's importance has declined sharply.
I see more inane and downright mean replies on Bluesky than I ever got on Twitter. In contrast, while Mastodon replies are much fewer, they far more likely to be worth reading.
I strongly suspect that many of my followers there are inactive (or bots).

posts.title: A Firefox addon for putting prices into perspective
posts.summary: DeVault introduces Price Perspective, a Firefox addon he built over a weekend that helps users understand purchasing power differences across incomes and countries. The addon lets you see how prices look relative to different economic perspectives — from someone in Afghanistan to a billionaire like Zuckerberg. Through examples comparing pizza delivery costs and Amsterdam real estate, he illustrates the staggering scale of global wealth inequality. The post uses a lighthearted, show-and-tell format but carries a sharp undercurrent of criticism about wealth concentration.
posts.key_insight: A simple tool for reframing prices across incomes reveals that the scale of global wealth inequality is so extreme it defies intuition.

topQuotes.quote: Yep. That level of wealth inequality is a sign of a totally normal, healthy, well-functioning society.
Zuckerberg over-bidding this apartment to the tune of €6.5B would cost him a proportion of his annual income which is comparable to me buying it for €5,000.
Have you ever bought something, considered the price, and wondered how that price would look to someone else?

posts.title: Tired: Irresistable Force vs. Immovable Object — Wired: Unchanging Prices vs. Nonsensical Tariffs
posts.summary: Gruber examines how Trump's sweeping tariffs, particularly the 54% levy on Chinese imports, clash with Apple's deeply ingrained practice of keeping retail prices stable across product generations. He illustrates Apple's pricing philosophy with examples like the trashcan Mac Pro maintaining its $2,999 price for years without updates, and the current M2 Ultra Mac Pro still at $6,999 even as newer M3 Ultra Mac Studios exist at $3,999. Apple's stock dropped 16.6% in 48 hours as markets reacted to the tariff announcements. Gruber predicts Apple will absorb the tariff costs rather than raise prices, but notes this would mean breaking even or losing money on every iPhone sold. The fundamental tension is between Apple's brand-defining price stability and the unpredictable, nonsensical nature of the tariffs.
posts.key_insight: Apple's near-religious commitment to price stability across product generations creates a uniquely painful collision with unpredictable tariffs, forcing them to choose between brand identity and profitability.

topQuotes.quote: In addition to being nonsensical, the whole thing is entirely unpredictable, which is not at all compatible with the way Apple has set retail prices for decades.
The erratic, illogical, nonsensical nature of Trump's tariffs is bad for everyone.
It's particularly troublesome for a company that sees retail price stability as part of the branding for its products.

posts.title: Raising $110 Million for Open-Source Data Analytics
posts.summary: In this DealMakers interview, Wes McKinney recounts his path from math competitions to building pandas at AQR as an unsanctioned skunkworks project that ultimately helped drive Python’s dominance in data science. He explains the case for open-sourcing pandas, emphasizing the benefits of external contributions and long-term ecosystem growth. The conversation traces his startup journey through Datapad, the creation of Apache Arrow, and the formation of Voltron Data, highlighting how market evidence and conviction shaped his decisions. He describes stepping back from an operational role as Voltron scaled and the macro environment shifted, to focus on a broader portfolio and investing. He closes with investing and founder advice centered on composability, technical leadership, and long-term integrity in relationships.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable impact in data infrastructure comes from betting on open, composable systems, validating real market need, and pairing technical vision with long-term integrity.

topQuotes.quote: Without pandas and the other projects that happened at the same time, it's unclear that Python would be where it is today as the number-one language of AI and data science.
It was totally a skunkworks project. It wasn't really sanctioned, authorized, or planned by company management.
The big lesson was that, first, I needed to collect evidence that there was a need in the market—that there was a product to be created and that a startup was actually needed.

posts.title: Gamer Games for Non-Gamers
posts.summary: Hillel Wayne offers a guide to video games for non-gamers, noting that despite gaming being a $500 billion industry, many people have never played because games assume existing 'vocabulary' of conventions. He proposes four criteria for good introductory games: no special hardware required, no assumed gaming knowledge, cultural significance, and personal verification that non-gamers enjoyed them. He then recommends four games—Baba is You (puzzle), Stardew Valley (farming sim), The Case of the Golden Idol (deduction), and Balatro (roguelike)—explaining each game's mechanics, appeal, and place in gaming history.
posts.key_insight: Gaming's accessibility problem isn't about complexity—Stardew Valley is more intricate than many 'complicated' games—but about whether the complexity is introduced in an approachable way that doesn't assume prior gaming literacy.

topQuotes.quote: It would surprise me if a friend has never watched a movie or listened to music, but it's pretty normal for a friend to have never played a video game.
The problem is that games are highly inaccessible. To most people, games are 'big budget games', which are targeted at people who already have played lots of games.
It shows that games don't have to be violent or intense to be good. Exceptional games can be slow-paced, relaxing, and ground themselves in everyday concerns.

posts.title: I'm Leaving Sentry
posts.summary: Ronacher announces his departure from Sentry after a decade, framing it as a hard but necessary transition to a new chapter. He reflects on how Sentry shaped his life and career, from early community ties to scaling a company far beyond his expectations. The post highlights pride in Sentry’s technical growth, open-source commitments, and the people who made it possible. Leaving is emotionally difficult because the company is intertwined with his personal milestones and identity. He concludes with a cautious but determined optimism about starting something new, driven by evolving motivations and a desire to build for a broader audience.
posts.key_insight: Leaving Sentry is a painful but purposeful step toward a new venture shaped by hard-earned experience, evolving values, and a desire for broader impact.

topQuotes.quote: There's a measure of hubris in assuming lightning strikes twice, and as humbling as that realization is, it also makes me that much more determined.
We stayed true to our Open Source principles, even when other options were available.
Sentry has been more than just a job, it has been a defining part of my life.

posts.title: Great AI Steals
posts.summary: DHH argues that AI's creative development follows the same trajectory as human artists and national economies: starting with poor copies, progressing to good copies, then achieving originality. He draws parallels to Japan's and China's economic rise through imitation, and to how artists learn by copying masters. He contends that AI is currently in the 'good copy' phase and that true AI creativity is inevitable. Critics fixating on minor flaws in AI-generated art, like the Ghibli trend, will only accelerate the push toward genuine originality.
posts.key_insight: AI's creative trajectory mirrors how artists and economies have always evolved — from cheap imitation to good imitation to originality — and the leap to genuine AI creativity is now imminent and unstoppable.

topQuotes.quote: AI has sped through the phase of cheap copies. It's now firmly established in the realm of good copies. You're a fool if you don't believe originality is a likely next step.
Stealing good ideas isn't a detour on the way to becoming a master — it's the straight route. And it's nothing to be ashamed of.
Whether we want AI to become truly creative is a fair question — albeit a theoretical or, at best, moral one. Because it's going to happen if it can happen, and it almost certainly can.

posts.title: France Fines Apple $162M for App Tracking Transparency, Taking the Side of Surveillance Advertisers Over Users
posts.summary: Gruber excoriates France's competition authority for fining Apple €150 million over App Tracking Transparency, arguing the decision sides with surveillance advertisers against users. He contends that ATT's simple yes/no prompt actually accomplished what EU privacy laws only pretend to do, as proven by Meta losing billions in ad revenue. The French regulator's complaint that two layers of consent is excessive is, in Gruber's view, really an objection that the effective layer (ATT) should be removed while the ineffective one (EU cookie consent) remains. He frames the fine as the result of advertising industry lobbying, noting that the groups who complained are precisely those whose tracking ATT successfully curtailed. Gruber argues EU privacy regulations bury users in confusion by design, benefiting the ad industry, while ATT's plain-language consent actually empowers users.
posts.key_insight: France fined Apple for building the privacy protection that actually works, at the behest of the advertisers whose tracking it successfully disrupted, while preserving the EU's own ineffective privacy theater.

topQuotes.quote: This fine boils down to France declaring that Apple shouldn't have actually done what the EU was pretending to do.

It's like a consortium of sketchy pawn shops complaining to the authorities after a popular retailer successfully cracked down on an organized shoplifting/pickpocketing ring, and the authorities then fining the retailer for the damage to the pawnbrokers' business fencing stolen goods — and for exposing the police as ineffective.

What the Autorité de la Concurrence is saying, in so many words, is that two layers of consent is too much, and the only one that's necessary is the one that advertising lobbying groups don't object to, not the one they do (but which users understand and like).

posts.title: I've been kidnapped by Robert Caro
posts.summary: Martin Fowler shares his experience of becoming captivated by Robert Caro's massive biographical works, starting with The Power Broker about Robert Moses and continuing through the multi-volume Lyndon Johnson series. Initially hesitant about the sheer size of these books, Fowler was won over by Caro's exceptional prose and meticulous research. He describes how Caro can make arcane political maneuverings into page-turners and puts readers directly into the human impact of his subjects' actions. As a professional non-fiction writer himself, Fowler admits Caro's work humbles him and shows how far he has to go toward mastery of his craft.
posts.key_insight: Truly exceptional non-fiction writing can transform any subject matter into compelling narrative, making even 1200-page books impossible to put down.

topQuotes.quote: Now Lyndon Johnson is a fascinating character, and I'd like to read more about him, but nobody is worth four-plus volumes.
He can take the arcane maneuverings of 1920's New York legislature and make it a page-turner.
I'm a professional writer of non-fiction, but reading this book reminds me how far I have yet to advance to consider myself anywhere close to mastery of my profession.

posts.title: ‘The Studio’ Is Fantastic So Far
posts.summary: Gruber enthusiastically recommends Apple TV+'s new series 'The Studio,' starring Seth Rogen, calling it fantastic after its first two episodes. He draws comparisons to Robert Altman's 'The Player,' noting both works are themselves intricate cinematic art about the struggle to create art within corporate Hollywood. He uses the show as a springboard to theorize that Apple's motivation for original content is a genuine belief that great cinema needs corporate champions willing to invest in art. He argues Apple TV+ has the highest hit rate for quality shows among all streamers, even if their movies lag behind.
posts.key_insight: The Studio exemplifies why Apple TV+ exists: Apple sees itself as an obligated champion of cinematic art in an era when other studios have abandoned that mission.

topQuotes.quote: No streamer has a higher hit rate for quality shows.
Netflix almost never makes shows like this. Neither does HBO now that's its been subsumed by 'Max'. Peacock et al don't even try.
In a media landscape where such champions of cinema-as-art and art-as-an-essential-public-good are fewer and fewer, it is Apple's not just opportunity but *obligation* to step up to the plate.

posts.title: The Convergence - Shifting Left AND Right
posts.summary: Data engineers are not just 'shifting left' toward closer integration with upstream data producers, but also 'shifting right' into ML/AI work like building AI-powered apps and agentic workflows. This dual movement signals 'The Convergence' - the inevitable blurring and merging of software engineering, data engineering, and ML/AI roles into unified, customer-focused product delivery.
posts.key_insight: The future of data engineering lies not in specialization but in convergence - as data engineers shift both left toward producers and right toward AI/ML applications, the boundaries between software, data, and ML engineering roles will dissolve into unified, customer-focused product teams.

topQuotes.quote: In the rare cases where data teams demonstrate 'business value,' they're lauded as heroes, invited to reveal their secrets to eager audiences on podcasts, guest articles, and keynoting data conferences around the globe. 'Oh wow, you worked with the business and delivered something of value?! Holy shit! That's amazing. Tell us more!'
Shift Left AND Shift Right inevitably means the old days of being singly focused will inevitably disappear and the siloes between software, data, and ML/AI blur and eventually merge.
Shifting Left is about delivering a quality product - using data - to satisfy a customer.

posts.title: The Year on Linux
posts.summary: DHH reflects on his first year using Linux, Neovim, and a Framework laptop after leaving Apple's ecosystem. He describes how the transition was difficult for the first couple of weeks but quickly became second nature. Rather than evangelizing Linux, he takes a deliberately modest stance, acknowledging that most developers may not have the will or desire to switch. He frames the move as a personal choice driven by curiosity and the joy of learning, comparing it to his discovery of Ruby.
posts.key_insight: The best technology advocacy isn't persuasion but demonstration — lead by example, stay humble about your choices, and let others find their own path.

topQuotes.quote: I don't know if most developers are better off on Linux. I mean, I believe they are, at some utopian level, especially if they work for the web, using open source tooling. But I don't know if they are as humans with limited will or capacity for change.
It's a dead end trying to convince anyone of an alternative with the sharp end of a religious argument.
Humans are rigid in the short term, but flexible in the long term. Blessed are the few who can retain the grit to push through that early mental resistance and reach new maxima.

posts.title: Live Like Youre Dying
posts.summary: Stancil argues that startups are defined less by raw workload than by the gnawing uncertainty of tenure—an endless sprint justified by the hope that the finish line is just around the corner. By contrast, the Harris campaign’s fixed end date rewired priorities, speed, and morale: a hard deadline made extreme effort sustainable, forced real commitment, and exposed how much “forever” thinking paralyzes decision‑making and life.
posts.key_insight: A real, irreversible deadline transforms how people work and decide, revealing that perpetual uncertainty—not effort itself—is the most exhausting tax of startup life.

topQuotes.quote: Uncertainty, it turns out, wears you down much faster than effort.
You really can do anything for two weeks, or even 84 days, if you know that there’s no hope that you’ll only have to do it for 83 days, and no chance you might have to do it for 85.
You think startups move fast? You have no idea how fast you can actually go.

posts.title: Vector Technologies for AI: Extending Your Existing Data Stack
posts.summary: The article distinguishes between vector engines (like DuckDB) that use vectorized execution for analytical processing and vector databases (like Pinecone, Weaviate) designed specifically for AI embedding storage and similarity search. Späti argues strongly against building parallel data infrastructure for AI workloads, advocating instead for integrating vector capabilities into existing data engineering stacks. He examines how traditional databases are adding vector extensions and questions whether dedicated vector databases will remain necessary long-term. The piece concludes by positioning DuckDB as a practical bridge between data engineering and AI workflows, offering vector capabilities without requiring separate infrastructure.
posts.key_insight: Rather than building parallel infrastructure for AI workloads, data engineers should integrate vector capabilities into their existing data platforms to avoid technical debt and leverage established expertise.

topQuotes.quote: What began as an abstraction layer for LLM applications has, for many teams, become a redundant orchestration tool that sounds and feels a lot like an ETL tool or orchestrator that teams already have in place.
As someone with two decades working with data, this lesson stands out above all others—avoid creating parallel systems for what could be extensions of existing ones.
The key is to not repeat ourselves.

posts.title: Singularity & Serenity
posts.summary: DHH reflects on the approaching AI singularity and argues that rather than fearing the unpredictable future it brings, we should embrace it with philosophical serenity. He traces the concept from von Neumann to today's rapid AI advances, noting that recent progress in image generation, robotics, and language models has made even skeptics take notice. He compares this moment to past paradigm shifts like the agricultural and industrial revolutions. Drawing on Christianity's Serenity Prayer, Stoic philosophy, and Buddhism, he argues that all great intellectual traditions converge on the same insight: let go of what you cannot control. He concludes that since nobody can pause or redirect AI progress, the wisest response is amor fati — loving whatever fate brings.
posts.key_insight: The wisest response to AI's unpredictable trajectory is not fear or false certainty but philosophical acceptance — embracing the uncontrollable future as the great traditions have always taught.

topQuotes.quote: This is all being driven by a million independent agents chasing irresistible incentives. There's no pause button, let alone an off-ramp.
That's the marvel and the danger of progress: nobody is actually in charge!
What good is it to fret about a future you don't control anyway?

posts.title: Rust Any Part 3: Finally we have Upcasts
posts.summary: Ronacher revisits his 2022 “As-Any Hack,” which worked around Rust’s lack of supertrait upcasting for `dyn DebugAny`. The core pain was that a `dyn DebugAny` couldn’t be treated as `dyn Any`, so `downcast_ref` and related `Any` APIs failed at compile time. He reports that Rust 1.86 finally fixes this, enabling the cast and making those downcasts work on trait objects that inherit `Any`. The fix is currently in beta, with stable imminent, which means old workaround patterns can be retired once MSRV advances. He closes with a thanks to contributors and provides an updated extension map example that no longer needs the hack.
posts.key_insight: Rust 1.86 finally enables trait-object upcasting to supertraits like `Any`, removing long-standing hacks and restoring ergonomic downcasting.

topQuotes.quote: The problem? Even though `DebugAny` inherits from `Any`, Rust wouldn't let you use methods from `Any` on a `dyn DebugAny`.
But there is good news! As of Rust 1.86, this is finally fixed.
That means a lot of old hacks can finally be retired.

posts.title: The Cynic Wasps in the Beehive
posts.summary: The post critiques Curtis Yarvin's dark enlightenment ideology, which frames monarchical and hierarchical power structures as natural and inevitable. Using the metaphor of the 'Queen Bee' misnomer, the author argues that Yarvin employs clever linguistic framing to obscure the viability of decentralized governance, and draws on archaeological evidence from Graeber and Wengrow's 'Dawn of Everything' to demonstrate that egalitarian complex societies have existed throughout history.
posts.key_insight: Archaeological and anthropological evidence demonstrates that hierarchical power structures are neither natural nor inevitable, and ideologies like Yarvin's that claim otherwise use linguistic manipulation to justify accelerating inequality.

topQuotes.quote: Just as the misleading term 'Queen Bee' obscures the decentralized reality of beehive governance, an obscure 'thinker' called Curtis Yarvin, similarly uses clever linguistic framing to make hierarchical power structures appear natural and inevitable, when they are anything but.
We live in a beehive and let the cynic wasps in.

Instead of criticizing this reality, Yarvin praises it. His fundamental argument is democratic, decentralized, institutions are ineffective, claiming we would be better governed by a monarchy-style system where power is concentrated in a single authority—similar to how corporations operate.

posts.title: Using linkhut to signal-boost my bookmarks
posts.summary: DeVault describes his discovery and rediscovery of linkhut, a social bookmarking tool hosted on SourceHut. After rethinking his relationship with social media and quietly leaving both Mastodon and BlueSky, he found renewed appeal in linkhut as a way to signal-boost interesting articles via RSS feeds. He draws an analogy to SoundCloud's music discovery feature, where you could see what artists you admired were listening to. He encourages others to sign up and share their feeds with him.
posts.key_insight: Social bookmarking with RSS feeds offers a quieter, more intentional alternative to social media for sharing and discovering interesting content.

topQuotes.quote: Then I realized that I also felt that way about Mastodon, and that was the end of that.

When I set out to evaluate BlueSky for its merits from an OSS framing (findings: both surprisingly open and not open enough), I also took a moment to evaluate the social experience – and found it wanting.
I've been rethinking my relationship social media lately, as some may have inferred from my unannounced disappearance from Mastodon.

posts.title: Introducing: Antimemetics (my new book!)
posts.summary: Nadia Asparouhova announces her new book 'Antimemetics: Why Some Ideas Resist Spreading,' inspired by qntm's science fiction novel 'There Is No Antimemetics Division.' She defines antimemes as self-censoring ideas including taboos, cognitive biases, and memory lapses that resist being shared despite their importance. The book examines how the social web is shifting from public virality toward private and semi-private spaces like group chats and DMs, where the most interesting ideas now circulate. She frames this as a nonfiction treatment of antimemetics grounded in real-world phenomena, connecting it to her previous work on open source sustainability. The book is published through the Dark Forest Collective, which she sees as fitting given that antimemes would be crushed by conventional publishing's virality-driven approach. She positions the work as a sequel of sorts to 'Working in Public,' arguing that the withdrawal from public spaces she observed among open source developers has now become universal.
posts.key_insight: The rapid spread of memes has paradoxically created a shadow ecosystem of antimemes—ideas that resist sharing—driving the most interesting discourse underground into private spaces, and we lack the vocabulary to understand this shift.

topQuotes.quote: Memes may dominate how we communicate online, but in the last few years, their rapid spread has also necessitated the birth of a parallel, shadow ecosystem, where the best ideas are paradoxically harder to find.
It's much more comfortable to live in our group chats and DMs, but doesn't it feel a little strange to watch the world burn from the safety of our miniaturized abodes?

Antimemes are delicate and messy and hard to look at; they would be swiftly crushed in the hands of a publisher trying to juice it up and make it 'go viral,' like a flower ground under the heel of a shoe.

posts.title: It's five grand a day to miss our S3 exit
posts.summary: DHH details the final phase of 37signals' cloud exit: migrating nearly six petabytes of files from AWS S3 to on-premise Pure Storage hardware. They're currently spending $1.5 million per year on S3 under a four-year contract expiring June 30, and missing that deadline would cost $5,000 per day. The replacement infrastructure—18 petabytes of NVMe storage across two data centers—is already racked and ready. Over five years, the move will save roughly $5 million compared to continued S3 costs, with hardware and support totaling about $2.5 million. The post frames the migration as a concrete, deadline-driven operation with real financial stakes.
posts.key_insight: The cloud exit's final S3 migration has a hard deadline with a $5,000/day penalty for delay, but the five-year savings of nearly $5 million against $2.5 million in hardware costs make the urgency entirely justified.

topQuotes.quote: In an earlier age, that egress alone would have cost hundreds of thousands of dollars in fees alone. But now AWS offers a free 60-day egress window for anyone who wants to leave, so that drops the cost to $0.
A combined 18 petabytes, securely replicated a thousand miles apart. It's a gorgeous rack full of blazing-fast NVMe storage modules.
The idea of paying $5,000/day, if we miss our departure date, is awfully concrete in comparison.

posts.title: The Website That Hacker News Is Afraid to Discuss (You’re Reading It)
posts.summary: Gruber documents and quantifies the long-running suppression of Daring Fireball articles on Hacker News. Using a new tool by Michael Lynch that ranks personal blogs by HN popularity, he shows DF ranked #2-3 historically but has plummeted to #72 in the last four years. He traces the pattern from the mid-2010s, when DF articles began mysteriously disappearing from the HN front page after gaining traction, to the present day where they almost never surface at all. He notes occasional exceptions that make the suppression more puzzling, and concludes that the unexplained nature of the blacklisting is what truly bothers him, calling it cowardly rather than censorial.
posts.key_insight: Daring Fireball's fall from the #2 most popular personal blog on Hacker News to #72 over four years, despite no obvious decline in quality, points to systematic unexplained suppression that HN has never acknowledged.

topQuotes.quote: What bothers me isn't so much that Daring Fireball is shitlisted at Hacker News — even though I really did enjoy reading the commentary on my posts back when they regularly surfaced there. What bothers me is that it's unexplained. Which, ultimately, seems not so much censorial as just cowardly.
You'd think there'd be a certain kinship between decades-old websites, typeset in small-point Verdana, which stubbornly refuse to update their general layout and design.

Maybe I've lost my fastball, and I just don't write so good no more. Or maybe it's not me, but the Hacker News audience that has changed in recent years. But it seems to me there's something fishy going on.

posts.title: To hell with forever
posts.summary: DHH argues that immortality is a curse, not a gift, and that the finitude of life is what gives it beauty and meaning. Drawing on cultural touchstones like Highlander, Queen, and Björk, he makes the case that satiation applies to life itself just as it does to individual pleasures. He reflects on passing the halfway point of his own life expectancy and finds comfort rather than dread in eventual endings — of racing, coding, and life itself. He concludes with the Stoic idea of holding life with a loose grip, embracing both fate and death.
posts.key_insight: Life's finitude is not a tragedy to be overcome but a mercy that gives meaning to every experience, and the wisest response is to hold it all with a loose grip.

topQuotes.quote: It's funny, people seem to have no problem understanding satiation when it comes to the individual parts of life. Enough delicious cake, no more rides on the rollercoaster, the end of a great party. But not life itself.
One day I will write the last line of Ruby code, too. Sell the last subscription. Write the last tweet. How merciful.
Immortality always sounded like a curse to me.

posts.title: It Might Be Time for Me to Collect Some Being Right Points for My 2023 Bluesky Prediction
posts.summary: Gruber revisits his May 2023 prediction that Bluesky would replace Twitter and achieve mainstream popularity, arguing that events have vindicated him. He contrasts Bluesky's intuitive, Twitter-like simplicity with Mastodon's nerd-friendly but confusing design, and Threads' soulless blandness. He reflects on the post-Twitter diaspora across Mastodon, Bluesky, Threads, and X, noting he spends less total time on social media now. Gruber argues Bluesky has become what Twitter once aspired to be — user-controlled, vibrant, and approachable — while acknowledging Mastodon remains his personal favorite for signal-to-noise ratio.
posts.key_insight: Bluesky succeeded where Mastodon couldn't because it prioritized being simple and familiar over being technically pure, proving that mainstream social platforms win by putting user experience first.

topQuotes.quote: It's a platform without a soul. It aspires to be anodyne, which is very different than empowering users not to feel like they've got to dodge a never-ending barrage of turds being thrown by the angry chaos monkeys who've overrun X.
Shitbirds get blocked and added to blocklists, not elevated to the top of reply threads because they paid for a blue-check power-up.
What makes Mastodon good for us nerds is that all the non-nerds aren't here.

posts.title: The Tragic Case of Intel AI
posts.summary: Intel is sitting on thousands of unsellable AI accelerators (Gaudi 2 and Data Center GPU Max 1450) due to fundamental software and hardware design problems. The Gaudi architecture is nearly impossible to program because its compute units aren't controllable by programmable cores, making it only useful for hand-coded workloads. The Max 1450 GPUs require exotic infrastructure to even plug in, and Intel's negotiations to sell them were paralyzed by committee leadership that couldn't make decisions. Hotz argues that Intel's AI roadmap is effectively dead, with multiple successor chips cancelled and no credible path forward, contrasting this with AMD's slow-but-steady progress under decisive leadership.
posts.key_insight: Intel's AI hardware isn't failing because of bad chips alone, but because committee-style leadership creates organizational paralysis where nobody can say yes to deals, investments, or strategic pivots - a death spiral for a company that needs radical change to compete.

topQuotes.quote: The problem with this is there's nobody who can say yes, just many people who can say no.
I wasn't able to find an individual with the power to make a deal, never mind actually make a deal. It's unclear any individual in the company has that power.
No point even bothering to listen to their roadmaps anymore. They have squandered all their credibility.

posts.title: Another observability 3.0 appears on the horizon
posts.summary: Charity Majors responds to Matt Klein's claim that bitdrift's ring buffer model deserves the 'Observability 3.0' label, while reflecting on the industry's shift from multiple pillars to unified storage models. She argues that while Klein raises valid points about cost control, his mobile-first approach of not sending telemetry by default doesn't translate well to complex, multi-tenant distributed systems that require real-time debugging. The industry is moving toward a data lake model with telemetry pipelines, tiered storage, and tail-based sampling as the next evolution of cost control.
posts.key_insight: The observability industry is converging on unified storage models with telemetry pipelines and tail-based sampling as cost control levers, but the real value lies in workflows and context, not just reducing spend.

topQuotes.quote: The fatal flaw of the multiple pillars model is that your levers of control consist of deleting your most valuable data: context and detail.
