posts.key_insight: Google and Apple's decision to show 'Gulf of America' in parentheses to the entire world outside Mexico reveals that fear of political retaliation, not cartographic integrity or controversy-minimization, is now driving how major tech companies respond to authoritarian edicts from the US government.

topQuotes.quote: Shakespeare would have us believe that a sea by any other name would taste as salty. Trump doesn't read Shakespeare.

Trump has a lifelong history of putting his name on buildings he doesn't own. He'd rather have his name emblazoned on a building he doesn't own than own a building that doesn't bear his name. To Trump, the name on the sign is more important than the deed.
Wrapping it in parentheses is like wrapping a dead fish in newspaper — it doesn't contain the stench.

posts.title: Unifying AI and Product Development
posts.summary: Reis highlights the persistent disconnect between Product teams and engineering/data teams, noting different expectations, vocabulary, and timelines create confusion and frustration. He proposes exploring ways to unify these two universes, pointing to a conversation with colleagues who wrote about integrating AI and product development cycles.
posts.key_insight: The gap between product vision and technical execution remains a fundamental organizational challenge that could be addressed by unifying AI and product development approaches.

topQuotes.quote: Something that's been on my mind for a while is the disconnect between 'Product' and those responsible for delivering on the product vision, usually engineering and data/ML teams.
Different expectations, vocabulary, and timelines, among other ailments. It's confusing and frustrating.
What if we could unify these two universes?

posts.title: Europeans don't have or understand free speech
posts.summary: DHH endorses JD Vance's Munich Security Conference speech criticizing Europe's lack of genuine free speech protections and its mass immigration policies. He argues that Europeans fundamentally misunderstand free speech, believing they have American-style protections when laws across the UK, Germany, and Denmark criminalize various forms of expression. He connects suppressed speech to Europe's immigration crisis, arguing that a narrow Overton window prevented honest debate about the downsides of mass immigration for decades. He contrasts Denmark's earlier willingness to question immigration with Sweden's sustained suppression of debate, which he blames for Sweden's current violence problems.
posts.key_insight: Europe's lack of genuine free speech protections created a fatally narrow Overton window that prevented honest debate about mass immigration, leading to the crisis now engulfing countries like Sweden.

topQuotes.quote: It's quite like how every dictator around the world pretends to believe in democracy. Sure, they may repress the opposition and rig their elections, but they still crave the imprimatur of the concept. So too 'free speech' and the Europeans.
It's the lack of free speech, and a catastrophically narrow Overton window, which has led Europe into such a mess with mass immigration in the first place.
The state of Sweden today is a direct consequence of suppressing any talk of the downsides to mass immigration for decades.

posts.title: Achievement Here And There
posts.summary: The post argues that Silicon Valley’s love of the “pivot” reveals a deeper truth: startups are less about specific missions and more about ambition, status, and building anything people want. That same social scoreboard shapes what counts as impact, which is why attention is now drifting from software to Washington—another pivot that shows achievement is relative and socially constructed.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley’s pivots and shifting obsessions show that achievement is less about fixed missions than about socially rewarded status, which can move from startups to policy on a whim.

topQuotes.quote: That’s why we built 140 characters instead of flying cars, and recreated our parents as apps: Because you can build a company on those ideas, and building a company is what we celebrate.
These things are instead circumstantial vehicles of ambition, the disposable boosters of a hopeful rocket ship.
A spoonful of startup sugar helps the civic medicine go down.

posts.title: Finding Flow: Escaping Digital Distractions Through Deep Work and Slow Living
posts.summary: Simon Späti explores how to achieve flow states and deep work in an age of digital distractions, drawing from personal experience as a self-employed author. He argues that tech companies manipulate us like lab animals through dopamine-driven engagement, while artists who focus on process over reward naturally enter flow states. The post offers practical strategies for creating focus-friendly environments, the importance of breaks and rest, and embracing slow productivity. He advocates for 'deep happiness' from mastery and meaningful work over 'shallow happiness' from quick dopamine hits, ultimately connecting flow to living a deeper, more fulfilling life.
posts.key_insight: True productivity and creativity come not from optimizing for external rewards and constant stimulation, but from designing environments and habits that support flow states and embracing the slow, patient process of deep work.

topQuotes.quote: Aren't they the same as the researcher, feeding us with dopamine to tell us to do what they want?
Unlimited freedom is super-blocking, especially for creativity.
There is no talking block, only writer's block.

posts.title: A holistic perspective on intellectual property, part 1
posts.summary: DeVault begins a multi-part series on intellectual property by first establishing a philosophical foundation for understanding property itself. He argues that property is a social convention, not a natural law, enforced ultimately through force, and justified primarily as a tool for managing scarcity. He then traces the logical progression from material property to immaterial property (like domain names) to intellectual property, noting that intellectual property fundamentally differs because ideas cannot be possessed or withheld by force. He concludes that intellectual property creates artificial scarcity to reward intellectual labor within a capitalist framework, teasing deeper critique in future installments.
posts.key_insight: Intellectual property is a social invention that creates artificial scarcity for inherently non-scarce ideas, and understanding this requires first recognizing that all property is a social convention enforced by force rather than a law of nature.

topQuotes.quote: We see the bounty of the natural world and impose upon it these imagined rights and privileges, divvy it up and hand it out and hoard it, and resort to cruelty if anyone steps out of line.

It is important to note that the very possibility that one could illicitly assume possession of an object is a strong indication that "property" is a social convention, rather than a law of nature; one cannot defy the law of gravity in the same way as one can defy property.
Our modern conception of property has the unintended consequence of creating scarcity where there is none by denying the use of idle screwdrivers where they are needed.

posts.title: How to add a directory to your PATH
posts.summary: Julia Evans writes a comprehensive guide to adding a directory to your PATH, a task that seems obvious to experienced terminal users but has many undocumented gotchas. She walks through identifying your shell, finding the correct config file, determining which directory to add, and restarting the shell. The post also covers common problems like running the wrong program version, duplicate PATH entries, and losing shell history when reloading config. She includes shell-specific syntax for bash, zsh, and fish, and discusses tools like cargo and Homebrew that use source scripts instead of explicit PATH additions.
posts.key_insight: Adding a directory to your PATH involves multiple shell-specific decisions and subtle gotchas that are rarely documented in one place, making a seemingly simple task genuinely confusing for newcomers.

topQuotes.quote: There are a lot of elaborate flow charts out there that explain how bash decides which config file to use but IMO it's not worth it to internalize them and just testing is the fastest way to be sure.
How do you find what directory http-server is in? Honestly in general this is not that easy – often the answer is something like 'it depends on how npm is configured'.

Sometimes fish_add_path will update the PATH for every session in the future (with a 'universal variable') and sometimes it will update the PATH just for the current session and it's hard for me to tell which one it will do.

posts.title: Epsio, IVMs, and Differential Dataflow With Gilad Kleinman
posts.summary: This interview with Epsio CEO Gilad Kleinman explores incremental view maintenance (IVM) and streaming SQL technologies. Kleinman explains how streaming SQL differs from traditional batch processing by incrementally updating query results rather than reprocessing entire datasets. Epsio's approach is distinctive in that it connects to existing PostgreSQL/MySQL databases via CDC, processes changes externally, and writes materialized results back to the source database. The conversation covers how IVM fits into the broader landscape of streaming databases versus stream processors, the technical approaches behind differential dataflow and DBSP, and why IVM is expanding beyond traditional streaming use cases into customer-facing analytics and real-time reporting.
posts.key_insight: Incremental view maintenance is evolving from niche streaming use cases to become a fundamental approach for delivering fast, cost-effective analytics by building query-specific data structures rather than optimizing for generic query patterns.

topQuotes.quote: I believe IVMs offer a pretty fundamental change in how organizations can work with their data—building data structures around specific queries vs. trying to store data to be 'generically' fast for any query.
No matter how fast or efficient a traditional database is, if you are running a heavy query and most of the dataset hasn't changed since the last run, there is a lot of wasted compute.

Adopting new database technologies is a pretty scary endeavor. We found that asking companies to install a new extension (that could potentially crash) on their production database was a pretty big ask to make.

posts.title: Corporate “DEI” is an imperfect vehicle for deeply meaningful ideals
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that corporate DEI programs were always ineffective symbolic gestures, but the ideals underlying them — inclusion, equity, and counteracting bias — are essential to building genuinely meritocratic, high-performing teams. She contends that real cultural change came from hearts and minds being changed through storytelling and persuasion in the 2010s, not from corporate mandates, and that the best way to spread these values is to build successful companies that embody them.
posts.key_insight: True meritocracy demands active inclusion and bias counteraction — not corporate DEI theater — and the most powerful way to spread these values is to build winning companies that authentically embody them.

topQuotes.quote: That's how you build a real fucking meritocracy. You start with 'do not tolerate the things that kneecap your employees in their pursuit of excellence', and ESPECIALLY not the things that subject them to the compounding tax of being targeted for who they are.
If you want your values and ideals to spread throughout the industry, the most impactful thing you can possibly do is win.
When I look at the long list of companies who say they are rolling back mentions to DEI internally, I don't get that depressed. I see a long list of companies who never really meant it anyway.

posts.title: Fox’s New Scorebug Graphic Design, and Our Innate Resistance to Change
posts.summary: Gruber analyzes Fox's completely redesigned scorebug debuted during Super Bowl 59, arguing that despite near-universal negative reactions, the new design is objectively superior. He breaks down specific design choices — huge team initialisms with color-coded boxes replacing logos, elimination of decorative chrome, and bold typography — explaining why each improves clarity. He draws a parallel to the original 1994 'Fox Box' innovation, which was also widely panned but became the universal standard for sports broadcasting. The piece uses this as a launching point to examine humanity's innate resistance to change, arguing that visceral negative reactions to visual changes are evolutionary instincts, not design critiques.
posts.key_insight: Visceral negative reactions to design changes are evolutionary instincts masquerading as aesthetic judgments — the same resistance that condemned the original on-screen scorebug in 1994 is now condemning its bold redesign.

topQuotes.quote: Different doesn't always mean better. But better necessarily implies different.
"Looks like something out of Fortnite" isn't the dis the last commenter thinks it is. A lot of people play and love Fortnite.
Who seems stupid now? Imagine the outcry if Fox had broadcast yesterday's Super Bowl without any scorebug at all.

posts.title: Nike’s ‘So Win’ Won the Super Bowl
posts.summary: Gruber reviews the Super Bowl LIX commercials, declaring Nike's 'So Win' spot the clear winner. He laments that most Super Bowl ads are offensively bland despite their enormous cost, failing to be memorable or meaningful. Nike's black-and-white celebration of women's sports, featuring Caitlin Clark and narrated by Doechii over Led Zeppelin, struck him as a brand-reinvigorating manifesto comparable to Apple's 'Think Different' campaign. He gives honorable mentions to ChatGPT's technology-history branding spot and Stella Artois's David Beckham twin comedy, while singling out Tubi's grotesque cowboy-hat-head ad as a dishonorable mention.
posts.key_insight: Nike's 'So Win' ad stood out from the Super Bowl's sea of expensive blandness by actually having something to say — a genuine manifesto about women's sports that reinvigorated the brand.

topQuotes.quote: The commercial time is famously expensive — Fox was supposedly selling 30-second spots for last night's game for $8 million — but the sponsors who buy that time tend to squander it with absolute mindless pap.
The message I took was that Tubi is the streaming app that will make you feel grossed out.
The problem with modern Super Bowl commercials is they're bland. Offensively bland.

posts.title: Bank CEO: Retract your debanking piece? Me: No.
posts.summary: Patrick McKenzie responds to a retraction request from Anchorage Digital CEO Nathan McCauley regarding McKenzie's earlier essay on debanking. McKenzie methodically dismantles each of McCauley's objections—regarding Silvergate's solvency, omission of regulatory guidance, and the definition of debanking—finding that none identify actual factual errors. The piece doubles as a meditation on the power dynamics between banks and writers, arguing that retraction demands from well-resourced financial institutions carry implicit legal threats that chill speech. McKenzie also highlights the irony that McCauley's own bank signed a consent order with the OCC for BSA/AML deficiencies while criticizing McKenzie's analysis of compliance failures at other crypto banks. The essay concludes by contextualizing Anchorage Digital as a trust company that cannot take deposits, and situating the retraction demand within the crypto industry's broader pattern of preferring to be judged on potential rather than track record.
posts.key_insight: A retraction demand from a bank CEO that fails to identify a single factual error reveals more about the crypto industry's desire to suppress unfavorable analysis than about any deficiency in that analysis.

topQuotes.quote: I have never signed a consent order with the OCC regarding deficiencies in my bank's BSA/AML program, however.
If one's bank does not employ an individual who understands the above paragraph, Anthropic will sell you artificial cognition for $20 a month.
Crypto advocates love a bit of risk, and love even more when somebody else has to buy them out of the downside, while they keep the upside.

posts.title: Seeking Purity
posts.summary: The post argues that “purity” in tech—especially Rust’s memory-safety absolutism—often shifts from technical rigor into ideology. Ronacher praises Rust’s innovations but criticizes how method purity can override practical outcomes, creating friction in ecosystems like the Linux kernel. He parallels this with the Python 2→3 migration, where purist stances dismissed pragmatic dual-support approaches and caused real community damage. Over time, Python 3 improved precisely because messy, incremental reality forced compromises. He concludes that a memory‑safe future is likely, but the path will be slow, impure, and shaped by lived constraints rather than doctrinal purity.
posts.key_insight: Purity-driven ideals can catalyze innovation, but durable progress comes from messy, pragmatic transitions that respect real-world constraints.

topQuotes.quote: The fundamental question of “Is this code memory safe?” has shifted to “Was it made memory safe in the correct way?”.
Not getting behind the Python 3 train was seen as sabotaging the entire project.
The painful and messy transition is here to stay, and that's exactly why, in the end, it works.

posts.title: The Tension of Orthodoxy and Speed
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that the tension between orthodox data modeling approaches and the pressure to 'move fast' creates technical debt that teams rarely pay down. He advocates for understanding conventional frameworks like relational and dimensional modeling before breaking rules, warning that ignorance of orthodoxy leads to either misapplication or costly reinvention.
posts.key_insight: True speed in data work comes from understanding orthodox approaches deeply enough to know when following or breaking them serves your specific situation.

topQuotes.quote: Stubborn table-centric people are the data equivalent of Flat Earthers.
For many teams these days, thinking is often replaced by managing a Jira backlog and slogging through 2-week sprints, all in the name of 'shipping.'
There is never enough time to do it right, but there is always enough time to do it over.

posts.title: Serving the country
posts.summary: DHH draws a historical parallel between WWII production chief William Knudsen and Elon Musk, arguing that Musk's proven track record of radical efficiency gains at SpaceX, X/Twitter, and Tesla makes him uniquely qualified to tackle the US government's two-trillion-dollar budget deficit through DOGE. He acknowledges Musk's erratic personal behavior and controversial tweets but argues his positive contributions to mankind overwhelmingly outweigh the negatives. DHH frames the US fiscal situation as a crisis requiring exactly the kind of ruthless efficiency optimization Musk has demonstrated in the private sector.
posts.key_insight: America's fiscal crisis demands the same kind of radical private-sector efficiency expertise that won WWII's production war, and Musk's track record of dramatic cost-cutting makes him uniquely suited for the job despite his personal flaws.

topQuotes.quote: He's a modern-day Knudsen on steroids (or Ketamine?). Let him cook.
Elon Musk is a modern day William S. Knudsen. Only even more accomplished in efficiency management, factory optimization, and first-order systems thinking.
Who wouldn't want such a capacity involved in finding the waste, sloth, and squander in the US budget? Well, his political enemies, of course!

posts.title: Welcoming Ghostty Subsystem Maintainers
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces eight new subsystem maintainers for Ghostty, the terminal emulator project that went public about a month ago but had a two-year private beta. Rather than appointing general core maintainers, Ghostty follows a subsystem model where contributors become authorities over specific areas like terminal emulation, GTK, fonts, rendering, and shell integration. All new maintainers emerged organically from the private beta community, having contributed dozens of PRs and actively helped others. The role grants autonomy to define roadmaps and merge PRs but explicitly carries no obligation to review anything—it's about trust and authority, not work requirements.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable open source governance comes from matching authority to organic expertise and explicitly removing obligation from volunteer maintainership.

topQuotes.quote: Notably, maintainers have NO OBLIGATION to review pull requests or issues in their subsystem.

This isn't a job! It is a role of trust and authority and the expectation is that maintainers will use their best judgement and work on what they find interesting and rewarding for themselves and the project.
Rather than promoting 'core maintainers' who are responsible for every part of the project, we've identified key subsystems within the project with subject matter experts.

posts.title: For The Brand I Guess
posts.summary: Stancil argues that elite VC firms increasingly prioritize being chosen over being good at picking, and brand has become the dominant lever for winning deals. In a world where reputations are built by viral attention and cultural signaling, firms hire for vibe and notoriety—even when the ethics are messy—because attention itself has become the business.
posts.key_insight: Venture capital has shifted from a quiet capital-allocation game to an attention-and-prestige business where brand, controversy, and cultural cachet drive hiring and deal access.

topQuotes.quote: Now, reputations are created by poasting.
He was hired for the vibe—and today, vibes are business decisions.
The business model is inverted—the media company and popular podcasts aren’t about deal flow; they are the point.

posts.title: Servers can last a long time
posts.summary: DHH reveals that 61 servers purchased in 2015 for Basecamp 3's launch are only now being retired after nearly a decade of service, at a fully amortized cost of just $142/day. The fleet cost about half a million dollars and supported a service that grossed hundreds of millions. He argues that server lifetimes are typically underestimated—their cloud exit accounting assumed five years, but these lasted nearly ten. The database servers will continue serving heritage apps even after the main fleet is refreshed. DHH uses this as further evidence that owning hardware is vastly more economical than cloud rental, potentially saving millions more than initially projected.
posts.key_insight: Server hardware routinely outlasts the conservative five-year depreciation schedules used in cloud-vs-own calculations, making the economics of owning your infrastructure even more favorable than initially projected.

topQuotes.quote: Owning our own iron has been a fantastically profitable proposition. Millions of dollars saved over renting in the cloud.
The dollar you keep at the end of the year is just as real whether you earn it or save it.
So you just might want to run those cloud-exit numbers once more with a longer server lifetime value. It might just tip the equation, and motivate you to become a server owner rather than a renter.

posts.title: My 2024 Apple Report Card
posts.summary: Gruber's 2024 Apple Report Card reveals a year where no category improved — every changed grade went lower. He gives high marks to Mac and iPhone (both A) for sustained excellence, but downgrades Apple Watch, Apple TV, Home, Developer Relations, and Social Impact. His sharpest criticism targets Siri/Apple Intelligence, which he'd grade a near-failing D if it were its own category, arguing Apple shipped features a year too early under competitive pressure. He notes that developer resentment over App Store policies is worsening and hurting VisionOS adoption, and criticizes Tim Cook's political maneuvering around the Trump inauguration as transparently self-serving.
posts.key_insight: Apple's 2024 was a year of maintained but unchallenged excellence across its lineup, with no single area improving — a stagnation made most dangerous by its failure to compete meaningfully in AI and its deteriorating relationship with developers.

topQuotes.quote: That's Tim Cook's hoagie-sized shit sandwich to eat, and it's disrespectful to pitch us on taking a few bites ourselves to help him get through it.
Tim Cook can't donate $1 million to Trump's inauguration slush fund when Apple donated a mere $43,200 to Biden's and expect us to do anything but laugh at the supposed justification.
There are several areas where Apple maintained consistent excellence in 2024, but I can't think of a single area where Apple levelled up. Not one. That feels like a problem.

posts.title: Bootable Mac Startup Drive Backups, and Another Ode to SuperDuper
posts.summary: Gruber examines the state of bootable Mac startup drive backups following a macOS 15.2 bug that broke SuperDuper's cloning ability, which Apple fixed in 15.3. He contrasts SuperDuper developer Dave Nanian's patience waiting for the fix with Carbon Copy Cloner developer Mike Bombich's suggestion that bootable backups were effectively deprecated. Gruber walks through the technical details of how modern Mac backup and recovery works with APFS, Apple Silicon, and macOS Recovery. He ultimately concludes that while he personally no longer needs bootable backups — his own backup drive was running macOS 12.6 on a machine running 15.3 — they remain essential for professionals in high-pressure production environments. The piece is both a practical guide to SuperDuper's backup options and a reflection on how Mac backup needs have evolved over two decades.
posts.key_insight: Modern Mac recovery infrastructure has made bootable backup drives unnecessary for most users, but they remain vital for production environments — and Apple, by fixing the 15.2 bug, showed they still recognize the Mac as a workstation.

topQuotes.quote: One of the ways that APFS is very 'modern Apple' is that it's largely a black box.
For as much as Apple Silicon Macs have become iOS-like devices in many ways, they're still Macs at heart: workstations. There remain people inside Apple who know that, too.
That feels like some shade thrown toward Nanian and Shirt Pocket, but here we are with MacOS 15.3, and bootable startup drive clones are back, so I have to question who needs to don sunglasses here.

posts.title: Some terminal frustrations
posts.summary: Julia Evans surveyed 1600 terminal users about their biggest frustrations and spent days categorizing the qualitative responses. The top complaints include remembering syntax, difficulties switching between systems, color configuration problems, keyboard shortcut inconsistencies, and copy/paste issues. Notably, 95% of respondents had 4+ years of terminal experience and 40% had 21+ years, meaning these frustrations persist even for experts. The results reveal that discoverability, steep learning curves, and legacy design decisions continue to plague the terminal experience decades into people's careers. Evans connects several categories as related problems: syntax recall ties to history search, discoverability drives the learning curve, and the outdated feel of terminals is precisely what makes them ubiquitous.
posts.key_insight: Terminal frustrations aren't beginner problems — even decades-long users struggle with syntax recall, system inconsistencies, and poor discoverability, revealing fundamental UX failures baked into the terminal's legacy design.

topQuotes.quote: The debilitating fear that I'm going to do some mysterious Bad Thing with a command and I will have absolutely no idea how to fix or undo it or even really figure out what happened.
Shell scripting. My tolerance to ditch a shell script and go to a scripting language is pretty low. It's just too messy and powerful. Screwing up can be costly so I don't even bother.
Tools that haven't really changed in 30 or 40 years have many problems but they do tend to be always there no matter what system you're on, which is very useful and makes them hard to stop using.

posts.title: Fat Rand: How Many Lines Do You Need To Generate A Random Number?
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that the Rust `rand` crate has become disproportionately dependency-heavy relative to its core purpose. He contrasts the current dependency tree and code size with earlier versions, showing significant growth in crates, lines of code, and compile time. The post stresses the auditability and security implications of pulling in many third-party maintainers and large platform crates for minimal functionality. He acknowledges the crates are high quality but questions whether their inclusion is necessary just to generate random numbers. He concludes by suggesting Rust’s standard library might be too small and that widely used “quasi-stdlib” crates should be more dependency-cautious, though he notes there is momentum to reduce `rand`’s footprint.
posts.key_insight: Rust’s de facto standard crates can become bloated enough to undermine auditability and trust, suggesting the ecosystem needs stronger dependency discipline or a larger standard library.

topQuotes.quote: It is however a crate that does not appear very concerned about how many dependencies it has, and I want to put this in perspective: of all the dependencies and lines of codes it pulls in, how many does it actually use?
All of these are great crates, but do I need all of this just to generate a random number?
Or maybe this is just how Rust works now.  That would make me quite sad.

posts.title: ‘Hot Tub’, a Hardcore Porn App for iOS, Hits AltStore in the E.U.
posts.summary: Gruber reports on Hot Tub, the first hardcore porn app distributed through AltStore in the EU under the Digital Markets Act, and dissects the deliberately misleading framing of it as 'Apple-approved.' He traces Apple's longstanding opposition to pornographic native apps back to a Steve Jobs email and through Phil Schiller's warnings about the DMA's consequences. Apple issued a statement emphasizing it does not approve of the app and is only required by the European Commission to allow alternative marketplaces. Gruber notes the schadenfreude of Apple's notarization process being weaponized to imply editorial approval, while also pointing out that EU officials have been conspicuously silent about this outcome of their own regulation.
posts.key_insight: The EU's DMA has produced exactly the consequence Apple warned about — native porn apps on iOS — while everyone involved plays a cynical game: AltStore exploits notarization to claim Apple approval, and EU officials who mandated this outcome stay conspicuously silent about it.

topQuotes.quote: Figuratively speaking, they're not fucking around. Literally speaking, that's exactly what they're aggregating — including an in-app 'teens' channel.
When it comes to native iOS porn apps, the EU now has an unequivocal advantage over not just the United States but the rest of the world. You'd think they'd want to celebrate the achievement.
However, we do believe we have a moral responsibility to keep porn off the iPhone. Folks who want porn can buy an Android phone.

posts.title: Why Pivot Tables Never Die
posts.summary: Simon Späti traces the history and enduring relevance of pivot tables, from their origins in 1986 at Lotus through Excel's dominance to their modern renaissance in BI tools. He argues that pivot tables succeed because they align with human cognition through simplicity, intuitive constraints, and instant feedback - functioning as a 'REPL for tables.' The post examines how modern data tools like Rill, Cube, and DuckDB are integrating pivot table functionality, powered by fast OLAP backends. Späti predicts that pivot tables combined with AI (GenBI) will become the primary interface for non-technical users to explore data, especially as they become capable of querying data lakes directly.
posts.key_insight: Pivot tables endure not despite their constraints but because of them - their rigid structure of rows, columns, filters, and values creates the intuitive simplicity that builds user trust and enables standardization across any tool.

topQuotes.quote: Is it worth spending hours perfecting a dashboard's aesthetics when you really need to understand how your company is performing?
A pivot table is a REPL for tables. You can drill and slice; you get direct feedback, and it's fast and instant.
Pivot is the OG of a dashboard. Without a pivot table, how would you create a dashboard?

posts.title: Is Big Tech Lame? Unhinged Rants and More w/ Carly Taylor
posts.summary: This is a brief newsletter update where Joe Reis shares his busy workload and points readers to a conversation with Carly Taylor about why Big Tech has lost its appeal. The post primarily serves as a curated list of weekend reads, podcast episodes, and upcoming events rather than deep analysis.
posts.key_insight: The shine has come off Big Tech, making it feel dated and less aspirational than it was a decade ago.

topQuotes.quote: Big Tech is so 2015.
Carly and I often joke that we are basically the same persona.

posts.title: As if Anyone Needed Further Proof, Patrick Soon-Shiong’s Los Angeles Times Is Now a Propaganda Rag (and They’re Bad at HTML)
posts.summary: Gruber documents how the Los Angeles Times, under owner Patrick Soon-Shiong, fundamentally altered a contributor's op-ed about RFK Jr. from a critical piece into one with an optimistic tone toward Trump's healthcare agenda. Eric Reinhart's original column argued that RFK Jr.'s disregard for science would inflict preventable death, but the LA Times rewrote it to suggest Trump's healthcare disruption could pay off. Gruber contrasts the original and published versions side by side to demonstrate the editorial malpractice. He then adds a technical discovery: his Markdown tools revealed that the LA Times editors left behind a vestigial HTML link from Reinhart's original draft, providing forensic proof of the editorial tampering. Gruber frames this as further evidence that the LA Times has become a propaganda outlet competing with the Washington Post to capitulate to Trump.
posts.key_insight: The LA Times under Soon-Shiong has devolved into a propaganda outlet, and its own sloppy HTML editing left forensic evidence of how it perverted an anti-RFK Jr. op-ed into pro-Trump pablum.

topQuotes.quote: The Los Angeles Times — currently in a race to the bottom with The Washington Post to determine which of the two venerable institutions can sell out to Donald Trump the hardest.

Editing out the most urgent point of an OpEd in the minutes before sending to press while then also assigning a title and image that suggest an argument entirely opposite to the author's clear intent is pretty shitty.
My only question here is whether Reinhart really believed the LA Times op-ed page was going to run his piece as written.

posts.title: Another One
posts.summary: Stancil argues that DeepSeek shattered the assumed economics and trajectory of frontier LLMs, proving that powerful models can appear cheaply and unexpectedly, undermining the moat of big labs. This shift changes AI from a predictable, centralized race into a chaotic landscape of many specialized, uneven models with unpredictable societal impacts.
posts.key_insight: DeepSeek marks a discontinuity that democratizes frontier-level AI, making model proliferation and downstream societal disruption far more likely than the industry’s neat, centralized roadmap suggested.

topQuotes.quote: Every LLM vendor is eighteen months from dead.
We are no longer living in a world where a few superpowers might build Skynet, but one in which Skynet could be anywhere.
AI cracks several more—that expertise takes time to accumulate; that reasoning has some foundational legitimacy; that creativity does not scale—and now, anyone can do anything, almost immediately.

posts.title: DuckCon #6 - State of the Duck
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #6 in Amsterdam, reaffirming DuckDB's mission to make large datasets approachable rather than intimidating. He presents evidence that single-node computing power has outpaced the growth of useful data sizes, citing analysis showing P99.9 of data warehouse sizes is under 300GB. He shares adoption metrics including 32 million monthly extension installs and 1.8 million monthly website visitors. The talk transitions to Mark discussing upcoming v1.2 features and the lakehouse focus for the year ahead, followed by Q&A covering architecture, Wasm, partitioning, and extension development.
posts.key_insight: The era of big data infrastructure is largely unnecessary for most workloads, as single-node hardware with efficient software like DuckDB can handle 99.9% of real-world analytical datasets.

topQuotes.quote: The P99.9 of data set sizes was under 300 gigabyte, which is really shocking if you think about it after a decade of talking about big data.
A tool that charges you 1,000 euros per person per year is going to have to be much, much better against one that doesn't cost anything, just saying.
He has shown that we can actually run the industry standard benchmark TPC-H with a scale factor of 300, which is roughly equivalent to 300 gigabytes of input data on a Raspberry Pi.

posts.title: It burns
posts.summary: DHH recounts his family's experience during the January 2025 Los Angeles wildfires that devastated Malibu and the Palisades. After narrowly avoiding the Franklin fire in December, his family evacuated again on January 7th as the Palisades fire destroyed nearly 100 of 150 houses in their immediate area. Though their home survived, the surrounding infrastructure was obliterated, forcing them to relocate to Copenhagen with their children. He reflects on how humans chronically underestimate disaster risk through bad pattern matching, and the bittersweet irony of leaving America just as he feels it's entering its most exciting period.
posts.key_insight: Our comprehension of catastrophic risk is always bounded by past experience, which makes us chronically underprepared when reality finally exceeds those bounds.

topQuotes.quote: But a house left standing in a disaster zone is still a disaster.
Of the twenty years I've spent in America, this feels like the most exciting time to be part of the exceptionalism that the US of A offers.
Because the past had failed to predict the future.

posts.title: How I Use AI: Meet My Promptly Hired Model Intern
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that AI has become a practical, everyday productivity multiplier for him, especially for writing and programming, and that blanket hostility to it is misguided. He frames LLMs as capable but fallible collaborators, akin to a competent intern whose work still demands human responsibility and verification. He highlights the rapid pace of model improvement and warns against outdated judgments from early ChatGPT experiences. His usage emphasizes controllability and openness, favoring local models, scriptable workflows, and voice interactions. He rejects “slop” output and undisclosed AI-generated content, insisting on critical engagement and intentional craft. The conclusion is that AI is best treated as an augmenting tool for thinking and creativity, not a replacement for it.
posts.key_insight: Used critically and intentionally, AI is most valuable as an accountable collaborator that accelerates thought and execution rather than replacing them.

topQuotes.quote: When someone throws me a pull request, an email or a text message that is obviously AI-generated without disclosing this, I immediately have a very low opinion of them.
Even the famous OpenAI which is not at all open is much more open than a Google search is.
I treat AI as I would a collaborator or a pretty good intern but I remain responsible for the final outcome.

posts.title: Forest And Desert
posts.summary: The Forest and Desert metaphor, developed by Beth Andres-Beck and Kent Beck, describes two distinct software development contexts that struggle to communicate. The Desert represents typical dysfunctional development environments with abundant bugs, uncultivated skills, and poor user communication. The Forest represents well-run teams practicing Extreme Programming with swift deployments, healthy code, and regular customer contact. The metaphor's key insight is that advice from one context sounds nonsensical to those in the other, and while transformation from Desert to Forest is possible, it requires difficult, counter-intuitive work.
posts.key_insight: Software development advice that works brilliantly in one context may sound absurd in another because the underlying environmental conditions differ so fundamentally.

topQuotes.quote: Two communities of software developers have great difficulty communicating to each other because they live in very different contexts, so advice that applies to one sounds like nonsense to the other.
It seems sadly easier for The Forest to submit to desertification.
The desert is the common world of software development, where bugs are plentiful, skill isn't cultivated, and communications with users is difficult.

posts.title: Pebble Founder Eric Migicovsky Is Bringing It Back
posts.summary: Gruber covers Pebble founder Eric Migicovsky's effort to revive the Pebble smartwatch after convincing Google to open-source the Pebble OS firmware. Migicovsky, who sold his messaging startup Beeper to Automattic, is starting a new company to build a smartwatch with Pebble's core values: e-paper display, long battery life, physical buttons, and hackability. Gruber draws an analogy to Panic's Playdate handheld as a model for achievable niche success. He uses the occasion to argue passionately that the tech world needs more small-scale, soul-driven projects rather than trillion-dollar moonshots, comparing indie hardware to indie filmmaking and art.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's obsession with trillion-dollar scale has crowded out the kind of small, passion-driven hardware projects that produce genuinely beloved products — and a Pebble revival could be a worthy example of indie tech as art.

topQuotes.quote: If your list of all-time favorite movies doesn't include a bunch that were made on shoestring budgets, your whole list probably sucks, because you have no taste.
My second idea: Quixote — because this isn't going to be a hit this time either.
Indie art is often great art, imbued by the souls and obsessions of its creators, and blockbuster art is often garbage art, imbued only by soulless corporate bureaucracies.

posts.title: My Spitball Theory on TikTok’s Current Semi-Reprieve in the U.S.
posts.summary: Gruber examines the peculiar split in how U.S. companies are handling TikTok's post-ban status: cloud providers Oracle and Akamai restored service on Trump's word, while Apple and Google continue to keep ByteDance apps out of their app stores. He theorizes this isn't coincidental but rather a deliberate strategy to slowly squeeze TikTok rather than kill it overnight, turning pressure on ByteDance to sell rather than on the U.S. government to reverse the ban. The instant ban created backlash from users directed at politicians, but the current arrangement — existing users can keep using TikTok but can't reinstall it or get updates — creates a slow decline that pressures ByteDance instead. Gruber questions what happens if China simply waits it out, noting the CCP has no financial pressure to capitulate and doesn't lack patience.
posts.key_insight: The current TikTok arrangement — functional for existing users but absent from app stores — may be a deliberate strategy to slowly pressure ByteDance into selling rather than triggering the immediate user backlash that an outright ban created.

topQuotes.quote: Slowly strangle TikTok over a period of months, rather than behead it overnight.
Nobody is really talking about the fact that ByteDance apps remain banned, legally speaking, in the U.S. right now.

Instantly banning TikTok's US operations resulted in instant and vociferous outrage from TikTok users — the pressure turned out not to be on ByteDance and the CCP, it was on the US government to give people back their beloved TikTok.

posts.title: Openvibe, a Multi-Social-Network App for Bluesky, Mastodon, Threads (Supposedly), and Nostr
posts.summary: Gruber reviews Openvibe, a multi-social-network client that combines Bluesky, Mastodon, Threads, and Nostr into one timeline. He finds the concept fundamentally flawed, arguing that mashing different social networks together creates confusion rather than simplicity. Threads integration barely works, the app itself is poorly designed with jarring UI issues like auto-scrolling timelines, and the claim that it appeals to newcomers is nonsensical. He contrasts Openvibe unfavorably with Croissant (cross-posting only) and Ivory (discrete per-account timelines), which he sees as solving actual user problems. His core argument is that different social networks have different cultures and features, and pretending they're one unified platform doesn't work.
posts.key_insight: Different social networks have distinct cultures and features, and combining them into a single timeline creates confusion rather than the simplicity it promises.

topQuotes.quote: The idea of explaining to them why they might want to use an app named Openvibe to access Mastodon, Bluesky, and Threads (and the weirdo blockchain network Nostr) is like trying to explain to your dog why they should stay out of the trash.
So useless it's amazing.
Mashing them all together in one timeline isn't simpler. It sounds simpler but in practice it's more cacophonous.

posts.title: Data Science Hangout with Wes McKinney
posts.summary: Wes McKinney frames his move to Posit as a continuation of long-standing collaboration aimed at building polyglot, open-source data tools. He argues that the real competition is open source versus proprietary software, not R versus Python, and that productive collaboration grows once language wars subside. He highlights persistent pain around cross-language packaging and deployment, pointing to tools like Conda, Mamba, and Pixi as partial remedies but not a full solution. On AI, he treats Copilot integration as table stakes while warning about ethical and workforce impacts, and he is interested in LLMs as research assistants for data exploration. His career advice centers on long-term relationship building and resilience to negative feedback in open source.
posts.key_insight: The long-term leverage in data tooling comes from open, interoperable infrastructure and sustained human relationships, not language tribalism.

topQuotes.quote: The downside of making developers more productive is in the future we need fewer developers, most likely.
I’ve made a personal promise to myself to never work on a Python packaging tool because we’ve all thought about it.
We’re not working R versus Python, we’re really open source versus closed source proprietary software.

posts.title: Communities Matter More Than Ever
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that in-person communities are becoming increasingly vital as antidotes to AI-generated content, toxic social media, and widespread loneliness. Writing from Data Day Texas, he emphasizes that as AI permeates every aspect of life, people will seek authentic human connections over algorithmically generated relationships.
posts.key_insight: As AI increasingly mediates human interaction and content creation, authentic in-person communities will become more valuable than ever as people seek genuine human connection.

topQuotes.quote: In an age of endless AI slop, toxic social media, internetenshitification, and an epidemic of loneliness, communities (especially in person) are an antidote.
I predict people will gravitate to strong communities more than ever as people tire of algorithmically generated relationships.
Spending time with my friends at Data Day Texas reminds me of the power of just…hanging out.

posts.title: Thin Is In
posts.summary: Gruber examines the implications of foldable phones approaching the physical thickness limit imposed by USB-C ports (2.6mm minimum). He outlines four possible futures: phones never getting thinner than 4mm, going fully inductive/wireless, Apple creating a proprietary thinner port, or the USB Consortium creating a thinner standard. He concludes that option (b) — dropping physical ports in favor of MagSafe and wireless — is the most likely path, probably starting with a rumored iPhone 17 Air while Pro models retain USB-C longer. He notes the EU's charging port regulations add regulatory complexity to any proprietary solution.
posts.key_insight: The USB-C port is becoming the physical bottleneck for phone thinness, and the most likely resolution is Apple dropping wired ports entirely on consumer models while EU regulations ironically accelerate the move to proprietary wireless charging solutions.

topQuotes.quote: Option (d) makes me laugh just thinking about it.

I believe it would neatly remain within the EU's charging port regulations, but would irritate proponents of those regulations, because it's unlikely Apple would choose to make such a Super Smart Connector an open standard.
Option (a) strikes me as guaranteed wrong. We will, sooner or later, see phones that are too thin for a USB-C port. The only question is when.

posts.title: Build It Yourself
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that dependency churn has become a default, celebrated habit that creates security, maintenance, and performance costs out of proportion to the value of tiny conveniences. He uses Rust and specific crate graphs to show how trivial functions balloon into dozens or hundreds of dependencies that require constant upgrades. He contends that the ecosystem punishes stable code and rewards integration with ever‑churning abstraction layers and security tooling. His alternative is a “vibe shift” toward writing small, self-contained code when appropriate, accepting minor corner cases and minimal updates. He concludes that dependency minimization should be celebrated, while acknowledging that complex domains still need big libraries.
posts.key_insight: The healthiest path out of dependency churn is a cultural shift that values small, self-written code over sprawling transitive graphs.

topQuotes.quote: In the Rust ecosystem stable code is punished.
It's that never-ending treadmill of updates, patches, audits, and transitive dependencies that we as developers love to casually install in the name of productivity.
That crate had 26 releases. My own version of that that I have stuck away in a project from 10 years ago still works without a single update.

posts.title: Good Lord
posts.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley’s celebrated contrarianism is less about fearless truth-seeking and more about indulging ideas that feel fun or flattering, while avoiding uncomfortable realities like the 1619 Project. As tech’s power surges again, he warns that the industry is choosing self-serving narratives and political convenience over moral courage, especially in the face of obvious abuses.
posts.key_insight: Silicon Valley’s self-image as fearless heretics collapses when truth threatens its comfort or power, revealing a lack of moral courage.

topQuotes.quote: My suspicion is that Silicon Valley contrarianism isn’t about a relentless pursuit of every truth; it’s about indulgence.
The talks at Hereticon aren’t hard realities that make the people giving and watching them uncomfortable; they’re forbidden fruits that make other people uncomfortable.
And if, despite all that, we cannot stand up to anything that is happening now—not even everything, but anything—perhaps we are not such brave heretics after all.

posts.title: ICE Raids Are an Escalation of Our Long-Simmering De Facto Cold Civil War
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Trump's ICE raids on undocumented immigrants are deliberately targeted at deep blue cities in blue states, constituting a de facto economic and political attack on communities that voted against him. He notes that while Trump promised mass deportation of all undocumented immigrants, the raids are conspicuously absent from red border states like Texas and Arizona. Gruber contends the second-order economic effects of removing undocumented workers will disproportionately harm blue-state economies, while red-state Trump voters get to enjoy the spectacle without bearing any costs. He frames this as an escalation in America's cold civil war, breaking his own post-election rule of ignoring Trump's actions outside tech.
posts.key_insight: The geographic targeting of ICE raids in blue cities rather than red border states reveals them as a political weapon against Democratic communities, not a practical immigration enforcement strategy.

topQuotes.quote: It's a big 'fuck you' to blue states.

If this was really about following through on the popular demand of the voters who elected Trump, who see undocumented immigrants as a scourge on their communities, wouldn't these raids focus on the states that voted for Trump — like, say, Texas and Arizona, which actually border on Mexico?

Trump voters in red states will get to enjoy watching footage of the raids on TV and social media, but it won't be their neighbors getting deported, not their contractors and custodians and dishwashers, and it won't be their communities whose economies suffer as a result.

posts.title: Join us to discuss transparency and governance at FOSDEM '25
posts.summary: DeVault reports the apparent cancellation of Jack Dorsey's FOSDEM '25 keynote following community protest, while pivoting to deeper questions about FOSDEM's governance and transparency. He thanks FOSDEM staff but notes the lack of official communication and the troubling revelation that 3 of 4 independent reviewers rejected Dorsey's talk proposal yet it was accepted anyway. The post calls for using the now-empty time slot to discuss what transparent, participatory governance at FOSDEM should look like. A later notice reveals the discussion session was also cancelled after organizers agreed that de-platforming Dorsey was sufficient victory, with governance reform efforts to continue after the event.
posts.key_insight: The Dorsey keynote controversy at FOSDEM revealed that one of open source's most important institutions lacks the very transparency and participatory governance that the movement itself champions.

topQuotes.quote: This year is FOSDEM's 25th anniversary, and it seems sorely overdue for graduating from the "trust us, it's crazy behind the scenes" governance model to something more aligned with the spirit of open source.
It strains our presumption of good faith when the talk proposal was rejected by 3 of the 4 independent reviewers and went through anyway.

It's kind of weird that we have to take them at their word — that the talk selection process isn't documented anywhere publicly, nor the conflict of interest policy, nor the sponsorship terms, nor almost anything at all about how FOSDEM operates or is governed internally.

posts.title: Siri Is Super Dumb and Getting Dumber
posts.summary: Gruber highlights how spectacularly bad Siri is at answering simple factual questions, building on Paul Kafasis's exhaustive test showing Siri correctly identified only 34% of Super Bowl winners. He then devises his own test — asking about the 2004 North Dakota high school boys' basketball championship — and finds that ChatGPT, Kagi, and DuckDuckGo all answer correctly while Siri with Apple Intelligence gives confidently wrong answers that change each time. Most damningly, old Siri without Apple Intelligence performed better by simply providing useful web links rather than fabricating wrong answers. Gruber concludes that Apple Intelligence has made Siri a regression, and that OpenAI's brand is being harmed by the partnership since ChatGPT gets blamed for Siri's mangled answers despite answering perfectly when used directly.
posts.key_insight: Apple Intelligence has made Siri worse, not better — turning it from a humble assistant that knew its limits into a confidently wrong one that bungles answers even with ChatGPT's help.

topQuotes.quote: If Siri were a quarterback, it would be drummed out of the NFL.
New Siri — powered by Apple Intelligence™ with ChatGPT integration enabled — gets the answer completely but plausibly wrong, which is the worst way to get it wrong.
As it stands, Apple is getting a scapegoat more than a partner out of this deal.

posts.title: Death of the Visceral
posts.summary: Hotz laments that modern life has lost visceral, meaningful experiences—the adrenaline of survival, the stakes of real challenges. As AI advances, even 'Type 2 experiences' requiring serious effort will disappear, leaving humanity disengaged and purposeless. He sees his mission at comma.ai and tinygrad as giving individuals deep, structural control over technology—not surface-level permissions that can be revoked. The coming AI revolution will outcompete 'spiritual bottoms' (passive consumers), bifurcating humanity into those who control technology and those who wirehead themselves out of relevance. His goal is maximizing the number of humans who retain real control, which he believes is his best chance of survival.
posts.key_insight: As AI eliminates meaningful human challenges, the only defense is building technology that structurally empowers individuals—not products that can be co-opted or permissions that can be revoked.

topQuotes.quote: Even when humanity goes to Mars, we will be going as cargo.
The ghost gunner is the real second amendment.
AI is the ultimate spiritual bottom.

posts.title: The Data Engineering Toolkit: Essential Tools for Your Machine
posts.summary: Simon Späti provides a comprehensive overview of the essential tools and foundational skills every data engineer needs, from operating system choices to Linux commands, Docker containerization, and development environments. He emphasizes that while the toolkit is extensive, engineers should focus on fundamentals and learn incrementally rather than trying to master everything at once. The article covers the practical building blocks: environment variables, virtual machines, CLI tools, simple orchestration with Makefiles and cron, IDEs, notebooks, and version control with git. He concludes that SQL and Python remain the core languages, with the reminder that sometimes the simplest Linux command can replace a complex toolchain.
posts.key_insight: Mastering foundational tools like Linux commands, Docker, and simple orchestration provides data engineers with more leverage than chasing the latest complex frameworks—sometimes a well-crafted shell pipeline is all you need.

topQuotes.quote: This foundation reminds us that sometimes the simplest solution is the most effective—a well-chosen Linux command can often replace a complex toolchain.
To be proficient as a data engineer, you need to know various toolkits—from fundamental Linux commands to different virtual environments and optimizing efficiency as a data engineer.
The core responsibility of a data engineer is to orchestrate different jobs in the correct order and fully automate them.

posts.title: Refactoring with Codemods to Automate API Changes
posts.summary: The article explains how codemods—automated scripts that use Abstract Syntax Tree manipulation—enable developers to apply large-scale code transformations consistently across codebases. Starting with the challenge library developers face when introducing breaking API changes, Fowler demonstrates how codemods parse code into ASTs, modify the tree structure, and rewrite the source. Through practical examples including feature toggle cleanup and React component refactoring, he shows how to write, test, and compose codemods using tools like jscodeshift. The piece also covers handling edge cases, applying these techniques in Java with JavaParser and OpenRewrite, and leveraging platforms like Hypermod and Codemod.com.
posts.key_insight: Codemods bring the same AST-based transformation power that IDEs provide locally to the scale of entire codebases, but their effectiveness depends on breaking complex changes into composable, well-tested pieces and acknowledging that edge cases will require human judgment.

topQuotes.quote: This approach aligns well with Test-Driven Development (TDD), even if you don't practice TDD regularly. Knowing exactly what the transformation's inputs and outputs are before coding improves safety and efficiency.
A more maintainable approach is to treat codemod logic like product code: break the task into smaller, independent pieces—just like how you would normally refactor production code.
Relying solely on the cases you can anticipate is not enough. You need thorough testing to avoid breaking unintended parts of the code.

posts.title: Waiting on red
posts.summary: DHH uses the Danish habit of waiting at red lights even when no cars are coming as a metaphor for how consistent norm enforcement creates profound second-order effects in society. He argues that Copenhagen's safety—where nine-year-olds ride the metro alone—comes from vigorously enforcing even minor infractions and having zero tolerance for antisocial behavior. He contrasts this with American cities where tolerance of disorder has driven families from public spaces. His core argument is that Denmark's progressive outcomes (paternity leave, public transit, child independence) are built on a highly conservative foundation of enforced social norms, and American progressives who admire Denmark must reckon with this paradox.
posts.key_insight: Denmark's progressive social outcomes that American liberals admire are inseparable from the conservative norm enforcement they tend to reject—you cannot have one without the other.

topQuotes.quote: When you take away the freedom from crackheads and bums to smoke up on the train or sleep in the park, you grant the freedom to nine-year olds to roam the city and for families to enjoy the park at dusk.
This is the fundamental error of suicidal empathy. That tolerance of the deranged and dangerous few can be kept a separate discussion from the freedom and safety of the many.
The progressive Nirvana of Denmark is built on a highly conservative set of norms and traditions. It's yin and yang.

posts.title: Let’s Not Mince Words Regarding Trump’s Executive Order to Pause Enforcement of the TikTok Law and Provide Supposed Immunity to Companies That Are in Clear Violation of It
posts.summary: Gruber criticizes NPR's framing of Trump's TikTok executive order, arguing that the order doesn't 'pause' the law but merely directs the Justice Department not to enforce it — a crucial distinction. He draws an analogy to a mayor telling police not to enforce a city council law, likening the situation to banana republic governance. Gruber emphasizes that the bipartisan PAFACA law passed with overwhelming majorities in both chambers, and that Trump is asserting a de facto post-hoc veto power in violation of the Constitution's Article II Section 3. He frames this as a test of whether Republican lawmakers will accept executive overreach against their own legislation.
posts.key_insight: Media outlets undermine democratic accountability when they frame executive non-enforcement of a law as 'pausing' it, obscuring the constitutional violation at the heart of the action.

topQuotes.quote: Give him credit: this is exactly how he said he'd act (or, perhaps, to choose a more provocative verb, rule) if re-elected.
Let's not mince words on this either: this man is a proud ignoramus. He relishes his ignorance.
The name of the town in my allegory above is Banana Republicville. The order doesn't change the law; it says to ignore the law.

posts.title: Fundamentals of Data Engineering - 2.5 Years Later
posts.summary: Joe Reis reflects on the 2.5-year journey since publishing 'Fundamentals of Data Engineering,' discussing how the book emerged from frustration with tool-focused learning and became a bestseller by teaching data engineering from first principles. He shares the intense personal challenges of writing—including working through COVID in a basement during a house remodel—and how the book transformed his career while attracting both devoted readers and inevitable critics.
posts.key_insight: Writing a fundamentals book is harder than writing an expert-level one because distilling complexity into accessible knowledge for a broad audience requires both deep expertise and radical empathy for learners at every stage.

topQuotes.quote: Having met countless data engineering practitioners of all levels worldwide, I've yet to meet even expert practitioners who have a complete grasp of all fundamental concepts in data engineering.
If you want to feel humbled, write a book. You start off feeling like you know your topic and quickly realize you're in for a harsh lesson in humility.
There's your career before and after writing a book. This is very apt. If you want to elevate your profile, write a book. For whatever reason, writing a book puts you in a different class.

posts.title: Failed integration and the fall of multiculturalism
posts.summary: DHH argues that decades of mass immigration in Europe have demonstrated that cultural integration between radically divergent cultures has largely failed, and that blaming host countries for not trying hard enough is no longer a credible explanation. He contends that multiculturalism as a compromise has poisoned national unity across Europe, and that suppressing dissent by labeling it racist has fueled populist movements. While acknowledging the dangers of combative nationalism and that correct diagnoses don't guarantee correct prescriptions, he calls for replacing naive hope with realistic new strategies for immigration policy.
posts.key_insight: Europe's decades-long experiment with mass immigration and multiculturalism has failed on a statistical level, and replacing naive idealism with clear-eyed realism about cultural integration is urgent — even if the right prescriptions remain unclear.

topQuotes.quote: Allowing parallel cultures to underpin parallel societies is poison for the national unity and trust.
World history is full of people who've had the correct diagnosis but a terrible prescription.
The old accusations have finally lost their power from years of excessive use, and suppressing the reality that many people can see with their own eyes is getting harder.

posts.title: MEGA
posts.summary: DHH celebrates Trump's return to the presidency, noting that two-thirds of young Americans (18-29) are optimistic about the next four years. He frames the moment as bigger than Trump himself—a cultural shift away from excessive self-criticism toward renewed American confidence. He contrasts American optimism with European pessimism, expressing hope that Europe will eventually follow suit. He acknowledges the piece is more faith than analysis, but declares optimism the only viable stance.
posts.key_insight: American optimism under Trump represents a broader cultural shift beyond politics—toward entrepreneurial confidence and away from self-loathing—that Europe desperately needs but may lack the agility to replicate.

topQuotes.quote: Optimism must win. Pessimism is literally for losers.

I can also well imagine Europe sticking with Eurocrat thinking, spinning its wheels with grand but empty proclamations, issuing scorning but impotent admonishments of America, and doubling down on the regulatory black hole.
As someone with a foot on both the American and European continent, I can't help being jealous with my euro leg.

posts.title: FOSDEM '25 protest
posts.summary: Drew DeVault organizes a peaceful protest at FOSDEM 2025 against Jack Dorsey and Block, Inc. being given a speaking slot, arguing their corporate track record is antithetical to the open source community's values. He emphasizes the protesters are long-time FOSDEM community members acting out of love for the conference, not hostility toward its organizers. The post lays out specific logistics for occupying the stage during Dorsey's scheduled talk and provides a strict code of conduct for participants. DeVault calls for greater transparency in FOSDEM's talk selection and sponsorship processes to prevent such conflicts in the future.
posts.key_insight: Open source conferences must guard their stages against corporate actors whose track records contradict community values, and the community itself bears responsibility for enforcing that boundary through collective action.

topQuotes.quote: We are not activism tourists.
That we are taking action demonstrates that we value FOSDEM, that we believe it represents our community, and that we want to defend its — our — ethos.

His complicity, along with his present-day activities at Block, Inc. and the priorities of the company that he represents as CEO — its irresponsible climate policy, $120M in fines for enabling consumer fraud, and the layoffs of another 1,000 employees in 2024 despite posting record profits on $5B in revenue — are enough of a threat to our community and its ethos to raise alarm.

posts.title: Automatic Server Reloading in Rust on Change: What is listenfd/systemfd?
posts.summary: Ronacher explains how Flask’s fast reload experience relied on a parent process holding the listening socket while a child process restarted, avoiding connection errors. He built the Rust tools `systemfd` and `listenfd` to recreate that experience and notes they’re still underused. The post walks through file watching with `watchexec` or `cargo watch`, socket passing via the systemd protocol, and the small code change needed to accept inherited listeners. He shows how to wire the commands together so recompiles and restarts happen without dropping requests. He concludes that the workflow is powerful but too clunky, and argues for tighter framework or cargo integration. The ideal would be a single `cargo devserver`-style command, but sustaining that polish requires more community effort.
posts.key_insight: Seamless Rust dev-server reloads are already possible through socket activation and file watching, but the experience needs first-class tool and framework integration to be broadly accessible.

topQuotes.quote: I wrote systemfd and listenfd, and I think those crates, and a good auto-reloading experience in Rust are largely unknown.
Ideally, this functionality would be better integrated into specific Rust frameworks like axum and provided through a dedicated cargo plugin.
The most important part of the developer experience for me was enabling fast, automatic reloading.

posts.title: BrikTok
posts.summary: Gruber covers the chaotic first day of the U.S. TikTok ban, which took effect one day before Trump's inauguration. TikTok went dark briefly, then returned with increasingly obsequious messages crediting Trump for its restoration — even though Trump had no legal authority yet. While Oracle and Akamai restored TikTok's backend service based on Trump's promise of immunity, Apple and Google kept TikTok out of their app stores, correctly following the actual law. Gruber highlights the absurdity of Trump's proposal for a 50% U.S. government stake in TikTok and the fundamental legal question of whether companies should follow enacted law or a not-yet-president's word. Republican senators Cotton and Ricketts pushed back, insisting the law has no provision for an 'extension' of its effective date.
posts.key_insight: The TikTok ban's first day revealed a stark divide between companies willing to follow enacted law and those willing to treat Trump's word as law, with Apple and Google choosing legal prudence while Oracle and Akamai gambled on political favor.

topQuotes.quote: Is the law the law, or is Donald Trump's word the law? Oracle and Akamai are seemingly good with the latter.

The idea of the U.S. government buying a 50 (not 51?) percent stake in a social media company is nutty, quite overtly socialist (perhaps owning the means of distraction is the new owning the means of production?), but Trump is nutty so who knows what he means here?
Apple and Google, seemingly, are following the law, not Trump's good-as-gold word and pinkie-swear promise that they won't be held liable for violating it.

posts.title: Nokia’s Next-Day Internal Competitive Analysis of the Original iPhone Largely Got It Right
posts.summary: Gruber examines a recently unearthed internal Nokia slide deck from January 2007, created the day after Apple unveiled the original iPhone. He argues the Nokia team deserves credit for largely recognizing the iPhone's significance — especially its revolutionary touch UI and carrier relationship — while noting their blind spots around third-party software and the mobile web. The deck's most prescient recommendation was that Nokia needed a Chief UI architect and a wholesale reinvention of its design priorities. Gruber frames the story as a cautionary tale about institutional inertia: Nokia's analysts understood the threat intellectually but the company couldn't act on it because its entire business model depended on the old paradigm.
posts.key_insight: Nokia's analysts correctly identified the iPhone as a paradigm shift but the company couldn't act on that insight because its entire institutional structure depended on the old paradigm continuing.

topQuotes.quote: But all of that software sucked, big time. Sucked to develop, sucked to distribute, sucked to install, sucked to use. Not supporting it was a huge win for the iPhone.
This speaks to Nokia's pre-iPhone mindset that mobile platforms were not really Internet-first devices but instead were mostly for running shitty software from carriers.
It is difficult to get a man to understand something, when his salary depends on his not understanding it.

posts.title: Is Growth Still Good
posts.summary: Benn argues that the AI boom is compressing adoption and revenue timelines so much that traditional signals of traction no longer imply durability or moat. When growth can be manufactured quickly by fear-driven enterprise spending and a flood of new startups, fast revenue may indicate a shallow, contested vein rather than a defensible business.
posts.key_insight: In the AI rush, explosive growth is no longer a reliable moat signal because spending and adoption are faster than the time it takes to build durable advantage.

topQuotes.quote: The best vendor is the one that walks in with a futuristic demo, a pile of cash from premier venture capitalists, and the wild eyes of an unhinged visionary.
The truth may somehow be worse: Rather than turning us into batteries, the machines will make us Jira admins.
There is no adoption curve anymore; there are only users, and the walking dead.

posts.title: No billionaires at FOSDEM
posts.summary: Drew DeVault calls for a protest against Jack Dorsey's keynote at FOSDEM 2025, arguing that billionaires should not be platformed at free software events. He details Dorsey's role in selling Twitter to Elon Musk, the resulting harm to the FOSS community which absorbed fleeing users, and Block's sponsorship as the likely reason for the keynote slot. DeVault connects Dorsey's company to layoffs, bitcoin mining powered by fossil fuels, and broader tech grift. He announces a planned sit-in to peacefully block the talk and argues billionaires should earn FOSS community respect through massive donations rather than keynote stages.
posts.key_insight: Free software community platforms should not be sold to billionaires whose wealth was built by harming the very communities those platforms serve.

topQuotes.quote: If billionaires want to participate in FOSS, I'm going to ask them to refrain from using our platforms to talk about their AI/blockchain/bitcoin/climate-disaster-as-a-service grifty business ventures, and instead buy our respect by, say, donating 250 million dollars to NLNet or the Sovereign Tech Fund.
I don't think that we owe this honor to someone who took a billion dollars to ruin their project, ostracize their users, and destroy the livelihoods of almost everyone who worked on it.

After Dorsey sold Twitter to Elon Musk, selling the platform out to the far right for a crisp billion-with-a-"B" dollar payout, the FOSS community shouldered the burden – both with our labor and our wallets – of a massive exodus onto our volunteer-operated servers.

posts.title: Infrastructure Vendors Are in a Tough Spot
posts.summary: Riccomini argues that legacy infrastructure vendors like Confluent are structurally unable to compete with lean, cloud-native startups like WarpStream. Using Confluent's acquisition of WarpStream as a case study, he explains how organizational baggage, the innovator's dilemma, and Conway's law prevent incumbents from building simpler, cheaper solutions that would cannibalize their existing business. The post contends that cloud-native architectures combined with AI-enabled engineering productivity have created a new class of 'apex predator' startups that can build competitive products with a fraction of the headcount. Riccomini warns that acquiring these nimble competitors may only delay the inevitable commoditization of infrastructure.
posts.key_insight: Legacy infrastructure vendors are structurally trapped by their own success—their organizations, tech stacks, and enterprise customers prevent them from building the simpler, cheaper cloud-native solutions that lean AI-enabled startups can ship in months with a fraction of the headcount.

topQuotes.quote: WarpStream was in the process of building a cheaper (and arguably better) version of Confluent's platform with a handful of engineers that could service the majority of Confluent customers' use cases. That's a pretty staggering statement.
I believe Confluent didn't build WarpStream because it wasn't set up, as an organization, to build such a product.

Why would Confluent build Freight without BYOC support? I think the answer is that it was too encumbered by its legacy tech stack, its customers (the innovator's dilemma), and its organization (Conway's law).

posts.title: One Bit of Anecdata That the Web Is Languishing Vis-à-Vis Native Mobile Apps
posts.summary: Gruber recounts a frustrating experience using his power company PECO's terrible mobile website during a 26-hour outage, only to discover during a subsequent outage that their native iOS app was surprisingly good. He uses this contrast as evidence that the mobile web is languishing relative to native apps, arguing that the gap between them is widening rather than narrowing. He notes the irony that a utility company — the last place you'd expect to need a native app — delivers a dramatically better experience through its app than its website. He concludes that while the web overall may not be dying, the mobile web specifically appears to be in decline.
posts.key_insight: The widening gap between native mobile apps and mobile web experiences — visible even in mundane utility company software — suggests the mobile web is languishing while native apps continue to improve.

topQuotes.quote: Basically, PECO's mobile website feels like it was developed using, and exported from, Microsoft Excel. You might say, 'Well that makes no sense, because you've never been able to build or export websites using Excel.' To which I'd respond, 'Yes, exactly.'
And the notion that mobile web apps are closing the gap with native apps is laughable. The gulf between them is widening, not narrowing.
