posts.key_insight: Apple's acquisition history shows a consistent pattern of respecting and preserving the value of acquired apps, making the Pixelmator acquisition more likely to produce a new Apple-branded pro tool than a shuttered product.

topQuotes.quote: Facebook Paper was so good, so forward-thinking, so innovative, that it almost got me to create a Facebook account for the first time.
I still can't fully forgive Adobe for doing this with FreeHand when they acquired Macromedia in 2005.
It's commonplace in the industry for a large company to acquire a small company that makes a very cool app, and then somehow ruin that app.

posts.title: Does Data Quality Matter
posts.summary: The post argues that while data quality is crucial for traditional analytics, it matters less than commonly claimed for many AI/LLM use cases because these models are robust to noisy inputs. The real reason data quality may gain attention now is not technical necessity but heightened executive interest in AI, which makes organizations suddenly care about the data feeding these systems.
posts.key_insight: AI’s hype may elevate data quality investments, not because models demand pristine data, but because leaders finally care about the systems using it.

topQuotes.quote: If data quality matters more now than it did before, it seems like that will be the reason why: Not because AI models care more about higher quality data than traditional reporting, but because, at least during the current AI circus, businesses care more about AI models than they ever did about reporting.
In other words, the approximate qualitative averages that get built by these models bulldoze bad data in ways careful mathematical computations do not.

Which is not a very good answer? And it’s certainly a much worse answer than saying “we need to invest in robust data quality solutions, so that you can correctly report how much money we make and don’t get sued by the SEC.”

posts.title: Jaguar is lost but Volvo knows the way
posts.summary: DHH contrasts Jaguar's widely mocked rebrand with Volvo's emotionally powerful advertising to make a point about authentic branding. Jaguar's campaign relies on hollow, cliché slogans and diversity pandering that feel completely disconnected from what a car company should stand for. Volvo, meanwhile, builds its brand on decades of genuine commitment to safety, backed by real actions like giving away the seatbelt patent and running crash response teams since the 1970s. The key lesson is that great branding requires congruence between what you say and what you actually do, repeated consistently over time. DHH argues that brand strength comes from authenticity and commitment, not clever marketing slogans. The result: he wants to buy a Volvo despite his natural inclinations toward Jaguar's speed and design heritage.
posts.key_insight: Authentic branding requires congruence between what you claim and what you actually do — no amount of clever slogans can substitute for decades of genuine commitment to a real value.

topQuotes.quote: You'd think these were slogans from a Will Ferrel bit about insufferable marketing trons, but nope, that's the 2024 campaign for a car maker that won't be selling any cars until 2026.
I wouldn't be caught dead in a Jaguar now. That's the power of advertisement: To lift and to diminish.
Volvo is safety, safety is paramount to parents, so Volvo is for parents. It's that simple, and it's that powerful. But only because it's actually true!

posts.title: New report: How altered states shape our minds
posts.summary: Asparouhova reports on her new research into jhanas, a series of intensely altered meditation states she has been covering since early 2024. She traces the rapid shift in public discourse from skepticism about whether jhanas are real to widespread curiosity about how to practice them. Partnering with Jhourney, a retreat company with unique access to practitioners, she surveyed alumni about how jhanas changed their lives. The resulting report is described as the most extensive study yet on jhana impact, aimed at identifying the right research questions rather than providing definitive answers. She notes the research challenges: few academics studying jhanas, difficulty recruiting participants, and unclear frameworks for measuring impact given jhanas sit between meditation and psychedelics.
posts.key_insight: Jhanas have rapidly moved from fringe skepticism to mainstream curiosity, but the research infrastructure to understand their impact barely exists, creating an open frontier that requires figuring out the right questions before seeking answers.

topQuotes.quote: Instead of debating whether they are real, the question I hear now is: 'How can I try it?'
Jhanas feel like self-induced psychedelic states, so – do they offer similar benefits?
Because jhanas occupy a weird space between 'meditation' and 'psychedelics,' it's not even clear which type of impact to look for, much less measure.

posts.title: A Place for Me
posts.summary: Hotz argues that most jobs have been fake for years, with society run by people creating meaningless work for each other to feel useful. He believes AI and automation are continuing a trend that will make the average person economically unprofitable, requiring a fundamental restructuring of Western governance. Drawing comparisons to China, which he sees as 'living in the future,' he suggests that liberal democracy was a product of industrial-era labor needs that are now obsolete. He predicts a future where most people live in comfortable 'retirement' but lose meaningful citizenship, while warning that protectionism and neo-luddism cannot succeed and will only drag the West down.
posts.key_insight: Liberal democracy was built for an industrial economy that needed mass labor, but as AI makes the average person economically unprofitable, Western governance will have to fundamentally restructure whether we're ready or not.

topQuotes.quote: Basically society is run by useless people making work for other useless people so that together they can all alleviate their deep concern about not having a place in society.
The industrial revolution required labor at mass scale, hence we got liberal democracy. But I think it's over.
UBI is a double edged sword. If you take it, you are no longer meaningfully a citizen.

posts.title: Cold reading an ADHD affliction
posts.summary: DHH argues that ADHD is massively overdiagnosed, comparing the diagnostic process to cold reading by psychics. He points out that the commonly listed ADHD symptoms—forgetfulness, attraction to novelty, difficulty with habits—are universal human experiences, not pathologies. He notes the explosion of Adderall prescriptions (45 million in 2023, up from 35 million in 2019) as evidence of a cottage industry convincing normal people they have a medical condition. He concludes that people want a medical explanation for ordinary human struggles rather than accepting mundane solutions like better sleep and exercise.
posts.key_insight: The broad, universally relatable nature of ADHD symptom lists functions like psychic cold reading, enabling a cottage industry that medicalizes ordinary human imperfection.

topQuotes.quote: If you want to supercharge your morning's productivity routine by popping a pill or two of amphetamines, own it! Don't hide behind some label.
Sure, you may recognize all those symptoms, but for true ADHD sufferers, they're just, like, worse! Okay, but what kind of diagnostic standard is that?!
No wonder we've seen an explosion of ADHD diagnosis. This list applies to most humans at least part of the time!

posts.title: Regarding — and, Well, Against — Substack
posts.summary: Gruber responds to Anil Dash's anti-Substack essay by disagreeing with the characterization of Substack as a Nazi-enabling political project, but agreeing—for entirely different reasons—that writers should avoid it. He argues Substack deliberately blurs the line between individual publications and its own brand, making all publications look alike and pushing lock-in through its social features. He notes that many prominent newsletter writers have already migrated to Ghost, Beehiiv, or custom platforms, all of which offer better branding independence and subscription management. His advice: consider Substack last, not because of content moderation politics, but because better options exist and Substack's goal is clearly platform lock-in.
posts.key_insight: The case against Substack isn't political—it's that the platform systematically undermines writer independence through uniform branding, social lock-in, and the deliberate conflation of publication identity with platform identity.

topQuotes.quote: In what world is 'No thanks' a good link to convey the meaning 'Let me read the thing I came here to read'?
A publication on Substack is no more 'a Substack' than a blog on WordPress is 'a WordPress'.
I really don't get why any writer trying to establish themselves independently would farm out their own brand this way. It's the illusion of independence.

posts.title: Exploring the Semantic Layer Through the Lens of MVC
posts.summary: This post introduces an analogy between the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern from web development and modern data architecture's semantic layer. Späti argues that just as MVC separates presentation from business logic through a model layer, data teams need a similar separation between data storage and presentation through semantic layers. The post serves as an introduction to a longer article published on Cube.dev, positioning semantic layers as the data world's equivalent to Active Record in Ruby on Rails. The core thesis is that proven software architecture patterns can solve similar challenges in data engineering around metric definition and maintenance.
posts.key_insight: The MVC pattern's separation of concerns, proven over decades in web development, provides a useful mental model for understanding why semantic layers matter in modern data architecture.

topQuotes.quote: Wouldn't it be convenient to separate the presentation from the storage through a data modeling layer, similar to the model layer?
A place where we can declaratively define our metrics, measures, and business KPIs so that we can update them confidently and version them.
This architectural pattern can solve similar challenges in data engineering that MVC solved for web development.

posts.title: Joining the Shopify board of directors
posts.summary: DHH announces he's joining Shopify's board of directors, framing it through his twenty-year relationship with founder Tobi Lütke dating back to the earliest days of Ruby on Rails. He highlights Shopify's enormous contributions to the Rails ecosystem, including employing a third of the Rails Core Team and developing the YJIT Ruby compiler. Beyond gratitude, DHH admits a selfish curiosity to engage with engineering problems at a scale far beyond his work at 37signals—a five-million-line monolith and thousands of programmers. He positions Shopify as a counterforce to web enshittification, enabling small entrepreneurs to compete with Amazon through fast, trustworthy online stores.
posts.key_insight: DHH frames his Shopify board appointment as both a tribute to Rails' most important commercial success story and a personal opportunity to engage with engineering scale he's never experienced at 37signals.

topQuotes.quote: Tobi more or less single-handedly killed the zombie argument that Rails couldn't scale by building the world's most popular hosted e-commerce platform and routing a sizable portion of all online sales through it.
While much of the web is decaying to enshitification and entropy, Shopify stores stand out by being faster to browse, quicker to checkout, and easier to trust.
It was also to satisfy a selfish curiosity to wrestle with problems at a scale that none of my own work has ever touched.

posts.title: There Is Only One Key Difference Between Observability 1.0 and 2.0
posts.summary: The fundamental distinction between observability 1.0 and 2.0 is not a marketing buzzword but a technical one: 1.0 scatters telemetry across multiple pillars, tools, and formats (many sources of truth), while 2.0 stores everything as wide structured log events in a single source of truth. Everything else—better developer workflows, sustainable cost models, high-cardinality exploration—flows as a consequence from this one architectural difference.
posts.key_insight: The entire observability 1.0 vs 2.0 distinction reduces to one technical question—whether your telemetry is stored once as wide structured events (single source of truth) or scattered across multiple pillars and tools—and every other advantage of 2.0 flows as a consequence of that single architectural choice.

topQuotes.quote: Poor observability is the dark matter of engineering teams. It's why everything we do feels so incredibly, grindingly slow, for no apparent reason.
The idea that developing your code and operating your code are two different practices that can be done by two different people is no longer tenable.
If you're trying to clear a path through all the sales collateral and marketing technobabble, you only need to ask one question: how many times is your data going to be stored?

posts.title: Dr. Oz
posts.summary: Gruber recounts a personal anecdote from the September 2014 Apple Watch unveiling event, where he found himself alone in a waiting area with Dr. Mehmet Oz before their respective press briefings. The piece is occasioned by Trump's nomination of Oz to run the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services. Gruber describes Oz as a man of striking celebrity presence but stunning vapidity, who chattered inanely about the Apple Watch prototypes on display as though performing for a nonexistent TV camera. The post uses this firsthand encounter to paint Oz as someone simultaneously accomplished in medicine yet remarkably hollow in person, implicitly questioning his fitness for a major government role.
posts.key_insight: A firsthand encounter with Dr. Oz at Apple's 2014 event revealed a man of pure celebrity performance — perpetually on camera even when no camera existed — making his nomination to run a major federal agency feel like a punchline.

topQuotes.quote: He was not talking to me, nor was he, really, talking to himself. It was like he was talking to a TV camera, as though we were being filmed for B-roll footage for his show — but there was no camera.
Simultaneously a brilliant mind in the field of thoracic surgery, and yet dumb as a rock in everyday human interaction.
I spent the first few minutes with him wondering if I should introduce myself. I spent the last few glad I hadn't, because he was so obviously a staggeringly uninteresting and uninterested man.

posts.title: It's Time to Merge Analytics and Data Engineering (Again)
posts.summary: Riccomini argues that analytics engineers and data engineers should be merged back into a single role, reversing the split that occurred over the past decade. He contends that the analytics engineer role, focused primarily on transformations, doesn't provide enough value to justify dedicated headcount. Several factors drove the original bifurcation: the shift to ELT architectures, Kafka adoption, shift-left philosophy, and vendors creating roles to sell products. Now that ZIRP has ended, data pipelines are commoditized, and LLMs can answer many data questions, the conditions that justified separate roles have changed. Tools like dltHub and tabsdata are emerging to facilitate this reunification.
posts.key_insight: The economic and technological conditions that justified splitting data warehouse teams into data engineers and analytics engineers have reversed, making reunification both inevitable and necessary.

topQuotes.quote: dbtLabs, Motherduck, and other MDS vendors were all too willing to create a new role to sell their products, which dovetailed nicely with analyst's desire to be engineers and get paid more.
Dumping garbage into an object store made it easy for data engineers to ignore transformations and gave analytics engineers something to do.
LLMs are replacing analysts in some cases. Screech all you want, but it's happening.

posts.title: Playground Wisdom: Threads Beat Async/Await
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that `async`/`await` is a poor abstraction for most languages because it breaks the ability to suspend anywhere, creates “colored functions,” and weakens back-pressure and debugging. He contrasts this with blocking semantics, where suspension is implicit and call stacks remain intact, and shows how promises can leave work unresolved forever. He traces how different languages arrived at `async`/`await` and notes that identical syntax hides fundamentally different runtime models. He contends that in languages with real threads (especially Python and C#), `async`/`await` adds complexity without delivering core benefits. His conclusion favors a return to thread-based models—ideally virtual threads like Java’s Project Loom—paired with structured concurrency, channels, and better primitives. `async`/`await` did spur valuable innovation, but future designs should center on threads with clearer, safer concurrency structure.
posts.key_insight: For most languages, `async`/`await` trades away the power of implicit suspension and clear call stacks, so the better path is thread-based concurrency—preferably virtual threads—paired with structured concurrency and channels.

topQuotes.quote: Promises/futures and `async`/`await` are making something strictly worse than not having them.
I’m now convinced that `async`/`await` is, in fact, a bad abstraction for most languages.
The worst offender here in my mind is Python.

posts.title: Importing a frontend Javascript library without a build system
posts.summary: Julia Evans explains how to import frontend JavaScript libraries without using a build system, a task she finds repeatedly frustrating because most library documentation assumes a build system. She identifies three types of JS files libraries provide: 'classic' global variable files, ES modules, and CommonJS modules. She walks through real examples (Chart.js, @atcute/oauth-browser-client, @atproto/oauth-client-browser) showing how to identify each type and use them in the browser. The post covers practical techniques including UMD files with script tags, ES modules with importmaps, and using esm.sh to convert CommonJS modules. She concludes that ES modules being a web standard gives her confidence in their longevity, and frames the post as understanding the JS landscape rather than complaining about it.
posts.key_insight: Importing JavaScript libraries without a build system requires understanding the three types of JS files (classic, ES module, CommonJS) and knowing how to identify and work with each, which is learnable detective work despite poor documentation.

topQuotes.quote: If I write some code using ES modules today, I can feel sure that it'll still work the same way in 15 years.

I ran into a problem where I needed to figure out how to import a Javascript library in my code without using a build system, and it took FOREVER to figure out how to import it because the library's setup instructions assume that you're using a build system.
I sometimes like to npm install the library even if I'm not planning to use Node to build my library at all — I'll just create a new temp folder, npm install there, and then delete it when I'm done.

posts.title: A One Way Bridge
posts.summary: Hotz proposes a satirical 'one way bridge' between Bitcoin and Ethereum where users send Bitcoin to an unrecoverable address in exchange for BBTC tokens. The bridge works by proving your Bitcoin transaction was included in the blockchain, then minting corresponding tokens on Ethereum. The catch is that no one has the private key to the receiving address, so the Bitcoin is permanently destroyed. The post is a tongue-in-cheek commentary on how crypto value is ultimately based on collective belief rather than any intrinsic backing.
posts.key_insight: Cryptocurrency value is ultimately a social construct based on collective belief, making a 'bridge' that destroys Bitcoin for unbacked tokens logically equivalent to any other crypto asset—if people believe in it.

topQuotes.quote: That's okay, they have been safely bridged to Ethereum and you have received your BBTC token.
Is it really that simple? Answer: yes, but only if people believe it is.
I'll note, nobody has the private key to 3bridgebridgebridgebridgebridge, so there is no way to recover your original Bitcoin.

posts.title: Blogging through the decades
posts.summary: Cantrill reflects on twenty years of blogging, tracing his journey from Sun Microsystems' pioneering blogs.sun.com initiative through WordPress purgatory to finally hosting his blog on Oxide's own cloud computer. He recounts how Sun's explicit trust in employees to blog publicly created extraordinary technical content, and how blogging became essential for documenting the human stories behind projects like DTrace, ZFS, Manta, and Oxide. The piece culminates in advice for aspiring bloggers: write from the heart, because even if no one else reads it, your future self will thank you.
posts.key_insight: The most enduring technical writing reflects the humanity in our endeavor—write from the heart, and your future self will thank you for the authentic record of your journey.

topQuotes.quote: This WordPress site had the curse of being just good enough.

There was so much great content that the only downside – such as it was – was that it was shackled to a listing corporate vessel. And when that vessel sank, much of the content went to a watery grave.
The beauty of blogging is that you needn't be regular and you also needn't be on topic: you can write only when you have something to say – and you can say whatever you want.

posts.title: Obscurity is the Enemy
posts.summary: The post argues that professional obscurity is the greatest obstacle to career success, especially in tight job markets. Reis emphasizes that networking must be cultivated continuously through genuine contribution rather than transactional outreach when desperate for opportunities.
posts.key_insight: Professional networks must be built through consistent, genuine contribution over time—not cultivated desperately when you need a job.

topQuotes.quote: Often, people neglect building and maintaining their network until they need to call in some favors. By then it's too late. Their network is stale and people can smell their transactional nature like roadkill.
For most people, their biggest enemy is obscurity. The simple and harsh fact is most people don't know you and very few care.
Obscurity is the enemy you must defeat at all costs.

posts.title: The Collective Marshmallow Test
posts.summary: Hotz uses an extended marshmallow test metaphor to argue that society faces a collective action problem where individual incentives undermine group outcomes. He connects post-election politics to economic anxiety, arguing that wokeism appeals to those who haven't exceeded their parents economically as a form of 'moral superiority' cope. He laments that wealth redistribution is necessary but trusts neither government nor crypto to do it fairly. The post reflects his growing isolation from hustle culture and crypto while defending his long-term commitment to comma.ai and tinygrad.
posts.key_insight: Society's collective action problems stem from misaligned incentives and the erosion of shame, while proposed solutions like government redistribution or crypto only make the map worse without changing the underlying territory.

topQuotes.quote: Wokeism appeals to people who have not exceeded their parents economically.
Money isn't real! It's not the territory, it's the map! And changing the map doesn't change the territory, it just makes the map shittier.
In order to justify that they have moved up in the world, they cling to a cult of moral superiority.

posts.title: My 2024 thoughts on the state of social media
posts.summary: Martin Fowler reflects on the fragmented state of social media two years after Elon Musk's Twitter takeover. He describes his preference for curated feeds over algorithmic suggestions and uses social media primarily to announce articles rather than for social interaction. While Twitter persists, its importance has declined as users migrated to platforms like Bluesky and LinkedIn. Fowler finds LinkedIn most effective for reaching readers despite its corporate atmosphere, while the Fediverse hasn't gained expected traction. He expresses hope for open protocols like Bluesky's AT protocol to provide resilience against future platform instability.
posts.key_insight: The fragmentation of social media after Twitter's decline has made open protocols like Bluesky's AT protocol increasingly valuable as insurance against future platform instability.

topQuotes.quote: Too many people found that choosing a server was too awkward, and that the advantages of federation were outweighed by the complexities of its usage.
I consider any post I make on social media to be ephemeral - anything I write that's meant to be long-lasting I keep on this site.
I have little control over my feed, and my notifications are full of 'someone liked your post' events.

posts.title: Obsessive problem solving followed by aimless wandering
posts.summary: DHH reflects on his creative process, arguing that his best work comes from intense, obsessive sprints on a single problem followed by periods of aimless wandering. He traces how his recent major projects — Omakub, Kamal, cloud departure, and Rails 8 — each emerged from curiosity-driven exploration rather than planned roadmaps. He argues that keeping your schedule free of recurring commitments is essential to being available when the next obsessive opportunity arrives. The post concludes that not knowing what's next is a feature, not a bug, because the best work emerges from following hunches rather than planning destinations.
posts.key_insight: The best creative and technical breakthroughs come not from planning but from keeping your mind and schedule open enough to follow obsessive hunches when they arrive.

topQuotes.quote: My best work is a series of sprints punctuated by periods of wandering.
At the executive level, almost all big leaps forward are sprints inspired by a hunch.
That to me is the best argument for making sure my plate isn't full of half-eaten commitments.

posts.title: Remembering Charles Beeler
posts.summary: Bryan Cantrill and Steve Tuck pay tribute to Charles Beeler, a venture capitalist who passed away from cancer. Beeler was an early investor in both Joyent and Oxide Computer, distinguished by his hands-on approach during difficult times and his genuine enthusiasm for creators and innovators. The post highlights how Beeler persistently encouraged people to do their best work, including pushing Cantrill to deliver a candid keynote that became one of his most meaningful talks.
posts.key_insight: The best investors distinguish themselves not by picking winners but by rolling up their sleeves during hard times and persistently encouraging founders to do ambitious, honest work.

topQuotes.quote: When companies hit challenging times, some investors will hide—but Charles was the kind of investor to figure out how to fix what was broken.
If the Joyent relationship with node.js had become strained, so much more the reason to speak candidly about it!
You were the best of venture capital. Closer to the heart, you were a terrific friend to us both.

posts.title: 15+ Companies Using DuckDB in Production: A Comprehensive Guide
posts.summary: This comprehensive guide catalogs 15+ companies using DuckDB in production across five key categories: zero-copy virtualized SQL, lightweight single-node compute, pipeline processing, embedded interactive apps, and enterprise security. The post demonstrates DuckDB's versatility through concrete case studies showing dramatic performance improvements, from Watershed's 10x faster analytics to FinQore's pipeline reduction from 8 hours to 8 minutes, and Okta's processing of 7.5 trillion security records. The author argues that DuckDB's simplicity, embedded nature, and performance make it uniquely positioned to simplify data architectures across diverse use cases, from browser-based analytics via WebAssembly to replacing expensive cloud data warehouses.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB has evolved from a lightweight embedded database into a production-ready analytics engine that dramatically simplifies data architectures while delivering order-of-magnitude performance improvements across diverse enterprise use cases.

topQuotes.quote: Modern computers are powerful enough that many organizations can effectively run their analytics workloads on a single machine, making DuckDB an attractive option for those seeking a more straightforward, more maintainable data stack without sacrificing performance or features.
This approach dramatically reduced their data processing costs from $2,000/day with Snowflake while maintaining system robustness and security.

In six months, their defensive cyber operations team processed 7.5 trillion records across 130 million files using thousands of concurrent DuckDB instances, handling data spikes from 1.5 TB to 50 TB per day without infrastructure changes.

posts.title: House rules in Fortnite
posts.summary: DHH explains why Fortnite is his family's favorite game, arguing it teaches kids valuable life skills through cooperative squad play. He enforces two simple house rules — stay together and no complaining — which teach teamwork and emotional regulation. Unlike competitive family games like Mario Kart, Fortnite puts the whole family on the same team working toward shared objectives. He concludes that not all screen time is equal, and that well-chosen games can be remarkably effective teaching tools.
posts.key_insight: Cooperative video games with enforced rules about teamwork and emotional control can be powerful tools for teaching children discipline, collaboration, and resilience.

topQuotes.quote: If you want to play more, and get better, you better start tempering your frustrations. Wonderful life skill to develop early.
All screen time is not created equal.
If you get all pissy and blamey when you're knocked out, the session ends, because nobody wants to listen to that (especially dad!).

posts.title: Disambiguating iPhone Model Names That Have the ‘S’ Suffix
posts.summary: Gruber examines the typographic ambiguity created by Apple's 'S' suffix on iPhone model names (3GS, 4S, 5S, 6S, XS), particularly when styled with small caps in HTML. He discovered that Apple Newsroom used a lowercase 's' inside a small-caps span for 'iPhone Xs', which renders identically to an uppercase 'S' in small caps but produces a lowercase 's' when copy-pasted — making it look like a plural 'iPhone X' rather than the model name 'iPhone XS'. He proposes Apple should use uppercase 'S' in the HTML source so copy-pasting yields the unambiguous 'iPhone XS'. He concludes by noting relief that Apple has moved away from S-suffixed model names.
posts.key_insight: Apple's use of lowercase 's' in small-caps HTML spans for iPhone model names like 'XS' creates unnecessary ambiguity that could be trivially fixed by using uppercase 'S' in the source markup, since small caps renders both identically but only uppercase survives copy-paste.

topQuotes.quote: For chrissake you're reading a footnote in a persnickety post regarding best practices in HTML markup for disambiguating product names in uncommon edge cases.
It's a mistake, and a suggestion of low literacy, to erroneously use the apostrophe-s sequence to pluralize words or names where the proper way to spell the plural is to just add s.
Is that the iPhone XS, styled with a lowercase s? Or are they referring to 2017's iPhone X, and pluralizing it with the lowercase s?

posts.title: Too much therapy at work
posts.summary: DHH argues that therapy culture has infiltrated the workplace to its detriment, drawing from a personal experience where a company meeting devolved into a group therapy session. He contends that while emotions at work are natural, the expectation that managers should process employees' feelings during work hours is misguided. He points out that licensed therapists operate under strict ethical boundaries that can't be replicated in workplace settings. DHH concludes that the rise of therapeutic language and methods in corporate culture has made people more fragile and less capable of handling normal workplace adversity.
posts.key_insight: Workplace therapy culture undermines resilience by importing therapeutic methods into a context that lacks the ethical boundaries and professional competence required to use them responsibly.

topQuotes.quote: Get therapy out of the office and let's hand it back to the shrinks.
It's a natural extension of "holding the space", of too much navel-gazing "mindfulness", of a posture of nurture and care that borders the patronizing.
An increase that's making people more fragile, more precious, more incapable of coping with the most basic expectations of disappointments or adversity on the job.

posts.title: The Quality Paradox
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that the tech industry's obsession with speed and arbitrary deadlines has led to a cargo-cult adoption of Agile that sacrifices quality for output. He contends that slowing down to build quality into processes—through proper testing, data modeling, and established conventions—ultimately leads to faster long-term productivity and less technical debt.
posts.key_insight: Organizations must resist the pressure to ship quickly at all costs and instead invest in quality practices upfront, recognizing that the short-term slowdown pays dividends through reduced rework and technical debt.

topQuotes.quote: Agile and XOps have both become ceremonial memes. Being 'Agile' often means hitting delivery deadlines, even if it introduces error-prone and buggy features.
A policy of 'Quality - If Time Permits' will assure that no quality at all sneaks into the product.
Every time you ship something subpar, you put one more chip on the debt pile. At some point, you'll either pay down those chips or burn the pile to the ground.

posts.title: New microblog with TILs
posts.summary: Julia Evans describes adding a new TIL (Today I Learned) section to her blog for saving interesting tools and facts she'd otherwise only post on social media. The motivation was that useful links and tips she shared on Mastodon/Bluesky were getting lost because she couldn't remember them later. She set up a lightweight system with custom styling, a Rake task for quick posting, and a separate RSS feed. She also reflects on the POSSE philosophy, concluding she prefers letting most social media posts be ephemeral while selectively archiving specific categories on her own site.
posts.key_insight: Not everything needs to be a full blog post or permanently archived — creating a lightweight TIL section fills the gap between ephemeral social media and polished blog posts.

topQuotes.quote: You might think "julia, why not use bookmarks??" but I have been failing to use bookmarks for my whole life and I don't see that changing ever, putting things in public is for whatever reason much easier for me

For me part of the appeal of social media is that it's a little bit ephemeral. I can post polls or questions or observations or jokes and then they can just kind of fade away as they become less relevant.
I find it a lot easier to identify specific categories of things that I actually want to have on a Real Website That I Own

posts.title: What if My Tribe Is Wrong?
posts.summary: Armin reflects on learning to wish others well, even when he is skeptical or disagrees, and how success made generosity easier. The harder problem for him is moral disagreement, where support can feel like complicity and trigger insecurity about his own values. He wrestles with the fear that his “tribe” might be wrong, especially in the context of modern politics and figures like Trump and Musk, while rejecting simplistic comparisons to historical extremes. He argues that caricature and side‑taking erase nuance and make real dialogue harder, yet he cannot ignore the concrete harms of certain policies. The conclusion is a commitment to listening and understanding while standing firm on values, without capitulating or excusing harm.
posts.key_insight: The post argues for holding nuance and empathy without surrendering moral clarity, especially when evidence of harm complicates charitable interpretation.

topQuotes.quote: Yes, not quite Hitler, but still deeply problematic for personal freedoms.
Supporting someone — even passively — can feel like tacit approval, and that can be unsettling.
Neither are “literal Hitler”.

posts.title: No Better Room
posts.summary: Stancil recounts election night from the Harris campaign data team, where extensive technical preparation became irrelevant once the loss was obvious. He argues that the unseen work and contingency plans still mattered as evidence of people fighting on, and that being in the room—win or lose—changes you. The piece ends by urging readers to act, not only to affect outcomes but because participating is less hollow than spectating.
posts.key_insight: Even when the data work can’t change the outcome, doing it alongside others in the fight gives the effort meaning and changes the people involved.

topQuotes.quote: They were flawlessly delivered disastrous news all night.
Never before have I been involved in trying to answer a more pressing question for a more important audience—and never before has the answer been worse.
It is easier to lose on the field than in the stands.

posts.title: How It Went
posts.summary: Gruber writes a deeply personal essay weaving together three threads: his mother's recent death from cancer, his father's loss and recovery of his wedding ring, and the devastating 2024 election night. His mother died suddenly and peacefully in June before cancer could take its toll, leaving his 86-year-old father — a lifelong Democrat, deeply honest man, and eternal optimist — to carry on alone. On Election Day, his father discovered he'd lost his wedding ring, adding a small heartbreak to an already anxious day. Gruber narrates election night through Steve Kornacki's data-driven county-by-county analysis on MSNBC, watching Trump's victory become increasingly inevitable. The morning after the gut-punch result, his father called with unexpected joy: he'd found the ring in the street by his car, nestled in dry leaves. The essay ends with Gruber in tears — not from the election loss, but from the recovered ring and the love it represents.
posts.key_insight: In the shadow of political defeat and personal loss, the recovery of a 52-year-old wedding ring becomes an unlikely vessel for hope and enduring love.

topQuotes.quote: Steve Kornacki isn't the weatherman. He's our finger in the air.

His entire life he's seen the Democrats as the party of and for the people. The party for working men and women. The party of equality and justice and minding your own goddamn business what people do in their private lives.
A gut punch is not a sleep aid.

posts.title: It Doesn’t End
posts.summary: Gruber reflects on the 2024 election results through the lens of Hunter S. Thompson's 1972 writing about Nixon's landslide reelection, drawing parallels between the despair of that era and the present. He revisits his own 2008 post celebrating Obama's victory, where he optimistically declared 'it ends here, today,' now acknowledging that sentiment as naive. He contextualizes Trump's 2024 win by noting it was far smaller than both Obama's victories and Nixon's 1972 landslide, arguing the situation is bad but not unprecedented. The post concludes that progress is real but never permanent, always met with backlash, and urges readers to keep perspective and persevere.
posts.key_insight: American political progress is real but cyclical — despair and hope trade places across decades, and neither landslide victories nor devastating losses are ever the final word.

topQuotes.quote: Barack Obama ran in 2008 opposing gay marriage. The Democrat. That was only 16 years ago. The iPhone was already out.
If every damn word of that doesn't ring true to you today, you're deaf.
That 2008 Obama win was euphoric. It remains our nation's high-water mark.

posts.title: I Wonder
posts.summary: Gruber collects the near-identical congratulatory statements posted by Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Sundar Pichai, Satya Nadella, and Tim Cook after Trump's 2024 election victory. He lets the hollow corporate boilerplate speak for itself before closing with a blistering commentary on the lack of dignity in these performances. He singles out Cook's exclamation mark and the technically incorrect use of 'President Trump' for someone not yet inaugurated. The post is a short, furious indictment of Big Tech leaders falling over themselves to genuflect to power.
posts.key_insight: The interchangeable sycophancy of tech's most powerful CEOs toward a returning Trump reveals that immense wealth and corporate power offer no protection against — and may even accelerate — the loss of dignity.

topQuotes.quote: I wonder too, what taste Cheetos-dusted 78-year-old testicles leave in one's mouth.
Whatever the flavor, I hope it lingers.
I wonder how much Cook dithered over that cheerful-looking exclamation mark.

posts.title: ...and now we see what is possible
posts.summary: Hotz reflects on Trump's 2024 victory and whether Republican control of all branches of government represents real power or merely the appearance of it. Drawing from his experience watching Elon Musk's Twitter takeover, he questions whether the federal government can be similarly restructured. He speculates about whether a 'deep state' actually controls power, and argues that the only way to prove genuine control would be cutting the government budget by over 50%. The post is ultimately about the nature of institutional power and whether it can be dismantled by those who nominally hold authority.
posts.key_insight: Real power is demonstrated not by holding office or titles, but by the ability to fundamentally dismantle and restructure institutions - if you can't shut something down, you don't actually control it.

topQuotes.quote: If you can't shut it down, you don't control it.
Hello, we are the Deep State. Here are the bounds in which you can act. Step outside them, and we will kill you.

It's possible this will be another completely boring presidency. Stupid squabbles over normal stupid American Overton window things, and the same trillions of dollars going to the same people, just slightly more red.

posts.title: The spells are spent
posts.summary: DHH celebrates what he sees as the collapse of progressive social accusations (racist, misogynist, fascist) as effective political tools, using the 2024 US presidential election as evidence. He argues these labels were overused to the point of meaninglessness, comparing them to rubber checks that eventually bounce. He frames the decline of DEI and 'woke orthodoxy' as the end of a preference falsification where people went along with prevailing norms out of fear. He concludes this is a universal win regardless of one's opinion on Trump.
posts.key_insight: The overuse of social stigma labels without substantiation has bankrupted their credibility, and the 2024 election proved the cultural moment that sustained them has passed.

topQuotes.quote: They just don't work any more, those baseless accusations that anyone we disagree with is a racist, misogynist, fascist.

Being professionally offended by every little thing, seeing micro-aggressions around every corner, and rendering the entire world through a narrow oppressed/oppressor lens is back to the niche disposition once more.
When even the most mundane political or moral positions were able to earn one of those *ist-y labels, the entire enterprise of throwing them around was bound to go bankrupt.

posts.title: BI-as-Code and the New Era of GenBI
posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that Generative Business Intelligence (GenBI) represents a paradigm shift in how business users interact with data, enabled by the declarative BI-as-Code approach where dashboards and metrics are defined in YAML rather than hidden in GUI configurations. The key insight is that declarative definitions provide the semantic foundation AI models need to understand business logic, relationships, and metrics. GenBI promises to overcome the traditional bottleneck of data teams by allowing natural language conversations to generate dashboards and metrics. Späti positions this as the next evolution of self-service BI, with tools like Rill Developer already implementing these capabilities through LLM-powered dashboard generation.
posts.key_insight: Declarative BI-as-Code is the prerequisite for GenBI because AI models need explicit, machine-readable definitions of business logic and metrics - not opaque GUI configurations - to generate meaningful dashboards and analytics.

topQuotes.quote: While AI can control visual interfaces, this approach is inefficient for complex business logic. The data model joins, metric aggregations and bus matrix require explicit declarations that AI can parse and understand; print-screened images are insufficient.
This is usually where technical data professionals care least, so a trained model that helps us would significantly boost productivity.
At its core, GenBI delivers an unreasonably effective human interface, where we iterate quickly, based on BI-as-Code.

posts.title: Using the Strangler Fig with Mobile Apps
posts.summary: This article demonstrates how Thoughtworks applied the Strangler Fig pattern to incrementally modernize a large legacy mobile application, avoiding the risks of a complete rewrite. By embedding React Native micro-apps within an existing native application and using domain-driven design to identify 'points of interception,' teams were able to replace functionality piece by piece. The approach leverages a modular architecture where each micro-app encapsulates a domain with its own Backend for Frontend acting as an anti-corruption layer. Results from the proof of concept showed first value delivered in 1 month (versus 2 years for a full rewrite) and a 50% reduction in median cycle time compared to the legacy application.
posts.key_insight: The Strangler Fig pattern, traditionally used for backend systems, can effectively modernize legacy mobile applications by embedding domain-encapsulated micro-apps that incrementally replace functionality while delivering value immediately rather than waiting years for a complete rewrite.

topQuotes.quote: A mobile application was never allowed to get so complicated, that it couldn't be torn down, split out or started from scratch again.
Taking an incremental approach to app replacement then is the key to avoiding the pitfalls associated with 'big bang releases'.

Our focus was not on delivering the most beautiful or cohesive full app experience. It was about obtaining confidence both in the stability of the iterative replacement pattern and also in how well the new product was being received.

posts.title: What you know that just ain't so
posts.summary: DHH argues that business decisions are inherently uncertain and that even successful experiments don't produce universal truths. He warns against treating past wins or losses as definitive proof of what works, since most business experiments are imperfect and context-dependent. The post cautions that locking in conclusions from past bets—whether wins or losses—makes you a prisoner of flawed narratives. Instead, he advocates maintaining an open mind that treats all past results as provisional and subject to reinterpretation.
posts.key_insight: Business outcomes are bets won or lost, not truths discovered—hardening them into rigid narratives closes you off to future opportunities.

topQuotes.quote: The key is to remember that what you won was a bet, not a trophy made of truth.
The hallmark of a good business person is their ability to make calculated bets based on half a story and a hunch.
If you don't, you'll eventually become a prisoner to every bet you've made.

posts.title: Kottke on the Art and Power of Hypertextual Writing
posts.summary: Gruber reflects on Jason Kottke's analysis of the NYT editorial board's densely hyperlinked one-paragraph endorsement against Trump, agreeing that it exemplifies the unique power of hypertextual writing. He argues that hypertext links function as an information-density multiplier, adding a third dimension to traditionally two-dimensional prose. Gruber notes that after 20+ years of writing Daring Fireball, hypertext feels like his natural mode of writing. He praises the Times editorial as remarkable precisely because the Times rarely writes in this web-native style. He also laments that social media platforms have largely kneecapped hypertextual writing by only supporting raw URLs rather than inline hyperlinks, calling preview cards a regression in information density.
posts.key_insight: Hypertext links are an information-density multiplier unique to the web, adding depth to prose even when readers never click them, yet social media platforms have largely abandoned this power in favor of raw URLs and preview cards.

topQuotes.quote: It's more like texting in public than writing for the real web.
It's not hypertext writing that now feels like a special form of writing. It's just writing. It's non-hypertext writing that now feels slightly weird to me. Limiting.
If anything, turning links into preview cards significantly decreases information density. That feels like a regression, not progress.

posts.title: DuckDB Is Not a Data Warehouse
posts.summary: Riccomini argues that despite DuckDB's popularity among data engineers for its portability, speed, and ability to query remote storage, it fundamentally cannot serve as an enterprise data warehouse. The deployment model of installing DuckDB on individual laptops and granting bucket access is untenable for organizations where everyone from PMs to finance needs data access. While most queries are small, the most valuable enterprise queries—financial reconciliation, recommendations, advertising—require scale DuckDB cannot provide. MotherDuck is building the centralized deployment and scaling layer DuckDB needs, but this essentially makes them another cloud data warehouse competing with giants like Snowflake and Databricks. DuckDB excels as middleware, similar to SQLite, but enterprises should view it as a tool rather than a warehouse replacement.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB is excellent middleware for columnar analytics, but its deployment model and scalability limitations mean it cannot replace enterprise data warehouses—the most valuable queries require scale that only centralized cloud solutions can provide.

topQuotes.quote: As much as MotherDuck would like to be the DuckDB vendor, they're a cloud data warehouse that just happens to use DuckDB.
MotherDuck has picked a fight with some of the nastiest apex predators out there: Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks.
It's untenable to install DuckDB on everyone's laptop, grant everyone access to data lake buckets, and ask them to run queries from the CLI.

posts.title: Influence Agents
posts.summary: George Hotz dismisses typical AI doom scenarios as Hollywood nonsense, instead presenting a more grounded concern: hyper-personalized AI influence agents. He traces the economics of advertising—noting Google earns $428 per American annually—and extrapolates to a future where AI agents study your behavior for years to craft perfectly targeted manipulation campaigns. The endgame isn't rogue superintelligence; it's teams of tireless, aligned AI agents whose sole purpose is altering your behavior, and the only reason this isn't happening now is that you're not yet worth the compute cost.
posts.key_insight: The real AI threat isn't rogue superintelligence—it's perfectly aligned influence agents that know you better than you know yourself, and the only thing stopping them is that you're not yet worth the compute.

topQuotes.quote: The only reason you don't have 10 agents doing this right now is that you aren't worth it.
Imagine a room of 10 CIA agents running at 1000x real time (they have 16 hours for each minute of yours). And their only job is to influence you.
Most AI doom scenarios are nonsense. The idea of a rogue computer behaving in a way no humans want never made sense to me, only to Hollywood who doesn't know all that much about computers.

posts.title: High Trust Society
posts.summary: Hotz argues that America has become a low-trust society with no path to restoration regardless of election outcomes, contrasting it with China where he perceives genuine civic pride and government delivery of tangible results. He dismisses American defenses of 'freedom' as hollow when basic security isn't provided, citing anarcho-tyranny where criminals operate freely while citizens face consequences for self-defense. He concludes that fixing American society requires either generational change or civil war, neither of which he wants to witness. His solution is to bet on the Internet as the next great empire and build for that future while physically locating in high-trust places like Hong Kong.
posts.key_insight: When a society loses the ability to deliver tangible improvements to citizens' lives and can't even maintain basic order, trust collapses irreparably, and the only rational response is to build for whatever comes next.

topQuotes.quote: The American empire is over, the Chinese empire is next, and the Internet is the empire after that. We build for that world.
A government delivering growth and better lives to people is one worth trusting. A government that kills squirrels that get too famous on social media, not as much.
When Americans are asked about this, they will defend some nebulous concept of freedom, and I ask, freedom to what?

posts.title: Asking Good Questions at Conferences
posts.summary: Joe Reis offers practical advice on asking good questions at conferences, emphasizing that poorly crafted questions waste everyone's time and can damage your professional reputation. He provides specific do's and don'ts, stressing brevity, relevance, and open-ended phrasing while warning against self-promotion, rambling, and contrarian showboating.
posts.key_insight: Asking questions at conferences is a skill that requires self-awareness and discipline—your question should serve the dialogue and audience, not your ego or sales pitch.

topQuotes.quote: If you do this to me, I will mercilessly mock you until you cry or leave.
Don't try to show off by 'schooling' the speaker or panel. Don't be contrarian just to look cool in front of your peers. This is a no-win situation for you. The speaker has the stage. You don't.
Promote your company, product, or service. Nobody - I repeat, nobody - cares.

posts.title: Fragile
posts.summary: Stancil argues that individual votes in U.S. presidential elections are mathematically insignificant, yet people still vote because they want to participate in a shared democratic ritual. That belief is a fragile “noble lie,” and preserving it—especially during a contentious election—is essential to keeping democracy functioning.
posts.key_insight: Democracy depends less on the literal impact of any one vote than on the shared faith that participation matters.

topQuotes.quote: The moral sense that our vote is important is not an inherent truth about democracy, but a noble lie we tell ourselves because, at least for now, we believe in its possibility.
No ordinary citizen has ever cast a meaningful vote in a presidential election.
It is that spell, and that spell alone, that makes us participate—and it us participating that makes it work.

posts.title: ASCII control characters in my terminal
posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the 33 ASCII control characters and how they actually work in modern terminals. She categorizes them into OS-handled codes (like Ctrl-C for SIGINT), literal keypresses (Enter, Tab), readline shortcuts, and application-specific bindings. The post reveals surprising details like how there are only 33 possible control codes, how Ctrl-M is literally identical to Enter, and how the original ASCII names bear little relationship to modern terminal behavior. She discusses the complexity of canonical vs noncanonical mode, the configurable nature of these codes via stty, and the surprisingly deep history behind something as simple as the Backspace key.
posts.key_insight: The 33 ASCII control characters are a messy historical artifact where telegraph-era codes, OS signals, readline shortcuts, and application bindings all collide in the same tiny namespace with no logical organization.

topQuotes.quote: I've used the terminal pretty successfully every day for the last 20 years without knowing literally any of this.
50% of the time the name in ASCII has no actual relationship to what that code does on UNIX systems today.
There's no real structure to which codes are in which categories, they're all just kind of randomly scattered because this evolved organically.

posts.title: Make It Ephemeral: Software Should Decay and Lose Data
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that most software is built to remember indefinitely, but the deliberate ability to forget is a stronger and more humane design choice. He contrasts digital persistence with the physical world, where objects naturally decay, and that decay provides useful signals about relevance and age. Persistent SaaS artifacts (dashboards, notebooks, docs) become stale, cluttered, and misleading, especially in shared environments. He contends the barrier is not technical difficulty but a mistaken belief that permanence is always a benefit. The conclusion: software should be “ephemeral by default,” with decay, hiding, or scheduled deletion to keep systems useful and current.
posts.key_insight: Software should be designed to decay and forget by default because permanence creates clutter, misleads users, and hides the real age and relevance of information.

topQuotes.quote: I'm not convinced that the work required to implement any of those things is why it does not exist.
Creating software that remembers is easy, but designing software that deliberately “forgets” is a bit more complex.
When data is kept indefinitely by default, it changes our relationship with that software.

posts.title: DuckDB Won by Refusing to Scale Out
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses how DuckDB was built as a radical bet on single-node, in-process analytics over distributed scale-out architectures. Born from research at CWI Amsterdam, DuckDB combined state-of-the-art vectorized query execution with SQLite-like ease of use, after an earlier experiment (MonetDB Lite) proved the in-process model had demand. The 1.0 release focused on storage format stability and backward compatibility. Hannes explains how DuckDB's SQL dialect innovations like GROUP BY ALL have been widely copied, and discusses the emerging lakehouse ecosystem with mixed feelings about potential re-centralization of data access behind APIs.
posts.key_insight: By refusing to distribute and betting everything on single-node performance, DuckDB exploited algorithmic advantages unavailable to distributed systems and captured the 99% of users who never needed Google-scale infrastructure.

topQuotes.quote: The whole field was chasing whales and the one customer in the world that has Google scale is Google.
Many DuckDB jobs now complete faster than a Spark cluster can start up.
The fundamental hill that we were willing to die on was to bet on single node and scale up instead of scale out.

posts.title: America's Future
posts.summary: Hotz argues that America faces three possible decline scenarios: European-style economic stagnation, Russian-style oligarchic capture, or South African-style collapse driven by social resentment. He observes that America's tech dominance depends entirely on attracting skilled immigrants to a merit-based society, but rising crime, difficult immigration policies, and government captured by corporate interests threaten this advantage. After spending three months in Hong Kong, he's seriously considering relocating tinygrad there, finding the society more functional and the government more competent. He sees a potential path forward only if Trump wins and Elon Musk influences policy toward reduced government spending and easier skilled immigration.
posts.key_insight: America's tech dominance is entirely dependent on attracting global talent to a merit-based society, and if quality of life deteriorates while immigration stays difficult, that talent—and the industry—will simply move elsewhere.

topQuotes.quote: The combination of unskilled immigration with expensive social welfare is suicide for your country.
Our society wasn't brought down by the tyranny in 1984 or the hedonism in Brave New World, but instead by the resentment in Atlas Shrugged.
The state is mostly just a cow to milk for money by large corporations and friends of the state.

posts.title: In Defense, I Swear, of the Magic Mouse’s Charging Port Placement
posts.summary: Gruber defends Apple's widely mocked decision to place the Magic Mouse charging port on the bottom, arguing it's a deliberate design trade-off rather than an oversight. He contends Apple prioritizes visual symmetry and the wireless-only experience, noting that a front-facing port would compromise the mouse's sleek appearance. He compares it to Apple's historical commitment to the single-button mouse and argues that the 9-year unchanged design proves Apple sees it as a feature, not a mistake. He acknowledges the inconvenience but notes it only affects users every few months, with battery warnings provided. In a disarming twist, Gruber admits he doesn't actually use the Magic Mouse himself, preferring a cheap Lenovo mouse for comfort and customization.
posts.key_insight: The Magic Mouse's bottom charging port is a deliberate, opinionated design choice that prioritizes form and wireless purity over charging convenience — and Apple's refusal to change it for nine years proves they see it as a feature, not a flaw.

topQuotes.quote: Apple sticking with this design in the face of vociferous peanut gallery mockery reminds me of the company's noble commitment to a single-button mouse.
With this design, the mouse looks better 100 percent of the time it's in use, and it looks a bit silly every few months when you need to charge it.
The Magic Mouse charging port placement is an opinionated design, not an absurd design.

posts.title: Using less memory to look up IP addresses in Mess With DNS
posts.summary: Julia Evans describes her journey to reduce memory usage in Mess With DNS, which was getting OOM killed on a 465MB VM. The main culprit was a 117MB in-memory IP-to-ASN lookup database. She tried three approaches: SQLite (worked but was 500x slower), a trie (used even more memory), and optimizing her existing array-based binary search. The winning solution combined deduplicating ASN name/country strings and switching from net.IP to Go's newer netip.Addr type, cutting memory from 117MB to 46MB. She also honestly describes how the real debugging process was far messier than the linear narrative suggests.
posts.key_insight: Simple data structure optimizations like deduplicating strings and using properly-sized types can dramatically reduce memory usage without sacrificing the simplicity and speed of a basic binary search approach.

topQuotes.quote: I always try to take my actual debugging process (total chaos) and make it seem more linear and understandable because the reality is just too annoying to write down.

I could very easily afford to pay for a VM with 1GB of memory, but I feel like 512MB really should be enough (and really that 256MB should be enough!) so I'd rather stay inside that constraint. It's kind of a fun puzzle.
This database by itself used about 117MB of memory, and a simple du told me that was too much – the original text files were only 37MB!

posts.title: Profiles in Cowardice: Owner Jeff Bezos and Publisher William Lewis of The Washington Post
posts.summary: Gruber excoriates Jeff Bezos and Washington Post publisher William Lewis for killing the paper's presidential endorsement of Kamala Harris, days after LA Times owner Patrick Soon-Shiong made a similar move. He dismantles Lewis's claim that this is a return to pre-1976 tradition, noting the Post began endorsing precisely because of Watergate. Gruber assembles a devastating chorus of condemnation from Post insiders including Woodward and Bernstein, former editor Marty Baron, and 17 current columnists. He argues the non-endorsements are worse than forced Trump endorsements because they create plausible suspicion, and frames Bezos's capitulation as anticipatory obedience to an authoritarian threat. Gruber announces he has cancelled his Post subscription and urges readers to do the same.
posts.key_insight: Bezos killing the Post's endorsement before Trump even won the election demonstrates that authoritarianism's power is freely given through anticipatory obedience, and the supposed institutional guardrails are just people who are already collapsing.

topQuotes.quote: What's the point of having so much 'fuck you' money if you're afraid to tell a petty tyrant like Donald Trump to pound sand?
Liberal newspapers breaking tradition to not endorse anyone is worse than if their owners had forced them to endorse Trump instead.
A Trump endorsement from the LA Times or Washington Post would be absurd. No one, not even the derpiest of MAGA trolls, would believe that. It would be like a steakhouse endorsing veganism.

posts.title: Venting and Constructive Communication
posts.summary: This post explores the distinction between venting and constructive communication, arguing that the key differentiator is whether our words serve our actual goals. Rocklin advocates for building mental models of conversation partners to craft language that will effectively influence them, rather than defaulting to emotionally satisfying but counterproductive venting. He acknowledges that venting is universal and normal, but notes it becomes increasingly inappropriate as one rises in organizational hierarchies because it damages the relationships essential for leadership. The post concludes that effective leaders learn to recognize the valuable insight behind their frustration and translate it into constructive communication tailored to their audience.
posts.key_insight: The difference between venting and constructive communication is simply asking whether your words will actually help you achieve your goal—and true leadership requires translating raw frustration into messages your partner can receive.

topQuotes.quote: These things might even be true but they're usually not constructive.
Will these words help me achieve my goal?
We're sacrificing our relationship (and thus any future ability to affect change) for immediate gratification of our frustration.

posts.title: Dont Be Evil
posts.summary: The post argues that companies (and people) often break ideals not because they are uniquely bad, but because staying true to them requires exceptional success and leverage. Google’s early “don’t be evil” era is framed as a byproduct of a wildly profitable machine, and the author connects this to Mode’s sale and broader startup pressures. Ambition can force compromises: the bigger the goal, the stronger the pull to recalibrate values and move closer to the line.
posts.key_insight: Ideals persist only when performance is elite enough to protect them; otherwise ambition and market pressure steadily force compromises.

topQuotes.quote: Deep down, Google probably had the same two values that every other company has: First, make money, and second, some other nice sounding stuff, in that order.
But idealism has a price, and most companies can’t afford it.

If every venture-backed company has two ordered values—make money, and some nice sounding stuff—they also have two unstated product values—build something that you can sell, and build the thing you want to sell.

posts.title: Vision Pro Bites Dog
posts.summary: Gruber pushes back against the narrative that Apple Vision Pro is a commercial flop by pointing out that actual sales (~420,000 units) are almost exactly in line with pre-launch expectations of fewer than 500,000 units. He notes that The Information's own reporter predicted these numbers in May 2023, making the same publication's framing of sales as a 'grand disappointment' disingenuous. Gruber argues that Apple launched Vision Pro for long-term strategic reasons, not short-term sales, given its $4,000 price tag, limited software, and physical constraints on manufacturing. He observes that Apple never launched a major advertising campaign, suggesting the company itself wasn't expecting mass-market adoption. The core argument is that media coverage is manufacturing a 'Man Bites Dog' story out of what is essentially 'Dog Bites Man' — expected results being reported as surprising failures.
posts.key_insight: Media coverage frames Vision Pro sales as a surprising failure, but the numbers almost exactly match pre-launch predictions — including those from the same publication now calling them disappointing.

topQuotes.quote: It's disingenuous, to say the least, for an October 2024 report to suggest that Vision Pro sales are surprisingly weak when they're almost exactly in line with uncannily accurate expectations set in a May 2023 report by the exact same reporter at the same publication.
It's almost as though — hear me out — Apple launched Vision Pro in 2023 for long-term strategic reasons, not with short-term sales in mind.
A headline like, say, 'Vision Pro Sales Are Exactly in Line With Expectations' is not going to hit people as a big story, but 'Apple Sharply Scales Back Production of Vision Pro' does.

posts.title: Virtual Machines Are Getting Better
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that virtual machines, containers, and serverless technologies are undergoing significant improvements that will reshape cloud computing. He highlights three key areas of advancement: GPU checkpointing (notably NVIDIA's cuda-checkpoint), unikernels enabling faster cold starts, and VM migration tools like Loophole Labs' Architect. These developments address longstanding pain points around slow cold starts, incomplete state snapshots, and poor GPU support. Riccomini predicts these improvements will drive multi-cloud adoption and potentially allow serverless functions to supplant traditional service-oriented architectures.
posts.key_insight: Recent advances in GPU checkpointing, unikernels, and VM migration are finally addressing the specific requirements of modern workloads, setting the stage for more flexible multi-cloud deployments and broader serverless adoption.

topQuotes.quote: Until recently, these technologies have offered fairly generic features. Consequently, they kind of work for many use cases, but truly work for relatively few.
Serverless functions might supplant service oriented applications, too.
Architect purports to migrate faster than a spot instance preemption occurs, which would allow for huge cost savings for many workloads.

posts.title: Ghostty 1.0 is Coming
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces that Ghostty, his terminal emulator project developed over nearly two years with 2,000 private beta testers, will be publicly released as open-source under MIT license in December 2024. The project aims to be the best drop-in replacement for existing terminal emulators by combining speed, rich features, and platform-native GUIs—a combination Hashimoto felt was unnecessarily absent in the market. The extensive private beta allowed him to prioritize family time after his first child while ensuring stability. Beyond 1.0, he envisions libghostty as a cross-platform library enabling a diverse ecosystem of terminal applications, and expanding terminal functionality to make text-based applications more attractive to build and use.
posts.key_insight: A terminal emulator can be fast, feature-rich, and platform-native simultaneously—users just didn't know they were settling for less until they experienced all three together.

topQuotes.quote: The most common feedback I've received from people before they've used Ghostty is skepticism that any of this makes a difference. The most common feedback I've received from people after they've used Ghostty is that they didn't realize how much of a difference it makes and they can't imagine going back.
I'm surprised how much criticism I received for this approach and the number of entitled people who seemed to believe that they were owed something just because I liked sharing my work and progress.
I am nostalgic for the days when software was released when it was ready.

posts.title: The Sordid Tale of Rudy Giuliani’s Yankees World Series Rings
posts.summary: Gruber digs into the backstory of Rudy Giuliani's four Yankees World Series rings, which are now subject to seizure in his defamation lawsuit. Drawing heavily on Wayne Barrett's 2007 Village Voice investigation, he details how Giuliani received the rings while mayor — likely in violation of New York's gift laws that bar officials from accepting gifts over $50 from entities doing business with the city. Giuliani paid only $16,000 for rings worth at least $200,000, and the timing of payments suggests they were retroactive cover rather than legitimate purchases. Gruber uses the episode as a microcosm of Giuliani's character and closes with a pointed comparison to Trump.
posts.key_insight: Giuliani's World Series rings scandal — receiving $200,000 in gifts while aggressively enforcing gift rules against others — perfectly encapsulates the self-serving hypocrisy that defined his career and foreshadowed the Trump presidency.

topQuotes.quote: Turns out, we eventually got exactly that kind of president, and might get him again. It's just that his name isn't Giuliani.
Needless to say, World Series rings were not available for purchase by anyone else, at any price.
I've quoted quite a bit here from Barrett's reporting, but there's so much more, all of it crooked as hell — a sordid tale of both large-scale graft and petty grift.

posts.title: Serendipity
posts.summary: This piece is a short poem about finding meaning in life through love and family. It begins with existence as empty and uncertain, marked by work and study that fail to fill an inner void. The turning point is a partner’s presence, which transforms darkness into light and leads to a shared life. From there, the poem celebrates marriage, children, and the everyday fullness they bring. It concludes with deep gratitude and a declaration that love gives existence its meaning.
posts.key_insight: The poem argues that love, partnership, and family transform an initially empty life into one filled with meaning and gratitude.

topQuotes.quote: Life begins. An empty page.
A hollow echo on an barren stage.
But then a hand, warm in the night, Turns the dark to softest light.

posts.title: So You Want to Be a Speaker?
posts.summary: Joe Reis shares his extensive experience with public speaking at tech conferences, breaking down the different types of events (community vs. commercial, vendor vs. vendor-agnostic), the significant work involved in preparing talks and managing travel, and the importance of understanding your value as a speaker. He emphasizes that speaking requires substantial time investment, can be emotionally draining due to travel demands, and speakers should increasingly expect compensation as their reputation grows.
posts.key_insight: Public speaking at tech conferences requires significant preparation and travel that most people underestimate, and speakers should advocate for fair compensation once their name carries value, while being wary of events that 'community-wash' their profit motives.

topQuotes.quote: You need to get a cut if they're getting a cut. Some events will gaslight you and say they don't pay speakers for some reason. Dig into this reason to understand if they're telling the truth or if they're full of shit. It's usually the latter.
Community-first means putting the community first, not one's pocketbook.
I don't think most people should do it because the ROI isn't there.

posts.title: Is Being Data Driven A Fad
posts.summary: The post argues that markets and companies may be drifting away from classical, data-driven valuation toward “vibes” and social consensus, using Trump Media as a case where fundamentals are irrelevant. It connects this to a broader shift in analytics culture: the data-driven movement delivered mixed results, and newer fashions like taste, founder instinct, and narrative are gaining ground. The core question is whether data-driven decision-making was a temporary vogue rather than an inevitable trajectory.
posts.key_insight: Both stock valuation and corporate decision-making may be less anchored in data than we assume, with social consensus and taste increasingly substituting for quantitative “fundamentals.”

topQuotes.quote: It’s a meme that trades on the Nasdaq; it’s a presidential election prediction market; it’s a commemorative collectable coin, sold as a soft con.
Do not value our business based on models and math and numbers, because we don’t use the numbers, we don’t know the numbers, we don’t track the numbers, and we definitely don’t audit the numbers.
A company’s hype factor may not be a red flag; it might be its only ground truth.

posts.title: DuckDB in the Wild
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents how DuckDB, an in-process analytical database, is being used in unexpected ways beyond its original interactive analysis purpose. He explains DuckDB's core design philosophy of end-to-end user experience and 'data confidence' — removing the terror of encountering messy datasets. He highlights three surprising deployment patterns: DuckDB as a pipeline component (e.g., Fivetran, Lambda functions), DuckDB on application servers replacing separate analytics infrastructure, and DuckDB on clients via desktop apps, mobile, and WebAssembly in browsers. His central thesis is that DuckDB liberates data processing from centralized server architectures, enabling data engines to run anywhere.
posts.key_insight: The future of data processing is not centralized server architectures but embeddable engines that can run anywhere — in pipelines, application servers, browsers, and mobile devices — eliminating the friction and scaling problems of traditional data infrastructure.

topQuotes.quote: Instead of the data warehouse, we have the data swamp. Here in the Netherlands we have experience with dealing with swamps. You drain them. But that's not what they do, they make it worse.
This is a kind of a departure from traditional work on data management engines, where it was kind of tradition to focus on the 47th join algorithm.
No matter what horrifying collection of files will be in front of you, you will be able to do something meaningful with it.

posts.title: Our cloud-exit savings will now top ten million over five years
posts.summary: DHH reports that 37signals' cloud exit savings have exceeded original estimates, projecting over $10 million in savings over five years.
