The 2024 cloud bill dropped from $3.2 million to $1.3 million annually, with the remaining spend on AWS S3 set to end next summer when they migrate to Pure Storage. The $700K hardware investment was fully recouped during the transition year. He argues the industry's all-cloud-everything mentality has peaked and companies should do the math on their cloud spending.
posts.key_insight: 37signals' cloud exit proved even more profitable than projected, saving over $10 million in five years while maintaining the same team size, challenging the industry assumption that cloud is always the right choice for established companies.

topQuotes.quote: The motto of the 2010s and early 2020s – all-cloud, everything, all the time – seems to finally have peaked. And thank heavens for that!
At 37signals, we're looking forward to literally deleting our AWS account come this summer.
There were no hidden dragons of additional workload associated with the exit that required us to balloon the team, as some spectators speculated when we announced it.

posts.title: The Enterprise Case for DuckDB: 5 Key Categories and Why Use It
posts.summary: DuckDB is an embedded analytical database that combines SQLite's simplicity with columnar-vectorized query execution for analytics workloads. The post categorizes DuckDB's enterprise value into five key use cases: interactive data apps, on-demand pipeline compute, lightweight SQL analytics, zero-copy SQL connector, and secure data handling. For enterprises, DuckDB offers significant benefits including reduced infrastructure costs, simpler deployment, and the ability to run complex analytics locally without network latency. The emerging 1.5-tier architecture, enabled by MotherDuck, allows the same DuckDB engine to run in both browser and cloud, fundamentally changing how analytical applications are built.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's unique position as an embedded analytical database enables enterprises to simplify infrastructure, reduce costs, and achieve faster insights by bringing compute closer to data and users.

topQuotes.quote: Loading CSVs and precisely correcting data types is still not a solved problem in 2024.
Simplify. Replace Apache Spark with DuckDB where possible. Spark is a complex setup, even more so for tuning and debugging.
Not many other databases can handle such a broad range of use cases, so it's hard to explain DuckDB to someone new.

posts.title: The Rise of the Declarative Data Stack
posts.summary: Simon Späti argues that the data stack is evolving toward declarative, code-first approaches where entire pipelines—from ingestion to visualization—can be defined in configuration files rather than imperative code. He traces declarative computing's history from Fortran to SQL to Kubernetes, showing how abstracting the 'how' empowers non-programmers while maintaining software engineering best practices. The post introduces a conceptual framework where a complete data stack could be recreated from a single function like `run_stack(serve(transform(ingest)))`, separating stateless configurations from stateful data assets. Späti acknowledges this unified declarative stack doesn't fully exist today but sees tools like Dagster, SQLMesh, and Rill as moving in this direction.
posts.key_insight: A truly declarative data stack would allow the entire pipeline to be recreated from configuration files alone, treating infrastructure like source code while cleanly separating the unchanging logic from the ever-changing data.

topQuotes.quote: Also, if you are declarative, you can make the code work for a very long time. For example, the browser runs the code from Craigslist from 1995, and it's only possible to this day because HTML is declarative.
As dbt doesn't know anything about its SQLs, it just runs them; it's limited to doing anything data-aware.

Call it Infrastructure as Code, Visualization as Code, BI as Code, or anything as code. YAML engineering, or DSL—no matter what, it's the way the data stack evolved to empower non-programmers to create data pipelines and infrastructure without losing the benefits of SW engineering best practices through declarative methods.

posts.title: The Inevitability of Mixing Open Source and Money
posts.summary: Armin argues that while open source ideals often assume money should stay out, in practice money mixes with open source quickly and inevitably. He points to corporate sponsorship, commercialization, and marketing-driven support as common realities that create stress, moral dilemmas, and conflicts of interest. He contrasts Rails’ institutional success with his own projects like Flask and Rye, reflecting on personal benefits and lingering doubts about not pursuing funding. He notes that even top-tier successes like WordPress can devolve into moral and governance crises, showing that legal tools don’t settle emotional or ethical disputes. The conclusion is that mixing open source and money may be unwise but is unavoidable, and we must build better funding mechanisms and norms—hence the Open Source Pledge.
posts.key_insight: Mixing open source and money is messy and often harmful, but it is already the reality—so the goal must be better, more sustainable funding norms rather than pretending purity is possible.

topQuotes.quote: Even when money doesn't directly fund an Open Source project, others may still profit from it, yet often those are not the original creators.
At face value, this suggests that Open Source and money shouldn’t mix, and that the absence of monetary rewards fosters a unique creative process.
Is it a wise to mix Open Source and money?  Maybe not.  Yet I also believe it's something that is just a reality we need to navigate.

posts.title: Capture less than you create
posts.summary: DHH reflects on the enormous economic value created by Ruby on Rails, noting that companies worth hundreds of billions were built with his framework. He acknowledges the temptation to feel shortchanged for not capturing more of that value personally, but argues this thinking is poisonous to the open source ethos. Instead, he finds satisfaction in the thriving ecosystem of happy programmers and successful businesses. He frames contentment with 'enough' as a special kind of wealth and defines the open source spirit as deliberately capturing far less than you create.
posts.key_insight: The open source spirit means deliberately capturing far less value than you create, finding wealth in sufficiency rather than maximizing personal extraction from communal tools.

topQuotes.quote: The moment you go down the path of gratitude grievances, you'll see ungrateful ghosts everywhere.
Thou shall not lust after thy open source's users and their success.
To let a billion lemons go unsqueezed. To capture vanishingly less than you create.

posts.title: Open source royalty and mad kings
posts.summary: DHH defends the Benevolent Dictator For Life (BDFL) model of open source governance but argues that Matt Mullenweg has crossed critical lines in his dispute with WP Engine. He traces the escalation from trademark claims to blocking WPE engineers from WordPress.org and expropriating the Advanced Custom Fields plugin. While acknowledging Mullenweg's legitimate achievements with WordPress, DHH argues that weaponizing open source registries sets a dangerous precedent that threatens decades of open source peace. He makes a personal plea for Mullenweg to de-escalate before causing irreparable damage to open source norms.
posts.key_insight: Open source registries must remain neutral infrastructure immune to commercial disputes, because weaponizing them — even by a project's rightful BDFL — threatens the foundational trust that makes the entire open source ecosystem work.

topQuotes.quote: Matt, don't turn into a mad king.
Please don't make me cheer for a private-equity operator like Silver Lake, Matt.
Half the advantage of the BDFL model is exactly in allowing for unpopular decisions to be made without the lethargic mores of committees and bureaucracy!

posts.title: To the crazy ones
posts.summary: DHH celebrates SpaceX's successful Starship booster catch and argues that people should marvel at genuine technological progress rather than letting social media commoditize spectacular achievements. He praises Elon Musk as a 'crazy one' whose ambitious vision drives humanity forward, referencing Tesla's Cybercab reveal and robotic developments alongside the SpaceX milestone. DHH criticizes people who claim to admire rule-breaking visionaries but recoil when confronted with actual nonconformists. He argues that partisan politics should not cloud appreciation for tangible engineering breakthroughs.
posts.key_insight: People romanticize visionary nonconformists in the abstract but resist them in practice, and we should override that instinct to appreciate genuine technological progress regardless of politics.

topQuotes.quote: Everyone wants to believe that they'd support 'the misfits, the rebels, the troublemakers'.
Most people are either aggressively or passively conformist. They squirm when The Crazy Ones actually attempt to change the world.
Ambition this crazy is only likely to emerge from someone equally and sufficiently nuts.

posts.title: Playing Not to Lose
posts.summary: Data teams are stuck in a defensive posture, passively receiving messy data and producing dashboards no one looks at, which perpetuates their status as cost centers. To break this cycle, data teams must actively integrate with the business and tie their work to critical initiatives rather than just going through the motions.
posts.key_insight: Data teams remain cost centers because they play defense instead of offense—to change this, they must stop passively processing data and start actively driving business outcomes.

topQuotes.quote: Data's still a mess. Most data initiatives fail. Data teams are seen as a cost center and not getting the support they deserve. Same as it ever was.
In most companies, there's no incentive to add value. It's easier to pass the buck to the people downstream from you. You play defense and make the next person pay.
The reality is most of you won't do this. It's hard. And I'll probably write the same thing in five years. Same as it ever was.

posts.title: Something Lost Something Found
posts.summary: Stancil argues that dbt Labs’ Coalesce has shifted from a quirky, community-first gathering into a more conventional, revenue-minded corporate conference, reflecting the realities of monetizing open source. While that change brings a sense of loss, it also creates stability and clarity about what the event is for. The lasting value, he suggests, is in the small human moments that survive despite commercialization.
posts.key_insight: Coalesce’s commercialization feels like a loss, but it also makes the event sustainable, and the real value was always the human connections that persist beneath the branding.

topQuotes.quote: This Coalesce made comfortably clear what future Coalesces will be: Mostly a means for the first two ends; a financially-motivated event, hosted by a financially-motivated entity, supported by financially-motivated sponsors; a corporate conference, not so different than the rest of them.
If the product is free, you are the product, the saying goes, and dbt had long been free.
But that’s ok, because even if the talks are sponsored, if the fun is HR-approved, and if the happy hours happen on a budget, the magic was always in the little moments.

posts.title: How Hard Should Your Employer Work To Retain You?
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that companies should build fair, transparent compensation systems and invest in ongoing career development rather than relying on heroic counter-offers when employees threaten to leave. She contends that healthy turnover is natural, that managers should proactively identify superstars and single points of failure, and that exceptions to compensation frameworks—while occasionally necessary—compound into cultural damage and cynicism.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable employee retention comes from building fair, transparent systems for compensation and career development—not from heroic counter-offers that reward brinksmanship and erode organizational trust.

topQuotes.quote: If the company's future depends irrevocably on the continued employment of any individual person, the company's leaders are fucking up, full stop.
The harder your employer is affirmatively working to do right by you, the fewer heroics they will be willing or able to do to retain you.
If superstars are the people you want to keep out of joy, SPOFs can be the people you need to keep out of fear.

posts.title: Small Batch, Artisanal ETL is Back
posts.summary: Riccomini reflects on the evolution of data pipelines, noting that simpler batch ETL approaches are making a comeback for smaller organizations. He references his 2019 QCon talk outlining stages of pipeline maturity, emphasizing that many companies don't need to progress beyond basic batch processing. The post highlights how pipeline ownership has shifted from dedicated data engineering teams to ML, AI, and product engineers who prefer Python-centric tools. He introduces dlt (data load tool) as a promising solution that fits modern workflows while incorporating SDLC best practices. While acknowledging challenges like duplication and unclear ownership, Riccomini sees this democratization of data pipelines as a positive trend driven by data consumers wanting more control.
posts.key_insight: The democratization of data pipelines through lightweight, Python-native tools like dlt reflects a broader shift where data consumers increasingly want direct control over their data flows, even if it means accepting some organizational messiness.

topQuotes.quote: Developers are thinking: if I can get by with a small query engine (DuckDB), maybe I can get by with a small ETL tool.
For many smaller organizations, these tools are overkill.
Data consumers want control of their data pipelines.

posts.title: Consider the Plight of the VC-Backed Privacy Burglars
posts.summary: Gruber dismantles Kevin Roose's NYT column arguing that Apple's new per-contact sharing controls in iOS 18 are anticompetitive. He points out that the previous all-or-nothing contact access was a privacy failure that companies exploited as a growth-hacking technique, and that granular controls are long overdue. He rejects the claim that Apple's first-party apps having full contact access is unfair, arguing that deep integration is the entire point of Apple's platform and what users choose it for. He draws an analogy between companies resenting new privacy controls and polluters resenting environmental regulations, framing the complaint as privileged actors objecting to losing an exploitative advantage.
posts.key_insight: Complaints about Apple's new per-contact sharing controls are just the predictable resentment of those who were profiting from an exploitative practice that never should have been permitted in the first place.

topQuotes.quote: It's rare that a story is worse than its provocative headline, but this one manages it.

Writ large, this psychology explains why granting social equality to minority groups feels to some in the majority group, the small-minded and bigoted, like a loss of privilege and a downgrade in status.
If iOS had debuted in 2007 with per-contact sharing controls exactly like those in iOS 18 today, no one serious would ever have complained that this was wrong or unfair.

posts.title: Automattic is doing open source dirty
posts.summary: DHH argues that Automattic's demand for 8% of WP Engine's revenues fundamentally violates open source principles and the GPL license. He contends that open source's success depends on the clarity and certainty of its licensing regime, where obligations are spelled out in the license and nothing more. Despite having tribal reasons to side with WordPress, DHH prioritizes the integrity of open source licensing over personal allegiances. He draws a parallel to Rails, noting that many billion-dollar companies built on it contribute nothing back, and that's their right under the license. He concludes that Automattic wants the prestige of open source while extracting payments like a proprietary vendor.
posts.key_insight: Open source licenses must be the complete and final word on obligations — allowing project owners to impose retroactive revenue demands on successful users destroys the clarity and trust that made open source viable.

topQuotes.quote: None of the major licenses, however, say anything close to 'it's free but only until the project owners deem you too successful and then you'll have to pay 8% of your revenues to support the project'.
That's a completely bonkers and arbitrary standard based in the rule of spite, not law.
They want to retain WordPress' shine of open source, but also be able to extract their pound of flesh from any competitor that might appear, whenever they see fit. Screw that.

posts.title: Some notes on upgrading Hugo
posts.summary: Julia Evans documents her experience upgrading Hugo from version 0.40 (2018) to v0.135 (2024), detailing every breaking change she encountered along the way. The most painful change was switching markdown renderers from Blackfriday to Goldmark, which required manually updating 80 out of 700 markdown files. She developed a systematic process of diffing old and new HTML output to find silent rendering differences. Despite the tedium, she considers the upgrade worthwhile because the new CommonMark-compliant renderer is more futureproof and actually fixed some long-standing rendering bugs she hadn't noticed. She also reflects on static site generator backwards compatibility, noting Jekyll and 11ty are reportedly better in this regard.
posts.key_insight: Upgrading a static site generator after years of stability is mostly a markdown renderer migration problem, where the hardest part is finding silent rendering breakages that produce no errors.

topQuotes.quote: I don't think I'd particularly recommend moving 700 blog posts to a new Markdown renderer unless you're really in the mood for a lot of computer suffering for some reason.
But what are blogs for if not documenting one's very boring yakshaves from time to time?
Almost all of the problems just silently broke the site, they didn't cause any errors or anything. So I had to diff the HTML to hunt them down.

posts.title: SatCamp Retrospective
posts.summary: Matt Rocklin reflects on SatCamp, a small unconference for the satellite/earth observation industry in Boulder, Colorado. Despite the incredible potential of satellite imagery for applications ranging from agriculture to disaster relief to defense, companies in this space are struggling to convert pixels into business value. The market lacks the mature ecosystem of horizontal infrastructure products and vertical specialists that other industries enjoy. Consulting companies and government/defense contractors seem to be the only ones thriving. Rocklin sees parallels to the early internet or oil before the automobile—amazing potential without clear applications yet—and appreciates the tight-knit, idealistic community that reminds him of early Scientific Python.
posts.key_insight: The satellite imagery industry has incredible data availability but lacks the mature ecosystem of products and services needed to convert that data into business value, leaving it in a pre-breakthrough state similar to the internet before search engines.

topQuotes.quote: The US Government seems to highly value observing the earth and its people.
Mostly it made me miss the days before Python was flooded with money and success (not that I'm complaining about the money and success).
When asked my favorite question 'How much do you think it costs to process 1TB of cloud data' answers ranged in the $20-50 range (real answer is $0.10).

posts.title: Is Excel Immortal
posts.summary: Stancil argues that Excel persists not despite its “anti-patterns,” but because those quirks are precisely what make it indispensable: local files, direct manipulation, and the ability to create private, versioned sandboxes. He suggests that any true successor must embrace these traits while adding scale, polyglot workflows, and optional cloud execution rather than trying to eliminate Excel’s messy flexibility.
posts.key_insight: Excel’s endurance comes from how well it supports messy, local, and opaque workflows that businesses actually need, so any replacement must preserve that flexibility while quietly extending its power and scale.

topQuotes.quote: Live, mysteriously-changing data is bad. Collaboration is bad. Restrictions on how to manipulate data are bad.
The answer—obviously, probably—is that these aren’t bugs of Excel, but features.

So far, most attempts to make a polyglot data tool stick notebooks in BI tools or stick spreadsheets in notebooks. But if you want to beat Excel, putting a notebook in a spreadsheet might be a better way to go.

posts.title: Field Notes, Early Fall 2024 Edition
posts.summary: Joe Reis shares observations from his international conference tour, noting that while GenAI enthusiasm remains high, actual production implementations are rare, and practitioners are growing cynical about the gap between demos and reality. He observes that Databricks is gaining momentum over Snowflake in practitioner conversations, data teams continue to be undervalued as cost centers, and the 2020/21 data vendor landscape is consolidating to clear winners.
posts.key_insight: Despite massive AI hype, the data industry's fundamental challenges remain unchanged—most initiatives fail, teams are undervalued, and the gap between vendor promises and production reality continues to widen.

topQuotes.quote: I'm long-term bullish on AI, but I feel this hype cycle is played out by a crowd of grifters and sameness. Show us the money!
Is OpenAI the new WeWork?
Data's still a mess. Most data initiatives fail. Data teams are seen as a cost center and not getting the support they deserve. Same as it ever was.

posts.title: Kamal 2: Thou need not PaaS
posts.summary: DHH announces the release of Kamal 2, an upgraded deployment tool that lets developers deploy containerized applications to their own hardware without Kubernetes or PaaS providers. The new version supports multiple applications on a single server and automatic SSL certificate provisioning via Let's Encrypt. He positions Kamal as the key enabler of 37signals' cloud exit and frames it as a tool for everyone from $5 VPS users to dedicated hardware operators. The core argument is that vendor lock-in from proprietary PaaS setups stifles competition, and Kamal removes migration friction across deployment stages.
posts.key_insight: Developers don't need PaaS or Kubernetes — Kamal 2 makes deploying to your own hardware simple enough to eliminate cloud vendor lock-in at any scale.

topQuotes.quote: Kamal was our ticket out of the cloud.
We can't have competition in the cloud as long as folks are locked into proprietary or overly-complicated setups that makes moving from one vendor to another a huge hassle and expense.
Imagine just how you can run on one of those amazing Hetzner EPYC 9454 boxes with 96 threads and 256GB of RAM that they sell for $220/month!

posts.title: Pledging $300,000 to the Zig Software Foundation
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto announces a $300,000 personal donation to the Zig Software Foundation, paid in two annual installments. He traces his journey with Zig from following the project in 2019 to becoming an active contributor and building Ghostty in the language. The donation reflects his family's philosophy of supporting independent software projects that embody building for the love of building. He emphasizes Zig's strong management, transparent funding model, and clear path to broader adoption as reasons for the investment.
posts.key_insight: Supporting independent, passion-driven software projects through substantial personal investment can both give back to the community and encourage a culture of building for the love of building.

topQuotes.quote: We want to support independent software projects that we believe can be transformative and impactful, both as a way to give back to the community that gave me so much but more importantly as a way to highlight and encourage a culture of building for the love of building.
As a technology, it is ambitious and innovative, but still practical and pragmatic.
Our philanthropy is generally private, but I'm making an exception since I think my public support of Zig has a chance to really help the project due to my background.

posts.title: Steve Ballmer was an underrated CEO
posts.summary: The dominant narrative that Steve Ballmer was a failed CEO who held Microsoft back until Satya Nadella saved the company is fundamentally wrong. While Ballmer faced intense criticism for missing mobile and other trends, he actually oversaw massive revenue growth (from $14-22B to $83B), made crucial long-term bets that define Microsoft today (Azure, Office 365, Bing, Xbox), and built the enterprise sales machine that powers the company. He inherited a company hamstrung by antitrust constraints and brutal internal politics, then cleared both obstacles while subsidizing multi-billion dollar moonshots that critics derided but which positioned Microsoft for decades of success.
posts.key_insight: Ballmer's real achievement was making massive, heavily-criticized long-term bets that set up trillion-dollar business units and clearing political obstacles so thoroughly that his successor's success made him look incompetent by comparison.

topQuotes.quote: Much like Gary Bernhardt's talk, which was panned because he made the problem statement and solution so obvious that people didn't realize they'd learned something non-trivial, Ballmer set up Microsoft for future success so effectively that it's easy to criticize him for being a bum because his successor is so successful.
Even Bing, widely considered a failure, on last reported revenue and current P/E ratio, would be 12th most valuable tech company in the world, between Tencent and ASML.

If we look at the broader history of the tech industry, Microsoft has had sustained strong execution from its founding in 1975 until today, a nearly fifty year run, a run that's arguably been unmatched in the tech industry.

posts.title: Writes and Write-Nots
posts.summary: Graham argues that AI will remove the practical pressure to learn writing, collapsing the middle tier of writers into a divide between those who can write and those who cannot. Because writing is a form of thinking, this shift risks creating a world of thinkers and think-nots, where clear thought becomes a choice rather than a default expectation.
posts.key_insight: If AI removes the need to write, it also removes a primary path to clear thinking, turning intelligence into an opt-in skill.

topQuotes.quote: If you're thinking without writing, you only think you're thinking.
There will still be smart people, but only those who choose to be.
To write well you have to think clearly, and thinking clearly is hard.

posts.title: Terminal colours are tricky
posts.summary: Julia Evans explores the surprisingly complex world of terminal color configuration, cataloging 11 distinct problems users face from unreadable blue-on-black text to vim colorscheme synchronization issues. She explains how the 16 ANSI colors lack standardization across terminal emulators, how 256-color and 24-bit color modes create additional complexity, and how colorscheme remapping can cause unexpected clashes with programs like ngrok. She shares her personal setup using base16-shell with Solarized Light and discusses the evolution of vim's color support from ANSI-only to 24-bit hex colors in 2016. Her biggest practical takeaway is enabling the 'minimum contrast' feature available in modern terminals like iTerm2, which automatically adjusts colors to ensure readability.
posts.key_insight: Terminal colors are a mess of unstandardized defaults, competing color modes, and colorscheme remapping conflicts, but modern features like minimum contrast and 24-bit color support are finally making the situation manageable.

topQuotes.quote: Personally I'm a zero-configuration kind of person and it's not that appealing to me to have to put together a lot of custom configuration just to make my colours in the terminal look acceptable.
The answer seems to be 'there's no standard, terminal emulators just choose colours and it's not very consistent'.
I find it SO FRUSTRATING to deal with it when I just want my colours to work! Being surprised by unreadable text and having to find a workaround is just not my idea of a good day.

posts.title: Wonderful Rails World Vibes
posts.summary: DHH recaps Rails World 2024 in Toronto, celebrating it as a triumphant event for the Rails community. Over a thousand attendees from 57 countries gathered at an outdoor venue, coinciding with the Rails 8 beta release featuring the #NOPAAS movement, Solid adapters with SQLite defaults, and Kamal 2.0. He argues Rails has found renewed confidence as a counter-melody to the industry's trend toward specialization, doubling down on the one-developer full-stack framework. The post credits the Rails Foundation, Shopify, and numerous sponsors for underwriting the event at a loss of over $100,000 to keep it affordable and mission-driven.
posts.key_insight: Rails is experiencing a confident renaissance by rejecting the industry's push toward specialization and platform dependency, doubling down on simplicity and the one-developer full-stack framework.

topQuotes.quote: You should never need a PaaS subscription to go live with Rails!

While venture capitalists are moving in on developer tech from all sides, Rails is reaffirming its commitment to open source generics, and deployment strategies that don't require paying big premiums for wrappers around AWS.

While everyone seems resigned to slice the expertise of web development into ever thinner specialties, Rails is doubling down on the one-developer framework, the full stack, and the compression of complexity.

posts.title: The Second Wolf
posts.summary: The post argues that VCs contain two competing personas: a sincere helper who loves collaborating with founders and a harsher, status‑driven operator who wants to be feared and seen as decisive. While both modes can be useful, the author stresses that performative toughness and insecurity are easy to spot, and authenticity matters more than cosplay.
posts.key_insight: VCs oscillate between helpful collaboration and performative toughness, but the insecurity behind the latter is transparent and damaging.

topQuotes.quote: The second wolf will shiv you for a dollar.
You can tell when someone is desperate to prove that they’re useful and not just rich.
Inside every venture capitalist there are two wolves.

posts.title: Some Go web dev notes
posts.summary: Julia Evans shares practical lessons from building a web application in Go, covering routing improvements in Go 1.22, SQL code generation with sqlc, SQLite configuration tips, and garbage collector tuning. She highlights a trailing-slash gotcha in Go's new router that silently breaks POST requests by redirecting them to GET. She explains why she prefers Go for small web projects: single binary deployment, minimal toolchain setup, and code that's easy to return to after long breaks. She contrasts this with Rails, where the magic that makes initial development fast becomes an obstacle when returning to abandoned projects.
posts.key_insight: Go's explicit, boilerplate-heavy approach to web development pays off over time because code you can read and understand after a two-year break is more valuable than code that was magical to write initially.

topQuotes.quote: In general everything about it feels like it makes projects easy to work on for 5 days, abandon for 2 years, and then get back into writing code without a lot of problems.
It feels easier to me to come back to my Go projects that are full of a lot of repetitive boilerplate, because at least I can read the code and figure out how it works.

I've tried to learn Rails a couple of times and I really want to love Rails – I've made a couple of toy websites in Rails and it's always felt like a really magical experience. But ultimately when I come back to those projects I can't remember how anything works.

posts.title: The New Era of Flexible Infrastructure Deployment
posts.summary: Riccomini analyzes Confluent's WarpStream acquisition through the lens of a broader industry shift toward flexible deployment options. He argues that modern infrastructure must support multiple deployment models—embedded, single-node, clustered, SaaS, BYOC, and self-hosted—rather than forcing customers into a single approach. Using ClickHouse as the exemplar, he shows how one system can scale from an in-process library to distributed clusters while supporting all deployment modes. The post identifies technical enablers like zero-disk architecture, Rust/C++ adoption, composable data systems, and WebAssembly that make this flexibility achievable. His conclusion is stark: infrastructure vendors without flexible deployment options will fail.
posts.key_insight: Modern infrastructure must be built from the ground up to support the full spectrum of deployment options—from embedded libraries to distributed clusters to multi-tenant SaaS—or risk obsolescence.

topQuotes.quote: Infrastructure vendors need to ask themselves: does our system deploy self-managed, BYOC, and as a SaaS cloud? Can we support in-process embedded, single-node, and clustered execution? If not, you're cooked.
Acquisition motivation and communication are rarely the same.
In hindsight, it's a comically obvious gap in the realtime online analytical processing (OLAP) space.

posts.title: Neurodivergence and accountability in free software
posts.summary: DeVault responds to claims that his criticism of Richard Stallman is ableist toward neurodivergent people. He argues that using neurodivergence as an excuse for Stallman's harmful views on sex and consent is itself ableist, as it infantilizes neurodivergent people and denies them agency. Drawing on conversations with dozens of neurodivergent people, he finds broad agreement that neurodivergence is context, not an excuse, and that accountability should apply equally. He concludes with practical recommendations for how free software communities can better accommodate neurodivergent participants through clear norms, asynchronous participation, and sensory accommodations.
posts.key_insight: Using neurodivergence to excuse harmful behavior is itself ableist because it denies neurodivergent people the agency and accountability that come with being treated as full human beings.

topQuotes.quote: Being neurodivergent doesn't make someone sexist, but it can make it harder for them to hide sexist views.
I'm not a huge fan of the narrative that I have 'the neurodevelopmental disorder that makes you a bigot'.

Using neurodivergence as a cover for sexism and problematic behavior in our communities is a toxic, ableist, and, of course, sexist attitude that serves to provide problematic men with space to be problematic.

posts.title: A Few Brief Thoughts on Meta Connect 2024
posts.summary: Gruber reviews Meta Connect 2024, noting Zuckerberg's confident solo keynote performance and the key announcements: the $300 Quest 3S headset, limited-edition translucent Ray-Ban smart glasses, and the Orion AR glasses prototype. He argues that while Orion is genuinely interesting as a concept, the 'almost normal glasses' framing is generous given their chunky, conspicuous appearance and $10,000-per-unit manufacturing cost. He frames the broader narrative as an exciting competitive volley between Meta and Apple over the future of spatial computing, with Meta effectively conceding that VR headsets aren't the endgame by pivoting toward AR glasses.
posts.key_insight: Meta's pivot from metaverse VR headsets to AR glasses represents a tacit admission that Apple was right to dismiss the metaverse, while the competitive volleying between the two companies signals the exciting uncertainty of a nascent device category.

topQuotes.quote: They look orthopedic, like glasses intended to aid people whose vision is so low they're legally blind.
Unlike going out in public wearing a VR headset, you'd just look like a nerd, not a jackass.
That's stretching the meaning of 'almost' to a breaking point.

posts.title: Panels, a New Wallpaper App From Marques Brownlee
posts.summary: Gruber reviews MKBHD's new Panels wallpaper app and finds it deeply flawed. He separates two strands of criticism: the dismissive 'wallpapers should be free' crowd, which he finds annoying, and the legitimate complaint that Panels is simply not a good app. He argues the app fails on nearly every front — poor UI, crashes, mediocre wallpapers, invasive tracking, and ads — while charging premium prices of $50/year. He contrasts it unfavorably with Iconfactory's Wallaroo, which costs less and delivers more. Gruber concludes that a premium wallpaper app could justify its price, but Panels is 'incongruous and incoherent' in charging premium while delivering sub-premium quality.
posts.key_insight: A premium price demands a premium experience across every dimension — design, privacy, content quality, and platform respect — and Panels fails on all of them.

topQuotes.quote: If Brownlee is down with how this app looks and feels, it explains quite a bit about how he's willing to spend large stretches of time daily-driving Android phones.
Premium apps don't ask to track you across apps. Premium apps don't make you watch ads to get to their free content. Premium apps look and feel native to their platforms.
It's entirely plausible for a premium wallpaper app to justify a price of $50/year. But Panels isn't a premium app.

posts.title: Legacy Modernization meets GenAI
posts.summary: This article argues that while GenAI in software development has primarily focused on code generation, its greatest potential lies in understanding and modernizing legacy systems. Thoughtworks developed CodeConcise, an internal accelerator that uses LLMs combined with knowledge graphs to make legacy modernization more cost-effective. The approach treats code as data, parsing it into Abstract Syntax Trees stored in graph databases, then enriching it with LLM-generated explanations at various abstraction levels. In a proof of concept, they reduced reverse engineering time from 6 weeks to 2 weeks per module, potentially saving 240 FTE years for a complete mainframe modernization program. The authors acknowledge this is early-stage work with context-dependent results, but see significant promise in addressing problems that have made large-scale modernization prohibitively expensive.
posts.key_insight: GenAI's transformative potential for legacy modernization lies not in generating code but in systematically extracting understanding from existing codebases through knowledge graphs, reducing the prohibitive cost and expertise barriers that have long made large-scale modernization impractical.

topQuotes.quote: We believe there is as much, if not more, value in understanding existing code than generating new code
Typically the produced code suffers from poor overall code quality, non-idiomatic code, and poor naming conventions
Often, they are stretched too thin across multiple teams just to 'keep the lights on'

posts.title: Tagged Union Subsets with Comptime in Zig
posts.summary: Mitchell Hashimoto demonstrates how to use Zig's comptime features to create type-safe subsets of tagged unions, solving a real problem in Ghostty where keybind actions need to be handled separately by terminal and application code. The naive solutions—using catch-all else clauses or explicitly listing ignored cases—either sacrifice compiler safety or result in verbose, poorly self-documenting code. By leveraging comptime type generation with @typeInfo and @Type builtins, he creates a ScopedAction function that generates union types containing only actions belonging to a specific scope. The solution provides compile-time exhaustiveness checking, self-documenting function signatures, and zero runtime cost for the type generation itself.
posts.key_insight: Zig's comptime enables generating specialized subset types from tagged unions at compile time, providing both type safety and self-documenting APIs without runtime overhead.

topQuotes.quote: Note that our scope() function above has no idea if it is being executed in comptime or runtime. It doesn't care. It's just a normal function. Comptime is fucking cool that way.
Since Zig has comptime code execution, and comptime code execution is the full language, we can use functions that take parameters at comptime and return things like... types.

I'm a type pragmatist. I believe leaning on the type system to enforce correctness is a powerful tool. At the same time, I believe some languages take this too far and make the type system a burden. Zig strikes a nice balance for me.

posts.title: FSL: A Better Business/Open Source Balance Than AGPL
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that for single-vendor companies, the AGPL is an inferior balance of business protection and open-source ideals compared to the Functional Source License (FSL). He frames “Fair Source” as acknowledging the reality that a vendor controls a project via trademarks and CLAs, but tempering that power with a guaranteed, short-term transition to true open source. AGPL’s promise of equality is often illusory in commercial settings because legal risk and multi-licensing create asymmetric rights for the vendor. FSL, by contrast, encodes a time-limited exclusivity period and an irrevocable path to Apache/MIT, which he claims better protects users if a company falters. His conclusion is that delayed openness is a more honest and practical equilibrium than perpetual copyleft in single-vendor contexts.
posts.key_insight: For vendor-led projects, a short, guaranteed path to open source (FSL) is a more honest and user-protective balance than perpetual AGPL copyleft.

topQuotes.quote: Single-vendor AGPL with a CLA is absolutely not the same as community driven AGPL project without the CLA.
Open Source is not a business model.
The illusion of equality.

posts.title: How I Almost Quit My Job, and How Being Selfish Saved Me
posts.summary: Matt Rocklin explores how positions of leverage—where small efforts yield outsized benefits for others—can paradoxically lead to burnout and self-sacrifice. He argues that the obligation to help others, combined with the good feelings it produces, causes people to suppress natural release mechanisms like rest and emotional expression. When this leads to a breaking point, the solution isn't simply working less, but creatively redesigning one's relationship to work around personal needs. Drawing from his experience nearly quitting as a startup founder multiple times, he shows how selfish actions like eliminating meetings and logging off Slack actually improved outcomes for everyone.
posts.key_insight: When you're in a high-leverage position, creative selfishness isn't just acceptable—it's necessary for sustainability, and the higher your leverage, the more power you have to reshape work around your needs.

topQuotes.quote: Sacrificing minutes of your time saves days of someone else's, so of course you sacrifice. You'd be a monster not to.
You can't fix yourself through more sacrifice. You can only fix things by selfishly prioritizing your needs.
Be selfish, creatively. Don't just stop doing work or helping others; figure out very specifically what you want and design work around what you want.

posts.title: Welcome To Bennventures
posts.summary: Stancil argues that workplace chat tools like Slack optimize for casual chatter, not thoughtful, triage-able work communication, and he contrasts them with Yammer’s threaded, inbox-oriented design. He then satirizes the AI-hype venture landscape with the fictional benn.ventures, using it to critique trend-chasing and status games in VC. He closes by reflecting on the stalled career arc of data leaders and the uncertainty of what comes next for a maturing cohort.
posts.key_insight: The post uses a critique of chat-first workplace tools and AI-hype venture culture to highlight how fad-driven incentives and status dynamics distort products, funding, and career paths in tech.

topQuotes.quote: Silicon Valley is a status game, and status is zero sum.
The bones are horrible.
Nothing is complete without AI.

posts.title: Accidental Spending: A Case For an Open Source Tax?
posts.summary: Armin argues that companies already tolerate “accidental” spend on infrastructure add-ons, yet funding open source remains a deliberate, scrutinized purchase. He suggests reframing open source support as a predictable, baseline obligation, akin to a tax or tip, to normalize giving. However, he doubts a universal “open source tax” collected by platforms would solve distribution because project value is context-specific and many projects lack governance to receive funds. As an alternative, he proposes the Open Source Pledge: donate an amount based on developer headcount to create an objective, visible baseline for internal conversations. The conclusion is that intentional funding is still essential, but a standardized baseline can make it easier to start and scale support.
posts.key_insight: Normalize open source funding with a measurable baseline tied to company scale, but keep allocation intentional because generic tax collection would misjudge value and readiness.

topQuotes.quote: I call those purchases “accidental” because they are proportional to the deployed infrastructure and largely acting like a tax on top of everything.
So the challenge with the tax — as appealing as it is — is that it might make the “purchase decision” of funding Open Source easier, but it would probably make the distribution problem much worse.
Companies are unlikely to fund something without understanding the return on investment.

posts.title: Ears rarely open until a rapport is established
posts.summary: DHH reflects on how establishing rapport and personal connection dramatically affects whether people will genuinely listen to controversial ideas. He uses his appearance on ThePrimeagen's stream as an example of how warm introductions and gracious hosts signal to audiences that a guest is worth hearing out. He acknowledges that many of his longstanding arguments have been dismissed by people who encountered them cold, without the rapport that warrants charitable listening. He concludes that while he wishes arguments were judged purely on logic, he's come to appreciate the power of set, setting, and personal vouching.
posts.key_insight: Controversial ideas are judged more by the rapport and emotional context in which they're delivered than by their logical merit, making warm introductions and personal vouching essential for persuasion.

topQuotes.quote: It's hard to open cold with a controversial take to a bunch of strangers.

That's the kind of introduction that makes all the difference as to whether someone is willing to grant you the grace of charitable listening -- or whether they'll immediately set their defenses to Max Stranger Skepticism.
Just like comedy, half the battle of winning over the audience comes from a solid introduction, good timing, and a broad smile to warm the room.

posts.title: FizzBee, TLA+, and (Practical) Formal Software Verification with JP Kadarkarai
posts.summary: This interview with JP Kadarkarai, creator of FizzBee, explores the challenges of formal verification in distributed systems and why existing tools like TLA+ have failed to gain mainstream adoption. JP describes how his experience at Google with large-scale systems like GFS, Bigtable, and Spanner revealed the importance of formal verification, while his later work at Lyft and Clumio showed how the industry often adopts distributed architectures without theoretical foundations. FizzBee aims to make formal methods practical by using Python-like syntax instead of TLA+'s mathematical notation, automatically generating visualizations, and enabling model-based testing. The conversation also touches on how AI-assisted coding will increase the need for formal verification tools.
posts.key_insight: Formal verification will only achieve mainstream adoption when tools prioritize developer ergonomics over mathematical rigor, meeting engineers where they already are.

topQuotes.quote: After leaving Google, I noticed a gap in the broader industry, where microservices and NoSQL databases were often adopted without a solid theoretical foundation.
When there's a tradeoff between quality and time to market, time to market almost always takes precedence.

The main reason was the complexity of TLA+'s syntax and semantics. Its syntax isn't intuitive—it relies heavily on ASCII representations of mathematical symbols, which can feel alien to most programmers.

posts.title: What Does Pangeo 2.0 Look Like?
posts.summary: Matt Rocklin reflects on the Pangeo project he helped create in 2018, which combined Kubernetes, Dask, XArray, and Zarr to enable scalable Python computing on the cloud for scientific users. While the original architecture generated significant enthusiasm and proved the concept was possible, Rocklin candidly admits it was 'fantastic demo-ware' that failed to address real organizational needs around costs, maintenance, and scalability. He surveys the current landscape of solutions including full platforms like 2I2C and Nebari, and specialized tools like his own Coiled (compute) and Earthmover's Arraylake (storage). Looking forward, Rocklin argues that Pangeo 2.0 should embrace SaaS architecture, for-profit development velocity, and lessons learned from Coiled's raw-cloud approach, while potentially adding cloud notebooks and built-in batch jobs.
posts.key_insight: A successful demo that generates enthusiasm is not the same as production-ready infrastructure that solves real organizational needs - the gap between proof-of-concept and enterprise-ready software is where most ambitious open source projects fail.

topQuotes.quote: The Pangeo stack was fantastic demo-ware.
It's easy to build a cool demo with the Pangeo stack, but difficult to actually solve the computational and business needs of larger organizations.
For-profit companies move faster than the community.

posts.title: The iPhones 16
posts.summary: Gruber's comprehensive review of the iPhone 16 lineup centers on Camera Control as the most significant hardware innovation — a dedicated photography button that represents Apple's boldest hardware addition in 18 years. He traces the philosophical lineage from Jobs's original elimination of hardware buttons to this purposeful addition of one, arguing the iPhone has always been a camera first and phone second. He finds Camera Control genuinely useful despite frustrations with its placement and lock-screen behavior, and praises the new non-destructive Photographic Styles as 'RAW for the rest of us.' The review's sharpest critique targets Apple's marketing emphasis on Apple Intelligence, which Gruber dismisses as riding the AI hype wave rather than highlighting the lineup's true strengths in photography and value.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone 16's true innovation is Camera Control — a bold hardware dedication to photography that proves Apple's real strengths, while the marketed tentpole of Apple Intelligence is just incremental features amplified by industry AI hype.

topQuotes.quote: Every phone on the market will soon be able to generate impersonal saccharine passages of text and uncanny-valley images via LLMs. Only Apple has the talent and passion to create something as innovative and genuinely useful as Camera Control.

If the iPhone 16 lineup was 'built from the ground up' with a purpose in mind, it's to serve as the best prosumer cameras ever made. Not to create cartoon images of a dog blowing out candles on a birthday cake.
Apple's just riding — and now, through the impressive might of its own advertising and marketing, contributing to — the AI hype wave, and I find that a little eye-roll inducing.

posts.title: Wonderful vi
posts.summary: DHH celebrates the enduring brilliance of vi-style text editing, marveling that a tool created in 1976 remains fundamentally unchanged and deeply productive in 2024. He explains vi's core appeal through its grammar-based approach to text manipulation, where actions, scopes, and objects combine into powerful combos. Drawing an extended analogy to Street Fighter combo moves, he argues that vi transforms mundane text editing into a satisfying, game-like experience. He recommends Neovim with the LazyVim distribution as the best entry point for newcomers, and shares his own positive experience after switching from TextMate earlier in the year.
posts.key_insight: Vi endures because it's not merely a text editor but a composable language for text manipulation, whose action-scope-object grammar makes editing feel like mastering a fighting game's combo system.

topQuotes.quote: Neovim from scratch is like climbing Mount Everest without oxygen. If all you're interested in at first is a peek at the view, book that LazyVim helicopter tour before bothering the sherpas.
In 1976, most computers didn't even have a mouse. In 2024, most vi-successor users don't even need one when programming.
Vi makes manipulating text into a key-based form of Street Fighter.

posts.title: The Things They Carried
posts.summary: Gruber reflects on Apple's 'It's Glowtime' event, praising the strong iPhone 16, Apple Watch Series 10, and AirPods 4 lineup, but confessing a sense of ennui about the predictability of it all. He draws a detailed contrast between Steve Jobs's Apple — quirky, risk-taking, occasionally reckless — and Tim Cook's Apple, which achieves utterly consistent excellence but rarely surprises. Gruber argues Cook hasn't missed any fundamental technology shift, but has eliminated the quirkiness and design gambling that made Apple thrilling to watch. He concludes that Cook's Apple is more financially successful and more consistently excellent, but less fun to obsess over, and acknowledges his dissatisfaction is ultimately selfish.
posts.key_insight: Tim Cook's Apple has traded the exhilarating peaks and embarrassing valleys of the Jobs era for utterly consistent excellence — a better business outcome, but a less thrilling one to witness.

topQuotes.quote: Jobs was driven to improve the way computers work. Cook is driven to improve the way humans live.
The obvious truth is that we all, including Apple, miss Steve Jobs.
Tim Cook's Apple doesn't make mistakes like that. That's ultimately why Cook's Apple is more successful.

posts.title: Modular Monoliths Are a Good Idea, Actually
posts.summary: Riccomini argues that modular monoliths offer a viable alternative to the reflexive jump to microservices that many growing companies make. Drawing on painful personal experiences at LinkedIn and WePay, he contends that microservices often just trade one set of problems for another—tight coupling, complex tooling, and operational overhead. The key insight is that monoliths become spaghetti not because modularity is impossible, but because we lack the tooling to enforce it. He outlines what modular monolith tooling would need: incremental builds, code isolation enforcement, and database boundary management. Rather than an either-or choice, modular monoliths can serve as a stepping stone that extends a monolith's useful life or provides a cleaner path to services.
posts.key_insight: Monoliths don't fail because modularity is impossible—they fail because we've invested in microservice tooling instead of building the tools that would make modular monoliths work.

topQuotes.quote: You have a fever, and the only prescription is more microservices.
We've spent decades building tooling for microservices. It's time to give monoliths the same respect.
I have microservice fatigue.

posts.title: Do AI Companies Work
posts.summary: Stancil argues that foundational LLM vendors face a brutal cost structure where model improvement is a perpetual, escalating expense, not a one-time investment. Because compute is leased and model quality decays quickly against fast-moving and open-source competitors, their moats are thin and short-lived, making survival depend on constant fundraising or massive revenues. The ultimate winners, he suggests, will be whoever is ahead when market hype and capital dry up—not necessarily the best or fastest builders.
posts.key_insight: LLM vendors lack durable moats because model advances are costly, quickly commoditized, and easily matched, so survival hinges on relentless funding or massive revenues rather than technological lead alone.

topQuotes.quote: Every LLM vendor is eighteen months from dead.
The market needs to be irrational for you to stay solvent.
The billions that OpenAI spent on building prior versions of GPT is not, because better versions of it are already available for free on Github.

posts.title: Conditionally Disabling Code with Comptime in Zig
posts.summary: This post explains how Zig's comptime feature enables conditional code compilation using standard Zig syntax rather than preprocessor directives or build system complexity. Hashimoto contrasts this with C's preprocessor macros and Go's build tags, both of which require separate languages or external tooling. The key advantage is that comptime conditions can be mixed with runtime conditions in the same expression, allowing code to reference libraries or types that may not exist in certain build configurations without causing compiler errors. He demonstrates this with a real example from Ghostty that checks both build-time and runtime library versions. The post also covers important gotchas around function boundaries and the need for explicit comptime keywords or inlining.
posts.key_insight: Zig's comptime eliminates the need for preprocessor languages and complex build systems by allowing conditional compilation to be expressed in standard Zig code that can seamlessly mix compile-time and runtime conditions.

topQuotes.quote: To drive the point home, let's consider how we might have done this in C. In C, we would have probably used a build system such as CMake that creates a one-time program and attempts to compile it to determine if it should define a preprocessor flag or not. Ugh.

I admit that when I first started looking into Zig, I thought this was a gimmick. 'How much could I possibly want to actually do at comptime, anyways?' I thought. Two years into using Zig for a non-trivial project, comptime is used all over and is by far my favorite feature about Zig.
It's a feature that I didn't know I needed until I had it, and one that I miss when I'm working in other languages.

posts.title: Going beyond Two Tier Data Architectures with DuckDB
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen presents how DuckDB can be deployed at every tier of a data architecture, replacing the traditional centralized database server model. He argues that DuckDB's in-process, embeddable design allows it to run inside operational servers (via PG-DuckDB), in serverless Lambdas replacing Spark clusters, within app servers eliminating database protocol overhead, and even in browsers via WebAssembly. He frames this as a paradigm shift: state-of-the-art query processing is no longer bound to a fixed server or cluster but can live anywhere creativity allows.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's in-process architecture represents a paradigm shift where state-of-the-art analytical query processing is unshackled from centralized servers and can be embedded at every tier of a data architecture, from operational databases to browsers.

topQuotes.quote: We have built a system to write papers maybe, or to look good in benchmarks, but we have not necessarily built systems to spark joy.
Blind parallelization alone isn't good enough. You can parallelize a slow Java implementation across a thousand cores and it won't matter because it's still slow single core.
We typically outperform Spark like a factor 10 or so on a single node. And Spark takes a lot of parallel cluster nodes to catch up.

posts.title: Reasons I still love the fish shell
posts.summary: Julia Evans explains why she still loves the fish shell after 10 years of daily use, expanding on a 2017 post with new reasons. She highlights fish's zero-configuration philosophy, smart history autosuggestions that validate against existing files, safe multiline pasting, terminal repair on every prompt, and sensible history defaults. She acknowledges downsides like occasional missing tool integrations and confusing PATH management with fish_add_path. The post concludes that fish is ideal for people who want great defaults without configuration overhead, noting its growing popularity at 16% in her Mastodon poll despite never being a system default.
posts.key_insight: The best developer tools are the ones that just work without configuration, and fish proves that smart defaults can eliminate entire categories of terminal frustrations most people have learned to tolerate.

topQuotes.quote: In 10 years of using fish I have never found a single thing I wanted to configure. It just works the way I want.
In bash, the maximum history size is 500 by default, presumably because computers used to be slow and not have a lot of disk space.
Fish disables Ctrl+S (which freezes your terminal and then you need to remember to press Ctrl+Q to unfreeze it). It's a feature that I've never wanted and I'm happy to not have it.

posts.title: Hiatus
posts.summary: Hillel Wayne announces a temporary pause in blog writing to focus on completing his book 'Logic for Programmers'. He expects to resume blogging in early 2025. He notes that his weekly newsletter will continue during this hiatus.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes you have to pause one project to focus on completing another.

topQuotes.quote: All of my budgeted blogwriting time is going to Logic for Programmers.

posts.title: Back in the market (Sonos Edition)
posts.summary: DHH recounts his frustrating experience as a longtime Sonos megafan whose multi-room music system became unreliable, pushing him to explore competitors like Bluesound and WiiM. After months of debugging hardwired Sonos equipment with no resolution, he discovered that cheaper alternatives worked instantly and that Sonos's historic app advantage had been eroded by Spotify Connect. He draws a broader business lesson: once you force satisfied customers back into the market to sample competitors, you risk losing them permanently. He cites Basecamp's approach of maintaining legacy versions for happy customers as the right model, arguing that the cost of keeping existing customers content is far less than the cost of losing them.
posts.key_insight: The greatest competitive advantage is customer inertia—never force satisfied customers to re-evaluate their options, because the market they return to may have changed dramatically since they last looked.

topQuotes.quote: That's why it's worth going the extra effort not to fuck with existing customers.
Don't push customers back into the market. They might just find that Spotify Connect is pretty awesome, and that amps half the price can do the job.
When you want to listen to some music at dinner, you want play to mean PLAY RIGHT NOW. Not "won't connect". Not "try restarting the app". Just play.

posts.title: Multiversion Python Thoughts
posts.summary: Armin proposes a path to multi-version Python imports so incompatible library versions can coexist, motivated by uv’s solver ambitions. The core obstacle is Python’s import cache: everything lands in `sys.modules` keyed by import name, so two versions clash. He argues for installing multi-version packages in separate paths, then using a custom import hook that registers versioned module names and proxies for unversioned lookups. The real difficulty is determining which version to load based on the importing package, which is hampered by missing distribution metadata and opaque C-extension import context. He concludes that while a workable approach exists with minimal Python changes, it is fragile and incomplete, especially around `sys.modules` patching and C-layer imports.
posts.key_insight: Multi-version Python imports are feasible with minimal language changes, but only if the import system gains reliable context and distribution metadata—especially for C extensions.

topQuotes.quote: The goal here is to enable multiple resolutions from the solver in uv so that two incompatible versions of a library can be installed and used simultaniously.
This means that in addition to the new entries in `sys.modules` we would also need to register some proxies that “redirect” us to the real names.
There are two challenges with this today and they come from the history of Python.

posts.title: Passwords have problems, but passkeys have more
posts.summary: DHH explains why 37signals abandoned their initial plan to build ONCE/Campfire's authentication entirely around passkeys. Despite investing significant engineering effort into a passkey-based system, they ripped it all out due to poor user experience. The core problem is that passkeys are tied to specific platforms and devices in ways users don't understand, creating lockout scenarios worse than traditional passwords. He argues that passkeys are essentially a halfway house to password managers but with less portability. Instead, they're sticking with passwords plus email-based second-factor authentication on first login from new devices. He concludes that passkeys aren't worth the implementation complexity or the UX pitfalls they introduce.
posts.key_insight: Passkeys solve a real problem but create worse ones by locking authentication to specific platforms and devices, making a good password manager the better practical choice.

topQuotes.quote: The problem with passkeys is that they're essentially a halfway house to a password manager, but tied to a specific platform in ways that aren't obvious to a user at all.

If you're going to teach someone how to deal with all of this, and all the potential pitfalls that might lock them out of your service, you almost might as well teach them how to use a cross-platform password manager like 1password.
Unless you accept the idea that all your passwords should be tied to one computing ecosystem, and thus make it hard to use alternative computers.

posts.title: The iOS Continental Drift Fun Gap
posts.summary: Gruber pushes back against the narrative that EU iPhone users are having more fun thanks to DMA regulations. He responds to articles by Allison Johnson (The Verge) and Federico Viticci (MacStories) celebrating the EU's expanded iPhone freedoms — third-party app stores, browser engine choice, default app replacement — by arguing the actual software available exclusively in the EU amounts to little more than Fortnite. Meanwhile, EU users are locked out of Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring, features Gruber considers genuinely useful and fun. He uses a Casino analogy to frame the EU side as the party insisting they're having a good time while sitting alone in a booth.
posts.key_insight: The EU's DMA-mandated iPhone freedoms are celebrated in theory, but measured by what users actually do and what software actually exists, the non-EU iPhone experience with Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring is currently more compelling.

topQuotes.quote: There are a bunch of articles informing EU citizens how to access Apple Intelligence by lying about where they are. There are crickets chirping regarding how iOS users outside the EU can cheat their way into the EU's new DMA rules. No one cares.

On the EU side, there is Fortnite and other games from Epic, a shady company that was justifiably booted from the App Store for bait-and-switch chicanery intended to provoke a lawsuit in which they got their asses handed back to them.
Imagine if Chrome could deplete your iPhone battery as fast as it does your MacBook battery.

posts.title: How Good Do You Need To Be At Your Job?
posts.summary: Reis contrasts the skill expectations of pilots versus tech workers, noting that pilots reach a competency ceiling where they simply need to safely transport passengers, while tech workers face constant pressure to upgrade skills. He questions whether the '10x engineer' mythology makes sense when applied to other professions, concluding that being 'good enough' varies dramatically by field.
posts.key_insight: The relentless 'level up or die' mentality of tech careers is an anomaly rather than the norm, and most professions simply require you to be reliably competent rather than exceptional.

topQuotes.quote: A pilot needs to have an arsenal of skills and nerves in the extremely rare case something happens. But they're not expected to be 10x'ing as a pilot every day. That would be weird and probably very concerning for passengers.
It's not like you'll get a bonus for doing rolls in a full-passenger plane at 30,000 feet.
In both cases, assuming you're employed, you're good enough for your job by definition.

posts.title: Getting Away With It
posts.summary: Stancil argues that “founder mode” is mostly a new set of rules masquerading as contrarian wisdom, and that copying Jobs-style leadership is just another cargo cult. The deeper point is that there aren’t real rules—effective leadership is doing something bold and making it work, i.e., “getting away with it.”
posts.key_insight: The real lesson of “founder mode” isn’t a new playbook but the permission to choose bold, unconventional approaches—and only success makes them legitimate.

topQuotes.quote: Founder mode is not advice; it’s something to yell when you bulldoze into a meeting and make the decision yourself.
You can do it, if you can get away with it.
Computers don’t tie us down; we do.

posts.title: Optimize for bio cores first, silicon cores second
posts.summary: DHH argues that programmer productivity should be optimized over computational efficiency, since programmers are far more expensive than computing resources. He calculates that one programmer's salary equals roughly 3,663 server cores, making even small productivity gains enormously valuable in silicon terms. This has been Ruby on Rails' philosophy for twenty years: trade cheap compute time for expensive programmer time. He acknowledges the aesthetic appeal of squeezing maximum performance from hardware but argues it rarely makes economic sense for business software. The post positions this argument in the context of AI hype, suggesting that productivity tools like Rails have been solving the same economic problem AI promises to address.
posts.key_insight: Since one programmer costs as much as thousands of server cores, even marginal improvements to programmer productivity vastly outweigh any gains from optimizing computational efficiency.

topQuotes.quote: Programmers continuing to become more expensive, computers continuing to become less so. Therefore, the smart bet was on making those programmers more productive EVEN AT THE EXPENSE OF THE COMPUTER!
Many of these squishy, biological programming cores have a distinctly human sympathy for their silicon counterparts that overrides the math.
The price of one biological core is the same as the price of 3663 silicon cores.

posts.title: The iOS Continental Drift Widens
posts.summary: Gruber examines Apple's latest DMA compliance changes for the EU, arguing that the European Commission's mandates — including a revised browser choice screen, deletable system apps, and default app settings — are technically ignorant, counterproductive, and ultimately harmful to users. He contends the browser choice screen will most benefit Google Chrome, the very monopoly the DMA should target, while punishing Safari users who genuinely prefer it. He argues the requirement to delete core apps like Photos and Camera is performative regulation that ignores how iOS is actually architected. Most significantly, he makes the case that the DMA's fundamental hostility to integration — Apple's core design philosophy — explains why Apple Intelligence and iPhone Mirroring won't launch in the EU, and predicts this pattern will repeat for every future integrated Apple product or service.
posts.key_insight: The DMA's fundamental hostility to integration — not just the App Store — means every future Apple product that ties hardware, software, and services together will arrive late or never in the EU, making the regulation's actual effect the opposite of its stated intent.

topQuotes.quote: This is performative regulation. None of this deletable apps nonsense increases competition; it merely increases the chances of profound user confusion.
The European Commission is beset by delusions of grandeur.
This is legally-mandated bad interaction design.

posts.title: Governing data products using fitness functions
posts.summary: The article explores how to apply architectural fitness functions to govern data products in a data mesh architecture. Traditional governance through data stewards doesn't scale with decentralized teams, so automation becomes essential. Fitness functions are tests that evaluate how close implementations are to design objectives, enabling 'shift left' governance where issues are caught early. The piece details specific assertions for key data product characteristics like discoverability, addressability, trustworthiness, and interoperability. Implementation patterns include running tests within data catalogs, using external processes with catalog APIs, and leveraging LLMs to interpret metadata quality. Publishing results on dashboards creates social incentives for teams to maintain governance standards.
posts.key_insight: Automated fitness functions that test data product metadata against governance standards provide scalable, continuous governance that empowers decentralized teams while ensuring interoperability across a data mesh.

topQuotes.quote: The term 'data product' has unfortunately taken on various self-serving meanings, and fully disambiguating them could warrant a separate article.
In one of our client implementations, we found that over 80% of published data products failed to pass basic fitness tests when evaluated retrospectively.
Traditional ways of enforcing governance rules using data stewards work against the idea of autonomous teams and do not scale in a distributed setup.

posts.title: Usage Based Pricing
posts.summary: Coiled is pivoting away from usage-based pricing because it created a fundamental misalignment: the better they made customers' code efficient, the less revenue they generated. Customers who saw 10-100x efficiency improvements ended up paying far less despite receiving enormous value, and even felt bad about the small amounts they did pay. The core issue is that Python workloads can swing dramatically in efficiency, and Coiled's real value lies in human acceleration rather than compute consumption. They're now experimenting with seat-based pricing for teams, charging a percentage of FTE cost that's meaningful to them but trivial compared to the productivity gains customers experience.
posts.key_insight: When your product's core value is making customers more efficient, usage-based pricing creates a perverse incentive where your success directly undermines your revenue.

topQuotes.quote: The vast majority of cloud usage today is misuse and our product stops misuse. Stopping misuse is great for the user (🎉) and bad for the cloud (🎉). However, it's also bad for us (😞) under usage based pricing.
We could make the product worse or stop giving good support and we'd probably earn more but this doesn't feel great.
Users value our product beyond what they pay (they're getting a great deal!) but they feel like they're getting screwed, and that's not good for anyone.

posts.title: Cycle Time
posts.summary: Cycle time measures how long it takes to move a feature from idea to production, and minimizing it is a core goal of agile development. The concept shifts focus from activity-based waterfall processes to iterative, functionality-based work that enables rapid feedback loops. However, cycle time is inherently slippery because teams define start and stop points differently—from first commit to initial idea, from canary release to full deployment. Fowler advises against comparing cycle times between teams without consistent definitions, and suggests using distinguishing adjectives rather than trying to separate 'cycle time' from 'lead time.' Despite the measurement challenges, pursuing shorter cycle times through value stream mapping, smaller features, and increased slack remains worthwhile for faster feedback and learning.
posts.key_insight: Cycle time is a valuable concept for driving faster feedback loops, but its inherent measurement ambiguity means teams should focus on consistent internal definitions rather than cross-team comparisons.

topQuotes.quote: Two commonly effective interventions are to reduce the size of features and (counter-intuitively) increase Slack.
What these questions tell us is that cycle time, while a useful concept, is inherently slippery.
This feedback is a core benefit of an iterative approach, and like most such feedback loops, the quicker I get the feedback, the happier I am.

posts.title: Search on PostgreSQL, Building Extensions, and pg_analytics with Philippe Noël
posts.summary: This interview features Philippe Noël, CEO of ParadeDB, discussing how they're building Elasticsearch alternatives as PostgreSQL extensions. ParadeDB offers pg_search for BM25 full-text search and pg_analytics/pg_lakehouse for columnar analytics, allowing users to avoid ETL pipelines to separate search infrastructure. Noël explains that PostgreSQL's extensibility allows it to stay current with data innovations while maintaining core stability. He discusses the recently announced pg_duckdb project, expressing cautious optimism but concern that MotherDuck may be too DuckDB-centric. The company is currently focused on commercial licenses and self-hosted deployments, with plans for BYOC solutions and Postgres platform partnerships.
posts.key_insight: PostgreSQL's extension architecture enables the database to innovate rapidly in areas like search and analytics while preserving the stability and reliability that made it dominant.

topQuotes.quote: I am worried that MotherDuck, who seems to be the primary company driving this, does not care enough about Postgres to really make the project what it needs to be.
Building analytics in Postgres is really hard. Doing it properly is much harder than what ParadeDB could do today.
We believe customers should pick a product by thinking about their business need(s) rather than about the infrastructure of the product itself.

posts.title: Progress
posts.summary: The post reflects on how people often grow more conservative with age, shifting from ambitious building to defensive protectionism. That mindset amplifies негативivity, fixates on past achievements, and overlooks future opportunity. Against this, the author argues that history shows progress and new ideas tend to win out over time, even if individual ideas fail. He frames the present as a choice between courageous exploration and clinging to an increasingly untenable status quo, warning that refusal to explore carries its own risks. The piece concludes that today’s regressive politics will likely be seen as short-sighted by future generations.
