posts.key_insight: Progress demands the courage to move beyond a fading local peak, because clinging to the past is as risky as innovation itself.

topQuotes.quote: Right now we find ourselves slowly sliding down from our local maximum and some people try to pull you back up to where we were standing.
As I'm getting older a lot of my social circles are becoming ever more conservative.
The focus shifts from building with ambition to fiercly protecting what one has achieved.

posts.title: Reflections on Founder Mode
posts.summary: Cantrill responds to Paul Graham's 'Founder Mode' essay by arguing that the key to maintaining company vision isn't mysterious founder intuition, but rather a writing-intensive culture that codifies values and builds trust. He contends that documenting decisions through processes like Oxide's RFDs allows founders to scale their thinking without micromanaging. The piece warns that founders may misuse 'founder mode' as license for seagull management, when the real goal should be building systems that foster mutual trust as the team grows.
posts.key_insight: The antidote to losing founder vision isn't mysterious 'founder mode' but rather building a writing-intensive culture that codifies values and creates the mutual trust needed to scale responsibility across a growing team.

topQuotes.quote: Founders are at grave risk of misinterpreting Graham's 'Founders Mode' to be a license to micromanage their teams, descending into the kind of manic seagull management that inhibits a team rather than empowering it.
Build the trust that allows a team to kick a Jobsian dent in the universe!
The most important quality in a leader is to bind a team with mutual trust: with it, all things are possible — and without it, even easy things can be debilitatingly difficult.

posts.title: Why don't more people use Linux?
posts.summary: DHH argues that Linux's low adoption rate among general users is analogous to why people don't exercise or read more — it requires effort that most people avoid. He acknowledges that casual computer users have no obligation to use Linux, but contends that programmers are different. Like firefighters who maintain physical fitness as a prerequisite for their job, developers should embrace Linux as a training ground for deeper computing proficiency. He points to his Omakub project as a way for developers to give Linux another look.
posts.key_insight: Programmers should use Linux not because it's easy or convenient, but because the effort of mastering it builds foundational computing skills that make them better at their craft.

topQuotes.quote: Putting it crudely, it's easier to be fat and ignorant in a world of cheap, empty calories than it is to be fit and informed.
They're like firefighters. Fitness isn't the purpose of firefighting, but a prerequisite.
If you're able to figure out how to setup a modern build pipeline for JavaScript or even correctly configure IAM for AWS, you already have all the stamina you need for the Linux journey.

posts.title: Founder Mode
posts.summary: Paul Graham argues that the standard management advice for scaling companies fails founders because it describes how professional managers run companies, not how founders should. He posits a distinct “founder mode” that preserves deeper founder involvement, challenges modular delegation, and better fits how successful founders actually lead as companies grow.
posts.key_insight: Scaling doesn’t require founders to become managers; it requires a distinct founder-led operating mode that keeps direct, hands-on influence while selectively delegating.

topQuotes.quote: Hire good people and give them room to do their jobs. Sounds great when it's described that way, doesn't it? Except in practice, judging from the report of founder after founder, what this often turns out to mean is: hire professional fakers and let them drive the company into the ground.
Imagine what they'll do once we can tell them how to run their companies like Steve Jobs instead of John Sculley.
Founder mode will be more complicated than manager mode. But it will also work better.

posts.title: When To Do What You Love
posts.summary: Graham argues that whether to follow your passion depends on your goals and context, not a simple yes/no. If you want moderate money, interest often conflicts with pay, but for massive wealth via startups, working on what fascinates you is a powerful way to discover outlier ideas. Under uncertainty, you should explore interests early, choose “upwind” options, and recognize that great work requires genuine curiosity.
posts.key_insight: Chasing what genuinely interests you is risky for moderate gains but is essential for great work and often the best path to truly outlier success.

topQuotes.quote: So there's something like a midwit peak for making money.
When you can't decide which path to take, it's almost always due to ignorance.
One useful trick for judging different kinds of work is to look at who your colleagues will be.

posts.title: Free speech isn't guaranteed to be forever
posts.summary: DHH argues that free speech protections in the West are under serious threat, drawing a parallel to the impermanence of all historical eras. He points to recent actions by Brazil's judiciary banning X, the EU threatening censorship of the platform, calls for Elon Musk's arrest, and the UK's crackdown on online speech as evidence of a rising authoritarian censorship movement. He warns Americans not to assume the First Amendment makes them immune, using a Game of Thrones analogy about how sixty years of peace can end abruptly. His conclusion is that the concepts of 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' are fig leaves for political censorship, and the only defense is rejecting the premise of benevolent censorship entirely.
posts.key_insight: Free speech protections are not permanent fixtures of Western civilization but historically contingent achievements that are actively being eroded by censorship movements disguised as fights against misinformation and hate speech.

topQuotes.quote: The only way to defend free speech is to nuke the idea of benevolent censorship from orbit.
In its place, we have the fig leaves of 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' failing to adequately cover the naked persecution of political adversaries.

Nobody has a monopoly on the truth, nobody can discern 'misinformation' from truth consistently or without bias, and nobody can define 'hate speech' in universally acceptable terms that don't recall blasphemy laws of centuries past.

posts.title: For what it'll make of you
posts.summary: DHH reflects on his relationship with goal-setting, distinguishing between treadmill-style material goals (hit a revenue number, then chase the next) and transformative goals aimed at personal growth. He shares two examples: aspiring to match Kent Beck's public speaking mastery after seeing him at JAOO 2003, and pursuing the 24 Hours of Le Mans after only recently starting racing. He argues that the best goals are ambitious but not delusional, and that the real value lies not in reaching the destination but in who you become through the pursuit.
posts.key_insight: The most meaningful goals are chosen not for what you'll achieve but for who you'll become through the pursuit.

topQuotes.quote: They make you reach for a million dollars in revenue, celebrate for a second, and then turn the chase to five million the minute after.
I don't like goals that feel like checkpoints on a treadmill.
Now there's a fine line between goals that are ambitious and goals that are delusional.

posts.title: Just Do It
posts.summary: Stancil argues that persuasive, data-backed arguments can still be wrong, and only real-world testing separates good ideas from bad ones. The Nike “data-driven” controversy illustrates how numbers can make debatable strategies feel like facts, creating a culture that prizes quantified rhetoric over experimentation.
posts.key_insight: Data and logic can make weak ideas sound true, so the only reliable way to learn is to test in the real world and measure what actually happens.

topQuotes.quote: A compelling rhetorical argument isn’t the same thing as the truth. The only real truth is what happens.

Doing quantitative analysis feels like figuring something out. It feels like a shortcut to the end; it reveals “insights;” it is a way to know something, or at least get reasonably close to knowing something, without doing something.
The opposite of a debate-driven culture is not a data-driven culture. They are actually the same thing, just with debaters wielding different weapons.

posts.title: We once more have no full-time managers at 37signals
posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals has returned to its original configuration of having no full-time managers, after experimenting with traditional management roles for several years. Instead of dedicated managers, managerial duties like performance reviews, onboarding, and mentoring are distributed among senior individual contributors. He argues this minimalist approach dodges Parkinson's Law by preventing managerial work from expanding to fill available time. DHH acknowledges limitations—particularly around handling conflict and poor performance—but contends the arrangement works brilliantly at their scale of 60 people when you hire self-directed 'managers of one.' He concludes that after twenty-plus years, and having tried the alternative, he's fully committed to this model.
posts.key_insight: Distributing managerial duties among senior individual contributors rather than hiring dedicated managers creates a minimalist management culture that dodges Parkinson's Law and lets skilled, self-directed teams move at maximum speed.

topQuotes.quote: It's impossible for a full-time manager to scale their interventions down to zero hours per week and still feel like they're doing their job.
We're going to make this company work without full-time managers or die trying.
We once more have no full-time managers whose sole function is to organize or direct the work of others.

posts.title: Rust for Linux revisited
posts.summary: Drew DeVault revisits his 2022 skepticism about Rust in the Linux kernel, this time with more compassion for the burned-out developers involved. He acknowledges the talent and dedication of Rust-for-Linux contributors while noting that Linux's decentralized maintainer culture has made the project a 'burnout machine.' Rather than continuing to fight LKML politics, he proposes an alternative path: building a Linux-compatible kernel from scratch in Rust, arguing this could move faster and attract more contributors without the political overhead.
posts.key_insight: Instead of fighting Linux's political culture to integrate Rust, the burned-out Rust-for-Linux developers should channel their talent into building a Linux-compatible kernel from scratch in Rust, where technical progress won't be bottlenecked by maintainer politics.

topQuotes.quote: Introducing Rust effectively is one part coding work and ninety-nine parts political work – and it's a lot of coding work.
Every subsystem is a private fiefdom, subject to the whims of each one of Linux's 1,700+ maintainers, almost all of whom have a dog in this race.
The consequences of these factors is that Rust-for-Linux has become a burnout machine.

posts.title: Unpacking the Buzz around ClickHouse
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini investigates the growing enthusiasm around ClickHouse, a realtime OLAP database that has gained significant attention in the data infrastructure community. He identifies three key factors driving the buzz: exceptional speed, remarkably easy installation via a single cURL command, and operational simplicity that allows scaling up on a single machine before needing distributed coordination. However, Riccomini highlights significant challenges including cost concerns (evidenced by Uber's migration away from ClickHouse to Apache Pinot), limited materialized view functionality for complex joins, and the looming threat from PostgreSQL's OLAP extensions like pg_duckdb. He concludes that while ClickHouse is currently a robust solution, its long-term position may be squeezed between PostgreSQL for smaller deployments and Pinot/Druid for enterprise scale.
posts.key_insight: ClickHouse's buzz comes from exceptional developer experience and operational simplicity, but its long-term viability is threatened by PostgreSQL's OLAP extensions from below and enterprise-scale systems like Pinot from above.

topQuotes.quote: If PostgreSQL takes ClickHouse's single-node and small-scale usage, and systems like Pinot and Druid take its large-scale market, there's not much left.
They have the mentality of a team on a budget. Like they didn't have 1000s of machines to throw at the problem. They had to make it work on what they had.
It's surprising to me that there aren't more single-process realtime OLAP systems out there; it seems obvious in hindsight.

posts.title: MiniJinja: Learnings from Building a Template Engine in Rust
posts.summary: Ronacher explains why he built MiniJinja: despite less server-side HTML, templating remains vital for text generation like YAML and LLM prompts, and existing Rust options were either static (Askama) or not Jinja-compatible. He describes the core runtime model, especially the `Value` enum and `Object` trait, and how Rust’s memory model shapes design tradeoffs such as reference counting and avoiding heavy unsafe tricks. The post digs into iteration semantics via enumerators, the VM and instruction model, and the compromises needed to support macros and closures without GC. He highlights the “creative API design” behind ergonomic filter registration through trait-based conversions. He concludes that MiniJinja’s patterns are broadly useful for Rust runtime and API design, and that he is pleased with the engine’s public API and documentation.
posts.key_insight: Building a Jinja2-compatible engine in Rust means inventing a dynamic runtime with careful tradeoffs in memory, iteration semantics, and API ergonomics, and those patterns generalize beyond templating.

topQuotes.quote: Now quite frankly I don't like that iteration protocol.
Rust is just not particularly amazing at tree structures.
In pure theory this is all wrong.

posts.title: Children of You
posts.summary: DHH addresses the global decline in birth rates, noting that no comprehensive theory explains why it's happening simultaneously across vastly different countries. Rather than proposing a political or economic explanation, he makes a personal case for having children based on the profound, almost inexpressible emotional transformation parenthood brings. He distinguishes between happiness and meaning, arguing that while children may reduce day-to-day happiness, they provide a deep, lasting sense of purpose. He contends that the case against having kids is easy to articulate (less time, money, freedom) while the case for it is nearly impossible to put into words, and this asymmetry is hurting prospective parents' decision-making.
posts.key_insight: The case for having children is losing to the case against because parenthood's profound rewards are nearly impossible to articulate, while its costs are obvious and easy to enumerate.

topQuotes.quote: If that lever controls a train heading for my kids or a million strangers, it's not a hard choice at all. I don't even have to think about it for a second.
It's easy to describe in words how having less time, less money, less freedom sounds like a raw deal. It's very hard to describe the purpose granted by parenthood.
I imagine it's like explaining 'green' to a person who can't see color.

posts.title: Most Companies Want To Do AI. Most Are Barely Doing BI.
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that while companies are rushing to adopt AI, most haven't even mastered basic BI, which historically has only a 20% success rate. He emphasizes that AI and BI are fundamentally different disciplines with different data requirements, and that the real challenge isn't the technology itself but the underlying difficulty of organizational change and data management.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental challenges of data initiatives—misunderstanding problems, lacking proper data, chasing technology over solutions—remain constant regardless of whether you call it BI, Big Data, or AI, and companies would benefit from realistic expectations rather than hype-driven adoption.

topQuotes.quote: Most companies want to do AI. Most are barely doing BI.
AI means you get more work done with fewer humans. Instead of hiring workers to complete a task, I can incorporate ML/AI into a workflow and augment or eliminate the pesky humans.

If your BI efforts are flailing, I'm guessing adoption of your dashboards and reports are pretty light or nonexistent. Will throwing a chatbot on top of this same data suddenly make people interested in data and analytics?

posts.title: Merchants of complexity
posts.summary: DHH argues that the tech industry thrives on selling unnecessary complexity to people who equate sophistication with value. He contends that vendors exploit insecurities—fear, vanity, pride—to convince buyers that simple, ownable solutions are beneath them, when in reality most problems don't require expensive, subscription-based tools. The post frames this as a psychological trap that people can break free from simply by choosing to. He closes by encouraging readers to embrace simplicity across their tech stack.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry profits by making simple problems look complex, exploiting insecurity to sell sophisticated subscriptions for what people could easily solve themselves.

topQuotes.quote: They'll own nothing and you'll convince them they should be happy.
By what sorcery? Fear, mostly. Vanity, sometimes. Sloth, occasionally. Pride, definitely.
Dare to be basic, babe.

posts.title: How A Startup Feels
posts.summary: Stancil frames startups as a “money machine” whose controls are opaque, interconnected, and delayed, making the core challenge less about sheer effort and more about radical uncertainty. The stress comes from not knowing what to do, and neither cautious analysis nor frantic action reliably works; the only workable posture is a confident, even delusional, commitment to a chosen configuration.
posts.key_insight: The defining hardship of startups is not the workload but the uncertainty of which actions actually matter, forcing founders to operate on conviction amid opaque feedback.

topQuotes.quote: The thing that makes the magical mystery money machine a magical mystery money machine is its mystery: You don’t know how it works.
It isn’t hard to operate because the switches are hard to flip—though they are—it is hard to operate because you don’t know what the switches do.
It’s not the amount of work that surprises us, but the nature of that work: Sitting in front of a mixing console worth of controls and not knowing what to do.

posts.title: Strangler Fig
posts.summary: Martin Fowler revisits his influential 'Strangler Fig' metaphor for legacy system modernization, inspired by observing strangler figs in Queensland that gradually replace their host trees. He argues against the common approach of wholesale system replacement, which frequently fails due to timeline pressures and specification difficulties. Instead, he advocates for incremental modernization that builds new components alongside legacy systems, gradually replacing functionality. The approach requires clear outcomes, identifying seams for decomposition, delivering small replaceable pieces, and crucially, organizational change to prevent recreating the same problems.
posts.key_insight: Legacy modernization succeeds through gradual strangulation rather than wholesale replacement, but technical change alone is insufficient without parallel organizational transformation.

topQuotes.quote: In a well-designed system, these seams would already exist. But such systems are unicorns.
We've seen this simple-sounding plan go down in flames most of the time.
Legacy systems become rigid and brittle because the design thinking and organizational processes that produced them built them that way.

posts.title: SlateDB: An Embedded Storage Engine Built on Object Storage
posts.summary: Riccomini announces the open-source release of SlateDB, an embedded storage engine built as an LSM tree directly on object storage like S3. The project emerged from his earlier writing about cloud storage tradeoffs and attracted collaborators from companies like Responsive and Jam Socket. SlateDB targets use cases in stateful stream processing, serverless functions, and durable execution where bottomless storage capacity and durability matter more than raw latency. The project is explicitly non-commercial with an Apache 2.0 license and a CNCF sandbox application pending.
posts.key_insight: The rise of object storage has created a gap for purpose-built storage engines that embrace its tradeoffs rather than treating it as a drop-in disk replacement.

topQuotes.quote: I am explicitly not going to do anything with SlateDB other than help make it a successful open source project.
I am not going to start a company around it or try and monetize it in any way.
We built SlateDB because we felt an open source LSM on object storage would be useful for stateful stream processing, serverless functions, durable execution, and more.

posts.title: Rye and uv: August is Harvest Season for Python Packaging
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that Python packaging needs a single, dominant tool to deliver a consistently great developer experience. He points to Astral’s `uv` as rapidly accumulating the functionality that previously required Rye, and urges Rye users to shift attention and feedback to `uv`. Fragmentation in tooling, he says, damages onboarding and forces developers through incompatible choices. The community should be willing to deprecate or stop recommending older tools once a clear winner emerges. He concludes that `uv` is the best shot right now, and even VC risk is manageable because the codebase is forkable and improves the ecosystem either way.
posts.key_insight: Python packaging needs to converge on a single, excellent tool, and `uv` is the clearest candidate worth rallying around right now.

topQuotes.quote: I can only re-iterate my wish and desire that Rye (and with it a lot of other tools in the space) should cease to exist once the dominating tool has been established.
But today I'm looking forward to the moment when there will be a final release of Rye that is no remaining functionality other than to just largely alias to uv.
Domination is a goal because it means that most investment will go into one stack.

posts.title: Software estimates have never worked and never will
posts.summary: DHH argues that software estimation is fundamentally broken and has been for sixty years, yet the industry keeps trying the same failed approach. He contends that estimatable work gets commoditized into products like WordPress or Shopify, leaving only novel work that's inherently unpredictable. Instead of better estimates, he advocates replacing them with fixed budgets and flexible scope — what 37signals calls 'appetites' in their Shape Up methodology. The key insight is that programmers deliver great results when given a time budget but allowed to negotiate scope during development.
posts.key_insight: Instead of trying to estimate unpredictable software work, fix the budget and let scope flex — programmers deliver better results when they can negotiate what to build within a time constraint.

topQuotes.quote: Yet the industry keeps insisting that the method that hasn't worked for sixty years will definitely work on this next project, if we all just try a little harder. It's the definition of delusional.

The fundamental problem is that as soon as a type of software development becomes so routine that it would be possible to estimate, it turns into a product or a service you can just buy rather than build.
What you asked for before you began building was based on the absolute worst understanding of the problem.

posts.title: Migrating Mess With DNS to use PowerDNS
posts.summary: Julia Evans describes migrating Mess With DNS, her DNS learning playground, from a custom DNS implementation to PowerDNS after users reported various RFC compliance issues. The migration involved eight distinct challenges including intercepting DNS queries, redesigning the API, managing record IDs, translating error messages, switching from Postgres to SQLite, upgrading Vue.js, improving frontend state management, and sequencing the rollout. She shares practical lessons about API design, particularly that internal APIs should be tailored to the frontend's needs rather than following abstract purity principles. The project was split into phases and deployed incrementally to keep changes manageable.
posts.key_insight: When building on top of an existing API for internal use, pragmatism beats purity — tailor your API to your frontend's needs, duplicate information freely, and translate error messages for your actual users.

topQuotes.quote: I wasn't very careful with the DNS implementation though (to quote the release blog post: "following the DNS RFCs? not exactly"), and people started reporting problems that eventually I decided that I wanted to fix.
I could just swap out my own crappy DNS implementation for PowerDNS.
Writing even more complex code in Javascript didn't feel that appealing to me – I don't really know how to write tests in Javascript and it seemed like it wouldn't end well.

posts.title: Where at least I know I'm free
posts.summary: DHH recounts his journey from dismissing American patriotic self-image as delusional to genuinely appreciating the First Amendment's value. He argues that recent crackdowns on speech in the UK, EU, and Canada—punishing 'misinformation' and 'hate speech' with harsh sentences and regulatory threats—have vindicated America's constitutional free speech protections. He uses the campy patriotic song 'God Bless the USA' as a framing device, admitting it now rings true to him as a Danish immigrant. His core argument is that America's First Amendment has proven a uniquely resilient bulwark against the new wave of censorship spreading across Western democracies.
posts.key_insight: Recent speech crackdowns across Western democracies have vindicated America's First Amendment as a uniquely effective protection against government-imposed censorship, even for a skeptical immigrant.

topQuotes.quote: There's a shockingly draconian crackdown on 'misinformation' and 'hate speech', the two stalwart euphemisms for 'speech we don't like', going on in Britain in particular at the moment.
I'd much rather live in a country that embraces everyone's right to BE FULL OF SHIT than one that pretends it can declare a priori what's true and what's false.

I used to find the American self-image of being this uniquely freedom-loving, freedom-having people delusional. Turns out I just needed to wait a little longer for the patriotic hymns to begin ringing true.

posts.title: 1:1 or 1:Many?
posts.summary: Joe Reis reflects on his career trajectory, contrasting the high-friction, one-to-one nature of consulting with the scalable, one-to-many impact of creating books and courses. After the unexpected global success of 'Fundamentals of Data Engineering,' he's shifting focus toward content creation that can reach and help more people simultaneously.
posts.key_insight: Shifting from one-to-one consulting to one-to-many content creation (books, courses) enables greater impact and creates a self-reinforcing flywheel of opportunities.

topQuotes.quote: You quickly realize that most companies - big or small, startups or massive - are essentially the same. Same personalities, problems, etc.
Since there's no recurring revenue, you're constantly chasing new work, 'feeding the beast,' as they say.
Even more challenging with consulting is that your impact is one-to-one, meaning one consultant and one client.

posts.title: A Very Particular Set Of Skills
posts.summary: Stancil argues that when a powerful new capability appears, the winning move is not to shoehorn it into existing workflows but to reshape the business problem to fit the capability. Applied to AI, that means abandoning attempts to make LLMs better baristas for today’s analytics tasks and instead finding problems that match what LLMs are uniquely good at, even if that means abandoning traditional “data-driven” rigor.
posts.key_insight: AI’s real business value will come from redefining problems to suit LLM strengths, not from forcing LLMs to improve existing analytics workflows.

topQuotes.quote: If you have friends who are mathematicians, don’t ask them to be accountants; come up with a way to make solving math problems very lucrative.

If this current generation of AI technologies is to produce its Renaissance, that seems like the sort of company it’ll be—one that all but abandons quantification entirely, so that it can take full advantage of the million researchers and writers who are offering it their services for $5.92 an hour.

Perhaps the way AI breaks through—or maybe more concretely, the way that the companies that figure out how to make lots of money with AI end up using it—isn’t by solving some existing problem with an LLM; it’s by changing the problem to make it easy for AI to solve.

posts.title: DuckCon #5 - Overview and Latest Developments
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen opens DuckCon #5 by reflecting on DuckDB's mission and extraordinary growth. He frames DuckDB's core purpose as replacing fear of data with confidence, comparing the goal to how VLC made video files approachable. He outlines three main use cases: interactive analysis, pipeline components, and creative architectures like mobile devices and satellites. Adoption metrics are staggering — 6 million Python downloads per month, 17 million extension installs, and 600K unique web visitors. He emphasizes DuckDB is not VC-funded, sustained instead by commercial support contracts through DuckDB Labs.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's core mission is replacing the fear and hostility people experience with data tools by making data as approachable as opening a movie file in VLC.

topQuotes.quote: The tools people generally are using to look at data, or to wrangle with data, they tended to be, I think, hostile is the right word.

We kind of want to get to the same point with this unwieldy collection of CSV files that you have. Data sets should just be harmless. It should be just like a movie file. You just throw it in a VLC and it's gonna be fine.
You can put DuckDB in a Lambda function. You can run DuckDB in a browser. You can run DuckDB on a satellite in space.

posts.title: The Framework 13 has a new high-res screen!
posts.summary: DHH reviews the new high-resolution screen upgrade for the Framework 13 laptop, which resolves his last major complaint about the device. The new 2880x1920 display delivers 256 PPI — beating the MacBook Pro — while retaining the matte finish and 3:2 aspect ratio he loves. He highlights that the screen can be retrofitted to existing Framework laptops for $269, taking about five minutes to install. He declares it the best laptop screen he's ever used for programming and argues the Framework 13 AMD version is a better deal than a MacBook Pro for developers.
posts.key_insight: The Framework 13's new retrofittable high-res matte screen eliminates its last weakness and makes it a better programming laptop than a MacBook Pro at half the price.

topQuotes.quote: But for programmers? The Framework is better.
It's a $269 upgrade, so it's not cheap, but it's a hellavu lot cheaper than buying a whole new computer. Not to mention way less wasteful.
I'd go so far as to say that this is the best looking laptop screen I've ever used for programming.

posts.title: Protecting our attention
posts.summary: Asparouhova expresses frustration at the tech industry's defensive reaction to Jonathan Haidt's 'The Anxious Generation,' which examines harmful effects of phone and social media use on young people. She argues that the destruction of attention through smartphones and social media is one of civilization's biggest threats, compromising our ability to make sustained progress on anything worthwhile. She wrote a piece for Arena magazine exploring why tech is psychologically avoidant of the social media debate. She contends that tech's defensiveness stems from historical and psychological scars rather than genuine disagreement with the obvious harms of doomscrolling. Her core plea is that the tech industry should demonstrate leadership in helping people live peaceably with powerful technology rather than dismissing legitimate concerns as Luddism.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's refusal to seriously engage with social media's harms stems not from genuine disagreement but from psychological defensiveness, and this avoidance itself is a threat to civilization's ability to make meaningful progress.

topQuotes.quote: The destruction of our attention – thanks, in large part, to the combination of smartphones and social media – is one of the biggest threats we face as a civilization, as it compromises our ability to make sustained progress on anything worthwhile.
Most people are so immersed in it that they can't see how bad it is; you can't ask someone to think clearly when their core operating system is compromised.
Running on a hamster wheel is not the same as running a marathon.

posts.title: Clock
posts.summary: This is a guest post by Sander O'Neil (likely Cathy's son) presenting a JavaScript-based clock/map/star chart that tracks the sun's position relative to Earth. The project combines orbital mechanics with geographic visualization, showing the sun's current and future positions on both a polar star chart and an equirectangular world map. The implementation uses Wikipedia-sourced orbital dynamics data and iteratively solves for Earth's position using mean anomaly calculations. The post explains each visual element and the mathematical principles behind the simulation, including handling Earth's elliptical orbit and axial tilt.
posts.key_insight: Accurately tracking the sun's position requires modeling Earth's elliptical orbit with iterative numerical methods rather than simple circular approximations.

topQuotes.quote: This is a star chart, but just for the sun.
The way I have actually gotten these positions is basically the most complicated possible method.
This is technically not an exact solution for the earths location in orbit but it gets 5 times more accurate with each correction so its indiscernible after 10 corrections

posts.title: Dangerous Misinformation
posts.summary: George Hotz humorously complains about Google's Knowledge Panel incorrectly listing his height as 5'4" instead of 5'10". He frames this trivial error as serious 'misinformation' and threatens a 'libel suit,' satirically invoking Google's own stated concerns about misinformation during COVID. The post ends with a mock demand for an apology from Sundar Pichai or a settlement of $3.50 via Google Pay.
posts.key_insight: If platforms are going to position themselves as arbiters of truth, they should at least get basic facts right about the people they're cataloging.

topQuotes.quote: Google, please take this misinformation seriously before you end up in a very large libel suit.
I know from COVID that you take misinformation very seriously.
This is not on a site you are linking to, this is first party misinformation from Google.

posts.title: The Mac Is a Power Tool
posts.summary: Gruber argues that macOS is losing its balance between security protections and power-user functionality, with permission prompts becoming increasingly infuriating. He traces the Mac's history from the laissez-faire classic Mac OS era to today's increasingly restrictive approach, acknowledging that many protections are necessary but warning that Apple has crossed a line. Using the analogy of SawStop table saws, he argues safety features should protect users without impeding legitimate work. He contends that users who need the level of protection Apple is now imposing on Mac users should be using iPads instead, and that the iPad's own weaknesses are partly to blame for pushing unsophisticated users onto Macs.
posts.key_insight: Apple's increasingly aggressive macOS security prompts are degrading the Mac's core identity as a power tool, partly because the iPad's limitations force unsophisticated users onto a platform that was never meant to be an appliance.

topQuotes.quote: At a certain point, a hammer needs to hammer whatever it strikes, and sometimes, alas, that's the user's thumb. That's the Mac.
It is not an appliance intended to prevent any possible malware or scamware from running. Apple makes such appliances. They run iOS.
The Mac is a platform where you need to be able to shoot yourself in the foot.

posts.title: How To Create A Winning Pitch Deck
posts.summary: Stancil argues that typical pitch deck market-sizing slides often signal confusion about who the product is for and substitute big, vague TAM numbers for real planning. The better approach is to size and focus on the painfully small initial market that will actually buy an MVP, then use that as a deliberate path to expanding the market. The goal is not impressing VCs with huge numbers but demonstrating a credible, specific plan to reach scale.
posts.key_insight: A winning pitch deck should anchor on the real, narrow initial market that will buy the MVP and show a credible expansion plan, rather than inflating TAM numbers to signal ambition.

topQuotes.quote: Most startups die because the first thing they build... appeals to some tiny idiosyncratic subset of the billion-dollar Gartner blob.
Aim for a big market, and miss big by building something for nobody. But aim for a small one, and you might crack it open.
Step two is a delusion and a distraction.

posts.title: DuckDB with Hannes Mühleisen
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen discusses DuckDB's design philosophy, inspired by SQLite's ease of use but built for analytical workloads. He explains why DuckDB was written in C++, how it can query data directly from CSV and Parquet files without ingestion, and its three main use cases: prototyping, local data analysis, and enterprise data pipelines. He describes the technical challenges of building a database from scratch over six years, including transactions for columnar bulk updates, graceful memory degradation, and storage format backwards compatibility. He also discusses DuckDB Labs' unusual self-funded business model and the future vision of DuckDB as an extensible platform.
posts.key_insight: By focusing on the 'tail scale' — solving data problems for the 99% who don't need distributed systems — DuckDB delivers a nimble, zero-configuration analytical engine that queries data where it lives without ingestion.

topQuotes.quote: People love solving Google's data problems in their research, in their work and systems because if it doesn't scale, it's useless. But we completely forget that 99% of data problems doesn't actually need any of that.
One of the cardinal sins people do with data systems is they have a columnar system and then they slap an old school row-wise API on top, like the Postgres API and protocol.
We set out to solve the data problems for the 99% of us, because it's so common to sort of solve the problems of the one percenters.

posts.title: Is It Time To Version Observability? (Signs Point To Yes)
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that observability tooling needs a version bump: 'Observability 1.0' (metrics, logs, traces as separate pillars) is fundamentally incompatible with 'Observability 2.0' (wide, structured log events as a single source of truth). She contends that metrics-based tools are increasingly expensive and inadequate for understanding complex modern systems, while structured event-based approaches offer superior debugging, cost efficiency, and developer experience.
posts.key_insight: The shift from metrics-based observability to wide structured log events isn't an incremental improvement but a breaking change that unlocks fundamentally different — and vastly simpler — ways of understanding, debugging, and developing complex software systems.

topQuotes.quote: Datadog and Prometheus are the last, best metrics-backed tools that will ever be built. You can't catch up to them or beat them at that; no one can. Do something different.
Metrics are a great tool for cheaply summarizing vast quantities of data. They are not equipped to help you introspect or understand complex systems. You will go broke and go mad if you try.
Teams are beating themselves up trying to master an archaic, unmasterable set of technical tradeoffs based on data types from the 80s. It's an unwinnable war.

posts.title: Go structs are copied on assignment (and other things about Go I'd missed)
posts.summary: Evans describes discovering that Go structs are copied on assignment, a basic language behavior she'd missed despite years of casual Go usage. A bug in a range loop where she returned a pointer to a copied struct rather than the original led her to the '100 Go Mistakes' book, which revealed additional gaps in her understanding. She catalogs several Go pitfalls including slice append side effects and the distinction between value and pointer receivers. The post serves as both a practical Go debugging story and an endorsement of the '100 common mistakes' learning format.
posts.key_insight: Years of casual experience with a programming language can leave fundamental misconceptions undetected, and structured 'common mistakes' resources are an efficient way to surface and fill those gaps.

topQuotes.quote: When I learned that I was mistaken about how assignment worked in Go I was really taken aback, like – it's such a basic fact about the language works! If I was wrong about that then what ELSE am I wrong about in Go????
I don't think this has ever actually happened to me, but it's alarming and I'm very happy to know about it.

I thought I more or less understood the basics of the language, but this week I've been writing a lot more Go than usual while working on some upgrades to Mess with DNS, and ran into a bug that revealed I was missing a very basic concept!

posts.title: 15 Years of Realtime OLAP (Part 2)
posts.summary: Riccomini chronicles his experience building fraud detection systems at WePay, revealing how this work deepened his understanding of realtime OLAP requirements. The fraud detection use case demanded sub-10-second data freshness and single-digit millisecond query responses—far stricter than human-facing dashboards. After evaluating Druid and finding it insufficient for their latency needs, WePay built a custom pipeline using Kafka, BigQuery, Dataflow, and Bigtable. This experience led Riccomini to distinguish between 'human interaction' OLAP (flexible queries, hundreds of milliseconds acceptable) and 'machine interaction' OLAP (pre-defined queries, millisecond response times required). He observes that realtime OLAP appears across domains—fraud, observability, logging, metrics—each with overlapping but distinct requirements, and that these specialized systems are now converging.
posts.key_insight: Realtime OLAP is not a single problem but a spectrum of use cases with varying requirements for latency, freshness, and query flexibility—and the systems built for each niche are now converging.

topQuotes.quote: The Kibana dashboards looked an awful lot like business analytics chart'n'graph dashboards to me. The data was just machine instances, CPUs, and disks.
I started to see realtime OLAP everywhere.
Data warehouses, real-time OLAP systems, time series databases, and search engines might all converge.

posts.title: Cookie banners show everything that's wrong with the EU
posts.summary: DHH uses the persistence of cookie banners as evidence that the EU is fundamentally incapable of effective tech regulation. He argues that billions spent on cookie banner compliance have produced no meaningful privacy improvements, yet the EU has no mechanism to self-correct this legislative failure. He extends this critique to newer regulations like the Digital Markets Act and AI legislation, suggesting that if the EU can't get something as basic as cookies right, more complex regulation is doomed. He concludes that Europe's regulatory approach is actively squandering its entrepreneurial potential and driving innovation elsewhere.
posts.key_insight: The EU's inability to fix its own cookie banner failure after years of evidence reveals a regulatory system structurally incapable of governing technology effectively.

topQuotes.quote: If the EU can screw up something as basic as cookie banners this bad, and prove so incapable and uninterested in re-calibrating its approach, what hope is there for the thornier issues?
Companies have spent billions on cookie banner compliance only to endlessly annoy users with no material improvement to their privacy.
Like many modern European laws, it's a vague, posturing, and suffocating mess that's way too specific about things it fails to prove the ability to understand.

posts.title: Apple’s Profits From Services Are on the Cusp of Surpassing Its Profits From Device Sales
posts.summary: Gruber responds to Jason Snell's observation that Apple's services profits are approaching parity with its hardware profits, raising the question of whether Apple is still fundamentally a products company. Gruber argues that services are best understood as another form of software — cloud-based software consumed on Apple's devices — rather than a departure from Apple's identity. He extends Alan Kay's famous adage about serious software companies making their own hardware, suggesting Apple's leadership believes serious device platform companies should make their own services. He concludes that services growth is a necessary evolution, not a distraction, analogous to Apple's successful retail store strategy.
posts.key_insight: Apple's services growth isn't a departure from its identity as a device company but a natural extension of it — services are just cloud-based software consumed on Apple hardware, and serious platform companies must own their services stack.

topQuotes.quote: Coins only have two sides though. If services constitute a new third dimension for the company, and we carry this analogy through, it's like a coin whose edge just keeps getting thicker. If the edge gets thick enough, it's no longer a coin — it's something else.
To extend Kay's axiom for today's world, I suspect Apple's leadership sees things this way: People who are really serious about device platforms should make their own services.

Apple has always been like one of those optical illusions that looks like one thing at first, but looks like another if you stare at it for a few seconds. A hardware company that makes great software. A software company that makes great hardware.

posts.title: Online Dating
posts.summary: George Hotz argues that online dating apps are fundamentally broken due to misaligned business models and poor user experience design. He proposes three major fixes: charge men more to balance gender ratios (citing Seeking's 4:1 female-to-male ratio as proof), de-emphasize pictures in favor of compatibility signals and linked social media accounts, and redesign the apps to function like CRM software with lead qualification metrics. He suggests these changes could be implemented as a third-party overlay on existing platforms, scraping their data to create a better experience.
posts.key_insight: Online dating apps are broken because they optimize for engagement rather than matches; fixing them requires charging men more, emphasizing compatibility over photos, and treating dating like a CRM sales pipeline with transparent success metrics.

topQuotes.quote: Charge men money per month. Only men. Every club promoter understands this.
First off, you need a business model that isn't mobile gaming.
The dating cycle is the same thing as the sales cycle. Find leads, connect, qualify leads, present, overcome objections, close the deal, and nurture.

posts.title: Ed Harris Would Like To Connect With
posts.summary: Stancil argues that LinkedIn has become a peculiar, self-contained social network where professional performance replaces genuine social life, creating an artificial feedback loop of engagement and influence. He likens it to a Truman Show-style simulation: a place that feels socially rewarding but is insulated from real-world culture, celebrity, and spontaneity.
posts.key_insight: LinkedIn succeeds by rewarding sanitized, professional self-performance with the feelings of popularity and connection, even as it remains cut off from real cultural life.

topQuotes.quote: LinkedIn offers us a deal: Don’t really be yourself; don’t talk about taboo things; put up a professional headshot; feign excitement about your new role managing enterprise IoT applications on the edge.
It’s not socializing; it’s editorial calendar crosstalk.
It’s a vault, locked on both sides.

posts.title: How good can you be at Codenames without knowing any words?
posts.summary: This post explores how a Codenames player could dominate the game by memorizing the 40 setup cards that come with the game, rather than relying on word associations. The author built a simple bot that uses only positional information from these cards and found it beats most human players, demonstrating that the game has an exploitable side-channel vulnerability. Despite this flaw, the author argues Codenames still has better replayability than most word games, which typically break down after just a few plays when players memorize common word associations.
posts.key_insight: Even seemingly simple games can have exploitable side-channels that reveal the gap between designed and actual gameplay, much like how AI tools are already 'good enough' for many software tasks despite not being anywhere near perfect.

topQuotes.quote: The relevant question isn't 'when will AI allow laypeople to create better software than programmers?' but 'when will AI allow laypeople to create software that's as good as phone trees and crappy voice recognition are for customer support?'

Most software that's in production today has fairly weak performance and correctness constraints. People basically just accept that software has a ton of bugs and that it's normal to run into hundreds or thousands of software bugs in any given week.
This very silly bot that doesn't guess until it can guess everything is much stronger than most Codenames players.

posts.title: Finding acoustical delight in THE THOCK
posts.summary: DHH shares his recent discovery of mechanical keyboards and the satisfying 'thock' sound they produce. After years of contentment with Apple's Magic Keyboard, he fell into the mechanical keyboard rabbit hole and found unexpected daily joy in the typing experience. He celebrates the incredible variety and affordability in the mechanical keyboard market as a triumph of capitalism and competition. He recommends two specific boards — the Varmilo Milo 75 and Lofree Flow 84 — as entry points for anyone curious about upgrading their typing experience.
posts.key_insight: The best tools aren't always about solving problems — sometimes they're about adding unexpected delight to the daily grind of work you're already doing.

topQuotes.quote: Mechanical keyboards don't solve any problems. In fact, if anything, they bring quite a few with them!
Both look amazing, feel amazing, and make a mockery of the $100 Apple Magic Keyboard that I thought was all I ever needed for so long.
The sheer variety and depth of this market would make Adam Smith blush.

posts.title: Instead of restricting AI and algorithms, make them explainable
posts.summary: Martin Fowler argues that regulating AI and algorithms by restricting their use misses the point—the real issue is opacity, not the technology itself. He contends that any decision affecting people's lives must be explainable, whether made by AI, complex algorithms, or simple chronological sorting. Using a personal Avis rental car dispute as an example, he demonstrates how opacity causes harm regardless of whether AI is involved. Fowler suggests that requiring explainability would naturally limit problematic AI deployment while incentivizing development of more transparent systems. He proposes that AI's best use may be as a tool for humans to better understand decision-making processes, potentially replacing opaque models with explicit logic over time.
posts.key_insight: The problem with AI and algorithmic decision-making isn't the technology itself but the opacity—requiring explainability is a more effective regulatory principle than restricting specific technologies.

topQuotes.quote: A regulation that says a social media company should forego its 'algorithm' for a reverse-chronological feed misses the fact that a reverse-chronological feed is itself an algorithm.
We should consider it as a smell for human to agree with computer suggestions too often.
The general principle should be that decisions made by software must be explainable.

posts.title: My Obsidian Note-Taking Workflow
posts.summary: Simon Späti details his comprehensive Obsidian-based note-taking workflow, explaining how he evolved from scattered notes across multiple apps to a unified 'Second Brain' system. He emphasizes using plain text Markdown files for future-proofing, combining PARA and Zettelkasten methodologies for organization, and leveraging Vim motions for efficient navigation. The workflow includes templates for different note types, a tag-based system for note maturity levels, and public sharing via Quartz. His core argument is that this system provides clarity, peace of mind, and the ability to find knowledge intuitively through graph-based connections rather than rigid folder structures.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed personal knowledge management system should prioritize finding information through intuitive connections and associations rather than rigid folder hierarchies, using open formats that outlast any single tool.

topQuotes.quote: Imagine if everyone would update their articles or notes instead of creating copies repeatedly; the internet would get a web of remarkable, highly valuable notes.

The satisfaction I get from you guys giving me feedback, telling me that it was helpful, pointing out some alternatives or just making friends online, is something you can't replicate in the real world.
Never would I have thought, as an organized Swiss person, that I would leave the path of putting everything into folder structures to find easily.

posts.title: All Watched over By Machines of Insanity
posts.summary: Drawing on Adam Curtis's documentary and a Nature paper on AI model collapse, the post argues that AI training on its own generated data leads not to a static world but a degenerating, nonsensical one. As AI-generated content exponentially grows and poisons both machine and human knowledge, rare perspectives and marginalized voices will be systematically forgotten, accelerating the worst effects of content bubbles.
posts.key_insight: AI model collapse and human content bubbles are fundamentally the same phenomenon — recursive self-training that erases diversity — and their convergence threatens to accelerate a global degeneration of knowledge that will erase marginalized perspectives first.

topQuotes.quote: The voices of marginalized groups will literally be forgotten as their experiences are improbable compared to the rest. Their experiences are the tails of the distribution of human experience, and with this forgetting we will forget ourselves and what the whole of human experience is.
Maybe Adam Curtis was wrong! Maybe we are headed, not for a static world, but a nonsensical one.

There seems to be a deep connection here between model collapse and content bubbles. After all, content bubbles are just people creating content and learning from that content, and only that content, reducing the variance of what they see and hear.

posts.title: Pragmatism, Neutrality and Leadership
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that leaders must prioritize business success above all else, but that banning political speech at work is a cowardly cop-out that protects CEOs from uncomfortable feedback rather than protecting the mission. She advocates for a middle path: maintaining neutral, mission-focused workspaces while genuinely listening to employees and doing the hard work of navigating tensions rather than silencing them.
posts.key_insight: Banning political speech at work is not principled neutrality — it's leaders using their power to avoid the hard, uncomfortable work of actually leading through conflict and listening to their employees.

topQuotes.quote: I believe that leaders who care about building a workplace culture rooted in dignity and respect have a responsibility to care even more about success in business. Let's show these motherfuckers how it's done.
If people want to work at your company more than they want to use your product, that's a bad sign.
But you signed up for this, bro. It's part of the job, and you're getting paid a lot of money to do it. You don't get to just nope out when the going gets rough.

posts.title: Building a CDN for Databases With Luke Kim
posts.summary: In this interview, Luke Kim of Spice AI explains how his startup addresses the gap in serving data lakehouse files in production. Spice AI functions as a CDN for databases, providing a caching and materialization layer that sits between applications and data warehouses/lakes. The architecture prioritizes single-node optimization with horizontal scalability, drawing from Kim's experience building Dapr at Microsoft. Spice can be deployed as sidecars alongside applications, stackable across infrastructure tiers from edge to cloud, minimizing data transit while maintaining query performance. The system supports both proactive materialization and reactive caching, with CDC support for consistency across distributed replicas.
posts.key_insight: The missing piece in the data lakehouse ecosystem is a CDN-like caching layer that brings data closer to applications, and the sidecar pattern perfected by service meshes provides the ideal deployment model for this use case.

topQuotes.quote: Don't fight physics; minimize data transit.
The history of the technology industry is a story of turning lower layers of the application stack into commoditized building blocks.
Like the cloud infrastructure example, lower level plumbing can be slow to build, expensive, hard to maintain and get right, and often offers little differentiated business value.

posts.title: 15 Years of Realtime OLAP (Part 1)
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini traces the evolution of realtime OLAP systems through his personal experience at LinkedIn, starting with the Who's Viewed My Profile feature 15 years ago. The initial solution, Avatara, used pre-materialized views stored in Voldemort but suffered from rigid query patterns, high write amplification, and data staleness. This led to the development of Apache Druid by Eric Tschetter at MetaMarkets and Apache Pinot by Kishore Gopalakrishna at LinkedIn, both solving these problems through columnar storage combined with in-memory buffering for recent data. The post explains how these systems enable millisecond-latency queries on fresh data without the limitations of pre-computation, setting the stage for a future discussion of ClickHouse.
posts.key_insight: Realtime OLAP systems evolved from rigid pre-materialized views to flexible columnar stores with in-memory buffering, enabling millisecond-latency user-facing analytics without sacrificing query flexibility or data freshness.

topQuotes.quote: OLAP developed many fancy concepts—cubes, dimensions, measures, and so on. But it's really just SELECT COUNT(*) FROM … GROUP BY queries.
It was a classic vanity metric. Naturally, the product was popular and drove considerable subscriber revenue.

Data warehouses are built to handle queries on large data sets but with slower response times (seconds to minutes). WVMP requires millisecond response times since end-users are waiting for the page to load.

posts.title: Apple Strikes Deal With Taboola to Sell Ads for Apple News
posts.summary: Apple has struck a deal with Taboola, the company best known for chumbox ads, to sell native advertising in Apple News and Apple Stocks apps. Gruber notes that Apple News ads already looked like Taboola-style chumbox ads before the deal, making the partnership unsurprising. He documents the poor ad experience in Apple News with screen recordings showing highly repetitive ads while reading a Rolling Stone article. While acknowledging this isn't good news, he sardonically suggests the partnership is unlikely to make the already-low ad quality any worse.
posts.key_insight: Apple's partnership with Taboola is less a fall from grace than a formalization of the chumbox-quality advertising already plaguing Apple News.

topQuotes.quote: If you told me that the ads in Apple News have been sold by Taboola for the last few years, I'd have said, 'Oh, that makes sense.'
So while I don't think it's good news that Apple is partnering with Taboola, I don't expect it to make any discernible difference in the ad quality or frequency. Maybe it will improve the variety?
Because the ads in Apple News — at least the ones I see — already look like chumbox Taboola ads. Even worse, they're incredibly repetitious.

posts.title: F*ck Around and Find Out
posts.summary: The article argues that the rush to add generative AI to data products is premature hype that distracts from fundamental unsolved problems like data quality, governance, and basic BI accuracy. The authors contend that most companies can't even get their dashboards to match, yet are being sold AI features that promise 'anyone can talk to their data' - a dangerous proposition when the underlying data foundations are broken.
posts.key_insight: Before chasing AI features, companies should focus on solving their fundamental data problems - you can't magically chat with data that's still broken at its foundation.

topQuotes.quote: 2024 is the year of f*cking around and finding out how well GenAI works in production.
May as well hand a loaded gun to business stakeholders by passing 'human in the loop' responsibility over to people who are woefully unprepared to suss out when something is blatantly wrong.
The user story for most of these features was probably 'As a SaaS vendor, I want to add AI to my product.'

posts.title: The AT&T Data Breach Shows Why RCS Can’t Be Trusted and the Downside of Apple Adding Support for It in iOS 18
posts.summary: Gruber argues that the AT&T data breach exposing millions of customers' call and text records demonstrates why RCS is a fundamentally flawed protocol that Apple shouldn't be adopting in iOS 18. He contends that any new messaging standard should require end-to-end encryption, which RCS lacks at the protocol level. Google's proprietary E2EE implementation only works between Google Messages users, and the RCS spec itself offers no encryption. Gruber acknowledges the pragmatic argument that carrier-based messaging is universal and should be improved, but counters that platforms like WhatsApp already serve as encrypted universal baselines in most countries. He suggests the effort spent pushing Apple toward RCS would have been better directed at getting Apple to release iMessage for Android.
posts.key_insight: Adding RCS support to iOS legitimizes an unencrypted messaging protocol at a time when the industry should be moving exclusively toward end-to-end encryption, and the real solution to cross-platform messaging was always shipping iMessage for Android.

topQuotes.quote: Even if you trust law enforcement agencies to handle this sensitive data securely, it's clear, from this latest data breach alone, that the carriers themselves cannot be trusted. They're inept. They always have been inept. And my money says they always will be.

Carrier-based messaging was best left as a legacy protocol. SMS wasn't dying, but it was slowly fading away, and should have been left for things like automated 'your table is ready' notifications from restaurants.
All of the effort spent pushing Apple to support RCS would have been better spent pushing Apple to ship iMessage for Android.

posts.title: So you want to compete with or replace open source
posts.summary: DeVault argues that the economics of open source are fundamentally rooted in collaboration, making them incompatible with business models that seek exclusive monetization rights. He examines how startups attracted by open source economics discovered that the model works for the industry as a whole but not necessarily for individual companies seeking sole profit. He critiques emerging 'post-open' and 'fair source' movements as attempts to retain open source's collaborative benefits while restricting commercial rights, which he considers irreconcilable. He respects these movements' right to exist but insists they not co-opt the 'open source' label, and maintains that successful open source businesses must embrace shared commercial markets.
posts.key_insight: The collaborative foundation that makes open source economically powerful is fundamentally incompatible with any model that reserves exclusive commercial rights for a single entity.

topQuotes.quote: The revolutionary economics of FOSS are based on collaboration, and are incompatible with competition.
No one is incentivized to work for you, for free, for your own exclusive profit.
Open source ate a lot of lunches. There are some kinds of software which you just can't make in a classic silicon valley startup fashion anymore.

posts.title: Toolbox languages
posts.summary: Hillel Wayne introduces the concept of 'toolbox languages' - programming languages optimized for quickly solving problems without third-party dependencies. He showcases five personal favorites beyond Python: AutoHotKey for GUI automation, J for terse array computations, Frink for dimensional analysis with units, Raku for scripting with powerful syntactic features, and Picat for logic programming with constraint solving. The post argues that good toolbox languages prioritize speed of writing over other concerns, with terseness, rich builtins, and good REPLs being key characteristics.
posts.key_insight: The best programming languages for quick problem-solving aren't necessarily the best general-purpose languages - specialized 'toolbox' languages that prioritize terseness and rich builtins can dramatically outperform Python for specific domains.

topQuotes.quote: A toolbox language is a programming language that's good at solving problems without requiring third party packages.
Autohotkey is basically 'shell scripting for GUIs'. Just a fantastic tool to smooth over using unprogrammable applications.
Really good at doing arithmetic on arrays, hair-pullingly frustrating at doing anything with strings or structured data.

posts.title: It’s the Guns, It’s the Guns, It’s the Guns
posts.summary: Gruber responds to the assassination attempt on Donald Trump by condemning political violence while refusing to accept the narrative that Democratic rhetoric caused it. He argues that Trump himself is the only candidate who has incited political violence, mocked assassination attempts on others, and genuinely threatened democracy through his refusal to accept the 2020 election results. Gruber pushes back on the notion that the shooting will hand Trump the election, citing historical examples of candidates who survived assassination attempts and still lost. He concludes by urging Democrats to seize the moment by forcing a vote on gun control legislation, reframing the conversation around America's broader epidemic of gun violence.
posts.key_insight: The assassination attempt on Trump should not silence criticism of his genuine threat to democracy, and Democrats should pivot the moment toward gun control legislation that exposes Republican hypocrisy on political violence.

topQuotes.quote: Donald Trump wasn't an inch away from assassination because of Democratic rhetoric against his threat to democracy. He is a threat to democracy. He threatened democracy on national television.
It's not just an outrage when your right-wing authoritarian hero gets his ear nicked by an assassin's bullet. It's an outrage when anyone is shot by a nut with a gun.
Give Trump credit: he fully admits that the only election results he will accept are results that declare him the winner.

posts.title: The Tortoise and the Hare - Horizontal vs Vertical Integration
posts.summary: The data industry faces a fundamental choice between vertically-integrated mega platforms controlled by big clouds and a horizontally-integrated ecosystem of interoperable open technologies. While horizontal integration is slower and requires extensive coordination (the 'tortoise' approach), it offers better long-term maintainability and staying power. AI complicates this picture because model training costs currently favor plugging into major vendors' APIs.
posts.key_insight: The data industry's future hinges on whether the slow, collaborative work of building interoperable open technologies can provide a viable alternative to vertically-integrated platforms before AI consolidates power in a handful of megacorporations.

topQuotes.quote: I'd hate to see a world where the AI-Industrial Complex is the gatekeeper to models. It already sucks seeing the big clouds as the tollroads for many companies' tech and data workloads.
The big question is who owns this stack - a handful of big megacorporations or nobody? For innovation and humanity's sake, I very much hope it's the latter.

What world do you want to live in? A vertically integrated one or one where it's horizontally integrated and best of breed? But it's really challenging to deliver cohesive user experiences in the horizontal integration world.

posts.title: Living with Linux and Android after two decades of Apple
posts.summary: DHH reflects on his successful transition from Apple's ecosystem to Linux and Android after two decades. He emphasizes that the switch was far easier than expected and has been genuinely enjoyable rather than merely an act of rebellion. He promotes his Omakub Linux setup and highlights Android's real openness advantages like sideloading apps and replacing default launchers. Rather than trying to convince satisfied Apple users to switch, he addresses those already dissatisfied, assuring them the alternatives are genuinely good on their own merits.
posts.key_insight: Leaving Apple's ecosystem after two decades revealed that the perceived difficulty of switching was mostly habit-driven fear, and that Linux and Android offer genuinely compelling experiences defined by their own strengths rather than as Apple alternatives.

topQuotes.quote: Running an open source operating system simply feels right for someone who owes their entire career to open source.
Their value isn't defined as not-Apple. There's no valor in that.
Falling in love with something new is a better and healthier motivator than hating something old.

posts.title: Bring Your Own Cloud, Nuon, and Hosted SaaS Challenges With Jon Morehouse
posts.summary: This interview explores the emerging Bring Your Own Cloud (BYOC) deployment model as an alternative to traditional multi-tenant SaaS. Jon Morehouse argues that B2B software vendors have unnecessarily adopted B2C multi-tenant architectures that many enterprise customers don't actually want, as customers increasingly value data ownership over convenience. BYOC decouples software management from deployment, allowing vendors to manage software that runs in customer environments. The conversation covers practical challenges (installation automation, day-2 tooling, updates) that Nuon aims to solve, and distinguishes between 'vertical clouds' like Snowflake/Confluent and application vendors who should embrace BYOC. Morehouse envisions a future where B2B SaaS vendors only offer BYOC, fundamentally changing SaaS margins to purely R&D and support costs.
posts.key_insight: The future of B2B software lies in decoupling deployment from management through BYOC, allowing vendors to provide SaaS-like experiences while customers retain control of their data and infrastructure costs.

topQuotes.quote: All of today's cloud products are meant for building software, not consuming software.
It feels like each B2B/infrastructure company has invested all this money, time, and energy into building SaaS offerings; yet their customer's do not actually want them. It just feels… broken.
Imagine a world where all SaaS companies are running in their customer's cloud account. SaaS margins would be purely R&D and support.

posts.title: Entering text in the terminal is complicated
posts.summary: The post categorizes terminal text input into four modes: baseline (no input library), readline-based, alternative libraries like libedit, and fully custom systems. Evans explains why entering and editing text at the command line is surprisingly complicated, primarily due to inconsistency between programs. She traces readline's keybindings back to Emacs, explains why some programs don't use readline (licensing, simplicity, minimal interactivity), and recommends rlwrap as a way to add readline support to any program. The core message is that understanding which input mode you're in makes the terminal feel predictable rather than chaotic.
posts.key_insight: Terminal text input is confusing because programs silently use four different input systems with varying capabilities, but learning to identify which one you're in transforms the experience from chaotic to predictable.

topQuotes.quote: It took me maybe 15 years of using the terminal every single day to get used to using Ctrl+A to go to the beginning of the line.
I'm extremely ignorant of most of readline's features. I only use maybe 5 keyboard shortcuts, so as long as they support the 5 basic commands I know I feel pretty comfortable.
A big part of what makes entering text in the terminal hard is the inconsistency between how different programs handle entering text.

posts.title: Plastic Clocks
posts.summary: Reis argues that labeling products as 'AI-powered' is meaningless marketing fluff, comparing it to the absurdity of advertising a 1950s clock as a 'plastic clock.' The technology should serve the product's utility invisibly, not be the selling point itself.
posts.key_insight: Technology like AI should be invisible infrastructure that makes products better, not a marketing badge that substitutes for actual utility.

topQuotes.quote: I can't imagine the manufacturer advertising the clock as a 'plastic clock.' It's just a clock that happens to include some plastic.
I don't believe the end customer cares whether a product is 'AI-powered.' People want products that work.
What does a product have to do with data or AI? If a product uses data or AI, it's there to serve the functionality and utility of the product, not the other way around.

posts.title: Make Lakehouse Catalogs Boring Again
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that the data lakehouse ecosystem should standardize on Apache Iceberg's REST API for core catalog functionality, with query engines like Trino and DataFusion driving its evolution rather than individual vendors. He distinguishes between 'core' functionality (metadata for query execution) and 'non-core' features (governance, lineage, discovery), suggesting vendors should compete on the latter. The post notes that major players like Databricks Unity, Snowflake Polaris, and Nessie are already adopting Iceberg's REST API. This standardization would allow query engines to deprecate fragmented integrations like Hive Metastore and AWS Glue catalog, creating a cleaner ecosystem.
posts.key_insight: The lakehouse ecosystem should let query engines drive catalog API evolution while vendors compete on enterprise features, turning infrastructure standards into a rising tide that lifts all boats.

topQuotes.quote: Traditional data catalogs have been unable to shift from nice-to-have to must-have in the enterprise; they remain a dusty tool that's often ignored once installed.
Data lakehouse vendors have accidentally stumbled upon the wedge that traditional data catalogs have been struggling to find.
Reorienting the API evolution around the query engines it serves means more projects can take an active role, thereby reducing the control of one or two vendors.

posts.title: Reasons to use your shell's job control
posts.summary: Evans explores the use cases for shell job control (fg, bg, Ctrl+Z) after polling her Mastodon audience for their habits. She explains the three process states (foreground, background, stopped) and the commands for moving between them. The post catalogs nine practical reasons people use job control, from killing unresponsive processes to suspending CPU-hungry programs. Evans admits she mostly prefers opening new terminal tabs but found several use cases compelling, particularly killing Ctrl+C-resistant processes and running tcpdump in the background alongside other commands.
posts.key_insight: Shell job control is an underused but practical tool with specific scenarios where it outperforms the common habit of opening new terminal tabs, especially for killing unresponsive processes and correlating interleaved output from background tools like tcpdump.

topQuotes.quote: This really surprised me because I usually think of having the output of lots of different commands interleaved as being a bad thing, but one person said that they like to do this with tcpdump specifically and I think that actually sounds extremely useful.

Many people replied that they didn't use job control intentionally, but that they sometimes accidentally ran Ctrl+Z, which stopped whatever program was running, so they needed to learn how to use fg to bring it back to the foreground.
I feel like I'd also be likely to switch terminal tabs and end up wondering "wait… where did I put my editor???" and have to go searching for it.

posts.title: The Right Kind of Stubborn
posts.summary: Graham argues that persistence and obstinacy are different behaviors, not just labels applied after the fact. The persistent stay committed to a goal while remaining flexible on methods, listening to criticism and updating their plans. Obstinacy is a reflexive resistance to changing ideas, which can work for simple problems but fails on hard ones.
posts.key_insight: The right kind of stubbornness is goal-driven persistence that stays open to evidence and keeps adapting its methods.

topQuotes.quote: The persistent are attached to the goal. The obstinate are attached to their ideas about how to reach it.
The reason the persistent and the obstinate seem similar is that they're both hard to stop.
Persistence often requires that one change one's mind.

posts.title: Mixed Model Arts - The Convergence of Data Modeling Disciplines
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that data modeling is evolving from siloed, single-discipline approaches toward a multi-disciplinary practice he calls 'Mixed Model Arts.' Drawing an analogy to how MMA fighters must now master multiple fighting styles rather than relying on boxing or wrestling alone, he contends that data professionals must become proficient across relational, dimensional, ML/AI, and semantic data modeling techniques to remain effective.
