posts.key_insight: Just as MMA forced martial artists to become multi-disciplinary fighters, the convergence of AI, data products, and cross-functional use cases is forcing data practitioners to master multiple data modeling approaches rather than specializing in a single technique.

topQuotes.quote: AI without good-quality data means you'll do dumb things more quickly.
Ignore this, and you're like the boxer in UFC 1 wearing a single boxing glove. You're not going to win at the game of data.
In general, Productland is the future and Enterpriseland is a dead-end.

posts.title: Ambition Then And Now
posts.summary: Using the Away CEO controversy and his own career arc, Stancil argues that Silicon Valley’s obsession with growth turns ambition into a default, socially enforced scoreboard. He contends that our ambitions are largely circumstantial—shaped by the environments we immerse ourselves in—and warns that we can spend years climbing ladders we didn’t consciously choose.
posts.key_insight: Ambition in tech is often an environmental artifact, so without deliberate choice we end up chasing success metrics that the ecosystem hands us rather than the ones we truly want.

topQuotes.quote: In Silicon Valley, we’ve drowned ourselves so deeply in this ethos that we no longer realize that we’re in the water.

Standing on the shore, your old pond—the pond where you did your life's work, in which nothing seemed more important than being a big fish—suddenly looks like all the other ponds you used to care so little about.
We created them to manufacture accomplishment for ourselves.

posts.title: Nadia Asparouhova
posts.summary: This post investigates whether prior meditation experience predicts success with jhanas, a series of altered mental states accessed through concentration. Analyzing data from 81 participants across Jhourney retreats, the author finds virtually no difference in jhana success rates between experienced and beginner meditators. While experienced meditators showed some tendency to progress further when successful, these differences were not statistically attributable to meditation experience itself. The author hypothesizes that jhana success depends on two underlying skills — the ability to invoke positive feelings and the ability to sustain attention — rather than meditation hours logged. She draws an analogy to Rich Sutton's 'bitter lesson' in AI, suggesting that brute-force practice of core skills may matter more than introspective understanding of one's mental barriers.
posts.key_insight: Jhana success appears driven not by accumulated meditation experience but by two specific underlying skills — invoking positive feelings and sustaining attention — suggesting we should measure aptitude rather than practice hours.

topQuotes.quote: Instead of introspecting heavily – trying to model where these feelings came from, and how they impact one's behavior – it could be more effective to simply 'brute force' one's way into sparking joy and cultivating attention.
Across all three dimensions of meditation experience, we see virtually no difference in success rates between experienced vs. beginner meditators.

What does it mean to be an 'experienced' meditator, in the way that someone is an 'experienced' swimmer? Number of hours meditated tells us how long someone has been practicing, but not how adept they are at cultivating and sustaining a quiet mind.

posts.title: What is meditation for?
posts.summary: Asparouhova continues exploring jhanas—altered states of consciousness accessible through concentration—after attending meditation retreats that profoundly changed her perspective. She wrote pragmatic, non-spiritual instructions for reaching jhana states that proved successful for newcomers. Partnering with the retreat company Jhourney, she investigated whether meditation experience correlates with jhana success and found no correlation. This finding leads her to question whether jhanas should even be classified as a 'meditation practice' and whether meditation itself is being confused with the deeper skills it aims to develop.
posts.key_insight: Meditation may be merely one method—a finger pointing at the moon—for developing underlying attentional skills, and we should stop conflating the practice itself with the deeper capacities it aims to cultivate.

topQuotes.quote: I went back to try it again, and accidentally discovered how to voluntarily turn my consciousness on and off, which occasioned an experience so profound that I decided not to write about it.
I think meditation is a finger, and we don't know what the moon is yet.
This finding casts into question why jhanas are described as a 'meditation practice' at all.

posts.title: Data Lakehouse Catalog Reality Check
posts.summary: Riccomini examines the recent open source catalog announcements from Databricks (Unity) and Snowflake (Polaris), finding both fall far short of their marketing promises. Snowflake's Polaris Github repository is essentially empty despite extensive marketing materials. Databricks released an API-compatible rewrite of Unity rather than their actual product, missing critical features like audit capabilities and ML/AI governance. A third-party Rust rewrite (unity-rs) has also emerged, but it's barely started. Riccomini concludes that while these projects will likely mature, the vendors appear to be flailing without a clear strategy.
posts.key_insight: The gap between vendor marketing and actual open source deliverables in the data lakehouse catalog space suggests these companies are rushing to stake claims without clear strategies or substantial code to back up their announcements.

topQuotes.quote: Out of all of these announcements, all this marketing, and all this noise, we've gotten one partial re-write, one empty Github project, and one hello world Rust file.
Yes, but have you looked at the code?
I'm not even sure they all understand why they're open sourcing these projects.

posts.title: What is Self Hosted? What is a Stack?
posts.summary: Ronacher argues that “self hosted” is muddied when modern tools depend on proprietary cloud runtimes, using Counterscale’s Cloudflare dependency as a case study. He contrasts earlier stacks like LAMP/WAMP, which were self-hostable because they relied on shared, open protocols such as CGI and SQL. Today’s equivalents—serverless runtimes, analytics stores, queues, caches—exist, but lack common, portable protocols and packaging. That gap pushes developers to target proprietary platforms with nicer abstractions, trading portability for ease. He concludes the web needs new, socially agreed protocols and a “SaaS in a box” experience to make small-scale open source software easier to build and host.
posts.key_insight: Self-hosting will stay constrained until the web agrees on portable, high-level protocols for modern primitives the way CGI and SQL once did.

topQuotes.quote: Is it really self hosted if your only way to deploy it is some proprietary cloud vendor?
We did not agree on protocols and we definitely did not really have that stuff today in a neatly packaged and reusable thing.
CGI in many ways is the original serverless.

posts.title: Apple Disables WebKit’s JIT in Lockdown Mode, Offering a Hint Why BrowserEngineKit Is Complex and Restricted
posts.summary: Gruber examines why Apple created BrowserEngineKit as a complex, restricted framework for EU DMA compliance rather than simply opening up iOS to third-party browser engines. He uses Apple's own decision to disable WebKit's JIT compiler in Lockdown Mode as evidence that JIT compilation poses genuine security risks, not just a pretext for anti-competitive behavior. He outlines three possible approaches Apple could have taken and argues that the middle path—a controlled framework with special entitlements—was the only reasonable choice. The post frames iOS gatekeeping as a legitimate security feature rather than mere anti-competitive behavior.
posts.key_insight: Apple's decision to disable its own WebKit JIT in Lockdown Mode proves that JIT security concerns are genuine, not just a pretext, and justifies the complexity of BrowserEngineKit as the only reasonable path for allowing third-party browser engines on iOS.

topQuotes.quote: One take on Apple's longstanding prohibition against third-party rendering engines is that they're protecting their own interests with Safari. More or less that they're just being dicks about it. But there really is a security angle to it.
Gatekeeping is a fundamental aspect of Apple's brand promise with iOS.
Disallowing third-party rendering engines from using JITs wasn't going to fly, and allowing them to run willy-nilly would be insecure.

posts.title: WWDC 2024: Apple Intelligence
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple Intelligence represents the correct strategic approach to generative AI: treating it as features integrated into existing products rather than standalone chatbot products. He draws a parallel to Steve Jobs telling Dropbox it was 'a feature, not a product,' suggesting LLMs are similarly best deployed as enhancements to existing apps. Apple built its own foundation models with no chatbot, instead weaving AI into Safari, Mail, Photos, Siri, and developer frameworks. Gruber notes Apple's focus on personal context via an on-device semantic index and Private Cloud Compute represents unique differentiation. He remains skeptical of the OpenAI partnership's value and cautions that none of these features are yet available to test, making all assessments provisional.
posts.key_insight: Apple's strategic genius with Apple Intelligence is treating generative AI as invisible infrastructure woven into familiar products rather than competing in the commoditized chatbot market, though the proof remains entirely in future execution.

topQuotes.quote: Leading up to WWDC last week, I'd been thinking that this same description applies, in spades, to LLM generative AI. Fantastically useful, downright amazing at times, but features. Not products.
Anyone can make a chatbot. And, seemingly, everyone is — searching for 'chatbot' in the App Store is about as useful as searching for 'game.' Apple, conspicuously, has not made one.
AI-generated slop is polluting web search results for technical help; Apple is using targeted AI trained on its own documentation to avoid the need to search the web in the first place.

posts.title: "Success"
posts.summary: Reis challenges the conventional notion that career success means climbing the corporate ladder, arguing that many high-ranking executives feel unfulfilled despite their titles. He advocates for defining success on your own terms, whether through influence, helping others, or impact, rather than defaulting to the traditional path of title and prestige.
posts.key_insight: Career success shouldn't be measured solely by title and rank—find your own definition of success and pursue that path instead of defaulting to conventional expectations.

topQuotes.quote: Based on the conversations (some might say therapy sessions) I have with many people in high-title/rank positions, the lamentation I hear is, 'Is this it? Is this what I worked hard to achieve?'
If this is the only path to success, we might need to rethink what success means.
The corporate executive route was never for me. Yet I'm having a lot of fun and consider myself 'successful.'

posts.title: Its Too Many Options
posts.summary: The post argues that in software and data tooling, the industry overvalues optionality and underestimates the cost of constant evaluation, suggesting teams should embrace deliberate lock-in to force focus on people and process. It extends the theme of too many options to advertising, warning that AI-driven personalization could make even the content itself variable and manipulable for each viewer.
posts.key_insight: Optionality in tools and media feels empowering but often erodes stability and focus, and AI personalization risks turning that tradeoff into a pervasive, profit-driven distortion.

topQuotes.quote: Chooselock-in. Buy the BI tool;burn the boats behind you; set your tools in stone. Force yourself to worry about people and process problems by taking away your other temptations.
The magic we’re looking for is in the work we’re avoiding.
But maybe not for much longer? After all, why show people a real tile, when a modified one might get them to buy an extra cup of coffee?

posts.title: The EU Is Reaping What It Sows With the DMA: Uncertainty
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's decision to delay Apple Intelligence and other new features in the EU is a rational response to the DMA's inherent uncertainty, not spite. He contends that the European Commission enforces the 'spirit' rather than the letter of the law, making compliance unpredictable for designated gatekeepers. He highlights Margrethe Vestager's own statements as evidence that the DMA is designed to hurt Apple's business model and profits. Gruber concludes that under threat of fines up to 10-20% of worldwide revenue, any company would exercise extreme caution before launching integrated features. He closes with biting sarcasm about the EU's supposed goal of fostering competition.
posts.key_insight: The DMA's combination of deliberately vague enforcement standards and enormous financial penalties creates a regulatory environment where the only rational response for any designated gatekeeper is to delay or withhold new features — the exact opposite of the competition the law claims to promote.

topQuotes.quote: Fortunately, because Apple is delaying Apple Intelligence and these other new features in the EU, all of the thriving EU-based smartphone and OS makers can jump in and compete on merit now, without Apple the gatekeeping bully in their way.
EC proponents keep telling me it's a feature, not a bug, that unlike the US, it's the spirit, not letter, of the law that matters in the EU.

Under repeated threats of fines up to $40–80 billion dollars (10–20 percent of worldwide revenue), it would be recklessly irresponsible for Apple, or any other designated 'gatekeeper', to launch any new services or integrated features in the EU without absolute certainty that those features are compliant with the DMA.

posts.title: By My Count Trump Is Batting .900 on the Ten Commandments
posts.summary: Gruber systematically walks through each of the Ten Commandments — which Louisiana's new law requires to be displayed in all public classrooms — and matches each one against Donald Trump's well-documented history of violating it. Trump publicly endorsed the law on Truth Social, calling it 'the first major step in the revival of religion.' Gruber lets the irony do the heavy lifting, citing news reports for nine of the ten commandments that Trump has plausibly broken, giving him only a pass on honoring his father (with a sarcastic aside about Fred Trump's arrests). The post is structured as a listicle that pairs each commandment with sourced evidence of Trump's hypocrisy, requiring minimal editorial commentary because the juxtaposition speaks for itself.
posts.key_insight: The man loudly championing the Ten Commandments in public schools has a well-documented public record of violating nearly every single one of them.

topQuotes.quote: Trump speaks highly of his father — from whom he inherited his handsome visage and enviable mane — so he's clear on this one.
'He is no longer president,' says one insider, 'so he doesn't have to go to church.'
Former president and convicted felon Donald Trump, on his floundering social network, approves.

posts.title: Training Large Language Models on the Public Web
posts.summary: Gruber corrects his earlier implication that Anthropic's Claude was not trained on public web data, clarifying that both Anthropic and Apple use publicly available web content for LLM training. He examines Apple's and Anthropic's training data policies side by side, finding them largely equivalent. His main critique of Apple is the timing problem: they trained on public web data before providing an opt-out mechanism via Applebot-Extended. Ultimately, Gruber argues that using public web data for training is fundamentally acceptable, drawing an analogy to how humans learn from published information. His standard for AI is the same as for people: learn freely, but don't plagiarize — attribute and link.
posts.key_insight: Training LLMs on public web data is as legitimate as humans learning from published work — the line isn't at training, it's at plagiarism and proper attribution.

topQuotes.quote: So at the moment, my robots.txt file bans just one: Perplexity.
You're free to learn from anything I publish, but not free to plagiarize it. If you quote it, attribute and link to the source. That's my standard for AI bots as well.
Clearly, a website that bans Applebot-Extended shouldn't have its data in Apple's training corpus simply because Applebot crawled it before Apple Intelligence was even announced.

posts.title: Begun, The Catalog Wars Have
posts.summary: Data lake catalogs have become a battleground as Snowflake launched Polaris, Databricks open-sourced Unity catalog, and Databricks acquired Tabular. Unlike traditional data catalogs focused on discovery and governance, these new catalogs serve as an information_schema for query engines like Flink, Spark, and Trino. Table formats alone are insufficient—catalogs provide the crucial layer that tracks all databases, tables, and partitions. The strategic importance lies in catalogs being the integration point for query engines, making them a natural stepping stone for vendors to move up or down the stack to protect their positions.
posts.key_insight: Catalogs, not table formats, are the strategic control point in the data lakehouse stack because they're where query engines integrate, making them the natural battleground for vendor consolidation.

topQuotes.quote: Table formats like Iceberg, Hudi, and Delta always struck me as an odd layer for companies to fight over.
The layer above the table format—the catalog—is the more important layer; it's where query engines integrate.
A dominant catalog company could represent a significant threat if the company chose to move up the stack by offering or even favoring a query engine.

posts.title: Composing TLA+ Specifications with State Machines
posts.summary: This post presents a technique for composing large TLA+ specifications using state machines and a 'Sync' operator. The traditional approach to composing specs requires manually stitching together actions, which becomes unwieldy for large specifications. Wayne's solution uses TLA+'s ability to treat primed variables as both assignments and constraints, allowing a central state machine to guard and trigger transitions across independent components. The technique involves writing an abstract state machine for the core component, keeping other components 'open' (not fully self-contained), and using refinement to verify correctness. While more complex than simple specs warrant, this approach scales better for large specifications that need independent development.
posts.key_insight: By leveraging TLA+'s dual interpretation of primed variables as both assignments and constraints, you can compose large specifications through a central Sync operator that guards and triggers transitions without modifying the original components.

topQuotes.quote: Composing two specs can be as much work as writing them in the first place.
This is just a state machine! Constraints on transitions are just guard clauses and assignments on transitions are just effects.
The point of composing specs at all is so we can work on them independently.

posts.title: WTF is a "Data Team"?
posts.summary: Data teams exist in two distinct universes: Enterpriseland, where they serve back-office IT functions and struggle to prove ROI, and Productland, where data IS the product and value is immediately clear. The key to data team relevance lies in shifting from internally-focused reporting to enabling customer-facing products and experiences.
posts.key_insight: Data teams only escape the cost-center trap when they shift from serving internal reporting needs to enabling customer-facing products where data's value becomes directly measurable.

topQuotes.quote: What happens if the data team disappears tomorrow? If you answer along the lines of 'all hell breaks loose', then keep your data team. If the answer is nothing, then why have a data team?
As long as data is internally focused, it will suffer from the same politics and shortsighted behavior that's plagued data teams for decades.
In Productland, data IS the product. There's a flywheel where more data is used in better ways, which means the product performs better.

posts.title: The Important Thing For You To Know
posts.summary: Stancil argues that modern security and compliance practices are less about preventing breaches and more about allocating blame when they happen. Using Snowflake’s incident as a case, he says the cloud’s convenience shifts liability to users while vendors lean on audits and PR to claim innocence. He extends the “not my fault” logic to corporate behavior broadly, then to AI-era startup valuations driven by hype and talent rather than fundamentals.
posts.key_insight: In tech, systems are often designed less to prevent failure than to ensure that when failure happens, no one powerful can be blamed.

topQuotes.quote: That’s the thing about data breaches, and about the entire software security industrial complex: It’s not about security; it’s about blame.

Every SaaS company has carefully-worded security policies that are written by lawyers. And then they have lived security policies, which are usually “most people don’t know how to write SQL, so it’s probably fine.”
If you show revenue, people will ask how much, and it will never be enough. But if you have no revenue, you can be a pure AI play.

posts.title: The Talk Show Live From WWDC 2024
posts.summary: Gruber recaps The Talk Show Live from WWDC 2024, recorded at The California Theatre in San Jose with Apple executives John Giannandrea, Craig Federighi, and Greg Joswiak as guests. The centerpiece of the post is the groundbreaking 3D livestream of the event for Vision Pro viewers, an initiative driven entirely by Adam Lisagor and his team at Sandwich, not by Apple. Gruber details the technical setup involving a custom dual-camera rig and spatial audio microphones, and distinguishes the result as '3D video with spatial audio' rather than true spatial video. The post includes extensive positive reactions from social media, with viewers describing the experience as remarkably immersive, like sitting in the front row.
posts.key_insight: The first-ever 3D livestream for Vision Pro wasn't an Apple initiative but a scrappy creative effort pulled off in eight weeks by independent filmmakers, demonstrating that compelling spatial content can emerge from outside Apple's own ecosystem.

topQuotes.quote: And, son of a bitch, they did it.
Apple's reaction echoed my own: sounds great but how?

True spatial video would be more immersive — 'Look left to see Joz, look right to see Gruber' — and the effect we achieved wasn't quite like that. But what we did get is immersive, and very compelling.

posts.title: Nadia Asparouhova
posts.summary: Nadia provides a practical, demystified guide to achieving the jhanas — eight progressive altered mental states culminating in cessation (loss of consciousness) — which she learned in roughly 20 hours across two retreats despite minimal prior meditation experience. She frames jhanas as a body-encoded technology akin to software, arguing that the Buddhist theoretical scaffolding surrounding them unnecessarily gatekeeps what is essentially a learnable skill. She details her specific techniques for each stage, from inducing euphoria through sustained attention on joy, to navigating the dissociative and reality-dissolving higher states. Ultimately, she argues that the jhanas themselves are not the point — they are an algorithm for arriving at fundamental insights about consciousness, after which the practice feels genuinely complete.
posts.key_insight: Jhanas are a learnable, body-encoded technology for systematically intensifying positive mental states through concentrated attention, but their ultimate purpose is not the altered states themselves — it is the fundamental insights about consciousness that emerge from completing the full progression through cessation.

topQuotes.quote: If you had recently figured out how to code, and realized it was really quite simple and teachable to others – then looked around, and all you saw were computer scientists warning off would-be developers from making software – wouldn't that make you want to open up a text editor and type out your own version of things?
One day, we might look back on psychedelics as an early, coarse attempt to do this sort of thing that came with all sorts of weird side effects, like dentists using cocaine in the late 1800s.
They are an Easter egg hiding in the game of life.

posts.title: Musings on Data Lakes and Kafka Connect
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini explores three interconnected topics in data infrastructure. First, he celebrates ParadeDB's pg_lakehouse extension, which turns PostgreSQL into a data lake query engine using Apache DataFusion and OpenDAL. Second, he examines approaches for serving lakehouse data back to production systems, favoring simple caching services like ROAPI over heavier databases like StarRocks or SingleStore. Finally, he discusses the aging Kafka Connect platform and the need for improvements around connector isolation and scheduling, noting that competitors like Bento and Redpanda Connect are presenting healthy alternatives.
posts.key_insight: The future of data infrastructure lies in composable systems built from reusable components like DataFusion, while established platforms like Kafka Connect need modernization to remain relevant.

topQuotes.quote: We had to dumb-down Connect's cluster management features and bypass the HTTP API in order to achieve our goals.
The 'Connect-as-a-Service' part of Connect was completely absent.
pg_lakehouse is proof that composable data systems are not only possible, but are already here.

posts.title: Announcing DuckDB Support for Delta Lake and the Unity Catalog
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen announces official DuckDB support for Delta Lake and the Unity Catalog at the Data + AI Summit 2024. He positions DuckDB as filling the gap between Excel and Spark for last-mile data analysis. The Delta Lake integration leverages Databricks' delta kernel rather than building from scratch. He also announces DuckDB community extensions, allowing anyone to publish and share extensions easily.
posts.key_insight: DuckDB's Delta Lake integration via the shared delta kernel exemplifies how open ecosystem collaboration reduces duplicated effort and benefits everyone, especially small teams building in-process analytics tools.

topQuotes.quote: DuckDB is really perfect for this last mile of data analysis, where you may not need a whole data center to compute something.
How are we going to do that? Obviously, we're going to use the best tool for the job available, right? CSV files. Maybe not.

As an open source project and a small team, we're really excited about Tabular and Databricks bringing Delta Lake and Iceberg closer together because for us, it means we don't have to maintain two different things for essentially the same problem.

posts.title: A Short Summary of the Last Decades of Data Management
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces data management from 5,000-year-old Sumerian clay tablets to modern cloud databases, arguing that tables, SQL, and ACID transactions form an unbreakable 'holy trifecta.' He recounts how the NoSQL era saw companies abandon SQL for MapReduce due to hardware constraints, only to bring it back within years—MongoDB added schemas, Cassandra added ACID, and Databricks restored integrated storage. He predicts relational systems will absorb key-value stores, document databases, time series, vector databases, and graph databases, since all reduce to trivial table representations. Finally, he argues 'big data is dead' because modern single-node hardware is so powerful that distributing computation often makes things slower.
posts.key_insight: The history of data management is a recurring cycle of abandoning and rediscovering the same fundamentals—tables, SQL, and ACID—and modern hardware has made distributed systems unnecessary for most workloads.

topQuotes.quote: If the idea is to say, let's have a separate database because embeddings are so fundamentally different, I need 200 million in VC money to start a new database company... Well, it's just a table that has a list of fixed size as its value. And that's all there is to it.
Even if we add 32 computers to this problem, and over 1,000 CPU cores versus the 32 we started with, Spark will still be slower than DuckDB on a single machine.
Tables were there before our civilization, and they will be there after our civilization.

posts.title: Generative AI is not going to build your engineering team for you
posts.summary: Charity Majors argues that the tech industry's belief that generative AI can replace junior engineers is dangerously misguided, because writing code is the easiest part of software engineering while the hard parts—operating, understanding, and managing complex sociotechnical systems—require human judgment built through years of apprenticeship. By refusing to hire and train junior engineers, companies are cannibalizing their own future pipeline of senior engineers.
posts.key_insight: Generative AI makes the already-easy part of software engineering (writing code) even easier, but does nothing for the hard parts (managing complex systems), so using it as justification to stop hiring junior engineers is a catastrophic mistake that will destroy the industry's pipeline of future senior talent.

topQuotes.quote: By not hiring and training up junior engineers, we are cannibalizing our own future.

Writing code is the easiest part of software engineering, and it's getting easier by the day. The hard parts are what you do with that code—operating it, understanding it, extending it, and governing it over its entire lifecycle.
The bottleneck is giving them their first jobs. The bottleneck consists of companies who see them as a cost to externalize, not an investment in their—the company's—future.

posts.title: Visions of the future
posts.summary: DHH reflects on the thrill of discovering technologies and ideas before they reach mainstream adoption. He draws a line from his early embrace of Mac, Ruby, and Campfire through to his current enthusiasm for Linux on the desktop and web development simplification via #nobuild and Hotwire. He argues that recognizing potential in rough, unpolished ideas requires a certain contrarian disposition and willingness to bet on being early. The post frames this pattern of early adoption as both intellectually rewarding and personally energizing.
posts.key_insight: The greatest rewards in technology come from recognizing the potential in rough, unpolished ideas before the mainstream catches on, which requires a willingness to be contrarian and bet on your own judgment.

topQuotes.quote: Following such a sense of wanderlust requires a certain disagreeableness. Even arrogance.
Nothing gets me quite as fired up as discovering the future early and undistributed.
You have to believe that the market place of ideas isn't perfectly efficient or perfectly rational.

posts.title: The Vibes Are Weird
posts.summary: Stancil argues that Silicon Valley used to run on familiar boom-bust cycles, but the AI era is breaking the old “physics,” replacing predictable rhythms with frantic rebranding, uncertain value, and career anxiety. The result is a tech industry that outwardly projects confidence yet privately feels unmoored, unsure whether this is another wave or a structural shift.
posts.key_insight: AI has turned Silicon Valley’s once-cyclical, legible boom-bust dynamics into a confusing, destabilizing moment where even veterans doubt the rules of the game.

topQuotes.quote: In Silicon Valley, there are two sets of physics.
The puck is teleporting.
OpenAI events are the reaping.

posts.title: Gurman’s Epic Pre-WWDC Leak Report
posts.summary: Gruber marvels at Mark Gurman's extraordinarily comprehensive pre-WWDC 2024 leak report at Bloomberg, which reads as though sourced from someone who already watched the keynote. He emphasizes that the leak is not deliberate and that Apple insiders are furious about it. Gruber then digs into the specifics, raising pointed questions about Apple's rumored OpenAI-powered chatbot — where it would live, how on-device processing would work across devices, and what distinguishes it from OpenAI's own ChatGPT apps. He also comments on AI-generated emoji, Messages improvements, and corrects Gurman's framing of Safari ad-blocking as a privacy headache, arguing it would actually be an antitrust headache.
posts.key_insight: Gurman's unprecedented pre-WWDC leak dump reveals nearly the entire keynote, but the most interesting unanswered questions are about the practical UX and architectural challenges of Apple's on-device AI strategy.

topQuotes.quote: Their new Mac app is quite sweet — written in AppKit and SwiftUI, not some turd of a web wrapper like most such AI chatbots.
Built-in ad blocking in Safari wouldn't be a privacy headache; blocking ads can only increase privacy. It would be an antitrust/regulatory headache.
Don't think for a second it's a deliberate leak. Folks inside Apple are, I assure you, furious about this, and incredulous that one of their own colleagues would leak it to Gurman.

posts.title: Introducing Omakub
posts.summary: DHH introduces Omakub, an opinionated, one-command setup that transforms a fresh Ubuntu installation into a fully-configured web development environment. He argues that Linux's power comes with a paradox of choice and a steep learning curve that deters newcomers. Omakub applies the 'omakase' philosophy—strong, curated defaults chosen by someone with taste—to lower the barrier for developers switching from Windows or Mac. The project targets future Linux converts who would trade bespoke customization for a cohesive, ready-made experience.
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier to Linux adoption isn't capability but curation—developers need opinionated, cohesive defaults more than infinite customization options.

topQuotes.quote: It's almost like there's a rite of passage in certain parts of the community where becoming an expert in the intricacies of every tool and its theming is required to prove you're a proper nerd. I think that's a bit silly.
Nothing in Omakub provides solutions to problems you couldn't also solve a million other ways.
There's a large constituency of developers who are more than willing to trade ultimate bespoke customization for a cohesive package of goods.

posts.title: Your Node is Leaking Memory? setTimeout Could be the Reason
posts.summary: The post argues that Node’s `setTimeout` can accidentally cause memory leaks because it returns a `Timeout` object, not a primitive, and that object retains internal state even after completion or cancellation. The risk grows with `AsyncLocalStorage`, which attaches hidden state to timeouts and persists unless the timeout reference is dropped. Converting the timeout to a primitive number would avoid holding references, but a current Node bug makes this approach unsafe and can cause unrecoverable leaks. As a workaround, the author suggests aggressively nulling timeout references and, in extreme cases, monkey‑patching timers. He concludes that Node should avoid exposing the internal `Timeout` object and that the ecosystem needs better ways to prevent async‑local context from leaking into long‑lived resources.
posts.key_insight: Node’s timer API leaks internal state, and with AsyncLocalStorage that hidden context can quietly retain large heaps unless timeout references are aggressively cleared.

topQuotes.quote: Except it doesn't (today).
You can very easily create memory leaks with the `setTimeout` API in node.
This means that the more async local storage you use, the more “stuff” you hold on if you don't clear our the timeouts.

posts.title: Test-Driving HTML Templates
posts.summary: The article argues that server-side HTML templates contain logic that deserves automated testing, not just manual browser inspection. It demonstrates a progression of testing techniques: first validating HTML soundness with parsers, then using CSS selectors to verify specific elements and content, then parameterizing tests for easy extension, and finally using Playwright with headless browsers to test JavaScript-driven behavior like HTMX interactions. The author shows that testing templates catches real errors and improves code quality, including forcing better accessibility practices.
posts.key_insight: HTML templates contain testable logic that benefits from the same TDD practices we apply to other code, using CSS selectors to focus assertions on what matters while ignoring irrelevant markup details.

topQuotes.quote: Relying on manual tests only is risky; what if we make a change that breaks a template, and we don't test it because we did not think it would impact the template?
We made the test code a little bit more complicated, so that the test cases are clearer: this is a very good tradeoff!
Testing HTML the same way users interact with it led us to use ARIA roles, which led to improving accessibility of our generated HTML.

posts.title: Open Source Deterministic Hypervisors Are Coming
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that deterministic simulation testing (DST) is gaining momentum, with Antithesis's hypervisor-based approach revolutionizing the field by making any containerized code deterministically testable without modification. However, Antithesis faces adoption challenges due to high costs (~$200k) and integration complexity. Riccomini predicts open source alternatives will inevitably emerge, citing projects like Meta's Hermit and QEMU-based efforts. He advocates that Antithesis should open source their hypervisor since most of their value lies in surrounding tooling, and community development could accelerate integration improvements. The post concludes optimistically with Antithesis confirming they plan to eventually open source their hypervisor.
posts.key_insight: Antithesis should open source their deterministic hypervisor because the real value lies in their surrounding tooling, and community development would accelerate the integration improvements needed for broader adoption.

topQuotes.quote: Most of Antithesis's value isn't in their hypervisor anyway.
Building a non-deterministic hypervisor is just too juicy of a problem. There will be open source implementations.
There's no doubt in my mind that their product is worth well north of $200,000 for a large company selling infrastructure to hundreds of customers. For early stage startups, it's a tough sell.

posts.title: Now Qualcomm Went Long
posts.summary: Gruber critiques Qualcomm's new ad featuring Justin Long, the actor from Apple's classic 'Get a Mac' campaign, calling it even worse than Intel's similar attempt three years prior. The ad depicts Long frustrated with his MacBook, but the supposed technical problems aren't actually real MacOS issues. Gruber contrasts these failed efforts with the original campaign's genius, arguing its success hinged on making John Hodgman's PC character lovable rather than loathsome. He notes that Apple wisely brought back Hodgman — not Long — for the Apple Silicon announcement in 2020. Gruber suggests Long has damaged his spokesperson value through serial brand betrayals, jumping from Apple to Intel to Qualcomm. The piece serves as both an ad critique and a meditation on what made the original campaign culturally enduring.
posts.key_insight: The original 'Get a Mac' campaign endures because its creative genius was in making the rival character lovable — and competitors who hire Justin Long to mock Apple only reveal they don't understand why it worked.

topQuotes.quote: What's next for Long, a spot in a Huawei commercial slagging on the Qualcomm modems in iPhones?
It's not like there's a joke here that falls flat. There is no joke, nor even an attempt at one. It's just 'Hey look, we hired the I'm a Mac guy.'
These supposed technical problems aren't actually problems on MacOS, and switching to a Snapdragon-powered Windows PC isn't going to stop his mom from emailing him.

posts.title: Why I retired from the tech crusades
posts.summary: DHH reflects on his evolution from an aggressive tech evangelist to a more accepting stance on programming language diversity. He recounts how Rails' success came not from pointed arguments against competitors, but from the famous 15-minute demo video that simply showed what was possible. He argues that you can't convert people through dunking or debate — you can only attract those already dissatisfied with their current tools. He concludes that programmers come in different intellectual shapes, and finding the language that clicks with your mind is more important than any objective trade-off analysis.
posts.key_insight: Technology adoption is driven not by winning arguments against alternatives, but by demonstrating something compelling and letting dissatisfied programmers discover it for themselves.

topQuotes.quote: I've never seen someone who was head'over'heels in love with, say, functional programming be won over by arguments for objected-oriented programming.
I would give up all the fancy cars I have in a heartbeat, if I was made to choose between them and programming.
You simply can't dunk someone into submission, and it's usually counterproductive if you try.

posts.title: A discussion of discussions on AI bias
posts.summary: Luu examines how discussions about AI/ML bias reveal a double standard compared to 'classical' software bugs, where people frequently deny that outputs opposite to user intent (like turning Asian faces white in professional photos) constitute bugs at all. He argues that encoding biases into automated systems isn't new to ML—it's been happening throughout computing history—but ML has made these biases more legible to lay people. The fundamental problem is that consumer markets reward velocity over quality, making it structurally difficult to address bias even when technical solutions might exist.
posts.key_insight: AI bias isn't a new problem but rather the latest manifestation of how automated systems have always encoded societal biases—the real challenge is that market incentives systematically favor shipping fast over fixing these issues, making proposed solutions based on 'caring more' or 'diverse hiring' structurally doomed to fail without addressing the underlying economic forces.

topQuotes.quote: AI apologists insist this is fine when it comes to things like changing someone's race or ethnicity...it would be absurd to argue that it's fine for my tire change appointment to have been converted to an oil change appointment because other companies have schedulers that convert oil change appointments to tire change appointments

Companies have overwhelmingly chosen velocity over quality...consumers overwhelmingly choose the lower cost or more featureful option over the higher quality option. Despite vehicular accidents being one of the leading causes of death for people under 50, paying for safety is such a low priority for consumers that Volvo has become a niche brand

We've encoded biases into automation for as long as we've had automation and the increased scope and scale of automation has been and will continue to increase the scope and scale of automated bias. It's just that now, many uses of ML make these kinds of biases a lot more legible to lay people

posts.title: History Matters
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that the tech and data industry has a troubling tendency to forget its pioneers and foundational history, unlike other professions that revere their contributors. Understanding the historical evolution of technologies—from perceptrons to transformers—provides crucial context for comprehending current innovations like generative AI.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the historical lineage of technologies—from foundational concepts to modern innovations—gives practitioners deeper insight into their field and prevents the industry from repeating past mistakes.

topQuotes.quote: Unlike other professions that revere their contributors (think medicine - Hippocrates, Pasteur, etc.), tech and data throw out the legends as soon as it's convenient.
History is important. It helps set the context of the why and what of your profession.
If you understand the building blocks of machine learning, you'll have a much easier time understanding generative AI. It didn't spring out of nowhere.

posts.title: The Rise Of The Analytics Pretendgineer
posts.summary: Stancil argues that dbt made data modeling easy but not simple, creating a generation of “analytics pretendgineers” who can write models but lack the architectural discipline to build durable data programs. He contends that the fix isn’t more features or education, but a framework with strong, opinionated guardrails—“dbt on Rails”—that enforces structure and prevents chaotic model sprawl.
posts.key_insight: dbt lowered the barrier to building data models but, without enforced structure, it also lowered the quality of data-program design, so the real fix is opinionated frameworks rather than more tooling or training.

topQuotes.quote: In other words, dbt’s explosive popularity turned a generation of data analysts into analytics pretendgineers.
dbt changed all this—mostly for the better, but a bit for the worse.
Behaviors are shaped by our environment and what that environment lets us do, not the other way around.

posts.title: Google Cloud Breaks UniSuper and Is Kind of Sorry
posts.summary: Riccomini critiques Google Cloud's handling of a major outage that deleted UniSuper's private cloud for over a week due to a misconfigured parameter. He argues the incident reveals three systemic problems: Google's fundamental misunderstanding of enterprise customers, their superficial incident remediation that only addressed the proximate cause, and growing brand damage as Google Cloud gains a reputation for instability. Despite being a self-professed Google Cloud fan, he finds their incident response tone-deaf—notably lacking any apology while defensively claiming to be 'the most resilient cloud infrastructure in the world.'
posts.key_insight: Google Cloud's dismissive incident response and superficial remediation reveal a company that still treats enterprise customers like consumer software users, failing to understand that trust—not just technical capability—is what enterprises are buying.

topQuotes.quote: If Google won't use their cloud service, what business do you have selling it to others?
Nowhere does Google's post even apologize. In fact, the only apology I can find is on UniSuper's website.
You don't want to A/B test user interfaces in an organization when they're in the middle of a production outage.

posts.title: Data Fetching Patterns in Single-Page Applications
posts.summary: This article examines data fetching patterns in single-page applications, addressing the challenge of maintaining responsive UIs while handling asynchronous operations. Fowler's co-author Juntao Qiu presents five key patterns: Asynchronous State Handler (decoupling fetch logic from UI), Parallel Data Fetching (avoiding request waterfalls), Fallback Markup (declarative loading states with Suspense), Code Splitting (lazy loading non-critical components), and Prefetching (loading data before it's needed). The piece uses a Profile component example throughout, progressively adding complexity to demonstrate each pattern. While React is used for examples, the patterns apply broadly across frontend frameworks.
posts.key_insight: Effective data fetching in SPAs requires combining multiple patterns—parallel fetching, code splitting, and prefetching—to eliminate request waterfalls while keeping the codebase maintainable through proper separation of concerns.

topQuotes.quote: The era of blank pages taking 5 seconds to load is long gone.
Don't implement complex patterns like prefetching until they are clearly needed.

Data fetching is challenging, not only because the nature of async programming doesn't fit our linear mindset, and there are so many factors that can cause a network call to fail, but also there are too many not-obvious cases to consider under the hood.

posts.title: So You Want To Do Your Own Thing?
posts.summary: Joe Reis argues that entrepreneurship isn't for everyone and offers pragmatic advice for those considering leaving corporate jobs to work independently. He emphasizes avoiding reckless risks, testing ideas with paying customers before quitting, mastering sales, and building genuine networks—while acknowledging that the discomfort and uncertainty of self-employment causes most people to eventually return to traditional employment.
posts.key_insight: Before romanticizing entrepreneurship, honestly assess whether you can handle the brutal uncertainty—and if you proceed, validate with paying customers while still employed rather than recklessly 'burning the boats.'

topQuotes.quote: Do you want to look back on your career thinking, 'I was thrilled I spent my time dealing with total assholes at work, just to make sure the data warehouse ran so I can make a bunch of reports and dashboards nobody looks at'?
Entrepreneurship isn't for everyone. I'll probably piss off some hustle bros when I say that most people shouldn't bother.
Working for yourself requires such a high level of discomfort and uncertainty that most people will crack under the pressure.

posts.title: Everything Is Still BI
posts.summary: Stancil argues that data products marketed as novel or specialized almost inevitably evolve into BI tools as customer demands, market incentives, and growth pressures pull them toward dashboards, self-serve access, and visualization. The real challenge isn’t clever differentiation but the stubbornness required to resist the gravitational pull of BI, even when it looks like the rational business choice.
posts.key_insight: Differentiation in data tools collapses into BI unless founders stubbornly resist market pressure rather than merely crafting a clever pitch.

topQuotes.quote: It is not having a vision that gets you labeled as a visionary; it’s refusing to let it go.
The biggest lesson, though, is that it's actually very easy to think different, but it’s very hard to stay different.

In other words, no matter when a data product was created or what its first features were, it will inevitably collapse into the same singularity: Database connectors; a code-based query editor; a code-free query builder; lots of charts; dashboards; self-serve.

posts.title: Linux as the new developer default at 37signals
posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals is shifting its default developer platform from Mac to Linux after over twenty years of Apple monoculture. He frames this as aligning the company's tools with its open-source values while gaining practical benefits like better understanding Windows-using customers. The change is a default, not a mandate — mobile developers still need Macs, and personal preferences are respected. DHH emphasizes supporting companies like Framework that represent the independent future he wants to see, while acknowledging Apple still makes great hardware.
posts.key_insight: A company's default tools should reflect its values, and for an open-source-driven shop increasingly at odds with Apple's closed ecosystem, Linux is the natural realignment.

topQuotes.quote: Apple has evolved into a company that's much harder to recommend for people who care about the future of computing, but they still make great hardware.
The year of Linux on the desktop. Who would have thought!
I love voting with my dollars for more of a future I'd like to see, and Framework represents just that.

posts.title: Writing a Unix clone in about a month
posts.summary: Drew DeVault describes Bunnix, a Unix-like operating system for x86_64 he built in roughly 27 days as a recreational programming project. The kernel is mostly written in Hare with C components for ext4 and terminal support, and includes PCI, AHCI, PS/2, and framebuffer drivers along with about 40 syscalls. The userspace assembles third-party software including dash, Vim 5.7, tcc, and DOOM atop a musl-derived libc. DeVault reflects on the challenges of filesystem design, terminal integration, scheduler implementation, and the ugliness of Unix signals. He concludes that monolithic kernels are far simpler to build than microkernels, and plans to bring lessons learned back to his microkernel project Helios.
posts.key_insight: Building a functional Unix clone in a month demonstrates both the surprising simplicity of monolithic kernel design and Hare's viability as a kernel programming language, while reinforcing that Unix's worst warts — especially signals — are just as ugly from the implementor's side.

topQuotes.quote: I also finally learned how signals work from top to bottom, and boy is it ugly.
I'm quite sure that putting together Bunnix in just 30 days is a feat which would not have been possible with a microkernel design.
At the end of the day, monolithic kernels are just much simpler to implement.

posts.title: Spotify’s Car Things to Be Rebranded as Car Bricks
posts.summary: Gruber uses Spotify's decision to brick all Car Thing devices — its proprietary hardware that only supported Spotify — as a lens to examine the hypocrisy in Spotify's anti-App Store crusade. He argues that Spotify built a closed hardware platform with zero third-party support while simultaneously lobbying the EU to force Apple and Google to give Spotify commission-free access to their platforms. He traces the history of the App Store's commission structure back to 2008, when iPhone had just 1.1% market share, demonstrating that Apple's fees predated its dominance rather than being imposed after achieving it. He concludes that Spotify's position is internally contradictory: it's fine for Spotify to create a completely closed platform, but not fine for Apple to charge commissions on an open one.
posts.key_insight: Spotify's willingness to build and then brick a completely closed hardware platform while demanding commission-free access to Apple's open one reveals the self-serving inconsistency at the heart of its antitrust arguments.

topQuotes.quote: No word in Spotify's Car Thing bricking FAQ about when they're dropping support for Apple Music, Amazon Music, and YouTube Music. Oh, that's right, they never supported any music services other than their own.
What do you think Spotify planned to do if Car Thing became a hit product? Do you think they planned to open it up to competing streaming services after it became popular? I doubt it.
And if you think they not only would have opened Car Thing up to competing services, but would have done so without charging significant commissions or fees, I have a bridge to sell you.

posts.title: S3 Is Showing Its Age
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that S3, despite being an engineering marvel, is falling behind competitors due to missing critical features like compare-and-swap operations, multi-region buckets, and object appends. These gaps are becoming problematic as new infrastructure increasingly uses object storage as their primary persistence layer. S3 Express One Zone has proven disappointing with its directory bucket type, missing standard S3 features, and high storage costs. Developers face a choice: abandon S3 for competitors like GCS that have these features, or build workarounds using additional services like DynamoDB. The post concludes that understanding S3 as an object store rather than a file system, and embracing a separate metadata layer, may be the path forward.
posts.key_insight: S3's missing features like compare-and-swap operations are forcing developers to either abandon AWS's object store for competitors or architect around its limitations with additional metadata layers.

topQuotes.quote: S3E1Z looks more like an expensive EBS with a half-implemented S3 API.
I'm squarely in the trough of disillusionment with S3.
S3 is the only object store that doesn't support preconditions.

posts.title: Using Footnotes
posts.summary: Fowler explains his evolving approach to footnotes in writing. He initially avoided them, believing important content belonged in the main text. While writing about Obama's 2008 campaign technology work, he discovered footnotes helped preserve narrative flow while retaining important details. He recently updated his site to show footnotes inline when clicked rather than jumping to the page bottom. He deliberately rejected sidenotes because they're too easy to read inadvertently, breaking reader flow.
posts.key_insight: Good footnote design requires intentional friction—readers should consciously choose to pursue tangential details rather than having them interrupt the main narrative flow.

topQuotes.quote: My general view was that if it was important enough to worth writing about, it should make its way into the main text, otherwise it should be discarded.
I like the need to click on the reference, because then it's easier for the reader to refrain from the effort to do it.
The trouble with this rendering is that it's too easy for the reader's eye to glance over and read the sidenote's text.

posts.title: Beautiful motivations
posts.summary: DHH argues that programmers wrongly dismiss aesthetics by associating it with superficial veneering. He contends that beauty is not just about surface appearance but serves as a powerful motivational force that drives great work. The pursuit of beauty requires extra care and effort that elevates the entire creation process. He calls on programmers to rediscover beauty in code, patterns, and tools—not as a monoculture of taste, but as a practice of making incremental improvements that fuel personal motivation and mission.
posts.key_insight: Beauty in software is not superficial decoration but a powerful motivational force that drives programmers to produce their best work through accumulated care in a thousand small decisions.

topQuotes.quote: Programmers are often skeptical of aesthetics because they frequently associate it with veneering.
We've managed to deconstruct and problematize so much of what is self-evidently beautiful that it's harder to take the chase for granted.
Discarding the value of aesthetic on behalf of cheap imitations is a mistake.

posts.title: The Design Flaw At The Heart Of Every
posts.summary: The post argues that most data products are designed around clean, demo-friendly narratives, but real businesses face messy data, noisy dashboards, and contradictory signals. Because products are built for the “happy path,” they fail users in the “bad place” where interpretation and deduction—not discovery—are the real challenges.
posts.key_insight: Data products often optimize for clean demo narratives, but real-world data is messy and ambiguous, so tools should prioritize helping teams interpret and reconcile conflicting signals rather than just surface more insights.

topQuotes.quote: The dashboard is a horoscope, capable of telling whatever story its reader is looking for.
Most of us are Red Eye Entertainment, plagued by messy data, erratic metrics, and graphs with too many series.
We need help with deduction, not discovery.

posts.title: Paranoia and desperation in the AI gold rush
posts.summary: DHH criticizes the AI gold rush mentality driving companies to make desperate, privacy-violating decisions. He uses Salesforce's announcement that Slack will train AI models on users' private messages by default as a prime example. He argues companies are acting out of fear of missing out rather than genuine excitement about AI's potential. The opt-out process—sending a specially formatted email to a feedback address—reveals how little these companies respect user consent. DHH predicts this pattern of trust and privacy violations will accelerate as companies scramble for any competitive edge in the AI race.
posts.key_insight: The AI gold rush is producing a corporate paranoia so intense that companies like Salesforce will sacrifice user privacy and trust for even marginal competitive advantage in the race to build AI products.

topQuotes.quote: If you can't beat the likes of OpenAI in scanning the sum of public human knowledge, maybe you can beat them by scanning all the confidential conversations about new product strategies, lay-off plans that haven't been announced yet, or private financial projections for 2025?
All you have to do is edit out, say, Asana in your layoff completion, and voila, you'll have saved at least 8 minutes typing out the corporate slop yourself.
I've never seen so much paranoia in technology about missing out on The Next Big Thing as with AI.

posts.title: System tests have failed
posts.summary: DHH declares that system tests, which he helped introduce as a default in Rails 5.1 in 2016, have not lived up to their promise. Despite the theoretical advantages of driving a headless browser through the actual interface, he finds them slow, brittle, and plagued by false negatives. He argues that model and controller tests handle business logic better, while UI testing is best done by humans clicking around in a real browser. HEY is cutting its 300+ system tests down significantly, acknowledging the sunk cost fallacy that kept them running too long.
posts.key_insight: System tests have failed to deliver on their promise of reliable confidence, and most should be replaced by focused unit tests and human exploratory testing.

topQuotes.quote: I've wasted far more time getting system tests to work reliably than I have seen dividends from bugs caught.
The method that gives me the most confidence that my UI logic is good to go is not system tests, but human tests. Literally clicking around in a real browser by hand.
System tests remain as slow, brittle, and full of false negatives as they did a decade ago.

posts.title: Using Rust Macros for Custom VTables
posts.summary: Ronacher explains why MiniJinja’s dynamic runtime values need reference counting and dynamic dispatch that Rust’s object safety rules block. He shows that an `Object` trait with `&Arc<Self>` receivers enables refcount-aware behavior but prevents building a standard trait object. To solve this, he constructs a custom fat pointer and vtable via a `type_erase!` macro that generates trampolines, refcount hooks, and downcasting support. The post walks through the unsafe machinery and the macro that produces it, then integrates the result into MiniJinja’s `Value` representation. He concludes that Rust’s restrictions can be worked around with creativity and unsafe code, and argues that better compile-time introspection would make such patterns far less contorted.
posts.key_insight: When Rust’s object safety rules block dynamic patterns, you can recover the needed behavior with a custom vtable and macro-generated unsafe glue—though it exposes how much the language lacks ergonomic compile-time introspection.

topQuotes.quote: Just because Rust tells you that you cannot make something into an object does not mean that you actually can't.
Zig programmers will laugh / cry about this since comptime is a much more powerful system to make something like this work compared to the ridiculous macro abuse necessary in Rust.
Given that building programming languages and interpreters is the developer's most favorite hobby, I will never stop writing templating engines.

posts.title: Open source is neither a community nor a democracy
posts.summary: DHH argues that open source projects are not democracies and using open source software doesn't entitle users to influence project direction. He contends that the word 'community' misleadingly implies egalitarian participation, when in reality projects are run by small groups of elite contributors who do the actual work. He defends this hierarchy as morally correct — productive effort should be the basis of power. He suggests 'ecosystem' as a better metaphor than 'community,' and frames open source as a gift exchange rather than an entitlement program.
posts.key_insight: Open source governance is rightfully meritocratic, not democratic — the people who do the work earn the authority, and the gift of free software is the only entitlement users receive.

topQuotes.quote: Using open source software does not entitle you to a vote on the direction of the project.
Elitism is good, when it comes to open source. You absolutely want projects to be driven by the people who show up to do the work, demonstrate their superior dedication and competence.
Open source is first and foremost a method of collaboration between programmers who show up to do the work. Not an entitlement program for petulant users.

posts.title: pandas and Beyond with Wes McKinney
posts.summary: Wes McKinney frames his career around building long-lived, sustainable data infrastructure, from pandas to Arrow and Ibis, with a focus on composability across languages and systems. He argues that open source success depends as much on social coordination and governance as on technical design, and that creating space for new maintainers is essential for project longevity. He highlights Apache Arrow as a standardized, language-independent in-memory format that enables zero-copy interoperability and faster, reusable algorithms across ecosystems. He sees WebAssembly and tools like DuckDB as transformative for running high-performance analytics in the browser without servers. He positions Ibis and related tools as enabling a multi-engine data stack where developers can move between local and scalable backends without rewriting code. The conclusion is that the ecosystem has made huge progress but is still early, with significant opportunity to keep simplifying and accelerating data work.
posts.key_insight: The long-term impact in data science comes from building interoperable infrastructure and sustainable open source communities that make powerful capabilities simple and portable.

topQuotes.quote: It turns out that, as is true with many open source software problems, the social problems are harder than the technical problems.
If you were building pandas now, you could build a pandas-like library based on Arrow components in much less time.
The mantra in pandas was: how do we make things one line of code? One line of code—it must be easy.

posts.title: Follow-Up on Apple No Longer Including Stickers With New Products
posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his initial negative reaction to Apple removing logo stickers from product boxes. After reader pushback, he acknowledges the stronger argument: the stickers are plastic, while Apple has systematically eliminated plastic from its packaging. He changes his mind, agreeing that making stickers available at retail stores is a good compromise. He also shares poll results from three platforms showing roughly 69% approval of the change.
posts.key_insight: The real case against Apple's in-box stickers isn't volume or weight but that they're plastic — an anomaly in Apple's otherwise all-paper packaging — and Gruber is willing to change his mind when presented with the better argument.

topQuotes.quote: The better argument against the stickers is that they're plastic.
If those stickers are 0.1mm thick, a stack of 1 billion of them would be 100km high. But that's still just one sheet amongst many others that Apple includes in every box.
So consider my mind changed: eliminating the stickers from the box, but making them available to those who want them at Apple retail stores, is a good compromise.

posts.title: Meta is shutting down Workplace
posts.summary: DHH argues that the conventional wisdom of buying from big tech companies for safety is outdated and wrong. Meta's decision to shut down Workplace, despite millions of paying users, demonstrates that big tech treats non-core products as disposable. Companies like Spotify and McDonalds now face forced migration because Meta's ad business dwarfs Workplace revenue. DHH contends that smaller vendors who depend on your business are actually the safer bet.
posts.key_insight: Big tech's enormous core revenue makes every secondary product disposable, making smaller vendors who depend on your business the actually safer long-term bet.

topQuotes.quote: Any company that hitched their wagon to Workplace just got served with an eviction notice.
Simply because Meta can't be bothered to maintain a platform that's merely used by millions when their consumer business is used by billions.
Buying from big isn't the sure bet they want you to believe. Buy from someone who actually needs your business to make the wheels go round.

posts.title: The endangered state of normality
posts.summary: DHH argues that the definition of 'normal' has narrowed dramatically since the 1980s and 90s, when kids with various quirks and differences were simply considered part of the normal spectrum. He contends that society now incentivizes identifying with marginalized categories, making it socially desirable to acquire diagnostic labels rather than be seen as ordinary. Referencing Abigail Shrier's 'Bad Therapy,' he points to a therapy industry eager to pathologize normal childhood behaviors. He calls for re-expanding the definition of normal to encompass a broader range of human variation, with fewer labels and fewer medical interventions.
posts.key_insight: Society has narrowed the definition of normal to the point where ordinary human variation is pathologized, and we should reverse course by expanding what counts as normal rather than labeling every difference.

topQuotes.quote: There's cachet of cool to be had in identifying with some margin. Preferably one that can claim to be oppressed by society via heteronormativity or neurotypicality or other big words for 'normal'.
This is the natural consequence of 'centering the margins'. Of making it socially desirable to be not normal and low status to be regular.
There's a large industry of therapists and other 'mental health professionals' eager to accommodate this flight to the margins.

posts.title: The M4 iPad Pros
posts.summary: Gruber challenges the longstanding consensus that iPad Pros are great hardware hamstrung by limited software, proposing an inverted perspective: if iPadOS is accepted as a deliberately simple, appliance-style platform beloved by millions, then the iPad Pro's purpose is to make that experience as premium as possible rather than to enable desktop-class workflows. He argues that 'pro' in iPad Pro means 'deluxe' rather than 'professional,' with Apple optimizing for thinness, lightness, and display quality over raw computational need. The M4 chip's extraordinary performance serves power efficiency and the tandem OLED display rather than demanding workloads. He draws analogies to Lexus vs. Toyota and first-class vs. coach, arguing premium hardware for a simple platform is no more absurd than premium experiences in any other domain.
posts.key_insight: The iPad Pro's purpose is not to be a powerful workstation constrained by weak software, but to be the most premium possible expression of a deliberately simple computing platform — and optimizing for niceness over raw capability is a legitimate product philosophy.

topQuotes.quote: Arguing that 'no one can do real work on an iPad' reminds me of Yogi Berra's 'No one goes there anymore, it's too crowded.'
Twice as fast for the same energy consumption is the Mac way of thinking. Same performance with half the energy consumption is the iOS way of thinking.
A better Mac is faster. Nicer too, but primarily faster. A better iPad is nicer. Faster, too, but primarily nicer.

posts.title: Nimble and Lance: The Parquet Killers
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini examines two new columnar file formats—Nimble from Meta and Lance V2 from LanceDB—that aim to address Apache Parquet's limitations for AI/ML workloads. Parquet struggles with point lookups, large values, wide schemas, and limited encodings that make it poorly suited for machine learning data with long float arrays and multimedia content. Nimble takes an incremental approach with extensible encodings and FlatBuffers for metadata, while LV2 radically eliminates row groups entirely in favor of a minimal ~200 line spec with pluggable type systems. Both formats prioritize extensibility, though this risks fragmentation across implementations. The author concludes that while neither format is production-ready for general OLAP queries, LV2's integration with Arrow and thoughtful design make it particularly compelling for the future.
posts.key_insight: While Parquet's limitations for AI/ML workloads have spawned promising new formats in Nimble and Lance V2, their embrace of extensibility trades flexibility for fragmentation risk—though LV2's implicit reliance on Arrow as the de facto standard offers a pragmatic path forward.

topQuotes.quote: They're dreaming. It's not written in Rust, so that's going to happen. There will be others, too. Engineers just can't resist reimplementing specs in their native languages.
Engineers love to make things extensible. It's an escape hatch for bad decisions, the promise of flexibility, and an openness to new ideas. But extensibility causes fragmentation.
And we're just talking about encodings. LV2's choice to ditch both encodings and type systems really freaked me out.

posts.title: How GitHub taught me to Micromanage
posts.summary: Matt Rocklin reflects on how his experience with detailed code review in open source communities shaped his approach to feedback, and why that approach failed when he became a startup CEO. In open source, thorough feedback on even trivial code was welcomed because it invested in the author's growth within a volunteer community of craftspeople who assumed mutual respect. In corporate settings, the same detailed feedback was received as micromanagement and criticism because the context and implicit messages differ fundamentally. Rocklin concludes that people can only absorb about one piece of feedback per interaction in corporate contexts, requiring feedback to be more selective, higher-level, and carefully framed.
posts.key_insight: Feedback that signals investment and respect in one context can signal criticism and hostility in another, making context awareness essential for effective communication.

topQuotes.quote: What before was a supportive behavior is now an abusive one. Everything depends on context.
Writing code is a vanishingly small fraction of software development.
Critique without constructive direction is just complaining, and adds constraint to the author without also helping with momentum.

posts.title: Programming Language Wars are Dumb
posts.summary: Programming language debates are a waste of time because they focus on irrelevant technical minutiae rather than what actually matters: whether users are happy with what you build and whether your business succeeds. The choice of programming language is almost never a factor in commercial success, and end-users don't care about your tech stack.
posts.key_insight: Stop debating programming languages and start focusing on whether your product actually solves problems for users - that's the only metric that matters.

topQuotes.quote: Cool if you're trying to win brownie points with other nerds. Debate about your tech stack all day long. The rest of the world doesn't care.
You need to upskill your business sense if you're trying to differentiate your product or service based on the underlying programming language or tech stack.
The choice of programming language is almost always irrelevant to an organization's commercial success.

posts.title: Attn Data
posts.summary: Stancil argues that traditional data analysis and user interviews miss a powerful third approach: attentive observation that captures patterns we didn’t think to ask about. He suggests transformer-style models applied directly to behavioral event sequences could scale this “watching and remembering,” potentially delivering the core value of analysis without the brittle machinery of metrics and dashboards.
posts.key_insight: Transformer models trained on behavioral event streams could bypass brittle metrics and deliver the true goal of analysis: uncovering patterns we didn’t know to look for.

topQuotes.quote: You don't need to know how to define dancing to know when something is a banger.
It’s analysis by anecdote, math by memory, and inference by vibe.
The numbers may have been the problem all along.

posts.title: DEI is done (minus the mop up)
posts.summary: DHH declares that DEI (Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion) as a corporate movement is finished, citing declining mentions in earnings reports as evidence. He frames the DEI era as a period of 'temporary insanity' driven by the premise that racial disparities could only be explained by systemic racism. He argues this led to a culture of intimidation where dissenters were punished, and characterizes the movement's decline as a welcome correction. He calls for vigilance against similar movements in the future.
posts.key_insight: DHH declares victory over corporate DEI, framing its decline in earnings reports as vindication that it was always ideological sanctimony rather than genuine progress.

topQuotes.quote: Anyone who dared question the theology of high priests of this new religion, like DiAngelo or Kendi, were hounded and frequently banished by ideological thugs.
DEI, as an obsessive, ideological preoccupation of the corporate world, is done.
An explosion of sanctimony, triggered by the fallacy that racial disparities in the office (and elsewhere) could only be explained by systemic racism.

posts.title: Dangerous protocols
posts.summary: Asparouhova traces her journey from exploring crypto and open source governance to a broader investigation of protocols as systems of control. She recounts an early, unanswered question about decentralized file hosting governance that revealed the tension between protocol liberation and accountability. She argues that protocolization has expanded far beyond technology to shape how we think, act, and prioritize attention, drawing a parallel between 1930s bureaucracy simplifying Industrial Age information and today's crisis mindset simplifying the Digital Age's opinion boom. She frames LLMs as the natural output of this compression impulse. The post introduces her essay on 'dangerous protocols,' written for the Ethereum Foundation's Summer of Protocols research group, which examines how protocols usurp agency and how we might subvert them.
posts.key_insight: Protocolization has expanded from technical systems to cultural life itself, compressing human agency and creativity into predictable patterns — and recognizing this is the first step toward reclaiming autonomy.

topQuotes.quote: LLMs are the expected output of this era: an attempt to compress human creativity into something predictable.
It seemed that everything – how we thought, how we acted, who we dated, the opinions we held, even our intellectual pursuits – had become protocolized, and always with a tinge of urgency.
