Most people would rather have one identifiable adversary to grapple with, instead of a thousand faceless ones with middling levels of motivation: or worse, not know who their adversary is at all.

posts.title: Hating Apple goes mainstream
posts.summary: DHH argues that the backlash against Apple's iPad 'Crush' ad represents something far bigger than one bad commercial — it signals that Apple has exhausted its reservoir of public goodwill. He draws parallels to Microsoft's fall from grace in the 1990s, noting that Apple's fan-boy defenders have gone conspicuously quiet. The company's transition from beloved underdog to near-three-trillion-dollar incumbent battling regulators has left it vulnerable to exactly this kind of tipping point. DHH predicts Apple's era of easy street is over, though the company likely won't adapt until after several more embarrassments.
posts.key_insight: Apple's backlash over a single bad ad reveals that the company has quietly exhausted decades of accumulated goodwill, and like Microsoft before it, may not recognize its own tipping point until it's too late.

topQuotes.quote: The real story is just how little saved up goodwill Apple had in the bank to compensate for the outrage.
It's awfully quiet on the fan-boy front.
It symbolizes everything everyone has ever hated about digitization.

posts.title: Brief Thoughts and Observations on Yesterday’s ‘Let Loose’ iPad Keynote
posts.summary: Gruber reviews Apple's 'Let Loose' iPad event, covering the new iPad Air (now available in 13-inch), the remarkably thin M4 iPad Pros, the Apple Pencil Pro, and the overall iPad lineup simplification. He emphasizes the M4 iPad Pro's unprecedented thinness (thinner than an iPod Nano at 5.1mm) and explains how the M4 chip was necessary to drive the new tandem OLED displays — a prime example of Apple's vertical integration advantage. He notes Apple came close to fulfilling his October 2023 wishlist for iPad lineup clarity, with the main miss being Face ID still absent from iPad Air. The piece also addresses Pencil proliferation concerns, pricing across the lineup, and various details like chip-binning and the nano-texture display option.
posts.key_insight: The M4 iPad Pro exemplifies Apple's vertical integration advantage: the chip and display were co-designed so neither works without the other, producing a device thinner than an iPod Nano that no competitor can replicate.

topQuotes.quote: The mistake that prevents Apple from being able to offer just one Pencil has nothing to do with any of the Pencil models, but instead was the company's yearslong resistance to moving the front-facing camera on iPads from the short side to the long side.
Anyone playing a drinking game that required taking a shot for each mention of 'AI' or 'machine learning' during the keynote probably passed out before it ended.
It seems impossible that the new iPad Pros are thinner than an iPod Nano, but read the tech specs and weep.

posts.title: The last RailsConf
posts.summary: DHH reflects on the announcement that RailsConf 2025 will be the last RailsConf, framing it as a positive transition rather than a loss. He argues that with The Rails Foundation now organizing Rails World (which sold out in twenty minutes), Ruby Central can focus on its core Ruby mission while the Foundation handles Rails-specific advocacy. He reminisces about the early explosive growth of RailsConf from 400 attendees to 1,800, and the memorable keynotes he delivered. He briefly alludes to pandemic-era controversies but pivots to optimism about Rails' future as a counter to ZIRP-era complexity.
posts.key_insight: The end of RailsConf isn't a decline but an institutional maturation, with The Rails Foundation taking the reins at the precise moment Rails' simplicity philosophy is most needed in a post-ZIRP tech landscape.

topQuotes.quote: The world has woken up from the ZIRP years with a complexity hang-over, and Rails is the perfect painkiller.
We bootstrapped something from nothing, turned it into an epoch-defining event.
The ideal of the one-person framework has never been more relevant.

posts.title: In Paradise...🌴
posts.summary: This is a casual weekend newsletter where Joe Reis shares curated reads while working on writing projects from Marbella, Spain. The links cover AI developments, startup accelerator scandals, the difficult white-collar job market, LLM limitations, and the rise and fall of influencer-driven products.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry is experiencing a white-collar recession that's making job searches difficult even for senior executives, while the AI hype cycle continues to reveal both opportunities and limitations.

topQuotes.quote: Anecdotally, I've been hearing from very senior people (C-level, VP, director) that finding work, especially in tech, is next to impossible.
While LLMs are super impressive for many tasks, they fail at things like Sodoku and Celluar Automata.
This is a good read if you're interested in the power of influencers, and why hype alone is hard for products to sustain.

posts.title: The Saas Counterculture
posts.summary: Stancil argues that the SaaS delivery model incentivizes shipping incomplete products, with customers effectively doing QA, and that this pattern has bled into hardware via cloud-dependent AI devices. He suggests this creates customer fatigue and financial strain for SaaS firms, opening space for a countercultural model: software sold as finished, one-time-purchase products. He then pivots to a related puzzle—why software hasn’t become a luxury status symbol despite its craftsmanship and ubiquity.
posts.key_insight: The SaaS model’s incentives to ship unfinished software may be unsustainable, creating an opening for non‑SaaS products and even new forms of software as status goods.

topQuotes.quote: Vendors ship incomplete software, the threshold for what makes a bad release slips a bit further, and the whole thing spirals in a slow devolutionary loop, until people are blaming reviewers for not liking useless garbage, because of some open-ended promise that it might one day not be useless.
Because hope isn’t optimism. Hope is a thing with features.
Build something that you don’t have to keep building forever; sell the product based on what it is today and not based on what you can promise it’ll be in the future.

posts.title: Appendix: non-statements
posts.summary: Dan Luu analyzes leaked internal FTC memos from the 2011-2012 Google antitrust investigation, revealing how the Bureau of Economics (BE) made fundamental errors about the tech industry that contradicted the Bureau of Competition's (BC) case. BE staff argued mobile was unimportant, switching costs were trivial, and Google's anticompetitive practices had minimal impact—positions that were demonstrably wrong at the time and proven incorrect by subsequent events. The investigation was dropped despite BC making a defensible case, highlighting a systemic problem: tech policy decisions are made by people who lack basic understanding of the industry they're regulating.
posts.key_insight: Effective tech regulation requires practitioners with hands-on industry expertise who understand how these systems actually work, not just economists and lawyers applying abstract models that contradict observable market behavior and incentives.

topQuotes.quote: The BE memo argues that defaults are essentially worthless and have little to no impact, saying multiple times that users can switch with just 'a few taps'... Google paid Apple $26.3B to retain its default status in 2021.
When the authors of the BE memo make a prediction, they seem to have a facility for predicting the opposite of what will happen.
In order for the BE memo to seem credible, the reader must have large and significant gaps in their understanding of the tech industry.

posts.title: Software Friction
posts.summary: Hillel Wayne applies Clausewitz's military concept of 'friction' to software development, arguing that the gap between theoretical simplicity and practical difficulty is a fundamental, unavoidable aspect of building software. He catalogs diverse sources of friction—from API changes and bugs to sick kids and stolen laptops—and notes that friction compounds: two setbacks are more than twice as bad as one. The post explores how friction accumulates over time and scope, and how attempts to address it can create new friction. Wayne concludes with open questions about categorizing friction, learning from other fields, and whether individual awareness of friction provides value.
posts.key_insight: Software development friction—the accumulated gap between theoretical simplicity and practical difficulty—is inevitable, compounds non-linearly, and fighting it can create new friction.

topQuotes.quote: Friction compounds with itself: two setbacks are more than twice as bad as one setback.
Word changes every font to wingdings. (This is a real thing)
Friction is inevitable and impossible to fully remove. I don't think it's possible to even fully anticipate.

posts.title: Magic machines
posts.summary: DHH argues that programmers irrationally trust cloud and SaaS infrastructure over their own machines, a phenomenon he links to imposter syndrome. He uses the example of moving CI back to developer machines, where critics invoked 'it works on my machine' as if cloud computers were fundamentally different. He contends that all computers are equally fallible, testing is about confidence rather than certainty, and running tests on multiple developer machines is actually a feature that uncovers hidden dependencies. The post concludes that the perceived superiority of cloud operators is an illusion created by opaqueness, not actual competence.
posts.key_insight: The trust programmers place in cloud infrastructure over their own machines is an illusion born from imposter syndrome and the opaqueness of remote operations, not from any real difference in reliability.

topQuotes.quote: If only you knew what kind of frazzled mechanical turk it takes to run most cloud institutions or SaaS operations, you wouldn't be so quick to doubt your own abilities.
"It works on my computer" is just the midwit version of "it works on THAT computer". It's all just computers.
There are no magic machines and no magic operators. Just the same kind of potentially faulty bits and brains.

posts.title: Swift Adds Ownership (Kind Of)
posts.summary: The author reflects on Swift's memory management approach compared to Rust's borrow checker after a friend suggested Rust should have adopted Swift's ARC model. Investigation reveals that Apple published an 'Ownership Manifesto' seven years ago proposing Rust-like ownership for Swift, and this work has now materialized in Swift 5.9 with noncopyable types, consume operators, and borrowing/consuming parameter modifiers. The author expresses ambivalence about these changes, appreciating Swift's simpler ARC model while acknowledging that deep infrastructure work requires the performance optimizations that ownership semantics provide.
posts.key_insight: Swift's evolution toward Rust-like ownership semantics in version 5.9 suggests that even languages with simpler memory models eventually need more explicit control for performance-critical infrastructure work.

topQuotes.quote: Infrastructure developers could just get over themselves.
For current Rust users, if Rust kicks the bucket, Swift is a good plan B.
Some of ARC's edge cases happen to matter a lot when building deep infrastructure—the kind of stuff you build with Rust.

posts.title: Hiring Sales
posts.summary: Coiled is relaunching its enterprise sales effort after improving product-market fit, having previously fired a premature sales team when the product wasn't ready. The company now has a self-service product generating consistent inbound leads and small-team customers, but realizes that customer support accelerates adoption and larger opportunities exist behind initial teams. Rocklin is seeking an Account Executive who can think critically about their specific situation rather than offering generic sales platitudes. He expresses frustration that qualified AE candidates project false confidence without understanding Coiled's unique context, when what he actually wants is someone who can demonstrate adaptive problem-solving over time.
posts.key_insight: In early-stage startups, the best sales hires aren't those who project confidence with generic frameworks, but those who demonstrate intellectual curiosity and the ability to think through your specific situation.

topQuotes.quote: A couple years ago we had a sales team, but our product was bad and so the sales team was premature. We fired them (and a bunch of other people) and focused on making a delightful product.
My assumption is that any answer you have today is likely wrong, so if you have strong conviction on that answer I'll probably assume that you're mentally inflexible.
Our current sales numbers suck, mostly because we haven't done anything.

posts.title: We're moving continuous integration back to developer machines
posts.summary: DHH announces that 37signals is moving continuous integration from remote BuildKite runners back to local developer machines. Modern multi-core CPUs like the Intel 14900K and Apple M3 Max can run their full test suite in under half the time of their cloud CI setup. He argues this represents a broader pattern of using hardware improvements to simplify development stacks rather than adding capability. He acknowledges this won't work for massive codebases like Shopify or GitHub, but contends that 99.99% of web apps are small enough to benefit from this approach.
posts.key_insight: When hardware gets faster, the best use of that performance gain is eliminating infrastructure complexity rather than scaling up what you build on top of it.

topQuotes.quote: Never aspire to a more complicated stack than what your application calls for.
Progress is a bonfire.
Getting to flush all of it down the simplification drain is an amazing step forward.

posts.title: Getting Buy-in for Data Modeling
posts.summary: Reis argues that getting buy-in for data modeling requires articulating value in terms stakeholders understand, showing what's in it for them rather than speaking in technical jargon. He emphasizes that the current AI hype cycle presents a strategic opportunity to finally get organizational support for data housekeeping and modeling initiatives that have long been neglected.
posts.key_insight: The AI hype cycle is a rare window of opportunity to secure organizational buy-in for foundational data work like modeling—seize it before the bubble bursts.

topQuotes.quote: Data isn't top of mind for most people, even though organizations are supposedly 'data driven' these days.
Too often, people try to get buy-in from others from a place of selfishness and lack of empathy. This is almost always a mistake.
If there's ever a time to move on building your data and AI foundation, now is the time (especially before the hype cycle bursts).

posts.title: Bis Third Form
posts.summary: Stancil argues that BI’s pendulum swing between generic platforms and domain-specific apps misses the real problem: companies struggle more with interpreting metrics than defining them. He suggests a third form of BI that is opinionated about interpretation, offering frameworks and guidance on what the numbers mean and what to do next.
posts.key_insight: The next useful wave of BI should be opinionated about interpreting results, not just flexible about defining metrics.

topQuotes.quote: Maybe it’s time to try another approach. Stop giving us more exploratory interfaces to get lost in. Stop waiting for us to become “data literate.”
Amazon isn’t Amazon because it’s great at defining metrics. It’s Amazon because it’s great at interpreting them.
If I had to guess, all of this is the real reason why BI tools are a tar pit: They solve the wrong problem.

posts.title: I could have been happy with Windows
posts.summary: After twenty-plus years on Mac, DHH describes his journey away from Apple, first to Windows (which he found surprisingly capable thanks to WSL), then to Linux on a Framework laptop. He discovered that Windows is a legitimate alternative to Mac for web developers, but ultimately fell in love with running Ubuntu despite its rough edges. He found the tinkering and troubleshooting inherent to Linux nostalgic and genuinely enjoyable rather than frustrating, comparing it to the IKEA effect. He concludes that we now have three viable personal computing platforms and encourages adventurous users to try the switch.
posts.key_insight: Breaking free from Apple's ecosystem revealed that both Windows and Linux have matured into legitimate alternatives, and the hands-on tinkering required by Linux can be a source of joy rather than frustration.

topQuotes.quote: Computers required some assembly, and as IKEA knows, it made us love them more.
It's hard to know whether you'll find some zen of motorcycle maintenance in knowing how to tighten the timing chain of Ubuntu before you try.
Do it not because it is easy, but because it is hard. See what kind of computing stuff you're made of.

posts.title: The gift of ambition
posts.summary: DHH argues that ambition is a rare and essential gift that gives work meaning, contrasting it with the existential dread of bullshit jobs. He pushes back against the idea that his 'calm company' philosophy at 37signals means coasting, insisting that calm is a method for achieving ambitious goals, not an end in itself. He defines ambition as a leap of faith that requires uncertainty and the possibility of failure. While acknowledging that leaders like Musk and Jobs inspire through ambitious projects, he argues you don't need to be abrasive to stir people's hearts. The core tension he explores is that people crave challenge and discomfort even as they pursue comfort and security.
posts.key_insight: A calm company isn't about coasting — it's a disciplined method for pursuing ambitious goals, because meaningful work requires uncertainty and the possibility of failure.

topQuotes.quote: I'd rather work in place where it was crazy all the time, if we're trying to get somewhere, than I'd work somewhere perfectly calm that's just spinning the wheels.
It's the lack of ambition that fuels the malaise of a bullshit job.
Being ambitious and calm is like being smooth and fast. Big, erratic, dramatic movements might feel like they're getting you somewhere, but the stop watch usually reveals the opposite.

posts.title: Villains may live long enough to become heroes
posts.summary: DHH traces the redemption arcs of tech companies he once opposed, focusing on Microsoft and Meta. Microsoft transformed from the sworn enemy of open source in the 1990s to one of its biggest champions through GitHub, VSCode, and WSL. Meta, through Zuckerberg's embrace of open-source AI with Llama 3, now serves as a critical counterweight to closed AI cartels and government-controlled content moderation. DHH argues that self-interest, not altruism, drives these positive shifts, invoking Adam Smith to make the case that competition and free markets matter more than trusting any single company's motives. He concludes that defending free markets, free speech, and open AI outweighs his earlier concerns about targeted advertising.
posts.key_insight: Tech companies should be judged by their current alignment with open markets and open source, not by past sins — and self-interested competition, not regulation or altruism, is the most reliable guardian of freedom.

topQuotes.quote: Zuckerberg's embrace of open source AI is an invaluable counter to the cartel-adjacent bullshit of 'AI safety & ethics' that would see the likes of OpenAI and Google conspire with governments around the world to determine what math should be allowed to predict the next token.
Whatever trust I may once have had in objective third-party 'fact checkers' have long since evaporated from the catastrophic track record of these anything-but-neutral, would-be arbiters of truth.
We don't need altruism to save us, we need incentives. We need competition. We need free markets for products, platforms, services, ideas, speech, and AI.

posts.title: New zine: How Git Works!
posts.summary: Julia Evans announces her new zine 'How Git Works,' designed for people who've used git for years but still find it intimidating. She explains that git's confusing terminology, misleading messages, and inconsistent behavior make it genuinely hard to learn from usage alone, but once you understand the internal logic, dealing with its quirks becomes routine. The zine focuses on git's observable behavior rather than deep internals, connecting what users see in the UI to what's happening under the hood. She worked with collaborators for 8 months and incorporated feedback from 66 beta readers to refine the explanations.
posts.key_insight: Git's real difficulty isn't its internals but the gap between its confusing UI messages and the straightforward logic underneath — once you bridge that gap, the fear disappears.

topQuotes.quote: I think it sucks to be afraid of the tools that you use in your work every day!
I wrote this zine for people who have been using git for years and are still afraid of it.
The more I heard from people how about how confusing they find git, the more it became clear that git really does not make it easy to figure out what its internal logic is just by using it.

posts.title: Base-Model RAM in Apple Devices
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Apple's base-model RAM in Macs has stagnated for over a decade, a problem now made more conspicuous by the demands of on-device AI features. He notes that iMacs have been stuck at 8 GB since 2012 and MacBook Airs since 2017, breaking Apple's historical pattern of regular RAM increases. While acknowledging that Apple silicon's memory efficiency may effectively double the usable performance per GB, he contends that more RAM is always better and more future-proof. The absurdity is underscored by the fact that all iPhone 16 models will match base-model Macs at 8 GB of RAM.
posts.key_insight: Apple's base-model Mac RAM has stagnated at 8 GB for over a decade, and the arrival of RAM-hungry on-device AI makes this penny-pinching increasingly untenable.

topQuotes.quote: Memory is like an orgasm. It's a lot better if you don't have to fake it.
It's impossible to look at Schaub's charts and not see that regular increases in base RAM effectively stopped when Tim Cook took over as CEO.
It's downright bizarre to think that come this fall, all iPhone 16 models will sport as much RAM as base model Macs.

posts.title: Safe Space or Trap? Creating Software like DuckDB in Academic Institutions
posts.summary: Hannes Mühleisen traces DuckDB's origin from CWI, the Dutch National Research Lab where Python was also invented, explaining how academic environments can be both safe spaces and traps for software development. He describes DuckDB's core philosophy: an in-process, zero-dependency analytical database that prioritizes the full user experience over just the query engine internals. He draws parallels to Python's origin story at CWI, including similar institutional tensions when research software gains real-world traction. The talk concludes with hard-won lessons about funding open-source companies, arguing for bootstrapping over venture capital to maintain project integrity.
posts.key_insight: Building serious software in academia requires gambling your career on impact over papers, and sustaining it requires escaping institutional constraints through bootstrapping rather than surrendering control to venture capital.

topQuotes.quote: Bootstrapping is this weird idea where you work for money. It's like, you know, you provide a service, and people give you money. It's totally mind-blowing.
At some point there's going to be a board that says either we fire you or you change the licence, and there's just not a lot you can do about it.
You need 60 computers to beat one, just because of the overhead of distribution.

posts.title: As we forgive those who trespass against us
posts.summary: DHH reflects on the aftermath of 37signals' 2021 decision to ban political discussions at work, now vindicated by Google adopting a similar policy. He describes the internet mob backlash as ultimately a gift that revealed true friends and allies. He argues that the social justice movement of the late 2010s swept up many well-meaning people into divisive ideology, and that the path forward is forgiveness rather than retaliation. He calls for a broad societal rejection of the ideas themselves while extending grace to individuals who were temporarily caught up in them.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way to defeat a divisive ideology is not retaliation against its followers but broad forgiveness that makes it easy for people to walk away from bad ideas.

topQuotes.quote: It kept eating its own for ever-smaller transgressions against an ever-shifting doctrine.
In the moment, it was awful, but in retrospect, it was a gift.
It swept up so many otherwise kind and caring people into an ideology predicated on dividing us all into oppressors/oppressed, privileged/not, and other false dichotomies and identities.

posts.title: The Cloud Storage Triad: Latency, Cost, Durability
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini argues that object storage (S3, GCS) is becoming the future of database persistence, but current systems require write caches due to latency and atomicity limitations. He introduces the 'LCD theorem'—a tradeoff between Latency, Cost, and Durability when persisting directly to object storage. With improvements like S3 Express and compare-and-swap operations becoming standard, he believes write caches will become unnecessary. He's building SlateDB, a cloud-native LSM tree database that persists directly to object storage, using write batching to manage costs while letting clients choose their tradeoff between waiting for durability or accepting async writes.
posts.key_insight: As object storage gains low latency and atomic operations, databases can persist directly to it by batching writes—but clients must explicitly choose between latency, cost, and durability.

topQuotes.quote: I believe that the future of database persistence is object storage—S3, Google Cloud Storage, and so on.
I don't think we'll need these write caches in the future. Object storage is changing fast.
This triad of latency, cost, and durability presents an easy mental model; something I'm facetiously calling the latency, cost, durability (LCD) theorem.

posts.title: Making a Mountain Out of Molehill-Sized M4 News
posts.summary: Gruber argues that Bloomberg's Mark Gurman dramatically overhyped a report about Apple's M4 chip plans, framing routine annual updates as a reactionary 'overhaul' driven by AI panic and declining Mac sales. He methodically lays out how Apple silicon has been settling into a predictable annual cadence since the M1 launch, and that everything in Gurman's report — updated Macs across the lineup, faster GPUs, bigger Neural Engines, more RAM — is the natural progression of the platform, not a strategic pivot. He pushes back on the narrative that Mac sales declined due to tepid M3 upgrades, attributing the dip instead to Covid-era laptop sales being pulled forward. The actual story, Gruber contends, is simply that Apple has successfully gotten its Mac lineup onto an annual refresh cycle.
posts.key_insight: Bloomberg's breathless framing of routine annual Mac updates as an AI-driven overhaul says more about the media's AI hype cycle than about Apple's actual product strategy.

topQuotes.quote: This year, product rumors need only three things: a noun, a verb, and 'AI'.

The actual story is 'the entire Mac product line is set for annual speed-bump Apple silicon updates' — not 'Mac sales are in the tank and Apple is overhauling the whole product line to change its focus to AI.'

That they will provide Apple with AI-related performance to brag about just means they'll have faster GPUs and bigger Neural Engines, which is exactly how Apple silicon has been evolving year-over-year for 15 years.

posts.title: Upcoming Data Engineering Course on Coursera!
posts.summary: Joe Reis announces his upcoming Data Engineering Specialization course on Coursera in partnership with deeplearning.ai and Andrew Ng, describing the intensive recording process. He explains this major project will delay his data modeling book but considers it worthwhile given the potential to impact hundreds of thousands or millions of learners worldwide.
posts.key_insight: Balancing theoretical foundations with hands-on practice is the ideal approach to data engineering education, and scaling that education globally justifies significant personal investment.

topQuotes.quote: Too much theory, and you lose hands-on capabilities. Too much hands-on, and you lose sight of the big picture.
They're not exactly useless, but they aren't universally useful.
The fact that Andrew Ng and the deeplearning.ai crew are investing a lot of resources into this course should tell you all you need to know.

posts.title: Do Software Companies Actually Have
posts.summary: The post argues that the classic Silicon Valley story—software has near-zero marginal costs and therefore huge profits—breaks down when you account for the ongoing development needed to keep subscription software viable. High gross margins in SaaS mask the reality that continuous product updates function like cost of goods, keeping operating margins negative for many companies. Stancil calls for a new business theory that treats perpetual redesign as a core cost rather than a temporary growth investment.
posts.key_insight: SaaS profitability is overstated because continual product development is effectively a cost of goods, making the promised high margins and eventual windfall largely illusory.

topQuotes.quote: But what if the theory is wrong? What if the most important cornerstone in Silicon Valley is actually a teetering liability?
In other words, software development costs are COGS.

We should build a new theory that acknowledges that software can’t be sold unless it’s continually being redesigned and updated, that software margins aren’t actually that high, and that step three is mostly a theoretical fantasy.

posts.title: We are a place of business
posts.summary: DHH applauds Google's decision to immediately fire 28 employees who occupied the Google Cloud CEO's office to protest the company's contract with Israel. He frames this as a vindication of the position he and Basecamp took three years earlier: that workplaces should not be platforms for political activism. He argues that Google's follow-up policy banning political activism at work signals that corporate America has passed the peak of ideological capture. DHH connects this to a broader pattern including Coinbase's similar stance and sees it as evidence that common-sense workplace norms are being restored.
posts.key_insight: Google's swift firing of activist employees and adoption of a no-politics-at-work policy validates what DHH and others advocated years earlier: workplaces are for business, not political platforms.

topQuotes.quote: Anyone watching that shit show would be right to wonder whether one of America's great technology companies had fallen completely into the hands of the new theocracy.

Three years ago, taking a common-sense position like this would have been met with drama and outage in the media and on Twitter. Today I doubt it'll bring more than a ripple outside of a few activist echo chambers on Mastodon. Amazing progress!

But then the most amazing thing happened. There was no drawn-out investigation. No saccharine statements about employee's rights to occupy offices, preventing work from happening, or advance their political agenda at work. Nope. They were just fired. Immediately. All 28 of them.

posts.title: Copyleft licenses are not “restrictive”
posts.summary: DeVault argues that copyleft licenses should not be called 'restrictive' compared to permissive licenses, as this framing reflects a shallow understanding of freedom. He distinguishes between obligations (which both license types impose) and restrictions (which no FOSS license imposes), arguing that copyleft's obligations exist to guarantee rights. Drawing on political philosophy and the concept of positive liberty, he contends that freedom emerges from obligations imposed on power, making copyleft licenses more free than permissive ones. He suggests 'reciprocal' and 'non-reciprocal' would be better terminology than 'copyleft' and 'permissive.'
posts.key_insight: Copyleft licenses are not restrictive but reciprocal — they create freedom by imposing obligations on power, just as constitutional rights create freedom by constraining government.

topQuotes.quote: Freedom without obligation is a contradiction. Freedom emerges from obligations, specifically obligations imposed on power.
Permissive licenses and copyleft licenses are both free software, but only the latter provides a guarantee of rights, and while both might be free only the latter provides freedom.
Copyleft is predicated on a deeper understanding of freedom in which freedom is a positive guarantee of rights.

posts.title: Face the Critic: Ian Betteridge Edition
posts.summary: Gruber responds to criticism that his stance on tracking has shifted from opposing Meta's tracking to defending it. He clarifies that he has always opposed non-consensual tracking specifically, not tracking itself, which is why he supports Apple's App Tracking Transparency — it requires consent rather than banning tracking outright. He argues Meta's 'Pay or OK' model in the EU gives users a clear, fair choice, and that the EU's opposition to it is paternalistic. He contends that the EU's proper role is ensuring informed consent and user control over data, not deciding on behalf of citizens that all tracking is inherently wrong.
posts.key_insight: Privacy is about informed consent and user control, not about banning tracking entirely — and governments that override individual choice in the name of privacy are being paternalistic, not protective.

topQuotes.quote: The privacy zealots leading this crusade in the EU do not think people are smart, and do not think they should be trusted to make these decisions for themselves.
It's paternalistic — infantilizing even — to believe that government bureaucrats should take these decisions out of the hands of EU citizens.
It turns out that when given the clear choice, the overwhelming majority of EU denizens prefer to use Meta's platforms free-of-charge with targeted ads.

posts.title: European Data Protection Board Goes There, Rules Against Meta’s ‘Pay or OK’ Model
posts.summary: Gruber examines the European Data Protection Board's draft ruling against Meta's 'pay or consent to tracking' model, which would require large platforms to offer a free tier without behavioral advertising. He argues this is a radical and selectively enforced stance, noting that EU-based companies like Der Spiegel and Spotify use identical or similar models without facing scrutiny. He outlines Meta's limited compliance options, each of which carries significant business risk, and suggests that if Meta caves to reduced ARPU in the EU, regulators worldwide will demand the same concessions. Gruber concludes that EU regulators may be surprised by how Meta responds.
posts.key_insight: The EU's selective enforcement of 'pay or consent' rules against large non-EU platforms while ignoring identical practices by domestic companies reveals the regulatory regime as protectionism masquerading as privacy advocacy.

topQuotes.quote: They're not saying 'pay or OK' is illegal. They're saying it's illegal only if you're a big company from outside the EU with a very popular platform.
Christ, they don't even let you look at their homepage without paying or consenting to targeted ads.
But Spotify is an EU company, so of course it wasn't designated as a 'gatekeeper' by the protection racketeers running the European Commission.

posts.title: Exploring altered states of consciousness
posts.summary: Nadia draws parallels between psychedelics, unmedicated childbirth, and jhanas — eight altered states of consciousness accessed through meditation — arguing all are 'exotic states of consciousness' that remain poorly understood by mainstream science. Her personal experience with unmedicated birth revealed how psychology influences physical outcomes, which opened her mind to jhana practitioners' claims of achieving MDMA- or LSD-like states through concentration alone. Despite deep skepticism, she attended a Jhourney retreat and within hours experienced profound altered states using only her mind. She highlights that only three notable academic studies on jhanas exist, and calls for more secular research into a phenomenon that could have significant therapeutic implications.
posts.key_insight: The jhanas represent a profound and accessible altered state of consciousness that mainstream science has barely studied, echoing the decades-long lag in understanding psychedelics and the psychology of childbirth.

topQuotes.quote: Within my first hour of practice, I found myself in the equivalent of an MDMA roll. With ten hours of practice, I had an out-of-body experience. Less than fifteen hours, a psychedelic experience that rivaled what might be had on LSD. All of this took place using only concentration.
Strangest to me, as an outsider, is that the jhanas are still firmly lodged in meditation circles and norms, rather than studied as a form of behavioral therapy.

Because birth manifests so clearly in the body, I had assumed it was something that happened to me – like getting the flu, or arthritis – rather than something could be influenced, to some degree, by my mind.

posts.title: PostgreSQL Extensions or Protocols: Architecture Roulette
posts.summary: Riccomini examines two competing architectural approaches for PostgreSQL-compatible databases: building extensions on top of PostgreSQL's query engine versus implementing protocol compatibility while building a new engine from scratch. He argues against the view that PostgreSQL is a 'near-perfect kernel,' noting that it constrains what you can build. With maturing components like DataFusion, Velox, and RocksDB, building custom databases is more feasible than ever. He predicts PostgreSQL's protocol, not its query engine, will be its most defining characteristic, while acknowledging both approaches will coexist in the market.
posts.key_insight: PostgreSQL's wire protocol and SQL dialect—not its query engine—will ultimately be its most valuable and enduring contribution to the database ecosystem.

topQuotes.quote: The more control you want, the more PostgreSQL is going to feel like a straight jacket.
Tying yourself to PostgreSQL is always going to be more limiting than building your own database. You're limited by what PostgreSQL can offer you.
My gut says that PostgreSQL's protocol, not its query engine, will be its most defining characteristic.

posts.title: Forcing master to main was a good faith exploit
posts.summary: DHH reflects on the tech industry's shift from 'master' to 'main' in Git branch naming, arguing he went along with it in good faith but now views it as an exploit of collective goodwill. He says the change accomplished nothing meaningful because the demands for linguistic changes never stop. He frames his compliance as naivety and promises to resist similar pressures in the future, using the metaphor of patching a security vulnerability.
posts.key_insight: DHH frames the master-to-main renaming as a cautionary tale about how good-faith compliance with symbolic demands leads not to resolution but to escalating expectations, and vows to resist such pressures going forward.

topQuotes.quote: Changing master to main changed less than nothing.
This was a classic exploit of good faith, and I fell for it.
People are not going to be this gullible twice. I am not going to be this gullible twice.

posts.title: Updates on a Lot of Content
posts.summary: Joe Reis provides a personal update on his various content projects including his upcoming data engineering course, data modeling book, and podcast appearances. He shares curated weekend reads covering LLM code generation benchmarks, Google's AI announcements, Spotify's data platform, and the impact of AI on education.
posts.key_insight: While the data industry obsesses over structured data and LLM code generation, the real untapped opportunity lies in the massive amounts of unstructured data—images and video—sitting idle on hard drives.

topQuotes.quote: I'm very curious whether generative AI will augment the learning experience or whether it's the road to Idiocracy.
There's a massive world of unstructured datasets out there, especially images and video. Most of this data just sits idle on hard drives. What are we doing to unlock meaning and value from this data?
Code generation is hard, and I'm hearing mixed feelings about it from developers. Some like it, some hate it, and others are meh. But, like it or not, code generation is here to stay.

posts.title: Playercoachai Investor Update Q1
posts.summary: Stancil uses a mock investor update for a fictional AI startup to satirize how startups contort narratives around growth, churn, and fundraising readiness. The piece argues that raising rounds to “pass the next grade” is a trap; money doesn’t fix a broken business, and founders should fundraise only when the current company can win the next stage.
posts.key_insight: Fundraising should validate that the current business can scale through the next stage, not mask fundamental weaknesses with fresh capital.

topQuotes.quote: A failing chatbot is still a failing chatbot, with or without the backing of some guy named David or Scott.
If foundercanclose an A round, butcan’t escape the feelingthat something will have to change to close the eventual B, don’t do the A.
Money gives them resources, but it doesn’t give them a new market, a new product, or a new idea.

posts.title: Imperfections create connections
posts.summary: DHH argues that imperfections and quirks in products create deeper emotional connections than polished perfection does. He draws parallels between the Porsche 911's rear engine, mechanical watches, and vintage computers to explain why he's fallen in love with the Framework 13 laptop running Linux. Despite acknowledging its drawbacks compared to modern MacBooks—inferior battery life, adequate-but-not-great screen, less polish—he finds the machine's quirky design choices and compromises endearing. He encourages programmers to try Linux, arguing it's finally good enough for desktop use, and suggests that embracing imperfection can lead to a more meaningful computing experience.
posts.key_insight: Perfection breeds indifference while imperfections create the friction and character that forge genuine emotional connections with the tools we use.

topQuotes.quote: The mac in particular has been massaged to within an inch of perfection, and has thus become harder to connect with.
The engine is in wrong place in a Porsche 911. It's hanging out the back, swinging the car like a pendulum. And that's key to why it's the most iconic sports car ever made.
I've smirked about 'This Is The Year of Linux on the Desktop' for over twenty years, but now that I've been actually running it for over a month, I've realized it's actually here.

posts.title: Enough problems to go around
posts.summary: DHH argues that companies with too little meaningful work are worse than those with too much. Drawing on David Graeber's Bullshit Jobs thesis, he contends that when real problems are scarce, people invent fictitious ones that drain morale. He acknowledges the paradox that humans want lighter loads but become unhappy when they achieve them. His prescription: keep teams slightly understaffed relative to meaningful problems, because idle knowledge workers will fill the void with made-up busywork.
posts.key_insight: Keep your team slightly understaffed relative to meaningful problems — idle knowledge workers don't rest, they invent bullshit to fill the void.

topQuotes.quote: Without enough real problems to go around, humans are prone to invent fictitious and dreadful ones.
Mojito island, all the time, is a curse, not a blessing.
The worst kind of company is usually not the one where there's too much real work to do, but the kind where there's not enough.

posts.title: Don't let Alloy facts make your specs a fiction
posts.summary: The post warns against overusing Alloy's 'fact' keyword to constrain specification models. While facts prevent the model checker from generating certain states, this can backfire when you need to verify that your software correctly handles invalid inputs. The author argues that 'making illegal states unrepresentable' works well in programming but creates problems in specification because specs must model both the software and its environment. Using predicates instead of facts keeps constraints explicit and testable. Facts should be reserved for narrowing problem scope, eliminating uninteresting cases, optimization, or expressing relationships Alloy can't handle natively.
posts.key_insight: In formal specification, making illegal states unrepresentable through facts prevents you from verifying that your software correctly handles those illegal states when they occur in the real world.

topQuotes.quote: If you cannot represent the illegal state, you cannot represent the world creating an illegal state that your software needs to handle.
Constraints are dangerous because you need error states in order to check that your program avoids error states.
Normally in programming languages, 'making illegal states unrepresentable' is a good thing. But specification covers both the software you are writing and the environment the software is running in.

posts.title:
Uncovering the Seams in Mainframes for Incremental
  Modernisation
posts.summary: This article describes Thoughtworks' approach to incrementally migrating a 40-year-old, 7-million-line-of-code mainframe system to the cloud for a UK client's data platform. Rather than attempting a risky 'Big Bang' cutover, they advocated for identifying 'seams'—junction points in the legacy code where new behavior could be inserted with minimal changes. The approach involved dual-running both systems in parallel, comparing outputs to verify behavioral parity, then gradually retiring mainframe capabilities. The team identified multiple seam types: external interfaces (batch inputs, APIs), internal interfaces (database readers/writers, batch pipeline handoffs), and granular data characteristics. While Thoughtworks was only involved for the first year and didn't reach production, they validated multiple approaches for incremental legacy displacement.
posts.key_insight: Successful mainframe modernization requires identifying architectural seams—natural boundaries in legacy code—that enable incremental migration with dual-run verification, rather than risky wholesale replacement.

topQuotes.quote: Unless you take steps to abstract the schema when replicating a database, then your new cloud products will be coupled to the legacy schema as soon as they're built.

We were wary of the risks and potential pitfalls of a Big Design Up Front, so we advised our client to work on a 'just enough, and just in time' upfront design, with engineering during the first phase.
How much this will cost depends on various factors, but you cannot expect to save on costs via dual running two systems in parallel.

posts.title: You're not guaranteed a spot on the team
posts.summary: DHH argues that while the 'we're like family' rhetoric at work is rightly criticized, people can't then expect that underperforming colleagues should be protected from being cut. He endorses Netflix's 'professional team' metaphor, where people earn their spot through competence and fit. Using soccer as an extended analogy, he argues that finding the right level matters more than clinging to a team where you don't belong. He frames letting someone go as potentially merciful rather than cruel, and positions decisive personnel decisions as a manager's most important job.
posts.key_insight: Rejecting the 'family' metaphor at work means accepting the 'team' metaphor's corollary: not everyone is guaranteed a spot, and decisive cuts are a manager's most important responsibility.

topQuotes.quote: I've always hated the saying "we're like family here" when it comes to work. Because it's obviously not true, and it's usually cynically invoked by management to entice an undue obligation of sacrifice.
You can't have it both ways. You can't scorn the use of "we're a family" and then also expect that colleagues who can't keep up are spared the cut.
Cutting them from the team would be an act of mercy, not cruelty.

posts.title: Notes on git's error messages
posts.summary: Julia Evans examines several confusing Git error messages, explaining why they're misleading and what she does to work around them. She covers messages from git push, git pull, git checkout, git switch, git rebase, and git clean, noting patterns like ambiguous references to HEAD and 'them' that change meaning depending on context. She acknowledges that improving error messages is genuinely difficult due to translation requirements, funding challenges, and the difficulty of knowing whether a new message is actually better. The post reveals that even experienced Git users develop workarounds rather than truly understanding every message, and that much confusion stems from Git's dual-purpose commands and inconsistent terminology.
posts.key_insight: Git's error messages are confusing not because users lack knowledge, but because the messages omit context, use inconsistent terminology, and overload users with options instead of guiding them toward the most likely fix.

topQuotes.quote: Sometimes I actually am confused by the error messages, I'm just used to being confused.
90% of the time when a git error message says 'reference' I just mentally replace it with 'branch' in my head.
My impression is that a lot of git users have literally no idea what a 'reference' is in git.

posts.title: FDO's conduct enforcement actions regarding Vaxry
posts.summary: Drew DeVault defends freedesktop.org's decision to ban Hyprland maintainer Vaxry from FDO community spaces, providing detailed context on the events leading to the ban. He argues that FDO's conduct enforcement team acted with restraint and within their mandate, while Vaxry escalated the situation by construing a polite warning as threats, minimizing his own hate speech, and threatening legal action. DeVault recounts his own failed private efforts over 18 months to help Vaxry change his behavior, culminating in Vaxry expressing openness to arguments for genocide. He calls on the broader Linux community to protect itself from the toxic culture Hyprland has fostered and condemns the harassment directed at the FDO officer involved.
posts.key_insight: When a project leader refuses every opportunity to reform toxic community norms, the broader open source ecosystem has both the right and obligation to isolate that community to protect everyone else.

topQuotes.quote: The last thing he said to me was 'I do believe there could be arguments to sway my opinion towards genocide'.
The fact of the matter is that Hyprland remains a toxic community, enabled and encouraged by its toxic leadership, namely Vaxry.
There's nothing left to do but to build a fence around Hyprland and protect the rest of the community from them.

posts.title: From the Department of Spending Tim Cook’s Money: Online Photo Storage Is Surely Expensive to Offer, but Apple Should Offer More
posts.summary: Gruber follows up on his criticism of Apple's commercial telling iPhone users not to worry about photo storage, arguing that iCloud's free 5 GB tier has been unchanged since Steve Jobs announced it in 2011. He compares Apple and Google's cloud storage pricing, finding them largely comparable at paid tiers but noting Google offers 3x more free storage. He argues that iCloud storage tiers should be periodically adjusted for 'inflation' — accounting for larger device storage, higher resolution photos and video, and growing user expectations. While acknowledging that offering more free storage to over a billion users would be expensive, he contends that if Apple is going to run ads telling users not to worry about photo storage, they should back that up with more generous iCloud offerings.
posts.key_insight: Apple's iCloud free tier has been frozen at 5 GB since 2011 while device storage and media file sizes have ballooned, making it hypocritical for Apple to advertise worry-free photo storage without actually providing it.

topQuotes.quote: Like the stingy U.S. minimum wage — which was last increased, to $7.25/hour, in 2009 — these tiers ought to be adjusted for 'inflation' periodically, but aren't.
If Apple really wants iPhone users not to worry about photo storage, they should offer more with iCloud, cost-to-Apple be damned.

It's very easy for me and you to just declare that Apple ought to just foot the bill to offer more storage for over a billion users worldwide, but we're not the ones making new TV commercials telling iPhone 15 users they needn't worry about photo storage.

posts.title: Guest post: Who gets to live in techno-utopia? Disability rights, eugenics, and effective altruism
posts.summary: Victor Zhenyi Wang, a data scientist with a disability, recounts attending a rationalist conference where philosopher Jonathan Anomaly presented on 'liberal eugenics' - voluntary genetic selection for traits like intelligence - to an uncritical audience. Wang challenges the utilitarian premise that disabled lives are 'worse off,' citing disability rights advocates who argue their lives are valuable and that disability-related challenges stem from societal structures, not inherent deficits. He critiques how metrics like QALYs inherit society's biases against disabled people and how cost-benefit analysis has historically been weaponized to deny disabled people basic rights and accommodations. Wang warns that techno-utopian visions built on these frameworks would systematically marginalize disabled people, returning to an era of institutionalization.
posts.key_insight: The utilitarian logic underlying effective altruism and techno-optimism, when combined with cost-benefit analysis, creates a framework that systematically devalues disabled lives and has historically been used to deny them basic rights and dignity.

topQuotes.quote: It is a surreal experience to be in a room full of people who believe that they would like to do good in the world and at the same time be a person who they would prefer not to exist at all.
It is tempting and beyond that terribly easy to use the language of cost effectiveness as an instrument of dispossession.
This metric is going to inherit all the biases society already has against disabled people.

posts.title: More Database Commoditization, I’m Caving on Rust, Zig Has Some Growing up To Do, and More...
posts.summary: Chris Riccomini returns from a break to discuss his renewed attempt at learning Rust, reflecting on the database commoditization conversation sparked by his previous post and Jack Vanlightly's thoughtful follow-up. He describes his 2022 failure with Rust during Advent of Code and how GPT-4 and Gemini have made his current learning experience dramatically better. While he appreciates Rust's mature ecosystem and excellent documentation, he criticizes its thin standard library that requires external packages for basic functionality. He contrasts Rust with Zig, noting that despite preferring Zig's philosophy, its immature ecosystem and questionable package management decisions make it harder to adopt.
posts.key_insight: Despite philosophical preferences for alternatives like Zig, ecosystem maturity and tooling quality ultimately matter more than language design when choosing systems programming languages.

topQuotes.quote: How do you know if someone writes Rust? They will tell you.
Rust is the opposite of what I want; it's a thick language with a thin standard library.
People have been making jokes about node_modules for a decade now, but this problem is just as bad in Rust codebases I've seen.

posts.title: New Courses
posts.summary: This newsletter edition announces a new executive coaching course for data professionals looking to advance to leadership roles, discusses analytical data modeling approaches from a community session, and curates weekend reads including the critical xz Utils backdoor security incident and Amazon's 'AI' grocery stores that were actually powered by human contractors.
posts.key_insight: Career advancement in data requires intentional effort and mentorship, while the field itself continues to grapple with the gap between AI hype and reality.

topQuotes.quote: Center of Excellence is a four-letter word in some places, and they're often centers of mediocrity, at best.
When van life became 'cool' during the pandemic, I felt a mix of nostalgia and dread...These poor saps bought into some weird social media dream they'd later regret.
Needless to say, there were as many correct answers as there were people in attendance. Everyone's situation differs, and there's not a truly one-size-fits-all approach to analytical data modeling.
